column7: testimony

Being a member of the RLDS Church since eight years old, I grew up knowing
the Lord Jesus. In my late teens I decided I knew better than the Lord how to
run my life, so, for a period of two or three years I wandered from the Lord's
ways. Only to return to what I know is the only way to live.
In a very repentant attitude I again joined the saints to help establish
God's church here on earth. For twenty-five years, I have joyeously tried to
be and do as Christ.
When I was about twenty-seven, I believe that was the first time the idea

of women in the priesthood came to my mind. At the time, it was very preposter-
ous to me. How could such a thing come about? Yet over the years as I worked

for and served the Lord in many avenues, I became aware that it was the willing
souls that were called to the work. Not the rich and famous - the wise and
learned, but those who could feel and love as our Lord Jesus Christ. So, as I
worked with thought over the years, I truly began to see that it was necessary
to bring about God's kingdom. At the Stake Conference, before the Revalation
was into being, I was compelled to stand on our conference floor and share the
spirit that would not let me sit. At that time I shared "that the time of women
being called into the priesthood was now - another time would be too late". I
felt the urgency of this with such impact that I wanted to shout. The authority
within me was overbearing. I was very happy I responded to speak, it's not
easy, but sometimes you can't do anything else.
At the Spring World Conference in 1984, women were calle through the Divine
Revelation. At that time I recieved a phone call from my daughter-in-law who
had just been informed of the revelation through a phone call. I will never
forget how I felt as she told me. I had two distinctive feelings at the same
time. One of elation, and one of humility. I remember laying on my kitchen

cupboard and crying. I was overjoyed. I recieved a lot of recrimination be-
cause of the way I believed, but I knew it to be of God without a doubt.

In the late summer of 1984, one evening, I was reading the scriptures and
the power of the Holy Spirit came in such power that I stood and heard "Diane,
you're called to my work. You are my body. You're my hands and feet.' 1 I could
feel the spirit pour through me, and I felt wonderful. I decided only to share
this with my .daughter as I wanted someone to know of my experience and yet let
the spirit work through the proper channels to bring this about. I also asked
her not to tell anyone.
At the time our pastor had spoken openly that he would not call any women.
So, the door was really closed in that area. I continued to do what I felt was
the work the Lord was calling me to. I must admit, my heart was heavy. Then,
we recieved a new pastor, and in the spring of 1986, I was called by Elder
Francis Porter to the office of Teacher. My husband, not of the priesthood,
and not believing in the revelation, asked me not to give him my answer for two
months, which I said I would do. During this time he became very antagonizing
and vertuallytried every way to discourage me, but, I had no answer but yes.

At the Fall Seattle Stake Conference my name was submitted to the confer-
ence and I shared my testimony and was approved.

I was ordained a Teacher in Seattle Stake at Highland Park Congregation on Nov-
ember 16, 1986. Robert Slasor, a Seventy from Sunnyvale, Ca, was the spokes-
man and Franz Winegar, Evangelist, assisted. Elder Francis Porter presided, &

Elder Ruth Winegar gave the morning message which was-
We have gathered today for the ordination of Diane Akers to the office of

Teacher. In this ordination is symbolized the outstretched hands of God con-
ferming power and authority to represent Him and His church. On Diane's part,

it displays her acceptance of responsibility tobecome a servant and leader in

holiness and all good works. This call emerges up of not only the divine gifts
that she has recieved from her Heavenly Father, but also as a result of her
faithfulness to this point in serving the Christ. This is her stewardship.
We're all called to magnify the gifts of God entrusted to them and failure to
do this is an affront to God and a loss to the church and society. I'm speaking
to the congregation regarding your calling. One of the greatest callings is to
dscipleship which comes to everyone on earth.
Jesus said "follow me and I will make you fishers of men." We answer that
call and give our dedication with our baptism and membership in to chruch. And
in one sense when we see Diane ordained today by the laying on of hands, those
who have accepted the call to membership may see in a measure their ordination
to the priesthood of the leader and to the followers of Jesus Christ. And in
that, they may also become servants and we also dedicate ourselves to God's
purpose. The ordained person, as Diane will be, is not just an individual
minister, but she is an enabler to others to perform the gifts which God has
given them. And I know that Diane has served in a way for many, many years
without benefit of ordination. She has served in the role that is required by
the teacher in many ways. Now, this is the formal confirming-of authority by
the church. As you have heard of Diane's call in the past, you have raised your
hand as an act of accepting her in this role and of supporting her in her duties
of her office and allowing her to function in that role in your homes and in
your lives. In other words, by the call of God of her ordination she is being

granted authority to act for God and the church in different specified respon-
sibilities. And as to the God-given power that has been promised to her to per-
form these acts and ordination, and grant her authority. There are really two

kinds of authority they say we have in the priesthood. One is the authority of
the church to us, the other power comes to us from within as we have answered

the call of the Lord, the manner of our life has shown that we have lived the
life expected of us and therefore can expect God to bless us. They say that
Jesus taught as one having authority. I think Diane has shown for many, many
years her authority and love for all people and has never sensed to put herself
out to help them and this envolves people of all ages, and all ages have felt
the ministry of Diane. We are challenged at this point to support her in her
responsibilities and to recieve the ministry which she will offer. This office
is one of the most important priesthood offices because they say in our book on
priesthood that they are "the salt of the earth". Diane is the salt of the earth
and she must season the lives of many people to make this growth possible to
the congregation to where this priesthood office id functioning as God intends
it. For she will watch over thchurch and act as a shepard. She's called to re
with and strengthen them, but the members will need to accept her in her role
that she will perform from this calling. The next things we'll be seeing from
this ordination of Diane are very important that we honor her in this office.
In the Doctrine and Covenant it says "if my people will respect the officers I
have called and set in my church, I will respect those offices. If they do not,
they cannot expect the riches of gifts and blessings of direction." I know that
Diane will do her part and I know that this congregation will do theirs, and
respond to her ministry but, remember too that Diane is a person and she will
need ministry from you also. That she won't have time for discouragement and
needs your support, your prayers, and your love. For we all have problems in
life and we all need each other, so I challenge you also as a congregation to
accEpther ministry and to pray for her and loveingly support her as she does

step out on the frontiers of faith as one of the first ordinances in this cong-
regation. So, I trust you to support her, and to live out your own disciple-
ship also.

I then came to the front stand and Robert Slasor said this prayer.

I
Diane, in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ, we place our hands upon your
head to ordain you to the office of Teacher in the priesthood of Aaron. It
is by the movement of the Holy Spirit that God has called you out of the world
and yet has called you into the world, now endowed by His Power to minister for
Him in healing ways.
Sister Diane's testimonies have already been given this day of the love
you have in your soul which flows faith from your presence. So it is with great
joy and humility that I am able to share this morning in this ordination. For
in more ways than you can know, you have touched my life, and you have helped
make this real. There are times when you have brought healing to me as you
have done so far for so many. our Lord has created you with giftedness, some
which perhaps much, of which is yet to be revealed. What God has in mind for
you in your ministry shall unfold as you move forward with Him in the caring
and loving and the feeling of the church. As has been shared this morning in
scripture, you are to move with faith and assurance into those situations where
persons are divide~ where the congregation is divided, you are to stand in the
middle as the Lord Jesus Christ and beacon both sides to come together that they
might be reconciled with Him that the Spirit of the Lord Jesus may be able to
flow within the body so His church may accomplish its mission. In your soul
God has planted a love for Zion. Zion is relationships lived out under the

power of God. The office of teacher is so significant in healing those rela-
tionships in calling people into service and to surrender to the Lord Jesus.

You have been gifted, and you have shown thos gifts in so many ways in your life
already. Sister Diane, know this morning that there is coming to you now by the
fact of this ordination, a power of healing like you have never known before.

You shall be freed under the power of the Spirit, and in the power of your mini-
stry in the office of teacher, you shall be freed by the Lord Jesus to heal and

bring reconcilation in ways that even you shall stand in awe of. So, it is
with joy and humility, with excitement, that we present you to our Father in
prayer. Almighty God, our Father in Heaven, by the authority of the power you
have invested in us, we ordain Diane to the office of Teacher, and I know Father,
that in so many ways in these past few months confirmed this call upon her and
sustained her through moments of loneliness and in those moments of her communion
with you, she wanted to reach out and heal those around her. Our Father, I know
you have given her glimpses of the healing power that she shall have. Now, I
pray unto thee, God our Father, unto the authority of her priesthood that those
glipses may become a vision, and that vision may become a reality. But, I know
too, our Father, of the deep love which she feels for people and know that out
of that love, there are moments when she shall try and bring reconciliation and
try and bring people together and they will refuse to do so. The hurt she feels
for them will be so very, very real. I pray, God, in the moments of her hurting
from your children that she may be sustained by your spirit and shall not be
discouraged, but shall continue to work to bring your brothers& sisters, your
daughters & sons together in harmony with the Lord Jesus. I pray, God, this
morning that she may have an endowment of your spirit that shall allow her to
minister in her own family in ways that are not verbal, but that the power of
her presence of God and the power of your presence within her. I pray before
her in healing ways that you will shall be done in her family as in her extended
family which is the world, God, our Father, I pray that you would help her dream
dreams so large and yet so much within the realm of her ministry. I pray that
she may be quickened with hat spirit of discernment so that she may take the
~!din reaching out to persons that need to be reconciled in you. I pray that
thosewho are hurting and broken-hearted shall be led to her that as an agertof

I, 1
reconciliation for your son Jesus she may be able to bring many unto thee and
shall be the means of healing many of your broken vines. Great God, our Father,
I do pray in your name this day, that the authority and power that this office
of Teacher may be so evident within her by your spirit, that she may be aware
this day that she is not the same as she was before our hands were laid upon
her head. Walk with her, God in a closeness that she has been designed by you
an created to experience a closeness which is different that many are able to
experience different, because she is called to heal, the first of your chosen.
So, God, our Heavenly Father, it is in the name of Jesus and the power of your
Spirit that we now have sent her forth as your servant ordained by your call to
serve your church, and your world.
Amen.

I guess many reasons inspire a person to follow the Lord Jesus. He has
always been very real in my life. I'm thankful for parents, grandparents,
teachers, priesthood members, everyone who has been insturmental about sharing
the Lord with me. It has been through times when my heart has been completely
destroyed by hurt and tragedies, that I have come to completely rely on my Lord.
I have found since ordination, a strength and love for my Lord more profound
than I knew I had. I realize that He is my closest friend, we are totally
ingrained in each other. It's like there~ no separation between Him and me.
I was thinking - Am I engrained in Him, or He in me? I guess through these times
of depths you take on each other. The many times I cried outand the comfortirg
Spirit was always there. The many times when my heart was overflowing with joy
and beauty, he was there to share. What I love most about my Lord is that He
is always with me and this is a promise I can believe in. It's been proven in
my life. I guess no one has been forgiven more, and maybe that's why I love him
so. Maybe that's why I see only beauty in others. Maybe that's why I love
everyone, because of the Divine I see within,

I'm very thankful that I see the world throught the eyes of Christ.
Christ has caused a very special ministry of service in me from a very
early age. I remember at my baptism in Moorhead, Iowa, the song that was sung.
"Bring Them In From the Fields of Sin"
I have always loved doing for others. I have always worl,.God's smile and
loved to.
I have always loved babies._
I have always loveJlife. There have been times when the tears in my eyes
have shadowed that love. But God's gentle spirit has always bouyed me up.
I guess Love is a word that God has caused to be alive in my life. When
and where it happens cannot be dated. It's a multitude of happenings and events
in a person's life. I was created and brought into this earth the fifth of nine
children in 1940. I was raised by humble, loving parents and brothers and
sisters. Being raised in the country automatically cuased a great appreciation
for everything.
Every season had its own special beauty. My favorite time was Harvest.
I guess all that had been sowed and worked on had come to fruitation and the
color and smell and storing and preparation for everything was an exciting,
wonderful time.
One thing I've noticed that when you serve the Lord, he always blesses you
mor than you could ever possibly give.
My first encounter with serving the Lord came as Bible School Director,
and since I had such loving memories of when I was young, Ipatterned my life
after my Oriole leader, teacher and friend - Jeanette Weldon. Then, I became
involved in every area of church work. Sunday school teacher, Women's Leader,
Treasurer, Secretary, Women's Commission, Youth Exchange Director, and Camp Cook.
Cooking has been probably one of my most enjoyable occupations for the Lord.
Every bit of me loves it. It's a wonderful way to get acquainted with everyone

who comes to camp or Reunion and I love meeting people. I have never felt that
qualified as a cook, but being in my home for eighteen years and cooking for my
family, sure helped prepare me. I have cooked for many camps and reunions over
the past fifteen years and it keeps getting better. I thoroughly enjoy it.
Never once have I considered it work.
Since my ordination, I have experienced many rich encounters with the Holy
Spirit. One that really touched me and I so appreciated was at my first sermon.
I was inspired a month before and wrote down everything. As I went to the
front of the church, I felt so calm, that it kind of scared me and I felt so
in control and the authority was there. Once, while I was preparing the service
i became afraid of being so uneducated and God's Spirit assured me that I was
His representative and He would be there in the audience, and not to be afraid.
I have noticed a depth of hurting more. I have become aware of my ministry
which I was not aware of before. I feel more open. I have experienced people
sharing deeper with me. Also I have noticed cooments from encounters. I have
felt healing from touch, I have felt love where my heart just wanted to shout
because of responses. I have been in situations where the flow of God's love
was just continuous. Probably the greatest thi~I've noticed since ordination
is, hew much GhrisV:and :r,ar-e--ehe. How·mu<ch ,He:: 1-oves me· aifi,I I love Him.
Before an Aaronic priesthood Retreat in spring this was an inspiration I
had about the teacher.
I believe the Teacher has the unique ability to love people as Christ did
when He was on earth. No other office has the ability to bring the lost,
lonely and hurt back into the fold as we are responsible to. The gift of healing
without administration is a gift we pass through listening, touch and caring.
The Spirit of Teacher as Christ as our example. We should never be a threat,
but like an oasis on a desert. A watering place for thirsty souls.
I believe the confidinces another shares with us should be very sacred.

We then become the healing agent for their soul. Make the Melchzedick
priesthood aware of our love and concern and needs for different ones, We
are not for ourselves but as ministers of the Lord Jesus Christ. He has called
us to be like Him. Our teacher and example. Did He ever turn anyone away?
Wasn't he drained many times? We are to be that quiet moving force in the world
that will bring about tremendous change for the kingdom. We become the foundation
and stepping stone for many trying to find the way to the kingdom, We are the
initial first step many take, We must make it beautiful, the Lord would want
it no other way,
Come, I'll slnwyou the way to the kingdom. Let me help you my friend.

In Christ's love,
Teacher Diane Akers

column7:
Response of June M. Anderson to questions concerning her
ordination.
1. The South Springfield, Missouri, congregation and the
Springfield district conference both approved my call to the
office of teacher during September, 1985. I was ordained at an
early service in the South Springfield branch on Nov. 17,
by pastor Eldred Spain, High Priest and regional administrator,
1985,

and Ron Anderson, elder.
2. My husband is an elder.
3. Ron and I were elementary school teachers in many remote
villages of Alaska. We wanted to move to Missouri when he
retired from teaching and help strengthen the church at Warsaw.
We had attended church there during summers for many years.
After living at Warsaw two years we moved to Springfield, Mo.,
because of a real estate business Ron was in. We found many
opportunties to grow as we worked with the Brentwood
congregation. The Brentwood building was sold and we merged with
the Westport congregation. The new group was called South
Springfield. Under the leadership of Eldred Spain as pastor
began to flourish. I

we

was inspired by Eldred and Carolyn's
devotion, energy and love for all the people. This group made
rapid changes as people from both congregations bonded and became
a wonderful working force.
Once when Elwin Vest was speaking to the South Springfield
congregation about the women's ministry to come, I felt as if I
was touched on my head by the hand of God. I
be called and accept an office of the priesthood.

knew then I would
4. My service of ordination on Nov. 17, 1985, was planned by
Eldred Spain, my husband and myself, along with several others
who shared the same service. We felt very accepted and ready to
serve. We felt our needs and concerns were supported by all the
congregation present. It remains a strong support in the back of
my mind today.
5. My very first feelings about the ordination of women were of
acceptance, joy and confidence that women certainly could add a
very talented ministry. I would gladly support any women
ordained. Then I thought that if I were ordained I would accept
even if it met with a lot of disapproval, as some predicted it
would. When I knew I was called, I was happy to accept.

(1)

6. After being ordained I found many women asking me if I felt
different as a church member (no), if I felt more valuable in the
eyes of God (1 did notl, or if I found I had more power to do
things. Some people seemed to be wary of accepting my new
but mostly the questions

role,
or comments were supportive and asked
out of a genuine need to know. My husband and I felt closer as
a team when he went to administer or to counsel with someone and
they would ask if I would come also.
7. My immediate familiy has been very reserved about accepting my
ordination. One of my sisters and her family will not support it
at all or discuss church when I am there. They were breaking
away from the church after women were being ordained. At present
I don't know where they are meeting or with whom. Another sister
has reluctantly agreed it might be alright but doesn't mention
it.
My husband's familyhas joyfully accepted my ordination and
given me all the support I could ever need.
After Ron and I returned to Alaska and now attend the
Anchorage congregation we've found some of the people here do not
approve of women in the priesthood. At first those few just
priesthood in Anchorage and
seemed reserved but were friendly. I was the only woman in the
was asked to speak and participate
several times. Most of the people were accepting and so happy
to have a woman here who was part of the new movement.
Since then we have had three more women ordained to the
priesthood in Anchorage. So now some of the dissenters are
breaking away to form their own group.
8. My ministry as Teacher has not changed my life to a great
extent. ~1 find that things I've always believed in and tried to
do as a caring person are the kinds of sharings I continue to
give as a Teacher. Ministry is awareness, living life as best as
possible with other people in our thoughts, helping others
whatever situation

fin

we find ourselves.

9. My concept of ministry has long been the same as I just
stated. Now I've added to it the importance of of the growth of
our church in recognizing the abilities of women to serve in new
capacities. Women have always had loyalty, talent, energy and
gifts. Now they will be more able to share these in
strengthening the church's effort to reach and serve more people.

column7:
WANDA ANDERSEN'S TESTIMONY IN CONNECTION WITH CALL TO TEACHER.
From the time I was a teenager I taught church school classes, played the small
organ for the children's church school worship, and have assumed many other and
varied responsibilities throughout my life, including working with people of
other cultures as an appointee wife.
I have felt that I had made a contribution to the work of the church and that
my ministry had been well accepted by those with whom I worked and ministered.
This was one reason it was difficult for me to accept the idea of women being
called to the priesthood long before it became a reality. I was among those
who expressed the feeling, "Women have ministered for years without priesthood,
why do they need it now?"
I had struggled with this for some time and listened to the pros and cons, but
was quite disturbed when it came before the conference as a revelation. I cried.
Surely the women's libbers" had finally gotten to the Prophet, I thought.
During the next year, I listened to the testimonies of others who had been having the same feelings I had had, but who received experiences that had convinced
them the revelation was from God. I also asked the women I knew who had been
called if they would mind sharing the testimony of their call with me. Because
of the power of the Holy Spirit that seemed to accompany their experiences, I
began to feel that the revelation must be from God; I could support those women
who were called, in their ministry. But as far as I was concerned, I was perfectly content to minister in the "office of member".
As Temple School courses were offered in our area, I attended as many as possible,
eager ta learn and became better equipped to witness, all the time hoping our
Pastor wouldn't get the idea that I was anticipating becoming a priesthood
member. I assured those who were discussing women in the priesthood in my
presence that I was happy serving as I had been and was not interested in being
in the priesthood.
The first Sunday in August, 1986, our Pastor took me aside and told me about my
call to the office of Teacher. I expressed some of my feelings with him and
told him I would have to have a testimony of my own before I could accept the
call, and I did not feel that I had a testimony. He asked me to take some time
to think and pray about it and let him know before the business meeting
weeks what I had decided to do. I appreciated his supportive attitude.

in two
A couple of days later, the Pastor stopped by the house to share more of the
details of my call and see how I was feeling. I had written down a long list of
reasons why I didn't feel I needed to be in the priesthood. He encouraged me
to think mare positively and see what opportunities it might bring. I later
tried ta write some positive reasons for my accepting the call, but had trouble
coming up with any.
I certainly wanted to do whatever God wanted me to do. There was no question
in my mind that I would accept the call IF I was convinced that was what he
wanted me to do and if I could get rid of the negative feelings I had been having. The Pastor's experience in connection with the call helped, but I still
felt the need for a testimony of my own if I were to be expected to minister
as a woman in the priesthood. I reminded myself that lots of calls were
accepted on faith [and it seemed like all the sermons and classes lately
mentioned this same theme) but I felt I couldn't do this and experienced guilt
feelings because my faith seemed to be so weak.

Wanda Andersen's Testimony -2
I dug out all the books on prayer I could find in our home library to read and
at the same time searched the scriptures for the promises that could be found
that said: nAsk and you shall receive", "knock and it shall be opened unto
you'', etc. Christ said this, so he undoubtedly meant it. ''Test his promises'',
I remembered hearing a friend say over and over. If God could let Mike, our
Pastor, know about the call (and another person who had also received confirmation), he surely would let me know, too. I just had to expect to hear from him.
I began to study and pray, and fast in different ways. I didn't know exactly
how to pray to get an answer or how long it would take. I was prepared to pray
for a long time, if necessary. First, I prayed rather generally that God would
show me in some way - whatever way he chose - that this was his will. Then when
that didn't seem to be working (I was getting impatient), I thought I'd try
another suggested approach and prayed specifically that he would confirm the call
to me through another person whom I felt was having a difficult time with the
concept of women in the priesthood. That way we would both have a testimony.
Finally, I felt maybe it was best to leave it up to God to decide how and when,
but kept telling him that I really NEEDED to know his will beFore I could
accept the call.
After about 21⁄2 months of prayer, fasting and tears, Dick and I were scheduled
to clean the church. While straightening up in the foyer, there was one book
out of place laying on the shelf. The title was, "A Slow and Certain Light" by
Elizabeth Elliot. Since no name was written in it, I borrowed it hoping it would
be helpful. The last chapter in particular seemed to impress me. It dealt with
the many and varied ways God uses to call people. "It is reasonable to expect
that God will use whatever means may be appropriate at the time .... We will know
when we need to know, not before .... He knows the best way to get our attention,
and if we are ready to listen or be shown, we will hear or see whatever it is he
has chosen as his means." I knew I would just have to continue ta pray and leave
it in his hands.
I understood that I had a year, if I needed it, to consider the call before I
gave an answer. But at the same time, I thought if the call has come now, and
since we are, as an appointee couple, always subject ta move at any time, it
must mean that God needed someone ta work in the office of Teacher in our present
congregation. So why was it taking so long to get an answer? During all my
soul-searching, I finally came to the conclusion that Gad just might be having
trouble getting through to me because I finally could admit to myself that I was
not only struggling with the call, but I was also still unconsciously struggling
with the issue of women in the priesthood. I had accepted it with my head, but
I hadn't really accepted it with my heart.
About three months from the initial conversation with Mike and the day after we
had returned Fram Regional Conference, Dick asked me if I had listened to Pres.
Smith's talk (in which he mentioned going forward in faith). I told him I had,
but still felt I needed my own testimony before I could accept the call.
The fallowing day I was working at the kitchen sink and thinking about the new
responsibilities I would have as Pastoral Care Commissioner for our congregation and specifically about the visiting program I hoped to organize. I had
just finished saying to myself, nI don't need to be in the priesthood to do
this," when all of a sudden it was as if my thoughts were interrupted with
someone telling me, "You need to accept the call." At the same time, I
received a strong feeling of joy (like I'd never felt before and really can't
describe). All the negative feelings began to leave. These two feelings

Wanda Andersen's Testimony -3
alternated several times during this experience - the awareness of extreme joy
and then the knowledge that the negative feelings were gone. Replacing them
was an eagerness to accept the call, which was so opposite from my previous
Feelings that I knew it had to be the Holy Spirit working within me as an
answer to my prayers and fasting.
In spite of this very positive experience, which still leaves me in awe, I
decided I wouldn't tell my husband or the Pastor yet. I would wait a week and
see if the negative feelings returned and if they did not, I would know for
sure it was from God.
The next day, I realized that I was rebelling. I had received the confirmation
I had been asking for in a way I never could have imagined and with a power I
never before had experienced and yet was hesitating. I determined then that I
would accept the call and tell the Pastor Sunday of my acceptance. There was
no need to doubt any longer that God wanted women in the priesthood, or that
my call was From God.
It was accepted at the Pittsburgh District Conference in January 1987, and I
was ordained Easter Sunday, April 19, 1987, prior to the 11 o'clock service,
at the Pittsburgh North Hills Church in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, by Elder
Michael Bates, through whom the call came, and my husband, Evangelist Richard
Andersen. (It was accepted unanimously on the congregational level.)
Since I had been giving ministry in areas that were the typical kinds of things
connected with the ministry of Teacher for some time before I was called, I
didn't see that my ministry would be changing much. It has been interesting
to see how readily a couple oF the older priesthood members have responded
positively; one asking me to pray for him during a visit at the hospital, and
the other in confiding a difficult situation he was experiencing, because he
"considered me a Teachertt and Teachers were to help give ministry in such
situations.
It has been very satisfying to me to have been able to share my testimony with
others who have been having similar negative feelings about women serving in
the priesthood, and to hear later that my testimony has helped them in being
able to begin to change their attitude.
I have been very hesitant to give public ministry, but besides an offertory
at our reunion, I have given two invocations at the 11 o'clock services - one
at our congregation and one at the request of the Regional Administrator at
another congregation. During all three times, when I expected to be very
nervous, I called on the same Spirit that I felt within me during the experience
I had had at the kitchen sink and Felt blessed in giving this public ministry.
I feel that my gifts are more in the areas of visiting, listening, peacemaking,
one-on-one skills that are a part of the Teacher's ministry, but realize that
I will be called upon from time to time to offer public ministry, and can only
expect because of my recent experiences that the Holy Spirit will continue to
guide me in my efforts to grow in this area oF ministry also, when I remember
to ask for this help.
I've been reminded oF several things through this wh~le struggle: One, that
God will answer even though we "only desire to believe" and may not Feel our
faith is strong enough to receive the answer we need; that patience is needed;
that God can use us even when we are sometimes rebellious (without realizing
it) and want to do things our own way; and that he can bend our will to meet
his iF we are willing to let him.

10-6-87

YOU ASKED FOR NEGATIVE EXPERIENCES ALSO, BUT I DO NOT WANT THIS SHEET USED IN
ANY WAY IN CONNECTION WITH ANYTHING THAT WOULD BE REPEATED, PRINTED, ETC.

I have not been happy with the pressure put on us in our congregation (and in
others ITve heard of) to preach as our first assignment after being ordained.

'L- 4

Shortly after my acceptance of the call, a new Pastor sent out a survey to the
congregation asking us among other things, if there was anything we felt uncomfortable doing in connection with the work of the church. I wrote, "I do not
want to preach or be in charge of prayer meetings. n,;, When I asked the Pastor if
he had read my survey sheet, he answered that he had, but made no comment, so I
assumed he accepted my feelings about this. (My mistake!)
However, the next time a schedule came out, my name was on it to preach without contacting me. This was very upsetting to me and I refused to do it. Later
during reunion, I asked to talk with him and expressed my feelings that new
priesthood members should have the opportunity to offer prayers, offertory talks,
and similar things before being expected to preach. He seemed to accept my
suggestions and respect my feelings. Later in the week, I was asked to give an
offertory talk by someone in charge of a service at reunion. Although I was
sitting directly in front of him for choir following this talk, he gave me no
word of encouragement, comment, or even acknowledged the fact that I had given
this ministry, when the service was over.
Since discussing this with the Elder through whom my call came, and who is the
new Pastor's counselor, the new priesthood members in our congregation have
been assigned mentors.
My feeling is that all new priesthood members - men or women - should be visited
by the Pastor and the two of them have the opportunity of discussing how they
both see the new priesthood member beginning his/her ministry.

Wanda Andersen 10-6-87

column9: n spite of this very positive experience, which still leaves me in awe, I decided I wouldn't tell my husband or the Pastor yet. I would wait a week and see if the negative feelings returned and if they did not, I would know for sure it was from God.

(It was accepted unanimously on the congregational level.)

column7:
ELDER MAXINE WEBB ARMSTRONG
INDEPENDENCE, MISSOURI

With approval of World Conference of 1958 I served as a member
of the Council of Women for the World Church for several years. Members
of the Council served as guest speaker/teacher/facilitator for retreats,
reunions, district and multi-district activities, principally for women
of the church. During such an assignment to a Central Iowa District
gathering, as I sat meditating prior to the closing service, the voice
of the Spirit spoke within me. The message given was clear, that there
would be a time when women would be ordained and the mantle of my
earthly Father (as I understood it) would rest upon my shoulders. I was
startled, and mentally posed questions regarding other family members.
As I raised each question in my mind, I received answers and so I
accepted God's will whenever it would occur.
not been discussed very much in the church at that time, and I
Women in priesthood had

personally had never thought of it as pertaining to me.

Later, in contemplation, additional questions arose and I
sought the Lord earnestly regarding how this would occur, how women
would be called, approved and ordained. The feelings I received were
that women should be called, approved and ordained through the same
channels as were men; that evidential testimony should be received by
both the official responsible for the call and the woman being called.
Then I requested of the Lord that my mind be put at ease concerning this
so it would not affect my work or service until such time as it would
occur. I trusted in his word to me and in the Spirit which had borne
witness of this truth, and I kept these things to myself.

Although I wasn't in attendance for the reading of the
document concerning ordination of women, I had lunch that day with
several who had served on the Council of Women. We shared pro and con
but my feelings continued to be that I did not feel the need of
ordination to serve, but I also was open to whatever was God's will.
This I uttered silently in prayer as I returned to my daily work as
Regional Administrator for the Northeast Jackson County Area of the
American Red Cross at that time.

When others would brooch the subject, I listened, agreed if
and when I could, and tried always to express my belief in confirming
spiritual experiences in priesthood calls. I did not argue the question
for we each have the right to interpret God's will and expression of
love as it touches our lives individually.

During December, 1985 I experienced a series of impressions in
which the Holy Spirit indicated a call to priesthood was forthcoming for
me. This usually happened during meditation in a worship service, and
seemed to indicate my calling to the office of elder.
share these with my husband, Elmer and he was a strong support and felt
I decided to
the spiritual impact of them as I related my experiences. Further
reassurance came on Wednesday night as I reluctantly accepted offering
the invocation at prayer service. I didn't want to hurt anyone in
attendance, but our pastor Roger Sullivan reassured me. After welcoming
the congregation, Brother Sullivan announced the first hymn would be
followed by the invocation to be offered by me. As we arose to sing two
persons walked out of the meeting. I felt a great sorrow that they had
been offended by my action in going up front. As I thought this, the
Spirit consoled me as it confirmed that the offense was not mine, for I

was called to be an elder and was to serve in that office.

Two weeks later, I was told of my call to the office of elder
and upon being informed of the call, both Elmer and I received
confirmation by the Spirit of its rightness. I was ordained December
28, 1986 in the Blue Ridge congregation by High Priest Ben D. Simmons
assisted by my husband, Elder Elmer L. Armstrong.

My feelings afterward included great joy that the Lord had
called me to his work but also a sense of inadequacy to meet the demands
of the work. There was a swelling within my heart with assurance of
Godly presence and assistance and a tremendous knowledge of the
wonderful love of our God and love bestowed by the congregation. I
sensed a feeling of greater responsibility as I was leaving the line for
I was asked to administer to a sister immediately. It was like a
oneness with God.

With acceptance and ordination has come a growing deeper sense
of the many responsibilities entailed. When standing as a minister, a
channel for spiritual ministry to persons, one prepares with much prayer
and fasting, deeper meditation and study, in order to be worthy in a
measure to serve as God's vessel for meeting another's need.

One of the distinct pleasures of my calling is that of
companionship ministry with my husband. Each of us has different gifts,
but we compliment each other in serving (as well as life). This allowed
me the unique pleasure of feeling more comfortable with the ordinances,
as well as being a united experience for all participants.

The majority of persons have been positive in response to my
ordination. There has been wonderful support given by the male
priesthood in our congregation. They have been quick to express their

support and appreciation, and to ask ·us to participate with them. The
first two persons to. request administration by direct approach were
fellow priesthood members. Both contacted me later to express the help
they received. Such support is great!

I have tried not to cause anyone hurt and have been able to
maintain good relationship although sometimes I've had to be the one who
kept speaking. But one lady whom I knew was not for ordaining women
because of previous conversations was eating in a restaurant when I
entered. Her response thrilled me because as I went toward her table,
she immediately rose and came to me. As we embraced, she said,
"Congratulations on your ordination, Maxine. I want you to know that I
feel that if anyone should be called, you are one I feel good about and
could accept." I told her how much her statement meant to me, and my
heart was filled to overflowing. We had worked together for the church
for many years and her opinion meant a great deal to me.

It is my observation that the combined services of men and
women in priesthood has brought an added quality of worship to
congregational activity. The women serve quietly in humility and
harmony, without making an issue of their womanliness. I sense a
revival of interest taking place as more persons feel the rightness of
women in ministry. They do not attempt to emulate men, but serve with
the gifts and talents that are innately theirs. There are results of
richer depths of worship in togetherness, or unity, with no regard as to
whether male or female leads the way to bring us to Christ.

As this milestone in life has evolved, it has moved me from a
somewhat ambivalent feeling about the need of priesthood for women, to
the sure realization that women ordained offer spiritual ministry which

could not be given within positions previously offered women in service
to the church. They will also bring a personal ministry which is
inherent with women to minister in situations which men do not feel
comfortable.

Many times we have spoken of the women who lived during the
earthly life of our Lord, as well as before that time. They are those
whom God honored with His Presence, either in the flesh or in Spirit, as
he imparted wisdom, instruction and encouragement. We have wondered
about the women who worked, supported Jesus in his work and served
alongside the Apostles of that day, and the years which followed. The
conclusions which seem most in tune with the intelligence of God is that
through this action of priesthood for women we are in our day living
nearer to the concept of the Kingdom than at any time since the days of
Jesus and his disciples. Jesus Christ honored both men and women with
his presence then, and he shall continue to do so in our day.
With this thought as basis, it follows that the same
principles of priesthood calling, approval, affirmation of support, and
finally ordination, each handled under identical guidelines, is
Christ's way.

column7:
PERSONAL TESTIMONY Sheri Ashley September 1987 My call to the office of priest came through Henry Knight, pastor of Royal Oak congregation in Detroit International Stake. The ordination took place in March, 1986. I was ordained by Elder John O'Neal, Elder Henry Knight, and Priest Jon Harris. My husband Ken was called to the office of priest at the same time and we were ordained during the same service. The radically different responses we had to the entire process of calling and ordination point out some of the problems I was having. Ken had been anticipating his calling and was making personal spiritual preparations. When he was told of the call he was not surprised in the least. I, on the other hand, had been fighting the very thought of a calling for many months. Deep within my spirit I knew it would come, but I couldn't face it. So I did many things to avoid being confronted with the reality of what God wanted me to do. When the elders came to share what they thought was good news, Ken accepted readily. My response was to cry the entire time they were there. I didn't want to accept their message and disappointed that they were unable to share any specific spiritual "evidence" other than their own opinions. Ken moved into service willingly after his ordination. I held back, not wanting to hurt some of my friends for whom ordination of women was not acceptable. It was necessary for the Lord to speak directly to me and tell me "The time to wait for these friends is past. You have a responsibility now to serve those who voted support for your calling and are expectantly waiting for the blessings of ministry you will bring them." Then I was willing to move out in active public ministry. Perhaps some background would help explain my hesitancy. For years I had struggled with the concept of ordination for women. I didn't know if it was God's will, but had never done any serious study of the matter. At one point I reconciled myself to the idea that God did not choose to ordain women, and after that time I was content to minister as I had always done. I had never desired to perform the ordinances and really felt no need for priesthood since I was allowed to function according to my gifts in most other ways. I had many meaningful outlets for my services. When I first read the revelation we now know as Section 156 a spirit of peace bore assurance to me that this was of God. The portions on the temple were especially powerful for me. My reaction to the part about ordination for women was a deep sadness. I remember thinking, "God; I wish you hadn't done this. I was hoping you never would." The powerful realization came to me that now I could potentially be called to priesthood.

This burden weighed so heavily on me that for many months afterward I struggled with the divinity of the revelation. My initial experience of divine confirmation was set aside as I questioned and agonized over whether or not this could possibly be God's will for the church. lived with confusion and "stupor of thought" (Doctrine 8 Covenants 9:3d) as I studied everything on both sides of the issue and listened to the many voices clamoring for attention. One day a flash of understanding came to me. I had never dealt with any other scripture the way I was handling this one. Always before I had taken seriously the promise of Alma 16 and had tested the word by assuming it was true and acting on it. By this method I had developed a strong testimony of the truthfulness and power of many scriptures. Why, then, was I testing this one in the opposite way by assuming it was false until some specific proof was presented to me? I resolved to change my thinking. I would "experiment on the word" (Alma 16:151) for a period of time by assuming it was true. I would talk, think, and behave as though ordination for women was God's plan and see what happened. Several changes occured al most immediately. I began to experience inner peace where before there had been turmoil and confusion. As continued to study scripture my eyes were opened to new understandings. Truths that previously had been hidden because of my closed mind were now made plain. Concepts that had not been part of my thinking were given to me by the still small voice naves come to recognize as God speaking to me. In a variety of ways God began to give me the assurances I needed. Eventually I reached the point where I was able to say, "Yes, I will accept the ministry of an ordained woman." However, when my own priesthood calling was presented to me just a few weeks later, my struggle began anew. Since there were no ordained women in my local congregation, I would be the first one. This required a much deeper level of commitment than my simple willingness to sit quietly in a congregation and accept someone else's ministry. Before I could say yes I needed very personal, strong, confirming evidence from the Lord. Because Henry Knight (my presiding elder) was out of town, his two counselors were the ones to deliver this call. And they were unable to give me such evidence. I received no assurance that this was more than an affirmation of my present gifts and ministries. How could I accept? What would possibly motivate me to bring such pain on the dear friends I had who would not accept such a call? How could I stand in public 85 a member of the

priesthood without clear direction from God that this was His I had never seen myself as a risk-taker or a person on the forefront. I shrank from this responsibility and began the soul -searching battle all over again. In my anguish I cried out to God for many nights. "Give me a sign," I demanded. And His answer came. Part of His reply was this messages "Your confirmation will come in service, and in mighty prayer. I love you. You must dwell in that under- standing. I will always be right beside you to counsel. Learn increasingly to listen to My voice and shut out all others. Go forward in faith. Knowledge will come." He went on to say, "You will not receive the personal validation you seek until you accept and begin to serve. My spirit has nurtured you and prepared you for this day. Go forth as an act of faith." With that understanding I reached a turning point. I remembered that I had made Him Lord of my life many years before this time. Part of my commitment was that I would not say no to any call He had on my life. I have tried to respond to all that He has asked of me. If this was genuine direction from Him, how could I now say no to my Lord? When I called Henry Knight to give my assent; he was able to share with me his own experience of confirming evidence which came in the form of a spiritual dream. There was no doubt in his mind that this was God's will. In March 1986 my former pastor, John O'Neal, came to share in my ordination. When he was able to bear his testimony of the divinity of my call, I received further assurance. He had been given evidence of it in 1983 when he called Ken to the office of deacon, but he had realized there was no official policy which would allow him to process my call at that time. He spoke of the nature of my ministry and was helpful in giving specific direction for my future. As I have moved out to serve in this new capacity I have found a very real divine empowerment resting upon me when I share in the ordinances. I am also aware of additional spiritual strength and support in various areas of ministry. I sense His hand of blessing, which provides the confirmation I once sought for this calling. My activities have changed very little. Opportunities to share in baptism and serving communion are the only additions to my previous experience. Many other ministries that we traditionally associate with priesthood have been a part of my life for the last 20 years.

I have found general acceptance of my ministry from local church members. One of my greatest fears was the hurt I would cause for dear friends who could not accept my calling. But even in the experience of having them refuse to take communion emblems from me there has been a mutual love and bond of sainthood that has kept our relationship intact. I thank God for this blessing. There are many who do not accept my call and have absented themselves from local worship services on various occasions. My heart and love are with them because I believe they are respond ing with spiritual integrity to what they believe is God's will. I also believe that He honors their decision and will show them a way to continue faithfully serving Him in some portion of His vineyard. God is bigger than our divisions and works with each of us according to our heart's desires. One of my most powerful realizations has been a deeper understanding of the phrase "all ale called according to the gifts of God unto them." (Doctrine and Covenants 119:8) I envision a future time when we truly comprehend that message as a body and begin to respond as a priestly people. What a blessing we can be to a needy world when each of us is functioning in unique calling, endowed with the spiritual power to bring healing ministry and hope to the bruised and broken-hearted. I have also sensed that in my willingness to respond I have helped raise the self-awareness and personal worth of other women. There are many who have a special desire to receive communion from a woman. Perhaps they have never identified the reasons why this is so important to them. I believe one reason is that the experience delivers a unique message. It has been expresed to me like this: "I have always taken communion from a man. While it has been a good experience, it carpied the subtle message 'You can never be as spiritual as I am or as close to God, simply because you are female. Now, in taking communion from a woman I affirm for myself that I am a true spiritual partner and companion with man. I do not need an intermediary to bring me to God. My femaleness is not a spi- ritual handicap, but a unique asset. I am challenged to nurture my gifts in ways which will allow me to stand in the presence of God as His loving child and servant. am free now to explore its possibilities. I too, have a calling. My nature as a woman is part of that calling and I rejoice in it." While the traditional titles are still being used for both male and female priesthood callings, I believe with all my heart that the callings of women are different from those of men. fear that because of the strength of our traditions and habits many women will allow themselves to be placed in a male mold. They will function as they have seen men function for many years and will adapt themselves to the male model of ministry.

I want to stand on the rooftops and shout to everyone, "Don't let this happen!" I believe that God made two distinctly different and highly complementary sexes. He designed us to function as teamsy each one offering those unique qualities that are a part of his or her nature. We have not even begun to explore the beauty of this model in marriage. Some, over a period of many years experience together, have developed a harmony and compatibility that is special and right for them. Others have fought their natures and have striven for sameness in the name of equality. Y fear that if we do not openly discuss and explore the possibilities for complementary ministry, we will fall into that same negative pattern. On the other hand, if we open our minds and rise to this challenge, we have a very special opportunity at this point in history. In my opinion our men have accepted a restrictive pattern for priesthood functioning. In the past it has discouraged individual expression and has resulted in a limiting model for what is expected of each priesthood office. Because of thig many who are called do not turn to God for an understanding of their unique ministries. They turn instead to a job description which has been carefully and thoughtfully prepared for them out of prayerful past experience. While there is value in the model and I affirm its rightness, I see it as a beginning point, a skeleton on which each of us is then challenged to build our own unique creation as a special servant of God. I have sensed some of God's unique thinking in His creation of me. I have delighted with Him as I cooperate in this processs of becoming. I have shared His joy as I begin, tentatively, to take first steps toward my unique priesthood ministries. And I have become sensitized to the reality that others with the same priesthood office are called to very different ministries from mine. Nevertheless I am aware that the bureaucratic and administrative structure of the church has very clear expectations for my priestly activities. I am being asked to fit into a pre- -defined male mold. Imagine with me the possibilities for new ministry if all the womem being ordained are challenged to spend prayerful time with the Lord and let Him begin to unfold their particular areas of giftedness. Let them then adjust those specific gifts to the necessary, traditional priesthood functions so that the essen" tial work of the church can be effectively accomplished. Then imagine the newly ordained men catching this concept as they observe freedom to explore and discover special ministries on the part of the women. They, too, will want to consider their uniqueness as individuals, while they respond to the necessary activities they are called to perform.

Anci finally, picture the excitement of many priesthood men when they realize that for years they have struggled with fitting a mold that was not made for them. Some who have served in ways that did not match their talents or personalities will be freed for truly powerful ministry. At the same time, the functions they have tried to perform without full effectiveness will be handled much more meaningfully by others who recognize their true callings in those areas. In relation to my own calling, I believe there are several special ministries the Lord has prepared me to offer at this time. The concept of angel ministry, which has been important to me for many years, needs to be explored. I want to share the experiences I have had and help create more sensitivity to this important aspect of the Aaronic offices. I feel called to share my understandings of the spiritual gifts as they relate to our public and private worship. I believe God has given me gifts of leadership in corporate worship that enable people to become more sensitive to the presence of the Holy Spirit in their midst. Teaching and encouraging people to develop the spiritual disciplines of prayer, fasting, and meditation is very important to me. Personal ministries of counseling, caring, and listening are among the skills He is challenging me to further develop. Teaching the skills needed to develop strong family units and truly Zionic homes has been one of my areas of involvement for many years. I find that I continue to have opportunity for this kind of sharing from time to time. Probably the most significant change that has taken place within me as a result of this priesthood call has been a new level of spiritual sensitivity and authority. I have been aware of it cor several occasions, particularly when serving as a channel of God's blessing through the ordinances. God has given me a sensitivity to people's needs and potential that is both a burden and a blessing. Although I do not know what the future holds, I expect that He will continue to challenge me to develop this awareness and the skills necessary to minister to individuals when opportunity arises. While my struggle with accepting this calling was genuine, I realize now that I am no longer living with any of my former regrets or fears. I am delighted that I am able to serve Him more effectively than in the past, and the desire of my heart is to continue growing in my capacity to be a servant of Christ among people.
