column7:
DOORWAYS TO LIFE ........ MY CALL TO PRIESTHOOD
by Marie Baker (Elder)
1436 Fairway Drive
Burlington, Wa. 98233
September 14, 1987

As a child I would do a lot of daydreaming. At a moments notice I would be
off into some imaginary world. The one thing I remember about the day dreams
was the doors - passageways from one episode to the next. Curiosity was
what keep me opening the doors: I had to know what was behind, so I would
gather enough courage to take hold of the door knob turning it very slowly
until I would have a large enough crack to peek through, then open it a
bit wider and wider until I could see what there was for me to explore.
The fascination of it all was the various kinds of doors: there was all sizes
and shapes. There were doors carved in beautiful woods, some were inlaid
with pearls and colorful stones, some were marble and gold. I remember that
these doors held special meaning - for each one was unique and needed to
be treated with special care. More often than not I found myself so caught

up in admiring the beauty of the door that I would forget to turn the knob-
to use the door for what it was designed ... a passageway to the room or garden,

field that was behind it.

Then there were the times when I would step through the door - very cautiously,
taking a small step at first, then two, then two more until I found myself
right in the middle of the events taking place or discovering some new and

unexpected sight or treasurer. Most of the time I was alone in these day-
dreams. When there were others about they seem to be under my tutelage and

under my instruction.

I am telling all of this because it correlates so much to my call to priest-
hood. Priesthood was "that room beyond" ... the one in which I was quite

satisfied to stand at the entrance in awe and wonder. That room was for
somebody else to explore and be in. I was the captive audience giving
support to the events being performed. I had other rooms that I could
explore and be comfortable in. In those rooms I could clean and put all
in its proper place - but not that room.

page 2

In real life, like unto the childhood day dreams, there has always been that
tugging - the beckoning from my innermost being to open the doors to life.
Giving in I would find myself walking (more often running) into the happenings
of life around me, ending up becoming the director of what-ever was ... be it
PTA President, Cub Scout or Brownie leader, Sorority Treasurer, President of

The League of Women Voters and Church Women United, Directing the County Cys-
tic Fibrosis Campaign, serving on State Boards, being a member of the RLDS

Women's Council/Commission. And when 1'!hat needed to be done wasn't being done,
I'd form a committee or an advisory council to get it done. Again, like the
childhood dreams, too often I would find myself in "a room" alone, not always 1/,,cocwt,·.)j
how to find my way around ... or how to get myself out.
The difference between my childhood dreams and adult reality was believing.
Soon after marriage I was introduced to the REORGANIZED CHURCH OF JESUS CHRIST
OF LATTER DAY SAINTS - and knew without a doubt that this was one door I would
pass through without hesitation. I was baptized on July 10, 1950 in the only
water-hole deep enough, a man-made watering tank for cattle and horses, in
West Texas. The church had not been formally organized - so there gathered
for this joyful occasion was a few of the "scattered saints" coming from all
parts of West Texas and New Mexico. I was born anew and I knew it! I was born
to a new life - a life I knew nothing about. I had no idea what was before or
expected of me. I had no religious background whatsoever ... for you see, I had
been raised in a family with no religious convictions. The only thing I knew

about Jesus Christ or God was a few unrelated concepts I overheard from child-
hood friends.

Without knowledge I knew! I knew that it was necessary for me to commit my
life to God - and this commitment would be forever. The door was opened wide
and once I entered into "the room" I knew that other doors would be opened for
me - but I would have to step through on my own accord. So I began to read
and study everything I could beg and borrow - even outdated Heralds and books
that smelled so musty that I would have to cover my nose as I read. I believed
it all!

I discovered that I was expected to "work for the Lord", I learned that there
was a thing called the "Holy Spirit" that would guide me, instruct me - if I
prayed, if I would but ask. The asking has been the hardest part. The only
religion by which I was raised was "you don't depend on anyone - for any

thing. You pull yourself up by your own bootstraps" so this part of my matur-
ing process has not come easy. I am continuing to learn "to ask" before all

page 3

else fails. The "work ethic" (another family trait) has been an asset.
I truly enjoy "work." Well, discovering I was expected to work and that I had
been "gifted" with His Spirit - I was now ready to set out to do something.
Looking around, I realized for the first time how many children lived in my
neighborhood, most were nine-twelve years old. They needed something to do on
Saturday afternoons. I went from house to house inviting these boys and girls

to come to "Junior League" - a program (which I produced on my own) of the Re-
organized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints. Now I had to find a

place to meet, the number planning to come would be too many for our small
house - and our little mission (Odessa, Texas - 1950-52) was meeting in the
Pastor's home. I found a community building in the neighborhood that I could
use free of charge. The "Junior League" began meeting - with about 15 regulars
with me as leader ... ME a leader! I had no idea what a leader was suppose to
do ... and at this point in my history I had not even heard of Zion's League or
Zioneer's or where I could go for "how-to's" ... I remember that first meeting
trying to lead the kids in singing "What a Friend We Have in Jesus" when I
had never sang the hymn before in my life and couldn't even sing a tune (in
a number two washtub, I had been told often enough by my older brothers).
But you see, that didn't matter. I believed! I believed that I could,
I could do what needed to be done - to serve Jesus Christ, to witness of Him
to my fellow-beings, my neighbors. There was nobody around to tell me "I cou- \,_1.);
ldn't" or "it's never been done before" or "you know~how". I knew I had never
done it before - but I believed that I could ... and I did. Thanks be to God!

What I did know was that I was being driven by a force not my own. I was driv-
en by His Spirit, beyond my "not knowing" to act upon what I did know - and

that was that Jesus Christ was real in my life. So in reality this event, the
"Junior League" opened a door to life. Doors have continued to be opened by
which I was given opportunities to give a ministry to many through the years.
Never have I measured up to the requirements necessary to give that ministry,
to answer the call to serve my fellowman. It was only after I had reached my
own limits "of knowing how" that I would call on the Lord with flashing S.O.S.
signals, "What do I do NOW, Lord" ... and always the answers were there. Many

times the answers would come one step at a time until the task at hand was co-
mpleted.

So you see, when the call for priesthood came, I asked the Lord, "WHY?" "You
know I am already your servant ... and together we have move about some pretty
big objects - like mountains! Girls Club of Odessa is but one example. What

page 4
can I do with priesthood that I have (with your help, of course) not already
being done? Do I need priesthood to call on you for help when times get "to
big" for me - it sure hasn't been necessary in the past 40 years. Excuse me,
but I need to think this over. I'll need to pray, to think it over in my own
mind before I can crack open this door."
Actually, I had already put my hand to the door knob. In my sub-consciousness
I had already accepted the call - it was not until I began to pray, fast, and

meditate over the call to serve in priesthood was I able to put it all togeth-
er. Now, I believe my first call to serve in the priesthood came through a

Moslem woman in a remote Nunnery located in the tea plantations of West Java,
Indonesia. To explain: Otis and I lived in Indonesia for several years. Soon

after our arrival there I began to attend a Bible Study offered by the Americ-
an Women's Asso.of Indonesia. Keeping to my true nature, I began to visit

with he ladies about the problems they were expressing, living in this third
world country (I jokingly called the group, "the hate Jakarata league"). Being
an "old hand" at women's retreats, and knowing some positive results from such
I felt this is just the answer for these ailing souls.
I got the job! Putting a retreat together is a totally different experience
in Indonesia than it is in the USA. That story deserves its own line. Again I
found myself alone - very alone. "Just you and me God" and everyday I would
fuss and fume and tell Him to forget I ever mentioned a retreat in Indonesia.

But He wouldn't do that. He wouldn't let me have my way. RESULTS: After kno-
cking on many government doors to secure a permit to have a "religious meeting

to finding a remote, isolated place in this over-populated nation were two of
the major hurdles. More than 50 women attended. We CELEBRATED OUR EXISTENCE,
a title borrowed from Lucille Oliver's book. The part of this retreat that I

refer to as "important to my call to priesthood" took place on the Sunday mor-
ning at the closing service of the week-end.

A Moslem lady (we had pledged to the Indonesian government to do no proselyting)
had come to the retreat with an American friend. Early Sunday morning she

comes to me wishing to speak to the group, "because I had something very unus-
ual happen last night, and I feel I must share it with you and the other ladies".

I agreed to give her this opportunity.
She begin by saying, "I had a dream last night, I am not sure what it all means,
but I am not given to dreaming - and I feel that there is something very special
about it - Mrs. Baker has given me, upon my request, this time to tell you
what i dreamed."

page 5
and that I sure didn't know what I was doing (at this point in my history,
I didn't know what Zioneer's or Zion League was.)
What I did know and was driven by a force beyond my "not knowing" to act
upon what I did know. So in all reality I opened the door to life.
Because doors have continued to open for me to bring a ministry, to answer
a need. Never have I measured up to the demands of meeting the need, of
answering the call to serve. it was only after I had reached my own limits
"of knowing how" that I would call on the Lord with flashing S.O.S signals
"What do I do Now" and always the answers were there, many times one step
at a time until the task was completed.
So when the actual call to serve in the Priesthood came, I asked, Lord, why?
You know I am already your servant ... and you and I have already been through
some tough times together, together we have moved about some pretty unbelievable
objects - like mountains, Girls Club of Odessa is but one example. What
can I do with Priesthood - that I have (with your help, of course) not already
done? I'll have to do some thinking about this ... I"ll pray before I crack
open that door.
Actually, I had already put my hand on the knob.
I had already accept the call - it was not until
longing for the "reasoning" was I able to put it

In the sub-consciousness
after praying, fasting and
all together.

My call to Priesthood first came through a Moslem woman in a remote Nunnery
located in the tea plantations of West Java, Indonesia. To explain: Otis
and I lived in Indonesia for a few years. Soon after our arrival there I
started going to a Bible Study offered through the American Womens Asso.
of Indonesia. Keeping to my true nature, I began to visit with the ladies
there about the problems they seem to be having living in this third world
country ... I jokingly called the group the "hate Jakarta League" ... these
visits lead to my offering to organize a Women's Retreat. Being an "old
hand" at womens's retreats, and knowing some positive results from such,
I felt this is just the answer for these ailing souls. Well, putting a
retreat together here in the states is tottally a different thing than what
it is in Indonesia. That is a story deserving it's own line - but again,
I found myself alone "just you and me God" and everyday, I was ready to
give it all up - forget that I even mentioned it, Lord! But He would'nt
let me. RESUTS: After knocking on many doors of National leaders, finding
a remote, isolated place in an over-populated nation were the two major hurdles
More than 50 women came. We Celebrated our existence ... a title borrowed
from Lucille Oliver's book. The part of this event that I refer to before
took place on the Sunday morning at the closing service of the retreat.
A Moslem lady (we had pledged to do no proseliting with the Indonesian Government)
had come to the retreat with an American friend. Early in the morning she
che comes to me - wishing to speak to the group, "because I had something
very unusual to happen to me last night, and I feel I must share it with
you and the other ladies." I agreed to give her the opportunity.
She begans by saying, "I had a dream last night, I am not sure what it all
means, but I am not given to dreaming - and I feel that there is something
very special about it - Mrs. Baker has given me - upon my request, this time
to tell you what I dreamed".

page 6

"In the dream, Mrs. Baker decided to have a party - a real celebration. She
invited all of us to come, as well as other friends. She goes to a lot of
work preparing for the party, making sure that there was enough food, her home
in proper order, all the invitations addressed and delivered. The hour of the
party comes - no one shows. She waits and waits, still no one comes. A little
discouraged, but not so much so that she decides to plan again. This time the
invitations are addressed and delivered to the same people,you, me and friends
But this time she tells us in the invitation that we are to come bringing all
of our dirty clothes, our dirty dishes, our sandals that need cleaned - and
while at the party, she will laundry our clothes, wash our dirty dishes, and

clean our sandals. We all come to the party - each and every one of us, brin-
ging our dirty dishes, our soiled clothes, we remove our sandals and watch

while she does the laundry, washes and drys our dishes and cleans each of our
sandals." "Ladies, I do not know what this says to us, but I felt I must tell
you my dream."
It was while she told the dream that it was made known to me the meaning.

Having already lived in Indonesia for a few years, in a land that every house-
holder had one or more servants - and having two who (we refused to call them

servants - they had become family) served us - I knew what the servant caste

system was. The servants who did the laundry, washed dishes, cleaned the san-
dals were of the lowest caste. I was called to be that servant - for the Lord

It was then and there when I realized why it was necessary for me to go to
great lengths - beyond personal desire, to have the retreat. I also knew that
in the future, if I chose to become the servant of the Lord, no task would be
menial for me to do. I elected to become that kind of servant. Still I did
not associate "priesthood" with this call. I thought of it as a continuation
of past service - but perhaps in another way.

When I was approached by my husband, the Presiding Elder of the Samish Cong-
regation, Seattle Stake, I was not surprised, but needed to know the answers

the questions I have stated previously. It was during the Stake reunion when I
was doing a juggling act with my schedule that I received the answer to my
questions, my prayers, concerning the acceptance of the call to priesthood.
I was teaching a class at the reunion (we live within driving distance) and
working with two teen-age mothers (through a program I had organized called

YES). Both girls was close to delivery, One had already arranged for adop-
tion, the other one was planning to keep her baby and enter our YES Mother's

Club where special care is given to both mother and child.

page 7

Tuesday morning of this reunion, I receive a phone call just as I was about to
leave to go to camp (Samish Island). It was one of the girls ... she was in
labor and had been for several hours. Since we make previous arrangements for

the girls delivery at the Birthing Clinic, I could take her in, go on to reun-
ion, still arriving in time to teach my class. No sooner had I hung up when

the phone rung again, it was girl number two - in premature labor. I tell her
to hold everything- I am on my way. I took both girls to the clinic. Get to
camp just as morning worship is over and everyone is going to the classes,
Whew!! After class, I go back to the clinic. Girl number one has delivered a
beautiful baby boy. Girls number two, is still in labor-scared to death. She
was only fifteen years old, left pretty much on her own in the world. I wanted
cry with her ... instead I begin to pray. I do this for all the girls - but
this was different, While I was praying for her, I heard a voice (in my mind)
"you have to pray for her" ... I kept on praying, and again I heard the voice,
"You have to pray for her." I look upwards and say (not aloud) "Lord, I am
praying." Again the voice says, "Pray for her." Then something clicked inside
my head, my heart. She needed administration ... and I could not administer, I
did not have the authority. Ashamed, I asked for forgiveness - I began to
pray, pray in a way that I had never prayed before - not laying on of hands,
but truly a prayer of administration. Within 30 minutes, my little friend was
in deli very. I was beginning to understand Wk I was called as an Elder.
As though that was not enough, the next afternoon I had arranged to meet the
adoptive parents, the representative of the adoption agency at the clinic.
Just as I was giving the baby boy to the new parents that I sensed the presence
of the Holy Spirit. I sensed that this child needed a special blessing. I
turned to the adoptive parents, asking them if it would be okay if we offered
a prayer asking God's care over them and the child. They joyfully, anxiously
agreed. Then I asked the representative of the Adoption Agency - he seemed
indifferent - but didn't seem negative about the idea, I close the door to
the chapel and began to offer a prayer in behalf of child - for the parents
who would nurture him. The room became filled with God's Spirit - so much so
that even the representative of the agency - indifferent as he was, was wiping
tears. The parents were moved and showed their gratitude.
When I was left in the chapel alone, I knelt in private prayer, thanking God

for not withholding His blessing from those who needed, because of my uncert-
ainty, my unfaithfulness. It was there that I knew that "yes" I was called to

the priesthood - as an Elder. No, I didn't need priesthood - but those needing
God's blessings did - and He had set in order whereby Priesthood would be given

page 8
It was when I was serving on the RLDS Womens's Council I was assigned to teach
at the Michigan reunion. Apostle Percy Farrow was serving there also. One
morning during the Prayer service he spoke to me under the influence of the

Holy Spirit ... a beautiful and needed blessing. Much of what I was told was con-
cerns of which I had been praying about having to do with my brothers and sister,

my birth family. I had been feeling that I had not witnessed to them of Jesus
Christ - and they needed to know of His love for them. I was burdened because I
felt I had failed them and God. No one at the reunion knew this concern except
Otis, my husband. The message delivered to me was a great comfort - but, the
message also told me that I had been prepared from my birth to do a work for the
Lord and from thence many doors would be opened unto me to do the work to which
I was called. The promise was that as I would go into places and asked to do
that which I felt I did not know how - I was to seek, knock and it would be made
known to me - His Spirit would always be there "WHEN I ASKED."
About a year ago, a friend at a Bible Study in Mount Vernon, says to me as we
were leaving the class - "Marie, you speak with such authority, the knowledge
of the scriptures." She, not being a member of our church had no way of knowing
of my ordination. I am eternally grateful that God has called me to serve Him
through priesthood, but I am more grateful for those who receive the blessings
God offers through prieshood. My call to priesthood is not for me - it is for
the body of Christ - for all people who call on His name. My intent to serve
them in whatever way, by whatever door is opened - passage way to servanthood.

Ordained Nov. 17, 1985
Samish Congregation - Seattle Stake
by Jim Chapman and Otis Baker

Elder Marie Baker

column7:
By Evangelist Partiarch William C. Ballantyne, Elders Elbert Thomas and E. B. Sherman
My belief in the RLDS Church stems from: many years of private study,
regular church attendence since childhood, and the many times that I have been
assured of the presence of the Holy Spirit bringing a ministry of inward peace,
acceptance by God, love, council, guidance, and yes, at times, rebuke. My
belief also is strengthened by the memories of the strong, firm, steadfast
belief and faith of my parents, Walter and Nellie Lingard; the teaching of the
Scriptures by my mother as we performed our daily tasks; and the gentle life of
love as was embodied in my father. I have always enjoyed "church work." Even
as a child I thoroughly enjoyed district conference and the youth retreats are
some of my fondest memories of my high school years.
During my adult life I have conducted a private early morning study of the
Scriptures, using some specialized study guide.
I believe the RLDS Church has a unique message concerning God's plan of
salvation for mankind. I believe that we as members; all of us, lay members as
well as priesthood members, need to study so that we have an "in depth" knowledge
of what that message is. As we continue our constant search for knowledge, we
should live by those precep,ts in the daily activities of our lives.
The church is-unique in the fact that we have as our spiritual guide one
who is directly designated by God to be Prophet, Seer and Revelator. Coupled
with the fact of our belief that we are members of the church divinely restored
upon the earth, we should hear and give heed to the words of our prophet.
Also coupled with the fact of a church divinely restored and a prophet
especially so designated by God, those duly ordained priesthood members are

of an order that was instituted by God from the beginning. This priesthood is an
everlasting priesthood (Exodus 40:15), an unchangeable priesthood (Hebrews 7:23),
a holy priesthood (I Peter 2:5), and a royal priesthood (I Peter 2:9). It is
not bestowed by a person's own choosing. The ordinand is chosen by God and so
designated through the proper channels within the church.
All these facts make priesthood very, very sacred and very, very special.
These facts also make priesthood responsibility a very awesome trust to be
nurtured with wisdom, dignity and love.
With these statements of belief in mind, and with the years of personal
devotion, I was not surprised when my pastor,Elder E. B. Sherman,came to my
home to present to me this call to priesthood. During the early fall season
of 1986 many times while worshipping through daily devotions, I was made aware
that I was thereby grooming myself for priesthood responsibility.
Some forty years ago as one of the young members of the Women's Department
of the Bloomington Congregation, I taught the lesson for the day. I was really
quite pleased with my presentation and even more so when a beloved elderly
member spoke up and said, "Beulah, if they ever call women to the priesthood,
you 1 11 be one of them."
Many times through the years of my adulthood I have felt a very great
frustration within myself because I felt I had so much to give and couldn't
find a way to give it. It seemed as though many times someone else was
designated for the opportunity. Sometimes it seemed it just wasn't designated
that I should "be there" when I felt that I should be.
However, it was of much comfort to me that a portion of the ordination
prayer given by Apostle Lloyd Hurshman, ordaining my husband to the office of

Evangelist Patriarch, was directed to me personally. A portion of the state-
ment reads as follows: "May his comp~nion of many years know that she shares

2

richly in this ministry, not only in support but in the forefront with him in
spiritual matters, in knowing Thy direction and seeking out in fasting and prayer
the best opportunities for service .. II
My call to the office of Aaronic Teacher was presented to and approved
by the Lamoni Stake Conference assembled on November 23, 1986. I completed my
classes required during February and March of 1987.
On April 12, 1987, which was also Palm Sunday, I was ordained to the
office of Aaronic Teacher. My husband of forty-one years, Evangelist Patriarch
William C. Ballantyne was the spokesman, assisted by my brother-in-law, Elder
Elbert Thomas, and Elder E. B. Sherman, my former pastor who originated the call.
I did most of the planning of the actual service. I spent a lot of time
in prayerful consideration. of who I wanted to do what. As a result Jerie Gail
Ramsey was the organist, her son Gerald Ramsey played Arioso by Bach very gently
and beautifully on his trumpet. His wife, Vivian, with equal skill and grace
accompanied him on the piano.
Charles Webb, Aaronic Priest, of the local congregation gave a very

inspirational sermonette for Palm Sunday and also words of praise and encourage-
ment to me, the ordinand. Elder Bill McElroy, present pastor, gave a talk on

the nature of priesthood.
The ordination prayer itself was extraordinary. It was more like an

ordination blessing than the usual ordination prayer .. Bill had made prepar-
ation for what might be.expressed in the prayer, but was given of the Spirit

what should be said as the thought patterns entered his mind. The words of
that prayer were very comforting to me for then I knew that God had been
mindful of my needs during those times of loneliness and frustration. For
one to have it revealed that the Lord was mindful of you while being formed
in your mother's womb gives one a glowing feeling of sweet, gentle love

3

beyond description. These and other words of assurance, challenge, and comfort
are precious beyond words to describe.
When Mrs. Wanda Derry sang "The Lord's Prayer" there was a sense of almost
breathless silence in the room. The benedictory prayer by Elbert Thomas was
also accompanied by a keen sense of spiritual awareness. A copy of that

prayer is also included with this testimony. To top the day, our young grand-
children, Chadley, age 9, and Jennif~r, age 7, played the "Old, Old Path" on

their violins. Seventy people stayed after the service for a pot luck dinner
and fellowship.
I hope that what I've written about the actual ordination service hasn't
seemed too wearisome to the reader, but that was about the most spiritually
magnificant day of my life. I had offered many prayers in preparation and
concern for that day and afterwards. The week just previous I had fasted from
the morning meal and worshipped with personal devotions both in early morning and
just before breaking my fast at the noon hour. Different ones of our friends
have expressed their opinion that they had never sat in a like service so
spiritually endowed as that one.
Since that day, I feel that my music at the organ for church services has
been enhanced. I have given the offertory statement and offered prayer before
administration to the sick. I am worship chairman in the women's department
and feel that I have been given an extra dimension of insight that has been
edifying to those present.
As I go about my route as an Avon representative, I have had sincere
inquiries about my call and also concerning Section 156 in general. With so
many dissenting voices being heard throughout the church today, calling for
people to "come here" and "go there" I find people with deep concerns and
frustrations asking me for comfort and advice.
4

Sometimes, as was stated in my ordination prayer, just my presence has
been a ministry in itself.

Before I leave home on my Avon route, I try to offer prayer that my priest-
hood ministry might be evident. (at least to me)

I have had people who have been dissenters display a more distant attitude
in their friendship, but I have not experienced any severe reactions at all.
To me, all of Section 156 is beautiful. I· find it thrilling to read
about the purposes for building the temple, I'm really looking forward to that,
I think I have made a lot of spiritual growth in my personal life since
the early 1970's. My spiritual outreach has been a continuous growth since I
can remember. Perhaps one of the most outstanding spiritual experiences has
been the fact that I have been permitted to see my mother since her death in
late 1973. On at least two other occasions her spirit has brought a ministry
of love and instruction that met my need at the time.
I've had times of real deep sorrow and frustrations but I've always sought
relief and guidance from God. Always there has been spiritual insight, strength
and support given to help me through the good times as well as difficult times.
In my opinion there is nothing on this earth nor in this life that is as
important as a profound belief in God and service to his cause. When we give
our all to divinity, the rest is enhanced and falls into place as needed,
column9: My husband of forty-one years, Evangelist Patriarch William C. Ballantyne was the spokesman, assisted by my brother-in-law, Elder Elbert Thomas, and Elder E. B. Sherman, my former pastor who originated the call.

column7:
THE WORKSHOP OF LIFE

This is a personal testimony of some of my experiences and feelings since
my ordination to the office of Teacher in the Reorganized Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter Day. Saints on August ~ ~986 through November, 1987.

By
Shirle;TJean
,,&_,~,;_ ~

Balsiger ~

On Sunday, April 27, 1986 at i::.15 P.M. the business meeting was
called for the purpose of approving the calls to ordination of four
women at the Portland Metropole in Portland, Oregon. The following
ladies were approved for ordination by the vote of the members. They are:

1. Norma Stickney - Priest
2. Nadine Riley - Priest
3. Sharon Poynter - Teacher
4. Shirley Balsiger-Teacher

At this meeting, I felt a warm, compelling sense of our Heavenly Father's
presence leading me further into His will as I shared my brief acceptance
thoughts to the group ghthered there. I humbly accepted this call to
specific responsibility in the church with a deep determination and
commitment.
On Sunday morning, August 24, 1986 at 11:00 A.M., Norma, Nadine,
and I were ordained to our various Priesthood offices. Each ordination
was as unique as is each person and very spiritually fullfilling.
Elder Kathey Gregory was spokesperson for my ordination. Our son,
Priest James M. Balsiger and our Son-in-law, Priest Richard E. Taylor
assisted. My husband Patriarch-Evangelist Donald E. Balsiger sat at my
side during the ordination prayer. The occasion was enriched by the
presence of each of our children, Debra L. Taylor, Donald R. Balsiger,
Janet E. Balsiger as well as the ministry of music freely given by our
son Steven J. Balsiger in a quartet accompanied by his wife Phyllis.
The deep, positive emotions I felt are beyond any expectation I previously
imagined! A sense of wholeness surrounded me. The presence of our
grandchildren Allison and Katie Taylor as well as Adam and Anna along
with their mother, Patty Balsiger increased our joy. Also, Don's father,
and friends completed the circle and made the day very bright for us.
The warm spirit of our Lord surrounded the orderly, quality worship hour.
My husband's challenge to servanthood was outstanding. I felt very calm,
yet excited----very relaxed, yet the uplift of God's presence engulfed me.
I need to express that from my early childhood I have felt God's
loving presence directing my l.ife. I have felt love for Him·and drawn
toward Him with a strong and simple trust relationship. The same loving
spirit that called me at age 17 to be baptized, called me to acceptance
of this ordination. I plan to serve out of a sense of love and appreciation
for who God is and what He is doing for me and for others. Also, because
I deeply appreciate all those who have lo~ingly and constructively touched
my life from the 'day c,f· my birtlh, I wish to share in servanthood where
God leads.
Initially, when talk of women being called to Priesthood responsibility
began around 1970, I was definitely not in favor. It seemed to me the
idea was coming from a demanding, rebellious spirit the "Womens' Rights
Movement" was portraying. Yes, very definitely the urnjust inequities
against women in our ~11orld needed change. However, I wasn't sure women's

me with gentleness and peace. My soul is fed and watered by it. Part of
me would wither and die without it. I praise the Lord with all my heart for
the gift of music. It is the food of God's love and direction in a language
that all can understand and respond in depth to. It comforts the lonely
and brings harmony in ,he discord of crowds. It is eternal in nature. It's
influence for good is immeasurable. It has soothed me in times of despair.
It has sweetened, uplifted and refined my life. It is the source of my_
inspiration reaching to the depth of my being. It brings about inner strength
and courage. It brings human beings together! Praise the Lord for that!
Another opportunity for service is to visit in the homes. When I
take the opportunity, I trust God to go before me so that we will share
Him as the need unfolds. This is an area that continues to require much
growth on my part, hut I find endless opportunity for filling needs and
serving as I do so.
I'm grateful for the Temple School courses. They assist me to deepen
my present understanding of God and His work. They enlarge and expand my

concept of what ministry is all about. The course ,onI·Preaching really in-
creased my appreciation for this form of ministry. Preparing a 20 minute talk

for the class took a lot of time, thought, as well as "blood, sweat, and
tears". I understand more fully the preparation that a minister must do to
have an effective sermon now.
Being deeply involved in the Christian Education Department at the
Portland metropole has provided a workshop of exciting experience. I feel
alive and have a sense of wholeness when involved in some way in this area of
service. Watching children's responses and openness is a great experience.
Feeling God's spirit in and through them as they develop is a rich privilege.
They inspire and teach us all .!12. much.
Recently, many people workd long and hard to make our Christman Bazaar
successful. What a tremendous contribution of time and talent it was!
Creative ideas unfolded and we all had fun and fellowship as we shared
together. It was good therapy for all! I like involvement of this sort
and believe it can be very healthy for people.
In the past year, our Lord has caused this child to mature a little
more, yet keep a childlike faith. He has replaced trust where there was
sometimes mis-y.rust and faith where there was sometimes doubt. He has
caused me to feel loved, forgiven, and motivated to continued committment
and service. He makes for me each day "A Bew Beginning". I am very
grateful for this because I know if He wi 11 do this for me, He wi 11 do this
for others, too.
God has given me the gift of a large workshop. I know I am not all
that Ht:wants me to be or not what I'm going to be, but I thank Him for
helping me to grow. Learning about the things that separate people from
one another and to teach about things that can unite and ~ncourage healthy
relationships is a goal of mine. I desire to love and obey God, have a
healthy love of self, show a healthy love for others, and to share so that
together we will produce the climate where God's peace is felt as an enabling
force in this mixed up world. Hay His will be done on earth as it is in
heaven is my prayer.
column9: My husband Patriarch-Evangelist Donald E. Balsiger sat at my side during the ordination prayer. The occasion was enriched by the presence of each of our children,

column7:
The R.L.D.S. Ribstone Church members
Affiliated with the R.L.D.S. Keberlas
District members approved my ordination
in 1986. It was ordained on April 27,
1986, in the Ribstone Branch of Ribstone,
Alberta, Canada, by Elder Wayne Skinner
of the Ribstone Branch who was assisted
by Apostle Phillip Caswell of Independence,
Missouri and Elder Eldon Dallyn of
the Lloydminster Branch. T was
ordained to the office of Deacon.
The ordination service was a
combination homecoming and ordination
service in April of 1986. All branches
of the R.L.D.S. churches were invited as
the Ribstone Branch is the initial roots
of numerous saints that now live in
various parts of the country and the
world. In 1986 Ribstone Branch Church
Celebrated its 78th birthday.
We shared a worship service together in
the morning and then enjoyed a lovely
dinner and fellowship." The ordination
service scheduled in the afternoon was
a spiritual awakening of the Lords
spirit within me which truly employed
my whole body and gave me perfect
– peace.
After I was ordained I again
experienced a spiritual enlightenment as
I expressed the ordination of Sister
Susan Dallyn Perkens to the office of Christ.
through our oneness in Jesus Christ
Sister Susan and myself share a faith
that is rich and full and we both
thanked God for the preciousness of
His spirit.
Some important life experiences and
event that transpired in my life and I
believe all tempered me gradually into
the already service of the Lord started very
early in my life." I did not, however
always take heed to the push of desire
given by the Lords spirit. I did not
always take heed to Gods calling to
serve and walk with Him. Thus
throughout my life there have been very
testing trials and tribulations for which
periodically I have suffered greatly.
In 1974 when my father Elder Thomas
Bates, became seriously ill. I quit my
working professions in accounting and
semi-truck driving to come home and
take care of my parents. One personal
conversation that my father and I
had before he passed away in 1777
(one of numerous personal conversations
with my father which I always
treasured then or throughout my life
and more so now) advised me that
women would be called to the
Priesthood in the R.L.D.S. Church
He also advised me to prepare
accordingly as I would be called
to the Priesthood and would serve and
walk with God in His ministry.
My father had advised me earlier in
my life (in my teens approximately 1956)
that one day I would serve in the
Lords ministry.
In 1974, Sister Evelin Skinner
Womens Dept. Leader, asked me to take
the leadership of the Ribstone Vacation
Bible School. This I served in for
two years. It was a growing experience
overall especially in the teaching area
In 1918. I was asked and accepted
the position of Leader of the Womens
Dept. of the Ribstone Branch which I
served for two years.
In 1918 I had the wonderful
opportunity to visit Australia. Fortunately
I was able to visit the main branch
of the R.L.D.S. Church in Sydney
Australia. I received such unity of
the Lord's spirit and love portrayed
by the Australian saints that it gave
me renewed vigor, inspiration and
faith to continue to serve the Lord
according to my gifts.
All my life I've known the presence
of the Lord's spirit directing and guiding
me and especially the years I was
driving semi-trailer trucks. Numerous
experiences inspired where my life
was in danger (I hauled fuel and
aviation fuel) and God intercepted
and spared my life. One very
special incident the Lord's spirit
guided, directed and comforted me as
I was confronted by an individual
who was totally possessed by the devil.
It was given to me as I offered a
silent prayer for direction to see the
devil within this individual. I
actually was talking to the devil. As
the devil was trying to manipulate
me he continually and eventually
became totally annoyed, aggravated
and furious when he realized that
my constant companion was the
Lord. This was truly an inspiring
experience. The Lord was working
through me manipulating the devil.
In my Patriarchal Blessing in 1974
it was given to me that the devil
was continually near and that I
would have to be on a constant watch
for his intervention. My Patriarchal
Blessing also advised and directed me
that I would be called to serve
in the Lord's ministry.
In 1982 I was elected as auditor
for the Ribstone Branch which I continue
to serve to the present time.
In 1884 I was appointed by the
world Church (recommended by the
Pastor Wayne Skinner) as Solicitor
for the Ribstone Branch. In the office
of Solicitor only from 1884 to April 1886,
I preached on the subject of Stewardship.
From April 1986 I've continued to
serve as Solicitor and continue to
preach on this subject, however, preaching
and teaching also has been asked of
me in the function of my priesthood
call serving and walking with God
in the ministry. The responsibilities
of the office of Solicitor has been and
continues to be a very rewarding
and challenging area in the service
of the Lord.
Since 1917 to the present. I have
served in the ministry of music
playing music instruments (Flute,
Saxophone, Piano), also choir functions.
In 1987 I was asked and accepted
the position of Music Director. This
responsability I share with my loving
faithful servant to the Lord Sister
Evelin Skinner. The ministry of music
has always been a special ministry
serving the Lord and was presented to
me by my father (Thomas Oates) at an
early age. Humerous evening as I
lay in bed my father would play the
piano in the dark. It always felt
I was very close to God in these joyous
moments and hours of the ministry
of music. May all of Gods children
cherish and acknowledge their gifts
of music and step forth to serve the
Lord in the ministry of music.
I was overwhelmed with God's spirit
when section 156 of the doctrine and
Covenants was brought forth by our
Prophet W. B. Smith. Women's
Ordination to the R.L.D.S. church
also confirmed and established my
fathers instruction to me and this
gave me tremendous joy and further
faith in the Lords work. It does
concern and disturb me greatly of
the negativeness, the region of
Women Ordinations in parts of our
Church. We are not to question the
Lord in His wondrous works. The
Lord requires and beckons all of his
children and we all should step
forth willingly and accept the Lord
and His various calls to walk and
serve with Him. We must remember
the work of all Gods children who
have labored with honesty and diligence
is acceptable to God and is equal
to that of the most gifted of his
servants regardless whether they be
men or women. I believe Womens
Ordinations will greatly serve the
Lord and assist the men in our
church in the tough trials and traits
of these precarious times. May Gods
blessings be bestowed upon us all
and may we treasure the contributions
of each one, yes men or women, to
the cause of the Kingdom.
One very special experience in the
ministry since I've been ordained took
place on August 2, 1987. "This special
day was the 75 Anniversary of our
neighboring community Chauvin
Alberta.
For the Sunday service
the three churches in Chauvin
(Presbyterian, Pentecostal and Catholic)
plus the R.L.D.S. Ribstone Church
held an ecumenical service. I was
asked by our Pastor Wayne Skinner to
preach representing our church.
Attendance of all churches was
approximately one thousand and branches
represented by the R.L.D.S. church were
from Lleydminster, Alberta, Wainwright,
Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta and our
branch near Serlac, of Saskatchuwan.
The service was held in the Chauvin
school. On the stage I sat with the
ministers of the other churches and
naturally I was overcome with
nervousness to a degree. I offered a
silent prayer asking the Lord for the
wisdom, for calmness, the quietness
of spirit to deliver my sermon
Immediately I felt the spirit flow
through me. As I took my turn
to speak, on behalf of the Ribstone
R.L.D.S. Church" my first few words
seemed very nervous and I was
very conscious of the other ministers.
Immediately to myself. I said
Dear Lord, and as I looked into
the Gym and saw members of the
R.L. D. S. Churches a perfect calmness
and stillness overwhelmed me. I
continued with my sermon free of
all nervousness and free of the fear
of criticism. As I viewed the congregation
the R.L.D.S. members were vivid and
outstanding to me. The members of
the various denominations were present
but I could not see them. It was
as if they were in a fog
As I.
continued to deliver my sermon and
focused on the R.L.D.S. members it
was as if a light was bearing from
them to me and I received further
calmness, support and love. I knew
the Lord was working through the
faithful members of our church
giving me their prayer's, their
unconditional support and love
I continued and finished my sermon
with the Lord's spirit flowing through
me. Upon completion of the
ecumerical service, It was approached
by both our members and numerous
members of the other denominations.
All expressed their joy, love and support
They not only were touched by the
Lord's message but acknowledged the
presence of the Lord's spirit. Truly
this has been a very deep and
emotional spiritual experience for me
in serving and walking with God
in His ministry.
Teaching a priesthood class was another
rewarding experience in the knowledge and
ministry of the Lord. In January of
1787 the Pastor, Wayne Skinner, asked
if I would teach such a class. In
prayer I asked for God's direction and
This spirit entered and beckoned my
desires to teach. I was overjoyed
and delighted as I continued to
prepare each class session. This was
a uniting, sharing experience with the
other Priesthood members of the
Ribstone Branch. We are endeavoring
to work and serve the Lord as a one
body, a unifying supporting priesthood
unit.
The responses of my call to the
priesthood was one of joy and love
by not just our worship family
congregation in various branches but
also of others of different denominations.
In priesthood call to the office
of Deacon, I not only adhere to the
regular duties bestowed up as Deacon
but also I was asked to take the
responsibilities of teaching and (preaching).
Preaching has given me great joy
(also teaching) and anticipation of serving
the Lord to the maximum of my gifts
and abilities. In 1987 I was
asked to take the responsibility of Pastor
of the Ribstone Branch. I was also
advised that my priesthood call was
eminent to a higher calling
I
seriously considered the Pastor request
and prayed to the Lord for guidance.
It was given to me through the
Lord's spirit, the inner voice so
precious to me, that others were to
fulfill this responsibility presently.
I was, however, to prepare myself
accordingly for a higher calling and
eventual responsibility in the future
as Pastor of the Ribstone Branch. My
priesthood call, the responsibilities I
have accepted “acknowledged and
considered in the service of the Lord
have been received with overwhelming
exceptional responses. This has
been greatly received with deepest
humility on my part.
I have
accepted all responses with a
determination to walk and serve with
the Lord to the sufferings of the cross
for His
sake.
My definition of memory as I
perceive it at this time is that Jesus
Christ came to this world for one reason:
to redeem it from sin. God so loved
the world that he gave his only
begotten Son, that whosoever believeth
in him should not parish, but have
everlasting life (John 3:16). The
amazing ministry of Jesus Christ
is a perfect example of a ministry
that functioned within the framework
of the gifts of the Spirit. He deliberately
confined Himself to minister within
the limits of the gifts of the Spirit,
and He did so for one purpose: that
He might be a perfect example to
all His children.
We can do the same works Jesus did
Jesus said in John 14:12, He that
believeth on me, the works that I do
shall he do also; and greater works
than these shall he do; because I go
assist my Father. Yes Jesus said it
These "greater works, cannot be
greater in quality; they can only be
greater in quantity
He was actually saying to His
disciples, "Greater works than these"
shall you do because I am only one
person; you are twelve- And He
sent them out to do the same works
He did." All of us, yes men and
women of today, are part of these
greater works.
It should give all of us great
courage and strength to know that
we are part of the great things God
wants to do in the earth. We are
definitly a blessed people to be alive
during this time.
I am so very
fortunate that God has permitted
me to live and minister today.
In my priesthood call to the
office of Deacon, It have been very
fortunate to experience the guidance
and direction of the Lords Spirit also
to have the ministry of encouragement
administered to me by numerous
members of my family and the worship
family of our congregation. All have
been Christ like in recognizing
my God given “abilities” and have
encouraged me to move ahead with
full support. Although the office of
Deacon is limited in the function
of the church duties it is not limited
in serving and walking with God
in His ministry.
The ministry of Jesus Christ has always
been present and evident throughout my life.
It was raised by parents, members of the
Church, guided and directed in the ways
and works of the Lord. In the earlier
years of my life although I Knew God
was directing my life, also my father
advising me one day of the ministry, I
Acknowledged the faith of my father and
the Lord's ministry within myself, however
I unfortunately and temporarily would
at times put everything on the back shelf.
I just decided in those temporary
moments I would deal with the
desires and callings of the Lord at a later
date. Periodically, the Lord's spirit
would let me know that It would
have the responsibility of (preaching one
day especially after a special sermon
that inspired me. However, again
since women were not yet called
to the Priesthood, due to my lack
of faith, the back shelf was always
available. Of course, the temptations
of the evil sources always pointed an
artistic beautiful view that.challenged
many years of my life. When Section
156 was brought forth, some of my
life seem to pass before me especially
the moments and desires of God's calling
in former years and especially my
father's instruction to me to prepare
for the ministry
I fully realized
then because of my lack of faith over
the years that It could have experienced
a higher calling and been more
prepared for the ministry of the Lord
in the first year of the Women's
Ordinations.
If it will be any assistance to
future generations. I give my sincere
advice to take heed immediately to God's
Spirit and let His Spirit enter and
beckon your desires to serve and walk
with God in His ministry.
Yes the
devil will beckon also and tempt and
challenge you but always have the
Lord as your companion
In 1977 when my father, Elder Thomas
Pater, became seriously ill It quit my
working professions in accounting and
semi-truck driving to come home and
take care of my parents. One personal
conversation that my father and to
had before he passed away in 1977
(one of numerous personal conversations
with my father which It always
treasured then or throughout my life
and more so now) advised me that
women would be called to the
Priesthood in the R.L.D.S. Church
He also advised me to prepare
accordingly as I would be called
to the Priesthood and would serve and
walk with God in His ministry.
My father had advised me earlier in
my life (in my teens approximately 1956)
that one day I would serve in the
Lords ministry.
In 1977 Sister Evelin Skinner
Womens Dept. Leader, asked me to take
the leadership of the Libstone Vacation
Bible School. This I served in for
two years. It was a growing experience
everall especially in the teaching area

TESTIMONY OF: SARAH ELIZABETH (LIZ) BAUMAN

ORDAINED TEACHER, MAY, 1986

The calling of Women to the priesthood of the Reorganized Church
of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints, as provided for in Section 156
of the Doctrine and Covenants, is a Blessing in which I rejoice.,
I attended the World Conference of 1984 when this instruction
came to the Church through The Prophet, Wallace B. Smith,. I bad been
ill before the document was presented to the quorums and Orders of
the Church for consideration 0 When my husband, an Evangelist/Patriarch
came back to our trailer on the Campus and announced that the Conference
would be considering this document in the afternoon session, I was
overwhelmed with a power that caused me to know that i HAD to be present
for this momentuous occaisiont I must have my personal testimony of
women being called: How could I witness to others of its Rightness, if
I do not have a first hand testimony7 I thought.
As I sat in the Conference session that afternoon, I was filled
with an awareness, NO, a Knowledge that this was of GOD.
The following months was filled with activities but with a new
searching and abserving the women of our congregation and District who
might be"Called", because of their giftedness and dedication to the
cause of God's Kingdom. I was so consumed in looking at others and
fitting their gifts into the work, that I didn 1 t consider whether I
would be Called or not; While I guess I thought, "possibly someday",
I really gave it no time or place in my thinking.
In the early Fall of 1985, the Pastor came to our home and as
we were talking he said, "the District President told me that your Call to the office of Teacher has been processed and I would like to
have you think about and let me know bow you feel." Before be left, I
could think of no reason to refuse; I had my testimony of it's validity;
I understood and thought it could be a blessing to the Church; I had
been looking over the needs of the local church and trying to match
them with the talents of persons; what was there to doubt? Before he
departed he bad my answer, 111 1 11 try.n
For the next three days, whenever I would think of my acceptance, I was filled with the Holy Spirit, in confirmation of it 1 s Rightness .. Many persons shared testimonies of their knowledge and happiness of
my Call to Teacher. Experiences began to come to me in greater power
than I bad been aware of previously. In January of 1986, before I was
ordained, I bad the privilage of offering the benediction at my Grand~
son's baptismal service, I was aware that my prayer, on that occaision~
was different somehow, than that of a Male offering; It was a prayer born of the sensi ti vi ty and Love that a "Grandmother" could offer: A
gift that only I could share at this special time.

In February of 1986, my husband, Varian,' and I journeyed to Omaha-
Council Bluffs Stake, where we were involved with a "Calling and Caring Workshop •~During those ten days, just pre cee ding our District Conference in Grand Rapids Michigan, I had opportunity to share with many people.

We were staying with Keith and Anna Mae Harvey and their parents, the
Newmans. They were a great deal of support and gave lots of encourage- ment as we talked about the implications of this call to priesthood. As
we left to journey back to Grand Rapids, I felt a mounting excitement which helped me to stand before my Conference, that Sunday afternoon in

LIZ BAUMAN TESTIMONY---page 2

February, 1986 9 and witness the support as I made my statement of accept
ance. During that time, I was concious of a new confidence, a louder,.
clearer speaking voice, and an excitement and desire to share the Love
of God and "Make Friends of God1 s Children~ This was a definate Growth
in my character, because I had previously been content to be in the
background and keep a "low profile"·• Many people have testified of
this change since the acceptance of my Cal].
The Ordination service took place at our home congregation in
Greenville, Michigan, during early May,. 1986 0 I felt good about the
service, where I was Ordained with others. My husband,. Varian was the
spokesperson, assisted by our Pastor, Roger Mier, and E]der Dale Wells.
Most of my experiences, however, have taken place with eonfirming Power,
since that service.
I feel to share with you some of those experiences which have
strengthened and assured me continuously, that this is a Divine Cal].
A Missionary R.eunion was held at Camp Manitou, Southern Michigan,.
in August of 1986. Huw Evans, a Seventy, and bis wife, Muriel, were
guests from Wales. They were in the u.s. for a Fourteen Month assignment.
One morning as I walked with my husband toward the auditorium, I was
"caught up" in the Spirit and envisioned us walking to the TEMPLE, to
worship. As I shared this later with my husban®, and even now as I !UTl
writing· this account, I am enveloped by that same spirit that lets me
know I am about the work of my Lord, in harmony with a Temple Building
people. Later, at this same Reunion, Brother Huw Evans and I were
talking. He told me, "You have a quiet Spirit, and many others have
testified that you have a quiet spirit which the Lord has given you; but
I bear witness that this is a quiet spirit of Power.n This has been a
blessing to me as I have reflected on it many times.
These have been strengthening times as I have accepted other
opportunities and responsibilities: Such as the time I was invited to
speak to our Junior High class about the call and work of a Teacher.
Or the in-ritation to share ~estimony of my call,. along with other Women
from Grand Rapids and Michiana District. There is a certain thrill of
Comradare 1 as I join my affirmative testimony with theirs.
Another experience which has been good for me, yet at times, most
frustrating, bas been my acceptance as chairperson of the local Worship
Commission. 'de have planned and directed several services this year.
Most of them were special emphasis, like Christmas, Thanksgiving,
Community Services Day, Children's Day, Mother.!!s Day, Father's Day,.
Health Ministeries Service, or Stewardship emphasis: However, the one
that stands out to me is our lovalthree day Branch Retreat., Along with
the Christian Education commissimn, we worked bard at putting this togeth
er. I felt a particular responsibility since the ,.Buck stops somewhere."
The Retreat went well, even though many things went wrong. Afterward, as
we were reflecting, I realized that my personal enjoyment didn't come
from being able to relax and enjoy the beauty, and serenity of that
,:,ccaision as I bad before: Instead, my greatest testimony and Joy was
;hrougb seeing others respond to the needs and the call for their contrib
ution. As I witnessed their growth, I realized that this is the Joy and
confirmation of Discipleship.,

LIZ BAUJY1,.AN TESTIMONY--page 3

I am very involved with the Greenville Area Christian Women's
Club. This is a Non-Denominational group an as such, I attend the
Prayer Breakfasts,, and Board Meetings each month, as well as the
Once per Month Luncheon. A friend of mine who is Chairperson of this club, wrote to me recently saying, how much she appreciated the q:uiet
unassuming way I moved ahead with my responsibilities and was very
impressed with my sesitivity to the Holy Spirit, during my Prayers.
She closed with tbl'l testimony, "The Lord really usas you, as an instrument in His hands.'t This observation bas helped greatly as a
Non-Member sees and bow like that which Seventy Huw Evans had said
much earlier.
God leads so gently, those He calls to particular responsibilities.
-Se were invited to a witnessing weekend at Plymouth, Michigan, in the
Detroit Stake. As we were invited to share why we bad com$ tbl'lre, I
really searched my soul for an answer; I wanted to be with the others,
I enjoyed sharing testimonies; I liked spending some time with my
husband, (we almost have to make appointments for that luxery); But alll
these were not suffieient reasons. As i continued to search the thought
came, and I shared, "I am here to share the LOVE which the Christ has
shared with me." Even as i said this i KNEW it was right. It seems that everything that happened that weekend was built on that which bad
taken place previously. On one of our visits I felt the Spirit of Love
encircle those who were there. A non member sister of one I was visiting,
bad dropped in unexpectedly and later reported, "though we didn't talk
much of the Gospel, I'm sure that my sister waa caught up in the circle
of LOVE we shared in that home; It was then I remembered my statement

of purpose for being there. How Blessed I felt for following Ris illirect-
ion. This weekend ended on "Endowment Day" 1 and we were richly blessed in those services we took part in.

As I close this testimony of my Call and acceptance to God1 s Holy Priesthood, I look forward with great anticipation to sharing in the
work to which we have been called. I have just learned that I will be
team-teaching a Temple School class on the "Teacher.,'' I a.'11. looking forward to this as a new challenge; it's the First Time I've ever done
it. I guess that's what resronding to Christ's Call is all about: A
series of FIRST TIME experiences., 0
column9: bad been ill before the document was presented to the quorums and Orders of the Church for consideration 0 When my husband, an Evangelist/Patriarch came back to our trailer on the Campus and announced that the Conference would be considering this document in the afternoon session, I was overwhelmed with a power that caused me to know that i HAD to be present
column10: I felt good about the service, where I was Ordained with others. My husband,. Varian was the spokesperson, assisted by our Pastor, Roger Mier, and E]der Dale Wells.

column7:
FROM FLOY L. BENNETT September 11, 1987
In my response to the request of Carol Anway,
Women's Ministries Committee, concerning my
experiences surrounding my ordination, I have
included the questions in your communication
as an outline for what I have written.

1. WHICH JURISDICTION(S) APPROVED YOUR ORDINATION BY VOTE OF THE
MEMBERS? WHEN? WHEN AND WHERE WERE YOU ORDAINED, AND BY WJ{OM
(ALL PERSONS INVOLVED)? TO WHAT OFFICE?
2. IF YOU WERE MARRIED AT THE TIME OF YOUR ORDINATION, WHAT PRIESTHOOD OFFICE DID YOUR HUSBAND THEN HOLD (IF ANY)?
At our Santa Fe Stake conference on September 22, 1985 I was one
of four women to be presented for ordination. I had been called to
the office of elder.
I think the four women presented at that time were perhaps the
first in our church to be presented for approval in the United States.
(I know less about Australia, England, etc.). If so, we were also the
first to be rejected. The night after our rejection I could not sleep.
The occasion had produced some trauma and sorrow for the troubled ones
who could not hear God's voice on the matter. During the night it began
to rain, ''These are God's tears'', I thought. How many times have we
brought him grief by our unwillingness to let him speak to our hearts.
Jesus knew sorrow. "He was despised, rejected, a man of sorrows and
acquainted with grief.'' The rain continued to fall and I wept also--
knowing I was not alone and that eventually God's will would be done.
Our names were presented again, along with others, to our stake
conference on February 2, 1986. We were approved. On February 9, 1986
I was ordained to the office of elder at the morning worship service.

!I -2-

My husband, William S. Bennett, a high priest, gave the ordination
prayer. He was assisted by our pastor, Richard Fortman. Our
daughter, son-in-law and our two grandchildren were present as were
one of my sisters and her husband. The next day I was called by a
reporter of the Kansas City Times for my reaction to the ordination
of women.
There were many expressions of love and support both orally and in
letters. The ones who were angry and critical did not write but some
did say hurtful things and shunned contact with me. I was comforted,
however, by a friend of many years who came to church the Sunday following the vote of rejection at our first presentation to our stake
conference. She entered the sanctuary and sat down on the opposite
side of the aisle from me. It was before the service started on
communion Sunday. She and her husband sat quietly for a few minutes;
then she stirred and seemed restless. Finally, she got up and came
over and sat down beside me. She said, "I don't want you to think that
I have anything against you, personally, when I voted against you. I
still love you; I just can't go along with the ordination of women.''
I understood her pain. (She just couldn't take communion until she had
made peace with me). I continue to have kindly feelings for her. She
is quite miserable. God bless her!
3. WHAT WERE SOME IMPORTANT LIFE EXPERIENCES AND EVENTS SINCE ABOUT
1970 THAT LED TO YOUR ORDINATION?
In answer to your question about life experiences since 1970 that
led to my ordination--how can I be sure? I have felt God calling
into his service since my earliest memory. I think perhaps, all my
me
life-experiences with God have led me to this event. As a small child

// -3-

I remember special times when I felt God was very close to me, com-
municating his love, his guidance and his hopes for me. I felt

challenged by these experiences to give myself and whatever gifts I
had to my church.
Long before the presentation of document 156 I had growing feelings about the need for women to share their gifts more fully in the
mission of the church. During the 1970s I was doing a lot of writing
for the church. For a year and a half I wrote weekly articles for
the back page of our church bulletin at Ridgewood. During this time
I wrote a series on the importance of growth as exemplified by little
children, and I often felt God's guiding spirit as I tried to put on
paper what I was not allowed to say behind the pulpit. Some of our
pastors would not allow a woman ''up front'' for any purpose except to
sing. Others were more open to the use of women for testimonies,
prayers, readings, etc. These far-sighted pastors discovered that
women had a real ministry to give in all areas of the church's
missions. They helped to prepare us for 156.
In February, 1985 (before my call to the priesthood was conveyed
to me by our pastor) we had our congregational retreat at Lake
Doniphan. The following Sunday I was one of several speakers to give
a report of the retreat. My assignment was to give ''My Thoughts on
the Retreat in Retrospect.'' I was the last speaker and in my attempt
to give a needed ministry that day, the Lord opened the heavens and I
was enveloped by the power of the Holy Spirit. Our Retreat theme had
been forgiveness. On this occasion the beauty of a forgiving spirit
wa3 brought home to us. His presence was so obvious that tears washed
the faces of many and they expreseed to me lat~r their recognition and
appreciation for the beauty of God'f loving presence in our service
thRt morning. Yes, I kr1ow he is always with us but only occasionally

JI -4-

ao we oper1 the doc 1 r of cur hea~ts and let him in. ~hat m0r~ing we did.
This 1s one event that helped to prepare ~e for my call and my

opportunity came late in my life and this bothered me but I remembered
that God takes what we have even in our later years and makes it enough
to meet the needs of those who look to us for ministry. My years had
never been idle years; God has just opened the gate to a larger field
of service.
4. DESCRIBE BRIEFLY YOUR ORDINATION SERVICE, INCLUDING YOUR RESPONSE
TO VARIOUS ASPECTS OF IT.

I feel there was really nothing very remarkable about my ordi-
nation service, except, perhaps my husband's ordination prayer. For

me it was beautiful and very, very special. The format for the
service was traditional but I knew the words "Here I'll raise mine
Ebenezer, Hither by Thy help I'm come'' were true and I must remember
them.
5- DESCRIBE BRIEFLY ANY CHANGE IN YOUR VIEWS AND FEELINGS ABOUT
WOMEN'S ORDINATION PROM THE TIME YOU FIRST HEARD ABOUT SECTION 156
TO THE TIME YOU WERE ORDAINED, AND SINCE YOUR ORDINATION.
I don't know that there has been any basic changes in my views

and feelings about women's ordination since 156 was given to us. How-
ever, my view has been broadened and italicized (re-enforced) so that

I can see more clearly that there is a place for women in a ministry
that is special because we are women. A telephone call late one night

asking me to come and administer to a young woman who was in great dis-
tress is a case in point. When I said of course I would come and would

l( -5-

bring my husband with me, I was told that I could not bring my hus-
band. The woman had been abused and would not allow a man to touch

her. Since no other ordained woman was available, I went alone. That

night I was made aware of another good reason for women in the priest-
hood. There are places where men cannot go or are not acceptable. No

doubt the reverse is true also. The Lord does anticipate our needs
and supply them if we will allow Him to do it.
I have also learned that giving a talk or lecture, as I have done

often during my life, is not the same as preaching a sermon--that giv-
ing a theme talk is not the same as "being in charge" of a prayer ser-
vice. Many things that have helped to prepare me for my priesthood

ministry are more clear to me now. I know that God has had a con-
tinuing interest in my life and has guided me in my pursuit of truth

and a growing understanding of my purpose for being.
6. TELL BRIEFLY OF TWO OR THREE OF YOUR EXPERIENCES IN MINISTRY
SINCE YOU WERE ORDAINED.
In addition to the experience of administration described above

I shall recount two other instances of ministry in which I was es-
pecially blessed. I was awakened one morning by a voice that was so

loud and commanding that I sat, startled, straight up in bed. I had
been awakened from a dream by a voice that said, "Go see your next
door neighbor!" That message in itself was hard for me to accept.
I had only one next-door neighbor so there was no mistaking who was
the object of the command. I knew my neighbor very well. She was a
quiet person, had had open heart surgery and has a profound hearing
loss. She liked her privacy and welcomed few visitors. Would I dare

knock on her door? She was very dear to me and I hesitated to dis-
turb her. 1 told my husband my dream and the instruction I had

11 -6-

received to go see my neighbor. His immediate response was, ''well,
go see her.'' I dressed, closed my own apartment door behind me and
stood facing my neighbor's closed door. Did I dare knock? Would
she hear me? If she did hear me would she resent being awakened so
early in the morning? I walked back and forth in front of her door
a few times before I found the courage to knock. Almost immediately
her door opened wide and she stood before me.
"Are you all right?" I asked. ''No, I'm not'' she replied softly.
I reached for her hand and stepped inside. "Let's sit down," I said.
"I want to tell you a most interesting thing that hannened to me this
morning.'' I told her my dream. She was moved by the impact of this
experience because she was experiencing angina and was in a state of
deep depression. At her request we made arrangements for her to
receive the ordinance of administration. Two other women were called
in; one of them, also an elder, assisted me. We had a remarkable
assurance of God's participation and blessing on that occasion.
Another occasion of God's intervention occurred in a time of my
own deep distress. I had entered a hospital for what was considered
minor surgery. Everything seemed to go well so in four days I was
sent home. The following day I developed complications and had to

re-enter the hospital. I was told that I would have to have medi-
cation, intravenously• around the clock for six days. What a blow!

I ~as missing my Templ~ School classes and here I lay helpless and
discouraged. I tried to pray but I could not communicate with the
Lord. Oh, I could say words but I could not relate to Him or feel
His presence. My mind was on my pain and my helpless state. I cried
and indulged in a lot of self-pity. I felt abandoned and alone.
Next morning, very early, a member of the hospital staff came into
the room I shared with another patient. I sensed immediately why she

I I - 7-

had come. ( At this point my own situation was put on hold). My
roommate, Barbara, a beautiful black woman, was told that she had a
terminal illness and that her condition was far-advanced. When the
staff person left the room I heard Barbara crying--great wracking
sobs that tore at my heart. I looked at myself--lying prone--
I.Vs and various other tubes attached to my tortured body and
wondered how I could help my suffering roommate. Slowly, I threw
back the sheet that covered me; with difficulty I scooted my legs to
the edge of the bed as I raised my body to an erect position. With
my feet on the floor I gathered the paraphernalia that encumbered me
and pushed it ahead of me until I reached the foot of Barbara's bed.
I touched her feet and said, "Barbara, I am a minister, may I pray
for you?'' She opened her eyes in surprise, looked at me for a moment
and then nodded her head in acquiescence.
As I started to pray the clouds that had encompassed me for days
dispersed and an overwhelming sense of peace came over me and over
Barbara. I felt God was not only standing with me and supporting me
in my effort to be an instrument of His peace and comfort to this
woman whom I did not know, but He was in me. My cup was full to
overflowing. Barbara and I were both God's children and we were
sisters in this exciting enterprise we call life.
It was interesting to me that when I was so full of myself and
my miserable situation I had found it impossible to feel close to
God and unable to communicate with Him. But as soon as I felt the
lonliness and sense of desertion of one who had been told that death
was near, I forgot my own unhappy state--God spoke to my distressed
spirit and I could hear. The ''still small voice'' erased the
thunderous clamor of my own troubles and made me whole.

II - 8-

ATTEMPTS TO SERVE IN YOUR PARTICULAR OFFICE? GIVE SEVERAL
ILLUSTRATIONS OF THAT RESPONSE, BOTH POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE, IF
POSSIBLE.

I suspect I have run the gam~t when it comes to variety of ex-

thing could have happened. We were defeated by a
segment of our church people who had not attended
any stake function for years. They came for one
purpose only--to defeat the ordination of women.
Of course there were several faithful, active
members also who voted against all women whose names
were presented. Their strength was simply augumented
to the point of majority by the afore-mentioned
segment.

When we were approved at the second conference there was an im-
mediate falling away of some very dear friends but by far the majority

of those with whom I had been associated through the years have re-
mained loving and supporting. Many wrote letters. Following are a

few quotes from those who wanted to assure me of their support:
''As you are aware, this will probably be a difficult
transition for many members of the church .... Please
know you have my full support and best wishes for
every success in your future ministry."
"We just wanted to let you know of our continued
love and support. It's always hard to be a pioneer.
Floy, you've been a lady throughout. You are poised
and caring and your insight and sensitivity mean
a lot to (my wife) and me."
"I just wanted to write you and share with you the
marvelous message that we are indeed, 'One in the
Spirit' and to let you know that I feel honored to
be a member of a church who recognizes truth in
calling one such spiritually endowed as you to their
priesthood. I hope you will believe me when I say
that I know it will not be easy to accept the
negative attitudes from those who refuse at this time
to 'let go and let God' have His way in His church, ...
