column7:
Carol Anway, Commissioner
Women's Ministries Commission

Dear Sister Anway:

August 28, 1987

Looking back on the 69 years of my life on this earth, I can see and know in my Spirit how the Lord has been leading me, I am a Jrd generation iLL.D.S. All my grandchildren ex- cept one who is only 5 years old are the 5th generation in the church.
From my earliest recollection I can remember sitting at my grandmother's feet and listening to her read bible stories
to me and to her other grandchildren.
bible IVIy mother also read stories to me and my sisters when we were little girls. She took us to Sunday school and church and all the while she had to bear the abuse and derision of my father who disliked the church, Through my grandmother and mother and my wonderful sunday school teachers I learned to love God and Jesus. There has
always been in my heart a love for the things of God. Ma~y times I have done things that I have been sorry for afterwards. I know I have grieved the Lord bit I rest in His
forgiveness. I was fortunate in my youth to meet a poor neighborhood boy whom I loved and married. Eere I can also see the hand of God in our lives.
Although my husband had no church affiliation he came to church with me willingly. He became active in the youth work of the church and was eventually called to the office of elder.
Ee later became pastor of our congregations for some 7 years. I supported him in his ministry and many times went with him
on his visits to the Saints, We raised 4 sons three of whom are in the priesthood. The other son is a loyal member of the church and supports it in
every way. Once a long time ago a member of the priesthood told me that I had the gift of discernment just as my grandmother had
many years ago, I know the Lord has revealed things to me many times and afterward I wonder why he chose me an unworthy person. About 1J years ago a beautiful unknown young girl came into one of our prayer meetings, At the time we were holding
prayer meetings in the church basement as we were
new church and the first floor had not been started, building a
The moment she came in the Spirit spoke to me and said, "here is one of the Lord's little wandering sheep'.'
the inspiration of the Spirit I spoke to her and told her she Fnder
was now on the right track and to continue in her search and
the Lord would lead her. It turned out that she was a member of our church up in the thumb of ri,ichigan. She was living near the church and was working as a legal secretary, She was trying to get over
an unhappy love affair.

/ ,, ")- Our son a few years older than she at that same time was r having a rough time over a broken love affair himself, Needless to say the Lord brought these two together in a wonderful way and they were married and have two healthy boys.
She became the church's music director, sunday school teacher,
Women's leader, church secretary among many other things,
Praise God!
My dear husband died in 1981 after battling cancer for 3 years. He ministered to the Saints until he was too weak to
go on. Three weeks before he died he said, "my work here is done. I want you to pray for me that the Lord will take me".
My prayers were directed in that way and he was taken home to his reward.
At the time I thanked God for relieving him of his suffer- ing and pain and I didn't realize how badly it had affected me.
I went through stages of utter grief, anger, resignation and
finally peace. In that time I suffered a heart attack and as
I lay in the hoirpital my beautiful
brought into the same hospital dead. Her heart had stopped 14 yr, old grandaughter was
beating on the school bus on the way to school. Through all this I was surrounded by the love and prayers of the Saints and after having major surgery and the mercy
of a loving God I got through it. A few years ago our pastor came to me and said he was con- sidering not to let his name run for presiding elder. He said
he was "burned out". The Spirit rested upon me and I told him
to let his name run and to hang in there there that the Lord would
send someone to relieve him. He continued to be our pastor
and not long afterwards the Lord sent high priest Leonard
Young into our midst and Leonard took over pastorship. A year or so ago one of my sons asked me, you do if you were called to the priesthood?" I answered, I'm "Ma, what would
not worried about that, I'm perfectly happy working where I am."
I was teaching sunday school, working in the Women's Dept,,
singing, church historian and worship commission chairperson,
In the back of my mind I wondered what my husband would have
thought. He was very conservative in his beliefs and many
times we had discussions over the scriptures where we didn't
always agree. I was also having difficulty with the idea because
I am given to tears quickly and I wondered in myself how I could
function in the priesthood under these conditions.--How could
I ever conduct a funeral? I had supported the calls to priesthood of the four women previously called in our branch and sincerely felt the rightness of their call--But not me, dear Lord, not me. I don't
want i ti
One day as church services were letting out and I was walking to my car Leonard Young called

ing home the thought hit me. He is going to discuss a priest- it. Then as I was drivhood call for you. I said to myself, "oh no, dear Lord, not

in the hospitals? He said, "Ginny, your ministry will be

You will have a compassionate, caring ministry and I look for- /
ward to the day when I can minister with J '' yov". I asked him to give me time to think it over, Within a week the voice of
the Spirit came to me and said, "my daughter, all these years
you have ministered doing things your way and in your own

My calling was approved by the Saginaw Valley District in the fall of 1986.
My ordination took place on December 28, 1986 at the King
My son, high priest Thomas H, Bennett, Independence,
Road Branch in Saginaw Michigan at the 11:00 a.m. service.
Mo, was going to fly to Mich, to ordain me but circumstances made it
impossible at that time. We had previously discussed my call and he was in total support of it as was my other sons and members
of my family, Tom told me that my ministry was not to be an
extension of my husband's ministry but was to be unique to my
own calling, So on that day my sons elder Guy Bennett and
elder Earl Bennett ordained their mother to the office of elder. Oh, by the way, at the same time "the little lost sheep" I previously mentioned was ordained to the office of priest by
her husband elder Guy Bennett! Since then I have received few negative reactions, A couple who were stalwarts of the church said to me they didn't believe in women in the priesthood and have gradually withdrawn
from services in the church, My heart breaks for them, I love them and pray for them that in time they will be able to accept
women's ministry, Presently I am teaching the adult class scriptures from Romans, I have taught a Temple School class and look forward
to teaching that same class this fall. I have occt,pied in the pulpit both presiding and in the spoken word and God has blessed me!
Incidentally I also presided at my first funeral a few months ago. It was a memorial service and I feel the Lord
blessed my ministry as I was able to occupy without breaking
down oncel Praise God!
Along with high priest Bobby Hardin I was asked to assist in the administrations to the sick at the Sanford Reunion this
summer, During this same reunion a woman asked me if she could
talk to me in private, We found a quiet place and she poured
out her problems to me, Later she said, "I'm so glad we have
women in the priesthood. I can discuss things with a woman
that I could never have had the priesthood been a man." I thought in my soul, please, God, may I always be worthy of your trust in me and the trust of my brothers and sisters. Amen

column9: Although my husband had no church affiliation he came to church with me willingly. He became active in the youth work of the church and was eventually called to the office of elder.
column10: My dear husband died in 1981 after battling cancer for 3 years. He ministered to the Saints until he was too weak to go on. Three weeks before he died he said, ""my work here is done. I want you to pray for me that the Lord will take me"".
column11: In the back of my mind I wondered what my husband would have thought. He was very conservative in his beliefs and many times we had discussions over the scriptures where we didn't always agree. I was also having difficulty with the idea because I am given to tears quickly and I wondered in myself how I could function in the priesthood under these conditions.--How could I ever conduct a funeral? I had supported the calls to priesthood of the four women previously called in our branch and sincerely felt the rightness of their call--But not me, dear Lord, not me. I don't want i ti

column7:
TESTIMONY OF SHIRLEY J. BLACKMAN

My ordination to the office of Elder was approved by the
Kansas City Stake in October of 1986. I was ordained on December
28, 1986, by my husband, High Priest John W. Blackman, and Elder
John L. Burlington.
My husband was baptized into the RLDS church as a child, but
was not always active. After we were married, his cousin John
Burlington, came to visit us often, always letting us know of
exciting things that were happening at church. John's parents
were not active in the church at this time, but decided to attend
church one day in San Diego, California. They had moved to
California only a couple of years previous to this time. The
Lord spoke to John's Dad through the Presiding Elder under the
influence of the Spirit. They wrote us a letter telling us about
this outstanding experience they had at church, This letter
carried a penetrating spirit with it. Upon reading it we were
deeply moved. We called John's cousin, asking him to explain
this deep feeling of excitement that we were experiencing.
Neither of us had ever been exposed to the Holy Spirit and did
not recognize what was happening.
This letter-experience changed our lives. We asked if I
could have cottage meetings, and I was baptized shortly
thereafter. I was 21 years old on the day of my Confirmation.
We became very active in the church. John received a call to the
priesthood. The pastor at that time explained that the wife was

-1-

13, ;)-
much of a calling as the husband. I supported John in all the
responsibilities that he carried. We were Stake Zion's League
Leaders for several years and were able to serve together. We
have always felt that our experiences in serving the church
together has caused us to grow together spiritually, and this has
given us a very strong relationship in our marriage as well as
with our children.
Before the revelation on women in the priesthood was given
we were living in Tulsa, Oklahoma. I kept having these feelings
that there was something more required of me. I bought several
books at the Christian bookstores on women in the ministry. I
was confused about a woman's role in the church. I saw so many
women offering a type of ministry that was unique to that
individual alone. So it was not really a big surprise to me when
the revelation was received.
My husband struggled with the document for awhile. But one

morning during a communion service the Lord gave him an under-
standing of the need for women to serve in this capacity.

The worship service the morning of December 28, 1987 was for
the ordination of Elaine Constance and myself - both to the
office of Elder and both of us wives of High Priest serving on
the Stake High Council. My feelings were particularly sensitive

to my ordination because I had asked my husband to be the spokes-
man. I knew that this would be very difficult for him in many

respects: one, he has great difficulty in expressing himself when
he is overcome with emotions and this was definitely going to be

-2-

an emotional occasion; second it is difficult to separate out
personal feelings and what the Lord has to say. But we had made
special preparations for this service for the past three months
and the Lord blessed him and he was able to give a very beautiful
and meaningful ordination. It is a moment in my lifetime I will
always treasure. It was also a momentous occasion because my
father is a life-time Catholic and he came to the service, as
well as all of the rest of my side of the family - none of whom
are members of our church.
Since my ordination, I have had several opportunities to
serve with my husband, not only in the pulpit but in
administration to the sick as well. It goes beyond any
experience that I have ever felt before, To serve together in
this way is a very humbling and demanding experience. I find
that it requires constant self-examination and challenges me to
become a better person. There is a certain feeling of closeness
as husband and wife, and when that is magnified in service to the
Lord it is beyond words to describe adequately.
I have had opportunity to serve the Communion on several
occasions, but just this past month (August, 1987), I had an
outstanding experience as I served the emblems to the members.
As I held out the tray to each one I had a sense of closeness and
unity with them and in some cases was very sensitive to their
particular need at that time.
I have become frustrated at times, because I have felt like
I was being asked to do more than I could possibly handle. I am

-3-

Jj- <:/
a person who needs time to think things through and make advance
preparation, but I have struggled with the fact that I do not
always have time to put as much preparation into each situation
as I felt I needed. One Sunday morning on the way to church I
talked to the Lord about this problem and I was made aware of the
fact that I needed to rely more upon His direction and power and
energy and quit trying to do it all by myself. That which I had
so painstakingly prepared to give for the Invocation that morning
was never used, and I let Him lead me in my prayer thoughts. It
was a beautiful experience. Since that time I have tried to be
more sensitive to this additional strength that is available to
me.
I have been very fortunate in that I have not had one person
come to me to express any kind of negative feelings about my
call. I have had a couple of women come to me and say that they
are still struggling with the document, but that they cannot deny
that I have been offering ministry. At the Women's Retreat last
Fall, I had two women come to me and say that they have been
waiting for women to be called to the priesthood because there
are some things that they cannot share with men. My heart has
been broken over the burdens that our women carry. I have felt
so inadequate and ill-prepared when called upon. I am learning
that listening is a very definite skill that the Lord wants me to
develop.
I feel that the Lord has developed some skills and talents
in my life in preparation for ministry. Patience and listening

-4-

/
/3/5
skills are two such skills. I am also finding that he has used
me to reach a wide age span of people. I am constantly being
urged to develop better communication skills and better writing
skills. I know that the Lord is not finished with my development
and I will continue to find those areas where He would have me
improve.
My desire is to improve my daily devotional life that I
might respond to the directions that he would give, I will strive
to improve my stewardship recognition that I might be able to
contribute more abundantly. I will seek ways to increase my
skills and talents so that they might be used to a fuller degree.

THE TESTIMONY OF
SHARON RUTH WHITE BLAIS
SEPTEMBER 15, 1987

7208 Raymond Drive N.E.
Albuquerque, New Mexico 87109
(505) 821-4955

THE TESTIMONY CJF SHARON RUTH WHITE BLAIS AND HER ORDINATION TO
THE OFFICE OF TEACHER SEPTEMBER 15, 1987

I was present at the World Conference of the Reorganized
Church o·f Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints when Section 156
came forth. I was quite surprised at first. I wasn't expecting
it at that time. As I attended a concert later that week in the
Auditorium, I felt the Spirit of God move through the audience
and I knew that God was in control. God often uses the medium
of music to speak to me. I knew everything was alright. As
November 1 ·7

; , 1985 came closer, there were seven women called in
the Pensacola District where we were living. I felt good about
them and knew their calls were right. At that point in time, I
could see it for many women, but not myself. I felt very
inadeqL1ate, very weak and insecure.
As time progressed, I found myself more involved in a
servanthood role. My husband and I first began a campfire team
that did outreach ministry to a low-income housing development.
Later we began a mission on the outskirts of Pensacola in
Cantonment, Florida. While there I was Christian Education
Commissioner and began a church school program. I was dedicated
to attending all Temple Schools offered in our area and
promoting them. We attended all district functions and
r!f?Uni ons. I served as co-youth leader and became very involved

1

with them. I enjoyed being a counselor at the Junior High
camps. I could feel the energy and growth taking place as I
began all these endeavors. I started visiting some of the
members and feeling a tremendous responsibility for them.
In the latter part of July 1986, my family moved to
Independence, Mi ssoLtri from Pensacola, Florida. On August 1, my
husband, George, became a full-time appointee for oLtr church.
Around the end of August, I received a telephone cal 1 from my
previous district president from Pensacola. He informed me of
my call. to the office of teacher. I was not totally surprised
for I had been very actively involved
mission

in the work of our smal. 1
at Cantonment and had felt God preparing me for more. I
had sensed that this particular call would be asked of me, bLtt I
trusted the Lord's timing to be right. Now that it faced me, I
questioned the Lord's timing. I had no home congregation.
was in the midst of a

r
great change in my life and going through
many personal struggles. I accepted this call with mLtch fear
and an><iety along with excitement and joy. My family and I
travelled back to Pensacola in one week-end for the Pensacola
District Conference. It was important to me that I be ordained
in the midst of those who loved me and had supported and
nurtL1red me.
opposition.

The conference voted for my call without

The following morning, September 14, 1986, I was ordained
to the office of teacher by my husband, Seventy George Blais and
a dear friend, Priest Steven Schinzing. The service was a part
of the district conference. It was very significant that my

2

husband ordain me because my call had come through him. He was
serving as the presiding elder of our mission when the call was
first processed. He had struggled for many months trying to
escape from this responsibility. It had weighed on him
·strongly. After several e>,periences with the Lord, George felt
he had no choice but to process the call. I knew that it had to
come from God for him to carry thrOLlgh tl1e process because he
knew all the Particulars of my life and the tremendous changes
and adjustments that were ahead for our family. There was a
spirit of gentleness during the ordination and I could feel the
love of God, those ordaining me, my family, and all the Saints
gathered with me, Several parts of the ordination prayer remain
with me. I was told that I had been nurtured to this point in
my life, and how pleased the Lord was. God addressed my fears
by stating: "Fear not when you have to stand before nations,
for my. servants of old have stood before nations before and by
the power of my Spirit they were subdued. Fear not when you are
persecuted, for my servants of old were persecuted and were
sustained by the power of my Spirit. Fear not, my daughter,
when you have to look evil face to face, for my servants of old
have overcome evil face to face by the Power of my Spirit." The
Lord promised to never leave me alone, that He would always
sustain me and go before me. The closing statement was, ''Now
receive the power of heaven that you might be ordained by the
Spirit." We did not tape the service or prayer so the things we
remember are cherished memories.
I felt great support and love.

It was a beautiful service and

3

As my family returned to begin our work in Independence,
Missouri, I was apprehensive. I had heard horror stories of
women Priesthood being rejected and opposed. I was unsure of
who to trust. We began attending church at the Woods Chapel
congregation in Lee's Summit. Part of our reason for choosing
this congregation was their openness to women. 0Ltr fami 1 y was
accepted very qLti ckl y and we soon felt a real part of the
family. I was allowed to move at my own pace. I remember
feeling very strange at the first priesthood meeting I attended.
Elder Ardis Everett was there which helped my nervousness, but
my inner feelings were, "I don't belong here. This is for men."
My tradition and background were struggling with the reality of
today.
My experiences in Woods Chapel were very pleasant. My
image of ministry changed and grew. At first I was very
perplexed because all the women priesthood I met were older and
didn't have children at home. I was 29 and had three children
aged 9, 7, and 3. They seemed free to go and do and I was
afraid of not measuring up to the work that they were
accomplishing. I could find no true role model for me. I had
never known a teacher at all. I had not been around any women
priesthood for very long and they were still attempting to find
their place. I finally came to the conclusion that I could find
my own way and not to compare myself to others because my
situation was totally different. I was freed to go my own way
and at my own pace. So much of my understanding seemed to
broaden as I studied and learned from those around me. I felt

4

very fortunate to be in the midst of such wonderful leaders in
Independence. I was able to attend several Temple Schools and
seminars. I was e><posed to new views and issues. It was such a
learning e><perience, Because George 1~as in the master's program
for orientation, we were able to meet people from all over the
world and gained more of an international perspective. My only
sermon, so far, was shared with my husband at the Woods Chapel
congregation. The Lord blessed us and we felt a special unity.
I stayed in "friendly territory" most of the time I lived
in Independence, As we visited various congregations in the
Santa Fe and Center Stakes, I felt accepted. My husband,
George, preached a series in the Oak Grove congregation of
Central Missouri Stake. It had the reputation of being very
conservative and against women priesthood. George had to be
careful as to his wording so that he could gain acceptance and
minister to them. I did not tell anyone there that I was
ordained, but the word got out quickly. I was informed of their
stand. Their reponse was to shun me.
and tense. I was most uncomfortable.

The atmosphere felt cold
I was relieved when the
week was over and we could return to our loving, supportive
congregation. I learned alot that week though. I felt the
persecution others have e><perienced. I felt the alienation, the
hurt, and the confusion. But I also felt God teaching me
compassion and empathy for their suffering. It was a learning
experience.
Now we are living in Albuquerque, New Me>:ico, where George
has been assigned to the New Me>:ico/West Texas Districts. We

5

haven't been here very long and I am struggling as I attempt to
find my place here. There is one other women priesthood member
living here now, Priest Georgia Rose. The word is not really
out yet about my priesthood. I am being cautious as I step out
into some unchartered trails. As George and I taught a Family
Ministry Temple School at their reunion, I felt much acceptance
and love. Thus far, I have been asked to use my gift as a
pianist in the two Albuquerque congregations and I have asked
the Lord to bless my ministry that His Spirit may be felt
through my music and may open some hearts. I feel that I do
have some support base now as we begin a new congregation.
my husband as leader, I anticipate a sense of freedom and

With

opportunity just ahead of me.
a people ready to serve.

There is much work to do here and
My husband and children, Chip, Tommy, and Shereehave been
very supportive. They have Prayed for me many times when I felt
inadequate to do what God was asking. George has been available
to me all along the way. It takes special consideration and
cooperation to handle our dual roles, especially with our small
children.
I was raised in this church by very loving and committed
parents. My family life was surrounded by church activities and
people in my small hometown of Bay Minette, Alabama. My father
was an elder and served as pastor several times and my mother
was very devoted to supporting him and her children while
serving in various roles such as women's leader, church school
teacher, etc. They gave me a very strong foundation in God and

6

our church.

I</ ✓ 1
During the latter seventies, problems developed and
due to an administrative mistake, my father was silenced. This
just about broke my family's hearts. There was much pain for
many years as we tried to understand and deal with this crisis.
Because I was married and lived in another city, I was able to
maintain ties with the church that otherwise I might not have
handled. Ten years later this matter was brought back up and an
official apology was given. My parents had felt too much hurt
at this point though and decided not to come back. They are
still very good Christians, ministering to those around them,
and I have come to terms with the whole situation as much as I
think possible. I have seen the best and the worst of our
church organization. My parents have been very supportive of my
priesthood calling. Their response was that they had raised me
and mY two sisters to be good priesthood wives. They figure
they did pretty well. My family and George's parents, who are
Catholic, were in attendance at my ordination service. My older
sister, Sheila Steiner, from Mobile, Alabama, was ordained an
elder in November 1986, just a couple of months after my
ordination. We have been able to share a new dimension because
of our new roles and have drawn closer.
I feel very comfortable with the teacher role although the
responsibility frightens me at times. I am a peacemaker. I
love and cherish peace. Peace between others and peace with God
is a need I have. I desire for my home to always be a haven of
peace - of oneness with God. I feel a responsibility to teach -
to share those lessons I have learned, to inspire and motivate,

7

and to challenge people to love each other. The topics that
most interest me are ones that deal with personal and family
relationships, subjects that draw us closer together. r feel a
call to listen - to listen with care and empathy, to be there,
to share a problem or pain. I feel I could study my whole life
long and still only conceive just a little of what God has for
Lts. The way seems so big and expansive. I feel hungry for all
the meaning and Ltnderstandi ng. I want to share it with all
those lonely and hurting people who seem to not know how to
love.
Ministry, to me, is serving. There are ministers all
aroLtnd us who give of themselves in loving ways to others.
Ministry is not exclusive to priesthood or to this church.
Everyone can be a minister. I am a big advocate of the ministry
of children. What a freedom, what a refreshing spirit they
possess. I have been ministered to by many people in my life
and they are each very special to me. I have been ministered to
when I have been touched by the love of another.
I hope to always maintain my hunger for learning and
understanding. I wish to develop my gifts and talents more
fully to the betterment of this world, I want to help others
find God and find their place in this work. I want to help
others see the loving God and the beauty of His world. I want
to share what I have and know that I might help another in need.
I want to be a follower of God and be His servant forever.
Sharon Ruth White Blais

I have always been very active in my local congregation in Liberty, Missouri. I have
taught a Sunday school class since my graduation from college and have worked in various other
ways with the Women's work and with youth groups. I have also played the piano and organ
from many of the services. I've always been willing to do my part to assist with the work of our
local congregation. I've also done work on the state level in Christian Education and Reunion
and Youth Camp work. Church has also been a very important part of my life.
Last summer as I was cleaning my house in preparation for the fall school terms. I was made
aware several times that I had a call to the priesthood. I just put the thought in the back of my
mind and didn't say anything to anyone. I had never really given much thought to being part of
the priesthood since section 156 was given. I just guess I thought it was meant to be, it would
come to pass. I was serving in the same ways as I always had in the past. The awareness come to
me several times of the period of a couple weeks.
During the later part of August, 1986 my husband said that our pastor, Roy Fraser,
wanted to come and visit during the week. I said fine. I still had not said anything to anyone of
my awareness of a call. When Brother Frazier and Brother Jim Ash, the associate pastor, came to
visit, they talked to Ron, my husband, first about his call to the office of Elder. He was serving as
a priest at the time. Then he said but that is not the only reason that we have come. He said,
Donna, we also have a call for you to the Priesthood. I told his of my awareness of the call. My
husband also added his awareness of the call at the time. In thinking of the offices, I thought I
would be called to the office of teacher. I was quite surprised when he said I was being called to
the office of elder. He said he had given much consideration to the call and was assured that it
was to the office of Elder. My parents were told of our callings before we were told and also
confirmed the calls. My father is an Elder and has served as the presiding Elder of our
congregation several times.
We enrolled in the temple school class for elders and my husband and I took the class
together. We were approved for ordination by the stake conference of the Kansas City Stake in
late October of 1986. Ron and I were ordained in the same service in November of 1986. I was
ordained by my father, Elder Raymond Armstrong and Patriarch Lloyd Siebert. It was a very
emotional and moving experience to have my own father ordain me.
I have been greatly challenged by the call to ministry. I have continued to work as a Sunday
school teacher. I have moved right into presiding and assisting with the services and preach my
first sermon on Children's Day this year (1987).
One of my first experiences shortly after being ordained was in the ordinance of
administration. I was helping to serve the communion on the first Sunday of the month. I got a
note during church asking if I would help with an administration during the service for a sister
who was to undergo major surgery. She was also to be ordained to the office of elder soon. It
was my first time to be assisted with the ordinance and I was to give the confirming prayer. I was
really scared for this was in front of the entire congregation not in the privacy of the pastor's
study. But I have found that when I put my trust in in the Lord, the Holy Spirit is there to bless
me in abundance.
I rejoice in the opportunity to bring ministry to my fellow saints and to those I love so
much.

Nancy Kaye Booker, Bryan
Priesthood Office, Teacher
Congregation, Bellview
District, Pensacola
Region, Gulf States

Dear Carol,
In response to your letter of July 15, 1987, regarding testimonies of women
ordained, I submit the following:
In 1970, Cleo and I moved from Iowa to Arizona because of my arthritis
problems, Following that move, I was blessed with reasonably good health
so, in the course of the next four years, I went to college and graduated
from Arizona State University in December 1974. Teaching elementary school
and having parent conferences were instrumental in releasing me from a very
real prison of shyness. Since we came to Arizona, Cleo has been District
President twice, so we have traveled to all of the Arizona congregations more
than once and have developed a sincere love for these people, This love and
caring hds helped in my personal ministry since my ordination.
My husband and I were not able to attend the 1984 World Conference, but as
the week's news developed, our daughter kept us informed by phone. It
seemed at the time that most of Section 156 was lost to the public except
the calling of women to the priesthood. I remember the day it was approved,
On the light side, our nine-year-old granddaughter had been listening to all
the conversations and asked her Mom, "Does this mean that women can stand
up front and talk, too?" Her Mom answered, "Well, I guess that is part of
what it means." Our granddaughter said, "Well, it's about time!" and went
on to play with her friends. That said to me that her generation will
probably not be bothered with the current problem but will have to deal with
something else.
Although I had always had a spirit of peace about the whole issue of women
in the priesthood, it never occurred to me that I would be called. When
the call came, my husband was District President. It was a real struggle
for him to present it to the officials. As he told me later, "I knew your
life would never be the same, You would have to bear burdens that you never
thought possible. I was not sure I wanted you to go through that." He
could have waited to present the call, but knowing that it was true, felt
committed to present it at that time. We fully realized that it was a time
of pioneering in the church and that some, maybe many, friends would be lost
to us. We also knew within us that time was short, at least for our ministry,
and there was no time to lose. I did not personally have a spiritual experience
to confirm my call, only a tremendous spirit of peace.

J(,, - Boswell - 2

The members of the Arizona District of the Pacific Southwest Region voted to
approve my call to the office of Elder at the Fall Conference in October 1985.
Before the ordination, I took the three required courses from Temple School and
was most favorably impressed with the quality of the material.
Choosing the spokesperson for my ordination was an interesting experience. As
Cleo and I were standing in church at Phoenix Central, I looked to my left and
was impressed to ask an old friend if he would ordain me. As we walked to the
lobby, I went to Brother Kenneth Henry and his wife Virginia and asked Ken if
he would consider ordaining me. He looked me straight in the eye and said,
"I would consider it an honor." Since that time, he has testified to me that
he had never been blessed so abundantly with the Spirit of God as when he was
preparing for and participating in that service.
On November 17, 1985, I was ordained by Elder Kenneth Henry and High Priest
Cleo Boswell at Phoenix Central. Since my name begins with a "B", I was the
first woman to be ordained an Elder in Arizona. The other woman was Kay
Sheridan. Two women, Lois Bohrer and Ruth Williams, were ordained Teachers
and Doug Hardy was ordained a Priest. The Arizona Republic press covered the
story, and it was circulated on Saturday, November 23, 1985, as part of their
religious section.
As we approached the ordination service with Apostle Spencer, there was some
discussion as to which would be most appropriate, to go down the center or
side aisle of the sanctuary. We decided that the side aisle would be best
and were seated on the front row on the right. Five chairs had been placed
on the platform to be used by the ordinands. I remembering whispering to Kay
that women would need to present a much different posture than the posture
typically used by men.
The sanctuary was nearly full, and the service went without unusual incident.
We taped and typed my ordination and gave a copy to Brother Henry. In retrospect,
I have found that having it for referral has given me strength and direction.
Family support was important on November 17, 1985. Our daughter flew in from
Independence, the other daughter and family drove in from Globe, and our son
and his daughter came from Mesa. There was a special feeling of unity and
love. A former classmate from Lamoni was also at the service. As a convert
to the church, I especially appreciated the privilege of being a part of a
world church.
I have had total support from my husband in every aspect of my ministry. We
have gone out for administrations together but have not made it a habit to
be paired in ministry. In fact, I have discouraged it, probably because I
wanted my own identity. After his 30 years in the priesthood, there would
not be too many experiences that I could have that he had not had. So it has
taken patience to let me express my excitement and enthusiasm without comparison.
This helps us keep things in focus.

J/q Boswell - 3

The acceptance of my ministry has ranged from the young to the old--no specific
age group. I decided early-on not to take adverse reactions to my ministry
personally, and it has, in my opinion, paid great dividends in friendships,
acceptance, and healing, not only of my contributions but of other women in
general.
It has been important to me that public ministries be done in a professional
and dignified manner. Consequently, I have never referred to myself as a
woman priesthood member when conducting or speaking at a service. It is my
opinion that we should be sensitive to the tender feelings of people and not
bring undue attention to ourselves in any manner.
The Arizona women priesthood met once as a specific group and discussed specific
things which are unique to women, such as: proper dresses, flawless hose,
proper jewelry, makeup, nail polish, etc. Most things discussed, other than
those things listed, would also apply to men. I suggested that in the future
we could possibly serve as mentors for the newly ordained women to discuss
unique women's problems but not separate ourselves from the men priesthood in
priesthood meetings.
I see one of the major ministries of women to be visiting one to one with
single, divorced, married, widowed, elderly, or ill women who are alone or
hesitant to talk to men.
My first preaching assignment was in Mesa and was attended by approximately
150 people, many of whom were winter visitors. My family, those who were in
the area, came to give their love and support. The Lord's Spirit ministered
to me that day in abundance and, by testimony, with some of the people. The
enclosed picture of the Mesa Congregation Directory was taken at that service.
Two of my special experiences have been:
1) The confirmation charge for our grandson. After the service, my
husband said, "I was so interested that I forgot to turn on the
tape recorder," and Clay Condit said, "Only a grandmother could
have said that in that way."
2) A 1987 Winter Reunion evening service. I was in charge of the
service, with Cal Carpenter preaching. The persons assisting
drew that service together without the slightest problem. A
long-time friend came up to me after the service, held my hands,
and said, "I didn't realize that you were in charge of the service
until it was nearly over. That's the way it should be. It went
so smoothly. I mean it as a real compliment. 11 And I took it as
such, thanks to a fine team.
My concept of ministry during the past decade has changed. I see ministers
talking with us instead of to us. I see a trend to get on with the work of
Christ and let other faiths do what they feel they need to do. The "worth
of persons" is a phrase that has been useful to me as I have talked with
people of other faiths, as well as people of our own. Before my ordination,
I was not so much aware of the need to be "ready to go" spiritually, physically,
and mentally. Although it is impossible to fulfill that kind of commitment,
it does cause me to make a more conscious effort to try.

;(, Boswell - 4

Directed study in preparing sermons has become not only necessary but has
been fun. When I have allowed the Spirit to direct the delivery, sermons
have had a very special place in my life. To be invited back to a congregation
for a second or third time humbles me. I have always prayed that if there was
one person to whom a message should go let me say whatever needed to be said in
the proper way. Time after time, testimonies have given me the assurance that
the Lord has, indeed, blessed them.
Only one scheduled preaching appointment was canceled, and that was by the
pastor. He considered it a poor time to introduce women from the pulpit.
As yet, we have not been invited to minister in that congregation.
I have never been aware of anyone walking out of
participating. There were some, and still are, those persons who come to
a service while I was
services because of loyalty to their commitment, not because a particular
person is "up front" serving in a priesthood capacity.
As of August 31, 1987, my priesthood activities have included:
10 Preaching assignments - Casa Grande (1), Cochise (1), Flagstaff (2),
Mesa (3), Prescott (1), Show Low (1), Yuma (1)
12 Administrations
20 Assisting in services
15 Hospital visits
1 Blessing
1 In charge of a Sacrament Service
1 Serving Sacrament
2 Confirmation charges
3 In charge of preaching services
1 In charge of Memorial Service
2 In charge of prayer services
60 In-home visits (approximately two a week)
Enclosed are copies of media coverage, which may or may not be of interest
to your study. If there is further information needed, please let me know.
Thank you for encouraging me to recall some very special memories.
Sincerely,

A. Marge, the fact that your husband, Lawrence D. Burgess passed

MY TESTIMONY: MY ORDINATION EXPERIENCE

By Ethel S. Carlile, elder

I was at the World Conference, 1984 and vividly recall my feelings
as I heard the document read, that was subsequently declared to be God's
revelation and became Section 156 of the Doctrine and Covenants.

MY REACTION
The sequence of my thinking was:
I- Isn't this wonderful? It is so right!
2- I wasn't expecting it to come at this conference.
3- I'm glad my age will exempt me.
A lady sitting a few seats from me asked at the close of the delegates'
and visitors' briefing, "Well, what do you think of this?" My response was
that I was pleased and felt it was right. Then she wanted to know why I
felt so.

I was rather surprised to be so bluntly challenged but
that an experience in a woman's class at Warsaw, MO (our home at the time)
I'm certain
had prepared me. So I answered by quoting words found both in the Bible and
the Book of Mormon, that in the sight of God there is no bond or free, black
or white, Jew or Gentile, no male or female.
The woman said she had never thought of that but she had reservations
of her own. Perhaps I might have had some also, had I not had the experience
mentioned before. In our women's class, one sister declared that she was
certain our church would never ordain women. The Good Spirit bore witness
right at that moment that such a judgment was not right, and the scripture
just quoted came to mind as verification of God's impartiality.
I have to smile at my own thinking that my age would exempt me!
Since my call and ordination, I see that in God's sight neither sex, life's
station nor age are points of prejudice with Him. In
to have

fact, I am grateful
this new challenge and opportunity to serve, come in my late
My four children are

sixties.
grown and now I have the time to study and prepare that

I would not have had, had my call come earlier in life.

FIRST INDICATION OF MY CALL
In late Dec ember of 1985, I had a dream in which I was preaching to
a congregation. As I awoke I reflected on some aspects of this dream. One
factor was that this certain congregation was one of the most vocal against
Section 156.

In the past,
4-_rr~ 4-a,:/::I of\ I had felt that many people used dreams as an all too frequent substaelA!-t-r.on

of what that person had alread deemed he wanted, and the dream would verify
it!
Interesting! The Heavenly Father was showing me another way in which
I must not limit Him: neither by age, nor now, by means of communication.
This has been a growing conceptual influence for me.

THE INTERIM

For the next six months, I felt a restlessness, an urg~hat I had
never experienced before. In early May, 1986, I wrote a note which I entitled: Foi.- Future Reference. In it was expressed a feeling of a "possible"
call. Also, I noted the office to which I felt I might be called.
The very next Sunday, our pastor asked if he could visit our home that
afternoon. At the appointed hour, Jim Estes, Jr., our pastor, and N. Glynn
Colthorp, Far West Stake President, knocked at our door.
The instant I saw the two men I knew why they had come. I had never
mentioned any of my feelings to my husband. I felt these experiences must
be between me and the Lord. (Anyway, I knew Jack would be be so happy,
this might color my own thinking.)
Brothers Estes and Coltharp promptly stated the purpose of their visit
and Jim asked me if I had had any evidence of a call. How glad I was that
I had written my Future Reference note. It was truly a strength and testimony
to each of us.

APPROVAL OF CAIL

June 29, 1986 was a significant date for me. It was my husband, Jack's
sixty-eighth birthday and our forty-fifth anniversary. It was also the day
my call to Elder was approved!
The Far West Stake Conference was convened at 2:30 P.M. at Camp Farwesta,
near Stewartsville, MO.- Nine women and six men were approved for ordination at
this confernece. (Significant only to me, perhaps, is that I was the first

woman approved in Far West Stake; that 1 only because our names were presented
in alphabetical order.)
Two others from Summit Avenue congregation were also approved, and our
ordination date was set for July 20, 1986.
THE ORDINATION SERVICE

Apostle William T. Higdon, whose father, Earl, was a first cousin of
mine, brought a message of challenge and encouragement to the ordinands and

the congregation. One statement he made lingers in memory. "With the ordina-
tion of women, the church can now walk on two legs rather than limp along

on one."
Hymn 1/409, "Touch Me, Lord With Thy Spirit Eternal" was sung at my
request. The seconJ stanza seemed significant and pertinent for this
service.

After the singing of this hymn, I was ordained by Apostle Higdon, who
was the spokesman, assisted by my husband John (Jack) Carlile and my son,
David, to the office of elder. It was very meaningful to have these family
members involved and supportive.
From the beginning, Jack, who is an elder himself, was truly elated
as he envisioned our being able to minister together. In the ensuing year,
my greatest joy has come by serving at my husband's side. I have drawn
strength and comfort, and unflagging support from him. I realize I have
been singularly blessed by having this support from him. He has showP
nothing but pride in my accomplishments and his encouragement is a constant
blessing.

MY ELDER'S JOURNAL

I started a journal of my activities as an elder and in reviewing
it, I see that I have spoken eleven times in twelve months, assisted in
administration thirteen times, presided at prayer services three times,
helped prepare and serve the emblems at a Maundy Thursday service, offered
invocation and benediction prayers at various local and stake functions
and given a pastoral prayer and a Communion message.

A few experiences might be of interest. I was asked by our present
pastor, Elder Gerald Sooter, to offer a pastoral prayer for a Communion
service. Praying is usually quite easy for me, but this was a unique experience. Ample time had been given, but I just could not gather significant thoughts. I awoke on Saturday morning and presented my dilemma to
the Lord, asking Him for help. Soon, thoughts were coursing through my
mind. I wrote them down and without changing a word, I read it next morning at Communion. This was the first time in my life that I had ever read
a prayer but as I re-read it, I realize that it came as a gift and was
couched in language greater than my own, and had I refused to use it as
dictated by God's good Spirit, the ministry given would not have been as
great.

ADMINISTRATION

Several administrations have had significant impact on me. My
first was when my husband and I were called to the bedside of a brother
who had struggled with the call of women to the priesthood. The warm
feeling of fraternity that we experienced was indeed rewarding.
Another experience with a dear friend was less than rewarding. Indeed,
it was alarming. As Jack and I placed our hands on her head, we felt her
body stiffen and the room seemed hostile and unfriendly. We prayed that
the Spirit of light and comfort would dispel the feeling of anxiety and
disquiet, but we left, troubled. Our prayers continue for her as she
fights for a return of mental health in a psychiatric ward.
The third experience brings happier results. A woman from another
city asked to visit us with Jack's brother and his wife. We had met the
lady a few times but knew nothing of her problems. The Good Spirit
prevailed during the ordinance, and in my prayer of confirmation I felt
led to ask that she be released from any feeling of guilt she might be
carrying, and that she might accept complete forgiveness that our Savior
offered through His crucifixion and the power of His resurrection.
A powerful sensation, almost as "electricity" accompanied our prayers.
At the close, she bared her soul to us. In the past few years, she said she
had written three suicide notes. She has three daughters: one a drug
addict who has been married and divorced twice; one is an alcoholic who
calls her mother daily to tell her how much she hates her. The third,
moved to a western state as an unwed mother.
This woman was carrying a crushing load of guilt. We talked following the administration and she said she felt for the first time in

years that she could get a handle on her life. Her departing comment was,
"Don't tell me women shouldn't be in the priesthood!" Our prayers continue
for her.

OTHER ACTIVITIES

There have been many rewarding opportunities. One was when I was
asked to participate in a service where President Wallace B. Smith spoke
on the Temple building project. Afterward, he was gracious enough to say
that he had appreciated my participation in the service.
The one time that I felt a bit uncomfortable with the uniqueness
of being the only woman in a service was at a stake conference which was
helij in a local high school. All participants were asked to assemble
back stage. I found myself surrounded by the stake president, his
counselors, the bishop and his counselors, the stake council members and
an apostle I had not previously met. Although every man made an effort to
greet me, I truly felt alone. For the first time, I felt the impact of
the uniqueness of the role of women in priesEhood.
My overall acceptance has been positive. I feel blessed that our
Summit Avenue congregation in St. Joseph is affirmative and progressive.
Only one family makes it a point of leaving, or not being present if a
woman participates.

BACKGROUND FOR CALLING

I'm not certain which activities in the last decade or so can be
said to have been contributing factors for this call to priesthood. Like
Nephi, I was born of goodly parents and their influence has always been
a guiding factor in my life. Opportunities have been abundant to serve
throughout my lifetime. At sixteen, I was asked to teach a class of eleven
year old boys, that the pastor and several others had given up on. Since
then, I have taught all ages,- from nursery to adults. I've been a women's
leader for several congregations and as a stake leader in Central Missouri.
I have been a worship chairman, music director, reunion class instructor
and pre-baptismal teacher and helped in cottage meetings.
Always, I have tried to make myself available, many times taking
jobs I felt ill qualified for, when no one else would do them. I have
found that being plunged in over my head, has caused me to lean upon a
Heavenly Father who is ever available to help.

A young man who was ordained an elder the same day as I, made the
statement that I might not be doing many things as differently as before

ordination, but now it would be validated. This may be true to some ex-
tent, but I certainly feel a deeper commitment and a stronger sense of

calling than ever before.
I am grateful for the opportunities that I am being given to serve.

Our stake president has asked me to give a fifteen minute talk at our up-
coming priesthood retreat, and our pastor has invited me to become one of

his associates for the coming year. To be ordained and never be given the
opportunity to serve would have been disappointing.
As we serve, we grow by disciplined study, prayer and dedication.
To this end, I lend all that I now am, or ever hope to become.
Ethel S. Carlile, elder

PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF MARY JANE CARTER

I am a wife, a mother, a grandmother, and an employee of the U.S.
Postal Service in Colorado Springs, Colorado. I supervise three persons,
and together we are responsible for adding ZIP+4 Codes to all addresses in
southeastern Colorado. And I am an elder in the Reorganized Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter Day Saints.
During the first year of my marriage I worked as a secretary in the
Kansas City Stake Office. I then moved to Colorado and settled into the
traditional role of wife and mother. I washed clothes, dishes, windows, and
sweet upturned faces. I bandaged knees, made trips to hospital emergency
rooms, and rocked babies to sleep. I canned peaches and strawberry jam and
baked apple pies.
I was a Girl Scout Brownie leader, a Cub Scout den mother and a Sunday
School teacher. I taught literally hundreds of children, and those children
in turn taught me--about patience, and long-suffering, and love, and caring,
and tenderness, and brotherly kindness.
I learned about caring from my three-year-old. He picked one of my
lovely flowers with almost no stem. "I brought you a flower, Mommy," he
said. Then a concerned look crossed his face, and he peered over the petals
closely. "I don't think it has a bee on it," he said.
I learned about brotherly kindness from a five-year-old. Soneone
brought a family of Negro children to Vacation Church School, and as the
Director I was concerned about how these children with a different skin
color would be received. After class I checked with the teachers. "Did you
have any problems? I asked.

(

"Well, no, not really," replied the teacher of the kindergarten class.
"When Donna came in to the class, Lynn went over and sat down beside her.
Then she rubbed her finger across the face of the black child. Then she
scooted up next to her and said, 'You've got black hair, just like mine."'
For the first time we were aware that every child in that class had blonde
hair, except for Lynn and the newly arrived black child.
And I learned about love from my two-year-old. I remember him knocking
on the back door, and when I let him in, he tugged at my skirt. "Mommy!
Mommy!" I knelt down so he could talk to me eye level to eye level. Throwing
his arms around my neck, he said, "I love you, Mommy." "I love you too,

a pile of dirt.
I would not have you think that all was sweetness and light. When I was

stand a stripe of red paint down the side of my Buick."

delegate session in 1984 when President Howard Sheehy read the

. . . "do not wonder that some women are being called .... ,"

I felt then that there was a possibility that I would
be called. I did not want to think about it, though, and I was
able to consider priesthood calls for women as priesthood calls
for other women. I was joyful and uplifted at God's new gift
to the church.

As time passed, the thought came to me that I might
eventually be called, and I deliberately set the thought aside.
That worked quite well for a time but the impression came more
and more frequently, Sometimes I could go for a week without
thinking about "being a priesthood member."

The assurance that I was being called of God grew
stronger and I no longer could avoid thinking about it. At the
same time, I who have always been active in church began to feel
a rather different kind of caring for my brothers and sisters.
There were many, many times when people seemed to respond. openly

2

to my ministry. Several times when I was teaching children, I
saw adults in the room listening intently and I knew I was
teaching them.

The agitation of the Holy Spirit affected everything
I did. It was always with me. It gave me energy, it gave me
creativity, its intrusion often annoyed me, but I could not
push it away. I knew I was called.

I was so sure of my calling that I thought it must be
in process by the Center Stake High Council of which my husband
is a member. That procedure is confidential and I did not want
to cause Ken any awkwardness by telling him what I already knew.
From time to time a comment would burst out, and I would immediately
wish it hadn't. It was difficult for us both at that time, both
of us wanted to talk, yet neither one could.

I felt very much alone, but I was alone with my God --
and that's not a bad place to be. out of my aloneness with him
came the understanding that the service of any person may not be

compared with the service of another. Jesus Christ is the stan-
dard and the only standard. That realization gave me the courage

I needed. It overcame my difficulty in trying to see myself as I
had viewed men serving God's people. I knew there was a place for
me in God's field of white harvest.

I had to know the office to which I was called, One
day as I was reading, I came across a description of an elder
(not the passage in the Doctrine and Covenants) and I felt I was
reading a description of myself. Later I tried to find it but
I could not. I do not even know from what book I read, but I
knew I was called to serve God's people as an elder.

3

Soon after that, Ken and I were in the kitchen and
with my back to him I said, "Don't look at me and don't answer,
I know I have a call to the office of elder." He said nothing
and we finished our work in quiet. Just voicing that to him
relieved some of the pressure I was experiencing.

Ken told me afterward that at the next meeting of the
high council, when my call was approved, he said to my pastor,
"Will you please tell her soon? She knows she is called. She
knows the office and it is as if we•ve had an elephant in the
living room that we couldn't talk about,"

My pastor, Scott Sinclair, with his counsellor, Scott
Roberson, told me about my call at Brother Roberson's home just
before prayer service the next Wednesday evening. My husband
was there with me and my reply was "Finally, we can talk about
it! fl

I accepted the call to the office of elder and was
one of the first women ordained at Stone Church June 1, 1986 by
Evangelist Patriarch Clifford Cole and High,. Priest Kenneth Chase.

A number of people who are unable to accept the priest-
hood ministry of women have left Stone Church. The ones who re-
main, for the most part, have been very supportive of women.

Many go out of their way to make positive comments to me, and
when I serve connnunion, women are particularly outgoing, smiling,
thanking me, patting my hand, whispering "God bless you."
In addition to rostrum ministry, connnittee work and

visiting, much of my ministry is in response to requests for ad-
ministration. These requests come from men and women, ordained

and unordained of various ages. Because of the number of people

4

who have sought my ministry and who began to seek it so soon
after my ordination, I realize that there has been a substantial
base of acceptance of and desire for women't ministry before that
priesthood ministry became available.

When I preach in other congregations, some people do
not attend but those who do are extremly supportive, kind and
very few make perfunctory comments to me at the door. They seem
to know exactly why they came and want to say so. One of the
blessings of the ordination of women, I believe, is that members
are clarifying the bases of their faith.

While many men in leadership positions are heartily in
favor of women's ordination, a number of them have not yet
learned how to use women effectively. There are also people who
expect women to continue to serve in all the ways they have done
in the past and add to that the responsibility of priesthood.
There must be a time of adjustment as leaders adapt to the usage
of women in church structure and as women allow the Holy Spirit
to help them select day by day their fields of service.

More and more people are accepting the fact that an
ordained woman is not an ordained man. Her words, her mannerisms,
her thinking are just a few of the differences. While there are
people in favor of women's ministry and people opposed to women's
ministry, I believe there is a congregation of good people who
are waiting to see how it will work -- waiting to see the word
made flesh--waiting to let God do what he will do.

I thank my Lord for that gift of "agitation" by the

5

Holy Spirit, I thank him for revealing his will to me. I
thank him for offering me a place with his people who serve.
He is my strength and he is with me always unto the end of
the world.

On March 15, 1987, I was ordained to the Office of Elder. My local
branch, Jacksonville, Florida, approved my ordination at our
regular business meeting on November 9, 1986, and further approved
at the North Florida District Conference on January 18, 1987. I was
ordained on March 15, 1987, at my local branch by my son, High
Priest R. Alan Smith of San Diego, California, and my pastor,
High Priest Jack E. Crabtree.
I am married to an inactive member of our church who supports my
ministry in that I am not discouraged in any way - in fact I am
encouraged to participate fully. He did attend my ordination.
I received my Patriarchal blessing on February 12, 1969. This was
after several years in my life when I was inactive in the church
by choice - a wrong choice, I might add - and then about five years
of active membership after a conscious decision to change my lifestyle and again serve my Lord in the way in which I feel I was
intended to serve. At this time there was no thought that women
in the priesthood would ever be called, much less accepted. It
was just that I had been greatly blessed with talents that I was
not using as a part of my stewardship in the service of our Lord.
I was told in the blessing that the Lord was "aware of your desire
that your life shall be lived from this time forth in a way that
shall be most pleasing to your Heavenly Father." At a later point
in the blessing I was told, "Therefore, continue to prepare yourself and develop for the future work that lies before you." Of
course, I had no idea then that it might be in the priesthood.
However, I reamined true to my promise to serve. When Section 156
was first heard about, I was pleased because I felt it showed
that our Lord truly created all of us equal, that He loved each of
us equally, that He realized potential in all of us regardless of
our sex, that He realized the ministry which could be given by
women as well as men. At the same time I felt a pang of fear as
I thought, "What if I were to be called?" All my life I had in
actuality served in fields in which I felt comfortable, i.e.,
music primarily, teaching a class if there was absolutely no one
else to do it, serving as branch treasurer. I allayed my "fears"
as I thought over my past life - not exemplary for some years,
the thought that "I already do enough," so rather dismissed the
possibility of a priesthood call from my mind. All the time I
supported the prophecy and wholeheartedly supported the first
priesthood call of a woman in our district.
Then on October 16, 1986, I had a scheduled meeting with our pastor
for what I thought was a discussion of the financial matters of our
branch in preparation for the local business meeting. I was told
at that time that I had been called to serve in the office of Elder.

PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF DAWN M, CLAVEY

My testimony about ordination to the Priesthood may not be
what is expected or anticipated under the circumstances, but I will
share it anyway.

-J

I was officially advised that I was called to the office of
Teacher in August, 1985, by High Priest Dale Luffman, who at that time
served as the District President of the Maryland Missionary District,
and Elder Tom Jeffries, the pastor of our mission. The call was
presented to and approved by the Maryland Missionary District
Conference on November 17, 1985, and I was ordained on January 17,
1986, by High Priest Richard Hollaway, our new District President, and
Elder Tom Jeffries.

My testimony, however, starts long before the appropriate
officials of the church received enlightenment concerning my call to
the priesthood, for I knew that I was called as far back as 1982. In
fact, in a broader sense, I have felt "called" ever since the day I was
baptized at age 27 in 1962 and rationalized it as being a call to
membership, as it may well have been. But the compelling drive I

experienced to study, to reason things out and to share my under-
standing with others is what I have witnessed over the years in men who

were called to the priesthood but not usually in those being baptized
into membership.

I was always involved in the life of the church and

experienced great fulfillment in everything that I did. I felt a love
and concern for the people in our congregation in a pastoral sense and
carried a real burden for the spiritual welfare of each one. I visited
in the homes, shared the spiritual insights I experienced, and

counseled and taught whenever the opportunity was there, all without
the "benefit" of ordination. In all of my conversations with others in
the 1980s concerning ordination of women, I was not opposed to it, but
rather, expressed the opinion that I didn't believe that it was
necessary for women, or men for that matter, to be ordained in order to
render a service in the name of Jesus Christ. I never felt that my
ministry was any less authoritative than that of the Aaronic priesthood
members, and in some respects, I think it may have been more received
because I was one of the group. So, except for the fact that I now
have had hands laid on my head with a prayer of ordination, nothing has
significantly changed in my ministry.

There were many events in my life that brought me to where I

am today, the most significant being:

1. In late 1971, after a period of searching, I concluded
that there were apparent differences in what the people of the church
were saying they believed and what the people of the church did. It
became such a problem to me that I left the church. For the next year
and one-half, like many women of my generation, I experienced an
"identity crisis," and the resultant search for answers brought me back
to the church. Over that period I came to the realization that change
has to be made from within, both individually and collectively, and
that I personally needed the strength and support of the fellowship in
order to be a complete person.

2. My husband, who is not a member of the priesthood, had a
change in heart at about the same time I was experiencing my "crisis,"
and began to encourage and support me in my eagerness to grow, which he

2

continues to this day.

3. In mid-1977 my husband, youngest son and I moved from
Baltimore, where we attended a fairly good size branch, to rural West
Virginia, where there was only an unorganized group meeting. For the
next three years our group merely held its own, not really connected to
the rest of the church, but establishing ourselves as a unit. Then in
1980 we became a mission in the Maryland Missionary District, and from
that point on, with new leadership, everything seemed to open up for
us.

One of the greatest opportunities I had to minister was when
we had a baptismal and confirmation service for five children and two
young women and I was asked to give the charge to the candidates. I
was so excited to have that opportunity because the experience of my
own baptism and confirmation was still so vivid and meaningful to me
that I wanted to help make it a memorable experience for them.
Another experience that stands out for me was one of an
entirely different character when I was asked to lead an early bird
fellowship service at Reunion this past summer. It was a real "cotton
mouth" experience because it was the first time I had ever functioned
as a member of the priesthood outside of my own district.

Some of my most heartwarming experiences came at my first
priesthood retreat where there were just a few women in a gathering
that was obviously accustomed to being all male. No one was the least
bit antagonistic or expressed negative thoughts, but it was apparent
that not everyone accepted us openly. And I could understand that and
had no desire to offend anyone. It was just a very humbling time for

3

me as I became aware of how many struggled to cope with a painful
situation that they weren't prepared for, and here we were in the midst
of them, and they had to deal with it.

I have never witnessed a negative response to my ministry. If
there is any, it has been under cover, for I certainly am not aware of
it. I have received positive responses, however, for which I am deeply
grateful, not for myself, but because the Spirit of Jesus Christ has
moved through me to touch peoples lives. One very memorable time was
when a teenage boy, who attends our church but is not a member, told me
he got more out of my one sermon than he had gotten the whole time he
attended our church.

At this point in my life I perceive my ministry to be that of
helping the people of our congregation to feel that they are persons of
worth; to understand their own uniqueness and how God works differently
within each individual to reveal himself through them; and, to try to
create an atmosphere where each one desires to give himself--a whole,
integrated, complete person, to the service of others who need their
unique ministry.

My perception of ministry has been heightened since I was
ordained and given the responsibility of presiding over worship
services from time to time. I try to get into the spirit of the theme
and the different avenues of worship available so that I can become a
channel through which the spirit can flow into the people who are
gathered. Again, it is such a humbling experience that I sometimes
feel overwhelmed.

4

him to use me as He would and to make me sensitive to his presence in
my life. In a sense I feel I have made a choice for my life time and
it is a life of dedication and service to others. It is my desire to
perform in my ministry in the best possible way and in a manner that
is pleasing to my Heavenly Father. In spending time talking with
those who find themselves struggling yet with Section 156 of the

and in sharing the Good News with those we come in contact with.

building process and together we compliment each other as we work
together and invite others to join with us in building the Kingdom of
