TO: Women's Ministries Commission
FROM: Mrs. Regina H. Colby, (P) 1519 Lexington
Pueblo, CO 81001
DATE: September 1987
RE: Personal Testimony Concerning Ordination
My ordination to the office of priest was approved at District
Conference of Southeast Colorado (October 6, 1985). I was
ordained December 1, 1985 at Pueblo, Colorado by my brother,
(HP) Joseph Holst; he was assisted by my husband, (HP) Lawrence
Colby. My husband held the office of high priest at the time
of my ordination. Since then he has been called to the office
of Evangelist-Patriarch. His ordination is pending.

PREPARATION

Events leading up to my ordination might include the following.
In 1972 at World Conference, the revelation to be acted upon
was given for the first time to the general delegation simultaneous with the priesthood quorums. I thrilled at this experience. No longer did I have to wait to hear about this type
of church business from my husband as he would return from
the quorum session. Though I really didn't feel any particular resentment, I knew God had given me a good mind and
I could act responsibly. As the years have progressed, I've
observed this general session grow in its influence and confidence. By 1984 it had a chairman and parlimentarian. It
could pass resolutions on its own. With some sadness I left
this body and attended the Aaronic Priesthood quorum session

in April 1986. I do rejoice in seeing this type of growth in
the church during my lifetime.
Personally, I was against women in the priesthood due to my
cultural background and interpretations of various scriptures.
But time, circumstances, and the Holy Spirit brought change
in my life. Two appointees, who were assigned to our area,
Kees Campier and Charles Kornman, continued to challenge me
about my narrowness. Brother Kees, especially would confront
me with deep spiritual matters and bring up the latest Bible
criticism and research on the role of women in the early
Christian church.
Just prior to World Conference 1984, I attended the funeral
of a dear friend. Her pastor was a woman. This lovely lady
brought a unique ministry to the family and to me. God was
working with me; I see now. Womanhood could bring ministry.

WORLD CONFERENCE 1984

We attended conference 1984 with a ho-hum attitude. The task
force on womens' ordination had recommended that this business
be postponed. Only usual business matters were at hand or so
it seemed. Then on Tuesday, the inspired document came forth
to the conference. In my heart, I was thrilled with the
thought of what freedom it would bring to me personally. I
could pray up front in church, teach without fear I was
usurping a priesthood member's place, and really utilize my
gifts more effectively. Before voting on the document on

2

Thursday of that eventful week, I arose early to study and
pray. Galatians 3:28 was so impressed upon my mind that later
in the day I voted affirmatively. Immediately I went to Park
College to get information on the Master's degree of Religion
for my own priesthood preparation if such a call should come to
me. I was unable to work out a satisfactory schedule with Park
College, so I literally spaced out any call for me. I believe
the Holy Spirit had a hand in this so I would not cause any
undue pressure on my husband. He had the duty of processing
the calls in our area, being the district president at the time.
I simply got busy with other things and forgot all about it.

THE CALL COMES

After a busy Saturday teaching a temple school course in Seibert,
Colorado, my pastor, Elder Don Carter, dropped by my home and
told me of my call to the office of priest and that it had been
approved. This was September 21, 1985. Sometimes a light
surprises! What a shock! I was totally unprepared for this and
at first I refused. I then spent a sleepless night. Darkness
invaded my soul and only did it subside when I submitted to the
call. The call came to three other ladies in my branch at this
same time. A wonderful spirit attended the branch business
meeting at the time the calls were voted on.
PRIESTHOOD EXPERIENCES

Getting a handle on all this took some time for me, as it was
so unexpected. Experiences that have been significant include

the following. The serving of the sacrament, including
serving at World Conference 1986, has led me to understand
servanthood better. I have sensed greater power in my teaching and counseling ministries. I'm more assertive and confident. Preaching is a delightful struggle at times. With
so~e hesitation, I attended the first Aaronic Priesthood
session in 1986 fearing I wouldn't be seated, but it worked
out well. My most outstanding experience since my ordination
has been to be used by God to bring His word to the people
at our 1986 and 1987 reunions. I have both the messages and
mechanics written up in my personal notes. Because they are
to a particular people at a particular time, I feel it would
be inappropriate to include them here. I will give this, how-
' ever. During the 1987 reunion, I had to go home to tend to
some business one afternoon. I was ''in the spirit'' for the
first half of the trip and recorded my thoughts on the back
of a grocery list, as I had no other paper. I edited them
later. I don't know if I ever felt as strong physically and
spiritually as when I presented the message to that congregation. Every time I recall this type of experience, joy
floods my soul.

OTHERS' RESPONSE

Most others have responded positively to this. Those who
have refused receiving sacrament from my hand concern me.
Family ministry and visiting has been frustrating. It seems
that people do not want ministry in their homes until a crisis

4

occurs and then they prefer someone from the Melchisedfc
Priesthood. I'm presently trying to circumvent this problem
and am setting goals and making plans for family ministry in
other areas. I'm planning a family worship workshop and a
series of family fun type activities this coming year in our
Pueblo branch. My neighborhood Bible study is active and
growing. However, I have been working with one family now
for over a year and still healing hasn't come. I'll still try
to help them.

MINISTRY

Ministry is a God-given privilege to lead people to the saving
grace of our Lord and Savior, Jesus Christ. I see priesthood
helping others to become disciples who in turn will help in
reconciling the world to God--its Creator. Why God wants to
use our hands, I don't fully understand; but, that is the way
He works. I interpret my office and calling as a priest much
like John the Baptist--there was a person sent from God to
bear witness of Jesus Christ--(John 1). I identify my call
to the priesthood with this fully.
CHANGE

In my ordination prayer, my brother stated that this act was
a fulfillment of my life. Over the years I've changed, hopefully for the better. My cultural bias has been broken and
has brought me great freedom--to risk, to explore, to serve.
The time has come for the fulfilling of the wedding supper

5

parable where any person--man, woman, or child--desiring to
serve God is called to the work. For are not all called
according to their gifts unto them, and we are all one in
Christ (

TESTIMONY REGARDING MY ORDINATION TO THE OFFICE OF PRIEST Carlay J. Cole September 10, 1997 I was made aware of my call in early September, 1985. The Crammond Mission of which I am a member, formally approved the call in early October, and the Alberta District business meeting approved the call in late October of 1985. There was no desenting vote in the mission or district. I completed the necessary Temple School courses by correspondence as we live 50 miles from the nearest congregation. I was ordained on February 6, 1986, at the Crammond Mission near Caroline, Alberta. Evangelist Wilbert Hayden gave the ordination charge, I sang "Touch Me, Lord, with Thy Spirit Eternal' as a solo expressing my prayer of committment. I was then ordained by High Priest Al Mogg (spokesman), Elder Vern Larson (pastor), and Elder Harvey Cole (my husband). Following the ordination my husband and I served Communion. It was an affirming, humbling, and joyous experience. Church activities which I was engaged in during the period of 1970 to my ordination are as follows: 1)I worked with the Indian Ministries teams which came to our area, at first cooking for camps, and later assisting in organizing as well as cooking. 2) I served the Alberta District as Worship Commission chairperson....suggested, planned and directed a Saskatchewan/Alberta Worship Workshop six months ahead of "Worship 81" and designed numerous worships for district and regional functions thru these years. 3) Served local mission as music leader and historian. 4) Attended a Wholeness Siminar, Worship 81, World Conferences, and Leadership Forum in Independence, Mo. 5) Enrolled in numerous Alberta Arts and Culture Workshops to gain skill in drama, ie. readers' theatre, clown, mime/movement, story theatre, etc. 6) Took a 30 hour course entitled "Introduction to Family Life Education" thru the continuing education department of the University of Caloray in 1982. 7) In 1983 I took a 72 hour course entitled "Leadership Training! Experience, Process and Design" also thru the University of Calgary. 83 Attended a weekend workshop on human sexuality 9) Continued autoharp and singing skills, also did some composing. 10> Staff member on Youth Caravan and camps. 11) Began doing creative writing for church publications 12) Continued my own personal study of scripture and reading of "self/relationships improvement" books. I am enclosing an article I wrote that appeared in the July 85 issue of the Herald that expressed/es my feelings prior to and following the 1984 Conference when Section 156 Was approved.

In spite of the aforementioned church involvement, I found myself feeling very inadequate after my ordination. I felt a real inadequacy in the area of being able to be "Prasent to persons" on a one to one basis. I therefore applied for and was accepted ante a 40 week bastoral counselling training program with the Canadian Association for Pastoral Education. It was an intense learning experience. We studied peychology, theology, counselling models, carried our own case loads (under supervision) and were required to undergo our own psycholotherapy with a certified psychologist This training I consider to have been one of the most valuable and impactful of any in my life. I will be wing it in numerous ways both personally, in my family, as well as in my formal ministry. I have many hours of study and reflection I wish to continue as a result of this year'o study. in conjunctiom with this training, I also took a 20 hour course in Neurolinquistics and a weekend workshop in sexual counselling. 1 have taken mine temple school courses and am currently working on two mora The format of my ministry has not greatly changed since ordination. I have given four sermons, served communion, assisted "up front", attended priesthood meetings, served as co-temp pastor at junior high camp for two years, and still involved in district worship design for reunions and gatherings. I have had some feelings of frustration in not knowing how or where to use all the things I have taken training Tor, and a sense of guilt in the indulgence of "learning" and net "using". We reside in a community in which there is not a congregation of this church and this complicates pastoral counselling as I'm not "recognized" by other denominations. I do see some possibilities for out reach and in-church ministry in doing pre-marital counselling and I now have a good foundation on which to add some specialized training. I have also been asked to teach the Family Advocacy temple school course in the Alberta District. This will be a practical use of my training. I have felt affirmed and supported both by the neliberahip and prieethood since my ordination. I have been the only ordained woman in the two congregations we attend up until this time. I am aware that some, both priesthood and others, are struggling with the ordination of women, but I have been sustained and treated respectfully. I respect their feelings and position and do not feel threatened by it. When serving communion, I and the pastor assure that I serve on the side of the building where these members rarely are sealed. I sense they are beginning to accept the ordination of women and, hopefully so, as there are now approsimately six other women to be ordained.

I have suffered a faith crisis since my ordination. Three factors have contributed to it. 1) A sudden awareness of the "aura" that many of our members place on priesthood. One day I was just a "member", the next I was an "authority". 23 The preparation of sermons.... I seemed responsible at a different level for what I said. The "authority" issue again 3) An in depth prodding by our supervisors in my pastoral counselling training of my theology for such. Probably again related to "authority". ..who am I to counsel? That question WHO AM I became central. Who am I to preach, to perform the sacraments, to counsel, to be priesthood???? I still deal with the issue, but most helpful have been Moses's burning bush experience and the concept of "in His Image". There are times when I would like to have a spectacular experience to confirm my convictions (a paradox, I know!) but that does not seem to be the "history" of my experience. My lot seems to be to "study it out". Such reflection always leads to an affirmation that I am, as awesome and Mumbling as it is, "set apart" to function in His Image of calling and serving persons for enhancement of life and for God's ourpose in the immortality and aternal life of humankind. Resectfully submitted to the History Commission of the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints.

TESTIMONY OF MADELEINE, AUG, Co RMAN, my calling to the Melchisdec priesthood. Several years before the World Conference voted on what has become Section 156 in the Doctrine and Covenants I had an experience that buzzled me and caused me to reflect. Early one morning as I was finishing making the bed the following thought came to me:" I must prepare myself through prayer, study, and fasting to more of a degree than I had been doing. I had obtained my goal to become a teacher and had used those skills to earn a living and to work with the youth of the church. These goals had been realized and now was the time to orepare." This experience lasted for only a few seconds, but was so strong that I can remember in minute detail exactly what I was doing and how I felt. This experience puzzled me as I was already heavily involved with church activities. I would like to say that I followed the admonition to pray, stude, and fast daily and to orepare myself, but I was not consistent in those areas as i should have been. We had some frjends who attended the World Conference in 1984, and learned of the document presented to the delegates. Many in our local congregation were very concerned, and we prayed for the leaders and delegates during that week. Several events helped me to acceot Section 156. When our delegates returned they bore their testimony of a soirit of peace moving among the majority of the delegates. I remembered the dedication of a few women in my home congregation, where I grew up, to keep our mission in Canton, Oklahoma going financially and spiritually. Then in October of 1984 our good friend Vera Entwistle of Australia visited us. I could see changes that had occured in her life as she had dedicated herself to serve in the priesthood. All of these events helped me to see the potential women could bring in further ministry by being part or the priesthood. My calling came through our pastor Elder Lean Benedict of the Eugene Congregation. I was very surprised and shocked that I would be called to the priesthood. I could not accept right away, and spent much time in prayer and fasting that I might have confirmation of my own. I received that confirmation as I offered a bendiction at a Christmas worship service. As I offered the prayer I felt hands were being placed on my head, and I was to accept the office of elder. It is very hard to put into words the feelings that I experienced at that moment for it was brief and yet so profound that it changed my life. I felt and continue to feel very humble that Our Lord sees in me the potential to serve in His holy priesthood. After I accepted the call it was voted on by our local congregation, and submitted to the Southern Oregon District Conference in March 1985. There were four women and two men approved at that conference for ordination from our local

congregation and we were confirmed in a beautiful service on May 4,1985. I was ordained by district president High Priest Bob Jendrey assisted by my husband, Elder Marry Coleman. After the ordinations were completed I served communion along with Priest Janice Wigle and our husbands. There was such a feeling of love expressed by the membership as we moved among them from smiles, to a squeeze of the hand, to tears of happiness. It was a day I will never forget. Following the service I was asked to assist in an administration, and since have found that many have called me to administer to them because they can share many concerns with me as a woman. I have felt very blessed to have been called to the priesthood in this area because I have been supported, loved, and upheld by the membership of our district. Even those who continue to struggle with women in the priesthood have come to me and reassured me that they love me. continue to pray that Our Heavenly Father will lead me as I offer ministry to his people for I know how inadequate I am in so many ways. I do desire to be a part of building His kingdom on earth and am willing to do His bidding to the best of my ability.

My father was a center stake delegate at the 1934 World

the conference, my father came home and told us of the mood of
his fellow delegates. My mother and I were astonished to hear
that God would be calling women to the priesthood. Personally, I
did not like the idea. I was not strongly opposed to it, only
uncomfortable with it. The more my father talked of how the Holy
Spirit rested on the conference in relation to this issue, the
more I accepted the idea of women priesthood members. After all,
even God said that "All are called according to the gifts of God
unto them" (Doctrine and covenants, 119 :Bbl. I was fifteen at
the time of this conference. Little did I know that I would be a
part of this historical movement.
I was very involved with Zion's League. I loved the
association with church youth like me, I held several offices.
I enjoyed doing things for other people and I felt that my
heavenly Father was pleased when I helped His children. The
possibility of priesthood service never entered my mind until a
trip I took with a group of Zion's Leaguers in the summer of
1986.
Historic Caravan 1986 was a tour of all the church's major
historical sites, including Kirtland, Palmyra, and N,,.uvoo, ,i._
turning point for me was our visit to Kirtland. we had a special
service in the temple. I came to the service in an expectant

of the testimonies about the outpouring of the Holy Spirit at the
dedication of this temple and I wanted some of those same

wonderful.

not think of myself. At the end of the service, I felt that God
had betrayed me. I felt utterly alone. After the service, we

working towards his teaching credential at the College.

Some of the Saints felt I was weak in the Faith by marrying out of

the Church especially since Dean rarely attended Church services. How-
ever I had made a commitment at the waters of baptism and would continue

to fulfill my promises to God of dedication, steadfastness in the Faith
and my calling to be a Saint in all it entails. I prayed continually for
my companion that he might find the Lord, but I continued my work in the
Church as usual.

Our son Dean Jr. was born in August 1950, and our daughter Katheen fol-
lowed 21⁄2 years later. Any thoughts of College for me at that time were

forgotton as Dean felt that I should be home to raise our family. Both our
children were blessed as infants by Elder H. Dean Hintz who also married
us and was our Chico pastor. I continued to teach Sunday School during
those years and became active in the Women's Department also. In Sept.
1953, Dean accepted his first teaching assignment at Fort Bragg, Calif.
51⁄2 hours drive away from our parents and the Chico Saints. We were on
our very own, so to speak, for the first time in our married life and the
challenge our our futurelay before us. I was anxious to become a part of
the Saints in Fort Bragg.
I cannot tell you how upset and fustrated I was upon learning there
was no Church in the Fort Bragg area. The closest one was in Santa Rosa,
2 hours drive to the south ! I had never been without Church priviledges
and my children, though small, needed the teaching of the Church to shape
and mold their lives as my had been. What was I to do? I could not drive
the distance every Sunday to Santa Rosa. That was out!
I wrote to Independence asking for names of Saints on the rolls in the
Fort Bragg area, which was then the Unorganized Area of Northern California.
They sent me a list of three narnes .... two women and one man. I wrote to
each of them to inquire if they were interested in meeting for study or
services if a priesthood member from Santa Rosa would come to our area.
I received no answer. So I wrote again ....... still no answer. I then
called each one on the phone. One I could never get. One told me in
no uncertain terms they were not interested and do not bother them again.
The man told me he had not gone to church in over 40 years and was no
longer interested in church. I couldn't believe it!

Testamony of Rosalie E. Crawford

Meanwhile, the children and I attended another Faith where I sang

in the Choir and went to Women" s activities. ,r also taught Sunday Sch-
ool there from stories of the Bible, but I never felt at home, though

they were very friendly and cared for us. I wrote to Chico Church for
old Sunday School materials and pre-baptismal lessons and with these, I
taught my children the Faith of their mother and of the Reorganization.
When they were old enough to be baptized, we took them back to Chico
where they were baptized in the Chico Creek by Elder H. Dean Hintz and
confirmed the following day at Church.
About six years after we had moved to Fort Bragg, the woman who I never
could get ahold of by phone or letter, called me. She explained that she
was a nurse who worked nights and slept days. She hadn't answered any
of my letters because she really wasn't interested in the Church anymore
since she hadn't attended in 50 years. She had aquired some habits that
were not in keeping with the Faith and felt ill at ease among church
people. BUT recently her youngest grandson had died and the stirrings
of her Faith began to come to the forefront of her life. She needed
the Church and its ministries and would do anything it took to see that
these came to Fort Bragg.
Rachael Witson then went to Santa Rosa for ministry. She drove as
far as Sacramento to find ministry. Both places said Fort Bragg was not
in their District or Stake. She camped on the door step of the Santa
Rosa pastor Elder Hyrum Crownover until he took pity on her plight and
agreed to come to Fort Bragg once a month for Communion Services. So
the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints came to
Fort Bragg for the first time!
There were 12 present for that memorable day. Rachael, her husband, her
daughter and her three children,Dean, myself and our two children and
Hyrum Crownover and his wife. We were served Communion and enjoyed the
outpourings of the Spirit as we rededicated ourselves to the Master.

Testamony of Rosalie E. Crawford

Within a year or so, Fort Bragg was taken into the San Francisco Bay
Stake. Ministry was sent to us twice a month. Another family moved into
our area; a woman and two children followed by afamily of 6 whose father
was a deacon. We now had ministry of our own to holJ Prayer Meetings
and every Sunday Worship services. Shortly after the Richard Schatz
family moved to Fort Bragg, he was ordained to the office of Priest and
and could serve us Communion. Another family came; a priesthood member.
Our cup surely runneth over!
This was a growing period for me in my service to the Lord and His
people. All of the members in Fort Bragg were either converts or had
come back to the church after many years of absence. ,J was the only one
who had a constant membership of many years standi.ng. I was appointed
Solicitor and sent weekly monies via inter-branch Banking to the Stake
Office and Bish~p Gene Hummel. Since I was the only filer in our Group,
this job was mine for as long as we remained in Fort Bragg. We were
organized into a "Group" by then Regional Administrator James Kemp.
Seventy John Muceus spent two weeks in Fort Bragg and gave each of us
such wonderful spiritual ministry that none will ever forget'. At a
Prayer Meeting where Communion was served, John Muceus spoke through the
Spirit and told us ''Be of good cheer, little flock" .... and went on to
say that if we would keep "His" commandments and remain faithful in all
things, our companions would enter into the Fold and be reunited with
us in the Faith. At this time, none of our companions were members of
the church except the families with priesthood spouses. What a bless
ing to me. The Lord truly wants my good husband into the ministry, so
we both can labor for Him in service to His people. At that time, I
gave up all my constant efforts on Dean and let go, and let God, so to
speak. I fullyconcentrated on the rearing of my children in the faith
and began to polish my life and soul that I might magnify my Lord more
fully in all ways.
In 1967, we left Fort Bragg for Hemet, California where we were met

Testamony of Rosalie E. Crawford I -5- 'J 5

again by a loving, caring Church family. Hemet was and is the most
loving congregation with whom I have ever been associated. I felt
right at home and plunged into the work of the Lord. Our children at
this time were 14 and 17 years old. They enjoyed the Zion's League
activities and were happy to be in a larger church home with more
services and planned activities. Even Dean began to attend church
more often because the Saints became friends to him and drew him into
the Fold.
Almost 22 years after we were married and on our daughters 18th
birthday, Dean was baptized by Elder Don C. Hinderks. For me at least,
it was a fulfillment of the words spoken to us in Fort Bragg several

years before concerning our faithfulness and our companions coming in-
to the Church in "His own time". How great and good the Lord God is

to His people: My heart was truly full and now, we both could go forth
in His service and accomplish so much more working together as a team.

In visiting, home ministry, preaching assignments, offices of responsi-
bilities in the congregation, etc, we worked together for the good of

all and for the glory of God.
During the following years, our son married and Kathy our daughter
attended Graceland College. Dean became our pastor for 4 years; also

Bishop's Agent for the remaining years until the present. I held al-
most every office open to women plus sang in the Choir, played for

services, taught Sunday School, was active in the Women's work, etc.
Our Stake President Nels Badder asked me to assume the position of
Stake Christian Education Director, an office I held for two years.
We obtained materials from the Kirtland Stake entitled "The Sermon
on the Mount" and all congregations were united with the same theme

and lessons on Sundays and Wednesday nights. Again, this was a grow-
ing period for me. I wrote Sunday sdpol lessons for the Stake on

special holiday themes and Church holidays to go along with the Ser-
mon on the Mount lessons. The years sped by and Dean and I were

happy and content in our service to the Master ~nd His people.

Testamony of Rosalie E. Crawford

When section#156 came forth, I was very upset and couldn't accept
the Section concerning the women. It was entirely out of focus in
the tradition of our church; in my heart I acknowledged that tradition
had no room in Christ's Church, but I was, nervertheless upset over
the revealation! I voiced my concerns with Dean, our pastor Crawford
David, our Seventy Cecil Robbins and our daughter. They all, though a
few had had concerns at first, accepted and could see the Lord God's
hand and mind in such a beautiful revealation. The need for further
ministry within the Church was necessay and they felt the hastening
times were upon us when all "who were called and responded were sent .... "
I earnestly prayed daily, even several times a day, that He would
show me that this portion of Section #156 was of Him. I prayed also for
the Prophet that He would be continually open to the Lord's will and
way in this matter. Voices from around the World Church were coming to

our ears in such negative reactions, that of leaving the church or form-
ing new ones, that I was concerned for their souls. I continued on in

the work of the Lord in the Hemet area and the L.A. Stake as usual. My
prayers to Him began to fill my life and I felt that He would give me
that necessary assurance that would lead me to accept .... or reject .....
this revealation in its entirity.
My service in the Church took on a different meaning during this time.
My life had been spent for Him and all that was near and dear to me was
reflected in that Church life and service. The only joy and happiness
I have ever known had been in my commitment and dedication to Him. If
I could not accept the revealation and the prophet, then I must leave
the church. I could no longer be a" fence straddler". The time for a
deceison to be made was now!

The Lord did not reveal to me the answer to my prayers and pleadings.
I must rely on the intellect and reasoning abilities He gave me and the
portion of His Spirit that dwells within me to know the truth. In June
of 1985, fourteen months after Section 156 came forth, I bowed my head

Testarnony of Rosalie E. Crawford

and surrendered to His Will and the knowledge that His Spirit gave to
me that all_ persons are His and that each has their own individual
gifts and talents, given to them by Him, thatthey might magnity Him
in their calling, whatever and wherever that calling might be or lead.
I recognized that He views us not strickly as male or female, but as
His children, sons and daughters, and that the hightest calling ''to be
a Saint" applys to all, regardless of gender.

I cannot put into words the peacefulness that envaded my whole be-
ing after my decision had been made! The Church was my whole life .... I

could never leave it or give it up! My commitment to Him all the years
of my life could never be denied. I was His to shape and mold and go

and do when and where He called or sent. My commitment took on a great-
er depth than ever before. Nothing else in this world was meaningful

unless He and His people were involved. All else was shallow and mean-
ingless and stagnant. I was His daughter and would remain so the remainder

of my life. I was His aND I could not or would not reject Hirn in any
way or means. I was a different persons inside; strengthen by His
love and concern not only for me, but all His people.
In September of 1985, our former presiding elder Crawford David and

the present presiding elder came to me after Church on Communion Sund-
day and told me that the Lord was pleased with the comrnitmemt of my

life and had called me to the office of elder. Thecall had come through

Brother David and was sent to the Stake President Nels Badder and ap-
proved at the Greater L.A. Stake High Council and the President of the

Church. I just sat there and looked at them and began to cry and kept
shaking my head "no" as they were explaining. I had never given any
thought to the possiblity of being called into God's priesthood. I had
just madc?my decision to fully accept a short while before! I was told to
discuss this with my husband and give them my decision in three weeks
in time for the brochures to be made up before the Stake Conference in
October. I left the church feeling small and unworthy of the calling.

Later, dicussing this with Dean who was overjoyed at the calling,
I asked that we both make this a matter of prayer that I might have

ll'estamony of Rosalie Crawford

His confirmation to this calling. I could not concentrate on my work at

my employment. Every time I thought about the calling and all it emcom-
passes, I got very emotional and the tears flowed freely. I wanted so very

much to talk this over with my mother, but she had lain upon a bed in a

Rest Home following a massive stroke that left her terminally unfunction-
able for almost a year. But the time was drawing short when I must give a

answer to Brother David. And to date, the Lord had not given me a confirma-
tion of the calling.

I called Seventy Cecil Robbins to our home one evening, only asking
him to come over for a short while. Not only was he the Seventy in our
area and lived in Hemet, but he and Donna were and are close, personal
friends besides being brother and sister in the Faith. As he came into

our home, he looked at me and said" The Lord has called you to the Mel-
chisedec priesthood". I asked him how he knew this and he smiled at me

and said he had known for a long time that the Lord had a calling for me
in the Melchisedec priesthood and that when my life was totaling His to
direct, then would it come forth. Cecil had known how I had felt about the
revealation and how I had come to accept it and change my life. I told him
how Dean and I had been earnestly praying for two weeks and no answer had
come to me concerning my calling.
the Lord desires us to accept the

He again smiled and said that many times
things of the Spirit on faith and that

perhaps at a later time a confirmation will be forth corning.
stressed that knowledge of my calling to him by the Lord and

He again
that He had
a work for both Dean and I to do for His people not only in the Hemet
area but wherever He sent us; wherever His people needed ministry.
One week later, I told Brother David that though! had not had a direct

confirmation from God concerning my callin.9 to the office of elder, I wou-
ld accept the calling on the faith of Seventy Robbins and his own testa-
rnony (Brother David's) about the diviness of the calling. Brother David

told me it took .him many months before he sent in my calling to the Stake
because he wanted to be so very sure of it and because it meant I would be
the first woman to be called in our congregation and all that it would en-
.tail. There were and ar€ a very few who do not accept women pristhood in

Testamony of Rosalie Crawford

our congregation. Whenever God had called to do a specific work or
responsibility, I have never hesitated nor refused, but have always
gone forward doing whatever it was to the best of my ability. And
God has never forsaken me! He's always been there to enahnce and make
the work wonderful and challenging. Though I had some misgivings to my
ability, I knew the people of my congregation loved me and would accept
me in the priesthood, and those who didn't or wouldn't, well, I would love
them extra special and let God deal within their heartys and lives.
In October 1985, seven other women in our Stake and myself were voted
upon at the Fall session of the Greater Los Angeles Stake with President
Nels Badder presiding. There was, to be expected, some who were against

Section 156 and voted dowm every woman, but they were in a very small ma-
jority and the eight women were voted favorably. Next came classes to

enable us to further the Lord's work correctly and to give us a background

for priesthood. The Spirit within these classes, mostly men, was so ac-
cepting and I thank God for this as it made it easier and more enjoyable

to the class as a whole and must was accomplished.

I was ordained on Sunday February 9, 1986 with Bishop John Muceus giv-
ing the Ordination message. He and his wife Marjorie have long been fri-
ends of our; Marge sang at our wedding and her father married us. George

Plante of our congregation was ordained an elder at the same service.
A large crowd was in attendance and only those who could not accept 156
was absent.,. Seventy Cecil Robbins was the spokesman and assisting were
High Priest Crawford David who initiated the call and my husband Elder
Dean Crawford. Brother Cecil spoke to me of the desire of the Lord to
enrich my life and that of my companions with this calling. Knowing of
the desires of my heart for service to Hirn and His people, this call had
come. Knowing of the enveloping love I have for everyone, the call had

come to me at this time that I might be a "pioneer" for him in the priest-
hood of His Church, I was told to remember that I was an elder every

minute of every day, waking or sleeping and that I was God's person chosen
and called to do a specific work for Him. Though the path ahead of me in
my ministry may at times be thorny, looking to God for direction, I would

Testamony of Rosalie E. Crawford

go forth with Him leading me as I have done in times past and He would
send me where persons needed His guidance, comfort and Spirit. I felt a
surge of power during that ordination and I knew truly that this is where
He wanted me and I dedicated anew my life, talents, abilities ..... all that
I am and have to the work entrusted me as an elder in His Church. The

Saints were very accepting and loving to me and many came from great dis-
tances to the ordination. One of the members of the Stake High Council

called me the morning, early, of my ordination to confirm again his
knowledge of my calling and to wish me God's blessings in all that I did
for Him and His people.
My ministry began almost immediately. Bishop Jack Boren of Seattle
came to our home for administration for an illness. Jack and Dorothy
have been winter Smowbirds to Hemet for years and were dear friends.(He
recently passes away as has Crawford DaYid.) When I first layed my hands
upon his head, I felt that same surge of power within me and the words of
love, healing and compassion flowed so freely from out my mouth. He was
given yet many months of life in which he always served His Lord. He was
to preach the follwing day at church and was so weak that he could barely
stand. The day following his sermon, he came down with Shingles, but the
Lord, in His great mercy and compassion, allowed Jack to speak .. I have
administered to many persons, Gave a Batismal and confirmation charge,
assisted in one and was spoksrnan for confirmation, presided at several
11:00 Worship Services, presided at Prayer Meetings and also assisted
with them, served Communion almost every month unless I was the speaker,
also have preached at Hemet, and in the Mohave Desert Area several
times and in Anaheim Congregation and am on the L.A. Stake preaching
schedule along with my husband. Throughout all these area of service, I
feel the hand of the Master upon me as I go forth, believing and in faith,
in His service. I am but an instrument for Him and of myself, I can do
nothing through the LordGod.
One evening during Prayer Meeting, I was called upon to administer to
my sister Trudy Tetzlaff who is suffering from Osteoporossis. As I lay
my handsupon her head, that power was forth coming and I was given to know
her illness would become dormant and the problems she had at that time
would not lessen but would not coninue to detiorate. The power of God
unto His people is great. Praise His Name'

Testamony of Rosa Crawford

During a Communion Service sermon, I felt a difference in the atti-
tude and acceptance of the congregation to the meaning of the Emblems. I

always end my sermons ·with a solo to punctuate the main thrust of my
thoughts. That sermon I sang 'Do You Believe?" from theconcert "HIS LAST
DAYS". The words said ... "This is my body that was broken for you, Never
forget what I've done, This is my blood that I shed for you, This is what
makes us one'" As I was singing this verse, the tears began to well up
in the eyes of so many as they stared at the Emblems on the Communion table
and I could sense the Holy Spirit working within their mind and lives

as they looked within themselves and found themselves wanting as they re-
alized afresh the sacrifice of the Lord Jesus upon the Cross for them. I

could feel their unspoken prayers asking forgiveness as they reached forth
to partake of the Emblems.It was an uplifting and special experience to me,
as I, too, need forgiveness and an uplifting of the soul.
Throughout the Stake and the Mohave Desert Area where most of my ministry
has been done, the majority of person have responded to me quite favorably,
I know who, in our congregation, is not acceting women priesthood and so
Igo out of my way to love these persons more and let them know that I care
for them regardless of their acceptace. Perhaps because, I, too, could not
accept at first, I can understand their searching and unhappiness and so
I can minister to them, when they let me, by being a kind, gentle, loving
sister to them all the time and in all ways. When I preach, they are not
in attendance, but I pray for them and continue in my love. One sister
didn't come to Choir for over a year because, she later told me, she would
have to sit by me and couln't reconcile herself to that. I told her that
we were sisters, and that nothing would ever come between us because of my
calling. I also felt that the congregation as well as the Choir lost a
great deal by her not participating. She since has been coming when I am
up front, though she would not take Communion from me as yet, but she has
embraced me and told me she loved me and always would ....... Again, I feel
I must let go ..... and let God care care of the pain within her heart.
On the whole, my ministry has been accepted and if there are those who
don't accept me, they have not made themselves known to me. Regardless of
their acceptance, I will continue to go forth in faith believing that I

Testarnony of Rosalie E. Crawford

have been called of God and therefore man cannot diet.ate to me in my ser-
vice to my Master who has put a "seal' upon my head and has sent me forth

in His name to minister to His people wherever they may be. I am in the
service of the Master and I cannot deny that now or anytime henceforth.
I feel at this time, my ministry is to be a "leaven" among His people
in our area. I relate well with people of all ages, perhaps my employment
in the public schools for the past 19 years has enriched this. I have been
active in the Stake for many years and with my husband have gone into many
congregations where he preached and I sang or gave other ministry. I have
sung in our Stake "Messiah" for many years and have been twice voted a

Delegate to World Conferencewithout my husband going. I am always con-
cerned about people and am the receipiant of many confidences of both men

and women without and within the Church. I am always mindful of my call-
ing in situations such as this that my advise and counseling to those who

confide in me, must always be God given and Spirit filled words of enlight-
ment and enrichment.

I enjoy preaching as I use the gifts of scripture knowledge, story-
telling, music, poetry, etc. in my sermons. I can guage my audience and

fit the sermon to the age group or need. I also enjoy Home Ministry
visits and feel that Dean and I as elders can minister to persons who need

reactivation within the Church family. Even those who as yet do not ac-
cept me, are over joyed when we make visits, priesthood orotherwise.

I find a great ministry in single or widowed women, who would only con-
fide in a woman, especially the older women. This, then, is a special part

of my ministry to other women and I find great joy in this area of His
service to the lone and often neglected women.
Dean and I are seriously considering moving from Hemet to Prescott, Az.
where the need for ministry is so great. They have a small Branch there,
that we feel, is dying on the vine for lack of priesthood leadership and
caring concern and an effective Home Ministry program for the in-active
and unconcerned members. We are placing this proposed move in the Lord's
hands
to an
and will let Him lead us. Our main thrust at the moment is going
area for however~'may take to minister, sustain and give leader-

Testamorny of Rosalie E. Crawford

ship to that church group on a self-supporting basis. We have just re-
tired in June 1987; Dean having taught 34 years and I in Food Services

at a school cafeteria as site manager and assistant cook. We are now
ready and willing to give the reminder of our years (Dean is 60 and I am
55) in His service wherever He leads us. Dean is very handy with his hands
and can do almost anything in areas of maintanance, repairs, etc. We
feel the Lord will show us where our ministry is most needed.
Hemet is a large Congregation with many priesthood members, though a

large number are elderly. Hemet is a retirement area. I have been in-
creasingly bothered over the past few years with allergies ..... those that

are airbourn in our valley ....... citrus, olives, apricots,smog, etc. and
feel so much better and clear-headed whenever we leave the valley in the
summer months. this then is an inducement to moving to an area that is
free of thses allergies, but still is warm and dry for Dean's arthritis
problems, which made us leave the coastal area of Fort Bragg 20 years ago.
We have a travel unit and" our house is in order", so we are free to go
for the Master. This, then, is our far-reaching plan.
I thank the Women's Commission and the History Commission for giving
me this opportunity to share my inner most thought and desires with them
and with whomever may read this. Forgive the mistakes .... I am not a typist, :
Please/tR!s testamony in whatever way you may deem necessary. I am only
thrilled to be able to share it with you for the honor and glory of my

Lord and for the use within His Kingdom building process. I am and al-
ways will be, His servant.

In Christian love,

My pastor asked to have my husband present when he told me of my call.
The Spirit of the Lord was With us in abundance at this meeting. By the
end of that day I was so filled with e.scitement that I called my pastor
and told him I accepted the call, Prior to this meeting I had already
signed up to take the Temple School course on Introduction to the Scriptures
with Bro. Spencer because I enjoyed his classes so much. The Lord had
already begun to prepare me before I was aware of His call. I then took
the other two repqired courses and began to plan for my ordination service.
I had requested to plan the service. It was important to me to have a
family member ordain me, so I asked my uncle to come from Montana to do so.
My pastor presided at the ordination service. The chior sang Hymn #362.

THE TESTir-KlNY OF A \\i'.JMAN PRIESTHOOD MEMBER: MABEL HYDE CURRY

My name is Mabel Curry, ordained to the office of priest along with four
other women (one elder and three teachers), on November 17, 1985. Initial
approval for ordination came from the Apple Valley, Minnesota, congregation
and the Minnesota District; final approval from the World Church. For the
November 17th worship service, devoted entirely to the ordinations, there were
many guests present with the congregation.
The responsive reading suggested in the Herald "Worship Resources" was
modified so as to make what we considered to be a more positive statement
(inserted at the end of this testimony). Because my husband, an elder at that
time, was the designated spokesman for one of the other ordinands, I asked my
oldest and youngest sons, both elders, to ordain me. It was not only a rich
experience in our church family, but also in our rn,-n personal family,
My life en=ounters with the Lord have never been dramatic, but there has
been a steady (but slrn,, I'm afraid) increase in my understanding of Christ's
message and the response we are required to make to it. My husband and I
retired from teaching at the University of North Dakota in 1984 and 1983,
respectively. We had lived in Grand Forks, North Dakota, for 29 years. When
we moved to Grand Forks in 1955 there was no RLDS church there. We could have
chosen to join the Fargo Branch (75 miles away), the Winnipeg Branch (145
miles aw-ay), worship with a different denomination, or hold services in our
own home. We chose to do the last -- with periodic visits to the organized
branches. Before long, we found other isolated members who joined us in
worship in our home and in 1970, a mission was officially established at Grand

Mabel Curry Testimony ?~~ -2-

Forks. In 191/ our local group bought a small day-care center building to
house the mission. My husband, Myron, served as pastor throughout the years we
were there.
Those were good years of preparation for ministry, We met virtually
every week, Everyone who was willing could have a job. Our church group was
small enough to require involvement in worship by as many as possible in order
to sustain the holding of regular services year-around,
We encountered problems in that small congregation -- but not in such
overwhelming volume that they could not be addressed. We had to deal with
gossip, death, retarded children, pregnancy outside marriage, disputes with a
landlord, adultery, divorce, poverty, differences of theological
interpretation, discouragement, and doubtless more that does, not come to mind
at present, but, always, the Lord's gentle Spirit seemed constantly to work
with us in showing effective ways of solving the problems and in bringing to
the fore the supporting scriptural study that would continually educate us,
It was a learning laboratory -- as I suspect is literally the case in any
church group regardless of whether it is so perceived.
When Myron and I retired from the University of !forth Dakota, we decided
to move to Apple Valley, Minnesota so as to be near a large air terminal that
would, to be sure, facilitate somewhat more frequent visits to and from family
members and friends; beyond that, however, we wanted to live in a university
community so we could continue to study and attend available cultural
offerings, Most of all we wanted to be near a church branch so that we could
continue to serve in such ways as might be possible, The Apple Valley

Mabel Curry 'l'estimony 7 ~,?, -3-

congregation welcomed us warmly; ever since, we've been working there and in
the Minnesota District.
At the beginning of the early discussions about women in the priesthood,
I had not found the idea illogical. But it must be said that, particularly in
the times when the subject first began to receive attention, I hoped that the
priesthood would not be my calling or responsibility. After the World
Conference of 1984, however, the ministry of women seemed so right to me that
when our pastor said he needed to talk with me about "something" -- I thought
it might be about a call. It took me some time to accept it; both Myron and I
prayed about it earnestly. My hesitation caine from perceiving myself as not
being well enough prepared and lacking explicit assurance that the call was of
divine origin, Some time later, however, World Church historian Richard
Howard, who visited Apple Valley during a period devoted to "Inquirers,"
related details about his first call and his own hesitancy. His pastor told
him that one accepts, then affirmation comes as one serves in office, That
helped me put into words my own perceptions, I had to accept the call because
of God's continual graciousness to me and my fainily. It would have been
profoundly ungrateful to turn down the opportunity to serve, As brother
Howard spoke of his efforts to perform the functions of the office, I, too,
found the confirmation of the Lord's Spirit.
One of the highlights of my ministry as a priest has been serving the
sacrainent. Two markedly different instances come to mind, In April, 1986, I
was one of the ordained women who joined the large group of ministers serving
the emblems of Christ's sacrifice to three assemblies of the World Conference.

Mabel Curry Testimony ~ ~ -4- 0

It was an historic moment, Nearly everyone accepted our ministry in the
loving spirit in >lhich it was offered. The general acceptance
notwithstanding, one of my friends from North Dakota was dismayed to find that
I was serving in her area. She did not take the emblems, at the same time
assuring me that her refusal to accept the sacrament from me had nothing to do
with me, personally. We touched hands briefly -- affirming our continuing

The other example took place in a small, intimate group -- a calling and

Confirmed again was the divinity of the fact that both men and women are now

authority to minister together, there could never have been such a partnership
experience in the service of Jesus Christ.
Early in 1987, I was called to the office of elder. This time I did not
hesitate to accept, I was no better prepared for the office than before, but
I now knew from firsthand experience that as long as I studied, was constant
in prayer and sensitive to the Lord's leadings, He would multiply >lhatever
efforts I could make.
On May 3, 198'7, another woman in our congregation and I were ordained to
the_office of elder; our daughter-in-law was ordained to the office of priest.
This again was a richly unique family experience. During the service, Myron,
by this time an evangelist-patriarch, consecrated the oil mich, then, was

presented to the two new elders to use in ministry. Following our noon meal,
after family members departed, Myron and I drove to Rochester (about 75 miles
away) in response to a request for administration to a hospitalized church
member from Hannibal, Missouri. As we visited with him prior to

to by a woman. He said he hadn't given it much thought; I was the first woman
minister he had encountered. I told him of the ordination service of that

Although these varied experiences were always blessed, one of the
responsibilities accompanying priesthood office --the one I dreaded most
was preaching, Much to my surprise, the task never seemed burdensome. Each
search of the scriptures proved to be an adventure; seeking God's leading
regarding the needs of the congregation always brought insight in one way or
another. As yet, I do not especially enjoy the delivery of a sermon, but the
discipline of an assigned preparation is good for me -- as it is for anyone
who might otherwise put off diligent preparation.
One of the most rewarding aspects of holding priesthood is a
colleagueship ~'ith our sons and the development of team ministry with my
husband. We have worked together for many years, but the ultimate
responsibility for ministry was always Myron's (in many ways, a comfortable

circumstance for me). Now that we both are responsible for providing
ministry, we find our study, our discussions, our prayers, our associating
with each other in like endeavors become ever-broader avenues for involvement
in the Lord's work,
My spiritual concerns before my ordination were largely directed to my
family and to those near and dear to me. I now find my concerns being
enlarged to include our charch family and far beyond, There is a growing
realization that Christ's message is for all (Oh! apostle Peter, how I relate
to you). I knew that in my own mind, but am just beginning to know it in my
heart and soul,
As my husband and I have taken Temple School courses in preparing for
ordination, we have found rich testimony from the course substance itself
reinforced by other class members. Organized study has brought a greater, and
I trust, more accurate understanding of Christ's message.
At this moment, my testimony would not be complete without an idea
gleaned from reading Dietrich Bonhoeffer's '!'he Cost of Discipleship, In
describing God's grace, Bonhoeffer constrasts "cheap" grace and "costly"
grace. My perception of ministry is that it is costly. It cost God his only
Son; it costs us our lives in service, Nonetheless, it is, as the apostle
Paul said, reasonable service. My life as a minister is not mine at all, but
a life at the hand of the Lord, to be kept at the ready to be of service to
our God and His creation. #

L/U-

Priesthood has always been an important part of my
life. I was baptized at eight years of age by my father,
Hobert Beavers. As long as I can remember he was a devoted
servant of the Lord. I have never forgotten the warm spirit
which pervaded my being as the elders laid their hands upon
my head t9 confirm me a member of the church. As I grew
in the church I was permitted to serve in many areas, using
my talents for art, music, and teaching.
In the fall of 1967, as I knelt in prayer, the thought
of my father's death crossed my mind. I quickly attempted
to dismiss the experience, ashamed that I would entertain
such a thought. A short time after, I was summoned to my

the membership of the chuch he serva:las branch president.
However, throughout that day and the following week I was

i'lly y9ungest brother, a student at Graceland College,

He received a healing following administration.
In the spring of 1968, my husband had ileostomy surgery.

During his hospitalization a tragic accident occured which

as my father prayed at the fencepost in the field, "I'll

took him for the purpose of preaching to the spirits in the
prisonhouse, Later, Brother ll'cBride questioned me concerning

gold.letters, in such manner as he had never before experi-
enced, That message has been a source of direction and

In the years following that experience it has been my

Christ hE'.s 'been the center of my lit'e.

My name is Sandra Dorman, I was ordained to the office of Priest.
This is my testimony of what happened to me.
Some of the questionnaires have talked about the time, like in the
year 1970, What are some of the important life experiences• and events
since about that time? I would have to say that in 1970, I didn't even
know that the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Ia,tter Day Saints
was even existing. I was a member of Faith Lutheran "hurch in Bay City,
Michigan, and was going through some very troublesome times. About
that time, I got a divorce and was in a very troubled time. Around the
year 1979, I began to get my life back together again and felt that I
was pretty much beginning to have some sort of charge and I began to go
back to church, to the Lutheran Church, and do some studying and some
inquiring and it that time I feel very strongly that the Lord was leading
me into a new life and I was following along pretty well. I was doing
a lot of studying in the New Testament and was coming to some things that
I was inquiring of my pastor. One of these things was being the "elect",
When I asked him the questions about being the "elect", he told me that
because we belonged to the church we were the elect and this was really
troublesome to me because of my feelings of what I had done and where
I had been and the uncertainties I felt that I couldn't actually feel

My call to the office of priest was voted on at the
St. Louis Stake Conference September 14, 1986.
My husband is not a member of our church. He was,
and is, most supportive of the work that I do in the
church, or what I feel needs to be done outside of the
church structure.
On reflection, I believed one of the reasons I
received a call to the priesthood was my decision to go
back to work after my children were grown.
I work as a school nurse, and have been for several
years. School nursing is quite different than hospital
or office nursing. I find I do much more counseling, not
only to the children, but to their parents, the teachers,
maintenenance people, and office workers.
I feel that my deep belief in the love of Jesus Christ
for everyone has given hope and comfort to many who felt very
much lost and or alone.
I was ordained February 22, 1987, in St. Charles, Mo.,
my home congregation. My two brothers, High Priests Clifford

Browne Jr., and Charles Browne ordained me. Evangelist-
Patriarch Charles Browne was the spokesman. During the ordin-
ation, I was impressed with the thought that a large part

of my ministry would be to people who are not members of the
Restoration.
I was very pleased when I heard that section 156 had been
approved. Like most, I have seen women most of my life who

appeared to be functioning in the role of a priesthood member.
I said at that time I do not want to be in the priesthood,
that should be for the younger women.
When my Presiding Elder, Gordan Swetnam, told me of my
call, I was surprised, and not at all sure that I should be
called. I could think of at least two other women in our
congregation who should be in the priesthood. I told him this.
He asked me who I thought it should be. I told him. He then
told me that they had been called. The three of us were
ordained the same day.
Shortly after my ordination, I was assigned several
families who needed home visits. The first family had not
been coming to church for sometime. They were in disagreement

with the direction the church was going. I felt comfor-
table going, because we are good friends, and had enjoyed

working together. The visit did not turn out as I had hoped
it would for them, but it was a learning time for me. The
next two families I visited were much better. The time was
spent listening, talking, and laughing.
learning ....... It seems to me that most of the things
I do now is a learning time for me. For someone who has a
long and beautiful heritage in the Restoration, I have a lot
to learn, and it is exciting.

Lois Marie Browne Dummit

TESTIMONY OF FRANCES IRENE ELLSWORTH

My name is Frances Irene (Holman) Ellsworth. I am now in my
7O's and a widow in good health.
I was notified of the call to the office of elder in the
Melchisedec Priesthood in the fall of 1986, about a year after my
husband passed away.

I had had no trouble with the concept of women in the priest-
hood from the first and had felt rather proud of our branch of

''old fogeys'' when we had the first ordained woman in our district.
When Elder William F. Scott, who was pastor of the Yakima
Washington branch told me he would like to speak with me privately
after service one Sunday morning, I suddenly felt I knew it would be
about a call to priesthood, maybe because two or three people had said
''you'll be called," to me before then. I had not sought ordination
because I had been teaching an adult class and was happy doing so,
and I knew I would find places to serve as a lay woman. But when he
said ''elder'' I was nearly floored. ''The Melchisedec Priesthood?

I'm not good enough for that!!" I still feel very humble and inade-
quate for such an honor, for it is an honor to be asked to represent

God in that capacity. I have so much to overcome. I procrastinate,
waste time, I'm too lazy, too self-indulgent. If I had tried harder,
put forth more effort, I'd have accomplished more in my life.
In 1969 I joined Church Women United and have been a part of it
ever since. I felt then that we as a church needed to be represented,
and though part of our women felt we should be aloof of any other
church organizations I have kept going and doing something each year.

(1)

r r/ ')-,
I was limited because of having to work and be a part of my family
too. I did work as Trick or Treat Chairwomen for Unicef for five
years until my husband objected and said I had done enough.
One year I arranged for us to host World Day of Prayer. It
was the year the ''Woman at the Well'' story was featured so I put on
a costume and did the monologue. Our district president at the time,
Brother Floyd Winegar gave the theme talk and the women served a "cup
of soup'' afterwards. It was well received and I was glad.

I found among these Christian women, both black and white, de-
votion, expertise and willingness to work greater than my own. I

have been treated well and I hope I have helped to foster better under-
standing all the way around.

Just this year one of our women who holds a Church Women United
state position has moved to our city and I am so happy to have another

R.L.D.S. sister at the meetings. I am glad to see our more enlight-
ened attitude that says we can value the work and contributions of

our sisters of other denominations as I know God does.
I have been given so much. I was born of parents who were both
devoted to God's church in these days and who were wise and loving
people. I was born with a good mind and was taught to respect my
body and live healthfully so that now I am well physically. I am
so thankful for that good health.
I have been loved and protected all my life. I was told so in
my patriarchal blessing and I've seen it too. In 1952 I was ill and
discouraged about family finances when the saints stepped in and
took care of me and convinced my husband they cared for us. They
all became very dear to him too. A wonderful testimoney to us!!

(2)

Once again, in 1985, I had put my husband into a nursing home
on the recommendation of the home health corps. That had been done
easily as the home I selected had just one bed available at the time.
I was facing surgery too and was apprehensive about it all. I was
alone that morning and had been reading my "Daily Bread" when the
warm spirit of love env~loped me and I knew everything would be all
right, and that love that I felt so intensely then would be with me.
I was being watched over, and I have been. My surgery went well. I
feel fine and though my husband died so soon after, things were made

easy for me. He was ill and in pain and he wanted to go. The heaven-
ly father can be trusted.

many blessings.

I know he is in good hands. I have had so

1. Wise and loving parents who taught me. I appreciated them more
as I sought to teach my children and grandchildren.
2. A good vocabulary (from my Parents) helps me to express my thoughts.
3. Thoughts that often come that are greater and better than I would
think.
4. A sound belief in God's words and directions.
5. Recognition of his prophets, including the present one.
6. A love of people, no fear of them; from association with devoted
servants of God, in my young tender years. Many missionaries were
entertained in our home.
7. Opportunities in the branch to serve, to teach children and now
adults.
8. And now recognition of the need to study more deeply.
9. A greater appreciation of the Book of Mormon, and an urge to help
others understand and love it too.
I was ordained February 15, 1987 in Yakima Washington, at the
11 a.m. service. My grandson who is an elder, officiated, a former
pastor, Paul A. Anderson assisted; and Brother Scott, through whom
the call came told of it, and charged me to be faithful.

(3)

My first official act as an elder and the second and third ones
too, were assisting in administering to the sick. I had seen my

father do it many times and I pray that everytime I'm asked, Gods-
spirit and the love I feel for that person will be present.

I have always felt that I should not refuse to serve when I'm
needed to do so. Aware of God's love and support I want always to
be willing and ready to serve with humility and love.
I need to be faithful and studious more so than ever before. I'm
free of many family duties and have time now. I do feel that my
branch and district support me. I am district Christian Education
Director, and even though I did not go to college and I feel that
lack, I want to help all the others who serve in that part of the
work throughout our district.
Visiting opportunities are opened to me. Though I had used my

phone to call and say ''I'm thingking of you," to many people, be-
cause I don't drive, and in this city public transportation exists

but it is limited. Another sister, recently ordained an elder, came
to me and asked me to go visiting with her. I thank God for her and
her initiative and faithfulness. We expect to visit nearly every
week. Already we have made seven visits in two weeks and they have
all been loving experiences.
As I am called on to serve in anyway, I pray I may do it well and
with God ':s love so that He may have the glory and His work may be done.
May people be led to him, nourished and nurtured in His love is
my prayer so that Zion can be redeemed and we can be worthy to live
with Christ wh,en he comes back to the world.

Therefore if we have desires to serve God you're called to the work for behold the field in white, already to harvest. Little did I know that my favorite scripture would be the catalyst that would verify deep within my soul not only the validity of section 156 but of my own call into the priesthood. Being a third generation our LDS family, I've been both blessed and privileged to experience a rich Latter Day Saints heritage. My parents are strong in their faith. And beliefs in the church accepting 156 has been very difficult for them, because of my administration and trust, admiration and trust in them. I was torn as to how I truly felt regarding such an important matter. However, I felt a nagging regarding the validity of 4156.

For a long time, however, I just sat on the fence with a wait and see attitude. During this time of wait and see I realized that I needed to become more involved within our mission in free post Maine and within my own family. As the wife and mother God wanted me to be our oldest daughter, Heidi became increasingly more ill with a multitude of allergies, juggling her illnesses and my work schedule became very difficult and demanding during a sacrament service a few months later, God impressed upon me that my career as a geriatric social worker, as contributing as it seemed, was not what he had in mind for me at this time. I was needed at home and in our mission. After much discussion, my husband and I decided to alter our finances and lifestyle for me to remain home with our children in early January of 1986, shortly after my return to being a full time wife and mother, our district President 70, Clift or Clifford gray dropped by my house for a visit after a brief conversation of ketchup news between our families. Cliff revealed his testimony regarding my priesthood call to the Office of priest I had truly I can truly say that I've never been more surprised and shocked and all of my life

next to us and rejoice that we were able to serve in that capacity Never had I experienced such a fullness of purpose and what God intended for his people. There were three of us experiencing such a bond with Christ. I felt and do feel a bond with my brothers, but this was such a special feeling of fullness or completeness. The past 14 months have been the most challenging and humbling of my life. I'm thrilled to be a member of the church and thrilled to serve the Heavenly Father in a way possible. What skills and talents that seemed unimportant a few years ago now seem to be in focus and in relations to the great overall program of God. merely being in business with the living of the church is not enough. I want to be busy with purpose insight. And a complete understanding of what he is endeavoring for his people today. I'm excited about the challenges that are before us and the resources available to help us discover our individual uniqueness and public ministry. We can as we continue to study and take advantage of the benefits that we gained from such opportunities as temple school and well paying commissions are going to be even more equipped to provide a wider range of spiritual and temporal ministries. The field is truly wide and ready to harvest. I do believe that counsel given to young Joseph that the design and purpose of God will not be frustrated. My prayer is that we as individuals and as a body will trust our father and move with a renewed sense of understanding a chemical and a completeness for the work needed today.

repaid a loan as quickly as possible, or as payments were planned.
I just wish that my finances were such that I could give people money
when needed, but I know that this would not always be good for them.
Everyone needs to live within their means, as we did, and strive to
make their way in this world with work, church life, and God. This
is my belief, and it worked for Dick and I. As we gave to the Lord,
it was returned to us many times more.
I see much opportunity for the women of the Reorganized Church

Of Jesus Christ Of Latter Day Saints. The time will not be long be-
fore the male and female roles will not seem as separate, but will be

as one.
church.

Then we can move forward with the important tasks of the
I remember a missionary once said, as he and Dick were
struggling with a grievance between two parties in the District--
''If we could just put aside our pettiness, and get on with the
