RILLA T. FIELDS - TESTIMONY
September 15, 1987

The ordination service on April 28, 1986 for me, Rilla Anne Tacy Fields, was unforgetable. Not only because it was the first
of its kind in the Southeast Region, and not only because my RA
Bill Ball, my apostle, Everrett Graffeo and my pastor and husband
Elder Ed Fields ordained me to the office of priest, and not only
because my family and friends packed our small blue house church,
but because it marked a complete change in my life - a passage of
no return.
The experience of ordination was surprising, since I had
never realized that the Holy Spirit would be so physically
powerful in the sacrement. I can still see and feel how my neck
strained to bear the weight of all the hands that were laid upon
it only later to find out that the one hand of each minister had
barely touched my hair.
The process to this calling did begin way before the actual
event. My childhood in the family of an appointee seventy
certainly provided me with an unquestionable recognition of Jesus
Christ and His Spirit. However, my father's untimely death in
1965 and my subsequent transfer to a new college, found me
isolating myself from the saints who lived about 70 miles away.
During this period of inactivity, I found that this personal
testimony of Christ in my life prevented me from participating in
other disciplines. I was even led to share this with my
boyfriend, Ed Fields. His interest in my dedication and testimony
to the Church of Jesus Christ led him to read the scriptures and
ask for baptism in July 24, 1977. Our marriage on Dec. 24, 1977
was a further committment to The Work as partners.
I can not say that our enthusiasm was met with open arms when, after diligent prayer and study Ed's new job brought us to
the Columbus-Ft. Benning Mission. My "Big Mouth" or blunders met
with criticism and discouragment as I began to realize that
although my newly ordained priest/pastor husband could serve the
Lord, I could not because I had no useable skills. This process
of seeking to determine how I could fit into The Work led me
thorugh various stages of growth and at times despair. No clear
answer came, but gradual milestones for growth and development
earmarked my quest from reunion 1978 to present. Stages such as
having to be willing to take even the most menial jobs so that
others would derive comfort, to the most difficult, that of
supporting my husband in his ministry. This last goal was
difficult for me in practice, not principle, because I found it

difficult to allow another individual to program and plan my life
in those most improtant areas - The Work for Jesus Christ. During
this period, I received much comfort from early church members
and their resolutions of problems through Christ by reading and
re-reading their testimonies in the Book of Mormon.
I was also given the opportunity to begin a Sunday School
Class for hearing impaired children who used cued Speech/English
techniques. This experience was growth provoking and was
exceptional because it was the first time that I ever really felt
that I had a function in Christ's Church.
In January, 1985, Everett Graffeo came to visit our little
blue house church. His story for the little deaf children was
impressive. He told of the beauty of the light of a candle that
needed a little match to set it 'a fire'. The day following his
visit, during devotional a thought presented itself into my
head, "You will be called to the office of priest." Now, this was
unique, because I could recognize that this thought was not
generated by me, but it was not printed words that i "saw" in my
head, but a strong, direct, active thought. Even though I
recognized this experience as different, I still questioned this,
because I did not consider myself as having the qualities to such
a calling. As the thought or idea continued to be present over
the next few weeks, I began to consider that "in the next 20
years, maybe a Southern woman could be called to the priesthood."
But, just in case it should be sooner, I enrolled in Introduction
to Scriptures Course from Temple School.
When my husband (pastor) asked me to consider my processed
call to the Office of Priest on Jan. 1, 1986, I was overwhelmed.
That was certainly the fastest 20 years the South has ever spent!
My response in the affirmative was made with much fear and much
excitement.
These two emotions continued to be present at various times
preceeding and following my ordination. The most memorable sad
experiences involved my telling of my acceptance to this call to
my friends and colleagues at work. Our relationships changed from
the day of my ordination. Comments such as your church is more
important than your work have become so frequent. They fail to
relaize that the work at the office is an extention and always
has been of my work for Christ.
A positive part of my change in my life's work has been the
healing of my mother's and my relationship. Although we've always
maintained contact, we've really never been able to communicate
well or accept each other as we were. Our meetings were often
stormy and disruptive. However, it was as if my mother and I
recognized the importance of each person in The Work. I was
surprised at her immediate, positive response to my acceptance of
my call and her recognition that even selfwilled, determined, and
non-traditional women were needed. This reunion of spirits has
been an unexpected blessing. She has seemed to be more supportive

of our endeavors here in the mission and also takes a personal
interest in our outreach - as if her outreach is to support in
prayer and substance our efforts here in Columbus. She continues
to re-evaluate her response to her office of member and to view
herself as a minister and I think this view of herself is new,
and follows my calling and ordination service.
I think that because I'm so confrontive, that I have not had too many negative in-church experiences. I have continued to take
"personally" and have said as such, any negative discussion
regarding D.C. 156, women in the priesthood, and myself. My
negative experiences tended to be more in my office related
world.

I find myself becoming more and more concerned with The Work
and the urgency to be doing much more. My job as speech/language
pathologist for the hearing impaired from Jan. to Aug, 1987 was
so full that my regular priesthood visiting program suffered
regretably. I now find that my job time has decreased so much,
that I am excited about the time to renew friendships in families
in our Church Community. I find myself thinking of myself as a
soldier, and advocate for Christ. This has placed me in positions
of fighting the adversary in court (for children's rights) and in
jail (for those whose habits have brought the full force of the
law) and in protecting those souls with out country or family.
And, I guess if I had to evaluate the impact of my ministry, I would want to see people reaching out to others on a regular
basis and then I would feel that I was an effective servant.
Those who choose lesser day to day concerns pain me, because we
have so much hope and healing to offer the world.

Dear Sisters:
It is a pleasure to respond to your request for my testimony
about my ordination. The preservation of women's stories has
historically been neglected and it is good to know that the
collection of these experiences has been instituted by the
church.
I was ordained to the office of Teacher on October 19, 1986,
in the Beavercreek congregation in Dayton, Ohio. Attached is a
copy of the service which contains the names of all those who
participated.
The ordination was approved by the Beavercreek congregation on
March 2, 1986, and by the Southwest Ohio District Conference on
September 14, 1986.
I am married and my companion is a member of the RLDS Church
but does not hold a priesthood office. We were married in 1960
and he was baptized in 1968. He has always been supportive of my

commitment to the church as a member and continues to be suppor-
tive now that I am in the priesthood.

In response to the suggested question about important life
experiences and events that led to ordination, my thoughts seem
to move to the influence of the women's movement. In 1970 I had
been married ten years and had three children - ages eight to
two. The pattern for women then had been to seek an education,
marry, have a family, and stay home and care for the children -
and I followed that pattern very closely. I worked for two years
as a nurse and left that job when our first child was born.
However, in the early ?O's I perceived the need to more fully
develop other potential. I returned to school for a B.A. in
psychology and then taught in a Jr. College for three years.
There were also opportunities within the women's department and
the congregation, for example, as a pastoral care commissioner
both in the congregation and in the district, lay counseling
program, health care coordinator for Camp Bountiful, and other

similar examples for service within the church. I also par-
ticipated in volunteer community organizations like the Red Cross

and Hospice.
As I reflect on these and other opportunities for growth, I
believe that I am now able to utilize these experiences in the

office of Teacher. I believe that I have gained understanding in
a number of areas of human concerns.
combined with my educational background in psychology seem to
My background in nursing

mesh well with my priesthood call.
At this point I will address my feelings about the ordination
of women. I was first aware of an urgency perceived by women in
other denominations for ordination and gradually became aware of
this same feeling by a few women in the RLDS Church. I, however,
although involved in women's issues, did not feel that ordination
to the priesthood was necessary for me to be fulfilled in my
efforts at service. I did understand those who did feel this
way, however. When I learned about the acceptance of Section 156
(I did not attend the conference) I remember a feeling of
excitement and energy as I thought about the potential both for
the personal growth of women and the potential for growth for the
church as a whole. At that time I did not expect or believe that
I would be called.
During this particular period of time I had been feeling a
need to either return to school, find a job, or make a commitment
to further study. In July following the April '84 conference
while I attended the SWO reunion, I decided to commit the coming
year to study through Temple School courses and personal study.
At that time I had still not felt that I would be called to the
priesthood.
During the following months an awareness began to grow that I
was moving toward a call to the priesthood. There were many
mixed feelings from the excitement and energy I have already
mentioned feeling for women when I learned about the acceptance
of Section 156 to a sense of great burden.
Near the end of 1985 I had a distinct experience in which I
was given to know that I would be called to the office of Teacher
and that Connie Galbraith (also in the Beavercreek congregation)
would be called to the office of Elder.
Since my ordination my sense of this being God's will for me
has been strengthened through the opportunities for service which
I have experienced, the opportunities for further education, and
confirmation through my prayer life.
will for the church has been confirmed as I have been ministered
My sense that this is God's
to and as I have observed others being ministered to by women in
the priesthood.
Yes, there are good people who are still struggling but many
have sought and found understanding. God continues to bless
their efforts and the efforts of those who patiently wait on
them.
The greatest change in my concept of ministry came not with
Section 156 but with the Faith To Grow program. It was then that
for me the concept of the "office of member" became reality. No
longer could we wait for the priesthood to take the lead or that
nebulous "they" - it was the responsibility of each one of us to
stand in Christ's stead.
The consideration of women in the priesthood while a change
did not seem to be the watershed experience for me that it was

for others.
Other significant points of growth and change in my concept of
ministry are occurring now as I experience the realization of
what is required in the responsibility I have accepted. Even in
a small congregation, sometimes the needs seem overwhelming. I
am learning the need for patience, hope, and the importance of
seeking often counsel through prayer.
The response of others to my calling within my congregation
has been very supportive and positive. First of all, I feel that
it has been extremely helpful that two other women were ordained
at the same time. There also had been the transfer into our
congregation another women Elder a few months prior to our
ordinations. Most of the male members of the priesthood by the
time our ordinations had occurred were supportive and welcoming
(although several had struggled to move from a negative to a
positive position.) At the time the ordinations occurred, there
was one male priesthood member in his 70's and two in their 30's
who could not fully accept the situation. They were not disruptive, only very quiet or discreetly absent.
The response from the majority of the women seems to be very
accepting. There were only perhaps two or three who maintained a
quiet sense of protest. The other male members and friends of
the congregation had a similar ratio of responses.
The youth, however, seemed very accepting, some even nonchalant, as if it was only a matter of time until women were
ordained. I sensed a special sense of excitement in the girls
and young women.
Our ordination service seemed to be wrapped in an air of
celebration. It was held on a Sunday evening and a number of
people from the SWO district came. It seemed to encompass a
feeling of energy, potential, and hope for the future.
I feel that my attempts at providing ministry have been well
accepted. I have been sought out in situations which were not
open to me before even although I have always functioned as a
listener and dispenser of occasional medical advise. There seems
to be a willingness for people to risk more personal concerns.
I would find it difficult to discuss directly any experiences
in ministry since they concern matters of confidence. There have
been some very serious, even life threatening situations which
have occurred. I remember praying as I drove the car on my way
to meet with a young person who had called me. I prayed that the
problem would not concern one certain issue with which I had
little experience. As it turned out, that issue was what the
problem concerned. I can testify that the Lord permitted me to
be an instrument of his care and counsel in that young person's
life and in the family. In another situation which happened to involve a young woman,
I believe there was a definite advantage in being a female
priesthood member. Prior to and after administration by two male
elders, the young woman was unable to discuss her problem or her
hope for the outcome of the administration process. Wheh she and
I were alone, we were able to discuss the situation. With some

follow-up discussions and direction toward professional assistance, she and her family were able to begin to communicate and
begin to enter into a healing process.
I am currently enrolled in the United Theological Seminary.
This decision was a conscious choice to seek to build on my
skills and work toward a M.A. in theological studies with an
emphasis in counseling. I believe that I can enhance my skills
through education and other avenues but I know that I can only
hope to be of service as I am able to be an instrument through
which God can reach others.
Specific to the office of Teacher I perceive that the most
pressing need is to promote right relationships within families
and among the congregation. There is a need for me to be able to
encourage trust in loved ones and friends. There is a need to
listen effectively and accurately.
The next most important responsibility is to be aware of those
who are at the edges of the group, for example, new members, less
mobile elderly, and some single people. They need to be incorporated into the church family through the use and appreciation
of their talents and skills. They need to be made to feel
necessary to the success of the congregation.
Finally, I think that the term, peacemaker, is appropriate.
It is more than peace as we usually think about it
of war or fighting. It means seeking God's peace, the sense of
- the absence
moving toward his kingdom. It means growing toward the ability
to see in myself and in all others the potential that God has
provided and act accordingly.
In closing, I would add that the inclusion of women in the
priesthood is, I believe, truly God's will for this church at
this point in time. I feel very blessed to be able to experience
this time of heightened awareness of the need for each person in
this membership to grow and develop and then share the results of
that development with the body of the church so that it can
advance the cause of Christ among God's creation.

THE TESTIMONY OF MY ORDINATION

On April 3, 1984, sitting in the Far West Stake delegation at World
Conference, I heard Apostle Tyree read what was to become Section
156 of the Doctrine and Covenants. When he read paragraph 9b and c
I sat spellbound.
b. "I say to you now, as I have said in the past, that all are
called according to the gifts which have been given them. This
applies to priesthood as well as to any other aspect of the work.
c. "Therefore, do not wonder that some women of the church are being
called to priesthood responsibilities. This is in harmony with
my will and where these calls are made known to my servants,
they may be processed according to administrative procedures
and provisions of the law."
After hearing those words I felt that I was experiencing a very important moment in the restoration movement. For a number of years
I had heard people discuss women in the priesthood and within myself
I always thought I didn't need to be unduly concerned about this
issue because God is the one who calls priesthood members and if
he wanted women to be in the priesthood he would give instructions
to the prophet. I was always thinking that perhaps some day this
would be an actuality. But on April 3; 1984 I discovered that that
"someday" had arrived. Two days later I knew I was helping to write
history when I raised my hand in support of section 156.
As the vote was taken that day, I felt God's beautiful spirit of
peace and love dwelling within me in a very real, moving way. Many
other delegates had the same experience. For lack of a better way
of describing it, it was like rays of sunshine coming down from above
warming my body. Where there were clouds of doubt the sunshine could
not get through so not everyone felt its warmth. That day I knew

The Testimony of My Ordination - Genevieve Hougas Francis -
I had been touched by God's spirit. I came home from that Conference
feeling uplifted knowing without a doubt that God had indeed spoken
to us through his prophet.
Almost two years went by and I began hearing of some women being
called and ordained. At the World Conference of 1986 I first saw
ordained women fulfilling their priesthood responsibilities. At the
communion service on the opening Sunday I was served by a female priest.
As I witnessed this I thought, "this is good" and I was thrilled when
I thought of the additional responsibilities the ordained women of
the church could now help with. In many congregations the load of
the work has rested heavily on a very few priesthood members, overworking them to their physical limits. Now the load for them could be
lightened as the women were ordained. Later in the week the conference was again asked to vote on the issue of women being called to
the priesthood and to me it had the same feeling of rightness that
it had two years previous. At the closing service of the conference
President Tyree gave a beautiful summary of the week and again I was
filled with God's spirit.
Probably like most women in the church I wondered if I would be called to the priesthood. I even had people ask me that question and I
would laughingly say, "No, I'm too old, but I am certainly looking
forward to younger women being called. 11 Then I discovered that
women even older than I were being called and ordained and I began
to think very soberly about the possibilities and remembering the
experiences of those two conferences, I finally began to think it
might be a possibility. As the next few weeks went by I found myself thinking of being called to the office of elder. I couldn't
get this out of my mind. I tried to supress those feelings thinking
I was "seeking" priesthood and I felt this was wrong. The more I
tried to rid my mind of these thoughts the stronger they became until
I had nothingbut turmoil within me. Then one night as I lay in bed

calling to priesthood responsibilities in ways that would bring

\f.nen Bro. Tyree and my husband, Bon, placed their h&"'lds on

~ware of anything around me except this voice speaking to me

As I think over the many wonderfu.l experiences I have had in

On December 1, 1985, my husband was in charge of the communion

NY sister, Ifyrtie Hough, was called to the office of Teacher.
Her ordination was to take p]a ce in Nason, Ni, on Nay 17, 1987,
and she asked me to be the spokesperson for this important day
in her life. As I made preparation for the service I was constantly reminded of my own ordination and hoit important the

content was for me. As I laid my hands on her head, assisted by

A close friend came up to me follovling the service and said that

As chairperson of the Regional Pastoral Care Commission, I was

ough

my relationship with these individuals.
As I reflect on my o,m. personal growth in regard to m1n1_s-.;ry,

WILLA JEAN FREY, ELDER
FAIRBANKS, ALASKA

It was during reunion in a discussion with a bishop from
Seattle stake that opened my eyes to the possibility of women in the
priesthood. God hadn't called women before, why would God do so now? I
felt that women were called to special responsibility but it wasn't as
part of priesthood, After all, if God was going to call women in this
day and age then why hadn't it been done? The bishop simply answered,
God has, but the calls cannot be processed at this time. This stunned
me. He went on to explain how women were needed and could function. I
remembered a small group of women in Livermore, California who had built
a church building and met regularly to hold classes and to have services
if they could get a visiting minister to come. A perfect place for
women priesthood members, I thought.

The whole experience started me to thinking about it. Being a
strong believer in a divinely called ministry, I still felt God would
have to move in authenticating it through revelation and in giving
calls. I was content to wait. When I did consider it might happen
someday, it was always for someone else - not me. At World Conferences
in 1978, 1980, and 1982 I experienced the picketing of the Auditorium
against women in the priesthood. It distressed me that such tactics
were being employed to pressure the delegates. I disliked the spirit in
which it was done.

Prior to World Conference in 1984, the pastor called a meeting
of the branch to discuss the various resolutions that had been in the
Herald and to give some direction to the delegates. I was an alternate.

Instead of discussing those resolutions most of the time was taken up by
two women who were very vocal against women in the priesthood, Despite
the fact that at this conference, there was no resolution calling for
women in the priesthood, they demanded that the delegates vote against
it if it should come up. Their attitude bothered me greatly.

As we boarded the plane for our flight to Independence, my
heart was heavy. I was so tired of all of this and wanted it to be
over. I didn't relish having to meet pickets again outside the
Auditorium, I couldn't sleep and so I began to pray and to cry out to
God, "How long, 0 Lord, do we have to wait before this is settled once
and for all so that it can no longer be a divisive influence in the
church?"

Quickly the answer came, "No longer. This matter will be
settled at this World Conference. There will be a revelation and among
other things, it will deal with women in the priesthood."

I responded, "But there are those who will not follow, who

will turn away if that happens!"

"I know, and some will turn away and be lost. But no longer
will a few people be allowed to hold back the progress of my church,"
was the answer.

I sat in stunned silence, scarcely able to believe what had
just happened, then started praying again for the coming Conference and
the delegates. I didn't say anything to anyone until Monday morning
just before my husband left to go to the Elders Quorum, I told him
there would be a revelation and that it would deal with the question of
women in the priesthood. He looked at me searchingly then grinned and
said "Okay," I was sitting in the back of the Auditorium that morning

as it was read. I listened carefully to the new appointments. to
directions regarding the temple and priesthood. Never did any
revelation seem so long as I waited for what I knew was there - and then
it came.

My next struggle came as the question seemed to be whispered
to me, "What about you? Would you be willing?" My instant reaction
was, "No, not me, Lord! It is fine for others, but not me Lordi"
The next question that came was, ''Well, if you had ·to vote.
how could you vote for it if you aren't willing to serve yourself?" I
knew as an alternate I was likely to be seated as a delegate since my
husband, a delegate, was scheduled for tests and surgery towards the end
of the week. It became a very real possibility that I might have to
vote. So I struggled for two days and finally convinced that God didn't
really want me to serve, but just wanted me to be willing to, I gave in
and said, "Yes. Lord, I'm willing, if you want me." As it happened the
vote was taken before I had to go in as a delegate, but I was there and
filled with an indescribable joy as I observed.

As the year passed there was a slowly growing conviction that
I would be called. I tried to prepare and waited patiently for God's
own time. In February of 1986, I was called to the office of priest and
ordained the following month after the Fairbanks congregation voted
approval. Since we are not in a stake or district. no other approval
was necessary.

I was ordained a priest by Elders David A. Swart and Alfred H.
Frey, my husband. Following my ordination. I confirmed one of my two
granddaughters. My husband confirmed the other one. We had baptized
them the week before.

Within the first six weeks of being ordained a priest-I
performed my first wedding for two non-member friends, served my first
communion, baptized my grandson, and was involved in some counseling by
some women in the branch. I taught a class for youth and adults on how
to develop self-esteem in the family (during the church school hour) and
a similar one for children during the eleven o'clock hour. I visited in
the homes of members taking along sample materials and a handout of
suggestions for family worship. I've preached many times and have been
richly blessed by the Holy Spirit as I have prepared and in delivery. I
have experienced a freedom of speech and clarity of mind that for me is
unusual. Never have I felt alone, but have felt God's presence beside
me. I really began expanding my Temple School work (I am Temple School
Coordinator for Alaska) being able to hold a number of classes and train
teachers in Anchorage and Fairbanks.

A year after being ordained a priest, I received a call to the
office of elder through my husband who was then serving as pastor. This
was approved by the Fairbanks congregation and on May 10, 1987 I was
ordained to the office of Elder by Elders Alfred H. Frey and David A.
Swart.

Almost everyone has responded affirmatively to my attempts to
minister. There were six who stood and testified to my call at our
business meeting as well as others across the state. Local priesthood
members have demonstrated their support through statements and prayers.
My husband has been my greatest help. He has guided me, answered
questions, given encouragement over and over. I have been sought out to
be a listening ear and asked for counsel on concerns by some of the
women who didn't feel comfortable taking them to a male priesthood

member. Children have responded to my sermons in a most delightful way.
Others have expressed their appreciation for various aspects. As I have
performed the ordinance of administration, I have felt richly blessed.
There have been some negative responses also. I see so much unhappiness
in those persons, I wish I could help.

I have felt significant points of growth take place in my
concepts of ministry in the last decade as I've come to accept and
acknowledge that women really are of worth and equal with men in God's
sight. I've experienced many times that women respond easier to other
women under certain circumstances. I see ministry as every saint's
calling and is greatly enriched as everyone tries to participate, What
the world needs is a practical demonstration of Jesus Christ and the
Gospel in everyday life, in terms people can understand.

Ministry involves sharing this practical gospel of Jesus
Christ with others in a meaningful way at their point of need. I'm just
beginning to see what this means in terms of serving as an elder. I'm
sensing the calling to effective leadership and the need to refine and
develop my counseling skills, and most of all to become more sensitive
to people and their needs. / PERSONAL T~STIMONY OF WILLA JEAN FREY

by

Jeanne Gandenberger

My testimony begins in 1977, when I accepted the challenge
from a dear sister in Christ, to devote a specific time each
day to prayer and reading the Book of Mormon, Although, I was
baptised at the age of eight, and felt the desire at that time
to follow Jesus, many things had drawn me away through the years,
actually I should say, many choices that I made had separated
me from an intimate relationship with my Lord. I was feeling
guilty and also feeling the estrangement from My God, I wasn't
feeling that I knew Him as I saw others knew Him in a personal
way. I felt that taking this challenge was the first step to
learning bow to follow the Lord again.
As I began to discipline my life by study and prayer daily,
the Lord began to open my mind and heart with wonderful new
understandings about Him and His Truth. I began to comprehend
a fullerness of God and to feel Him alive within me. I took
questions to Him in prayer when I felt confused, and step by
step He brought me to understand. One thing in particular that
I was confused about was the three-in-one concept. As I was
reading the Book of Mormon I read from Mosiah 8:28 that "God
Himself would come down among the children of men and redeem
His people,,.but would be called the Son because He dwelt in the
flesh,. My mind was confused as I had always separated God and
Jesus and the Spirit like the church taught, only I was feeling
a new understanding inside. I went into my bedroom, knelt down
by the bed and with deep concern and genuine desire to know
God in His truth and fullness, I asked Him to help my mind and
heart to understand. Of course, the understanding didn't come
immediately, but over a period of time as I continued to read,
pray and meditate on this the Lord helped me in many ways to

comprehend a fullerness of who He is and that He dwells within

all things!!! Wow, what a marvelous thing it was for me to real-
ize that He in fact dwells within me, and all persons and all

of creation!! From this point on my mind began to open more
quickly as the Lord, within my own heart and mind shared Himself

with me. I really felt Him living and working within me! I be-
gan to cherish and love beyond words this wonderful thing happen-
ing wi tb in me, I also began to feeJ. deep sorrow for the people

who didn't know Him as Friend, as well as Almighty God and
saviour. Who didn't reaJ.ize that He, Jesus, is in fact the very
EternaJ. Father, because tbey weren't reading and studying the
Book of Mormon where the Eternal nature of Christ emerges!!

I was feeling deep concern for my fellow Gentiles who were hard-
ened of heart and closed of mind. As I shared these things with

my God I began to feel Him calling me to be apart of sharing
this understanding with them, I am basically a shy person, and
not confident about my abilities, so was shoving those feelings
and thoughts far back in my mind.
It was about this time in our lives that my husband and I
decided to pay our tithes up completely. We bad paid a little
each month, each year, but quite a large sum remained and we
chose to be fully committed in this way and keep it paid up.

I believe this also bad a great deal to do with the new awaken-
ing within me, and not surprisingly as I was growing my husband

We were living in a town that was isolated by at least

TESTIMONY OF JUDY GARDNER
SEPTEMBER 1987

After the 1984 World Conference when Section 156 was accepted, I was not

S 1 -1

sure I fully agreed. I had seen the times changing, which included the roles
women were taking, and I had mixed emotions about some of these roles. I felt
this way about women being called into the priesthood.
The more I heard about this subject and as women were being called and were
giving their testimonies and others were giving testimonies about women being
called and their acceptance and ordination, my view on women in the priesthood
started swaying more to acceptance.
When I was approached regarding my call to the office of Priest, I was surprized.
I had not even given a thought that I would be called. District President Archie
Nunn and one of his counselors, Robert I. Richard, presented the call to me and
told me I had time to think about it before I made my decision. After a couple
weeks, in which I read the scriptures and articles I could find on women in the
priesthood and after much prayer, I made my decision to accept.
My call was presented to our Mission at a meeting on April 13, 1986, along with
two other calls--one, to a women to the office of Deacon and the other to a man
to the office of Priest. The congregation accepted these calls.
My ordination was approved by the Western Montana District at Spring Conference
in May 1986 and on June 22, 1986 I was ordained in a Sunday service in Helena,
Montana, by my husband Dale Gardner who is an Elder and by Elder Robert I. Richard.
After the ordination service I was approached by several members of the congregation congratulating me, offering their confidence in my carrying out the new
responsibilities I had accepted, and promising me their support. I felt very
happy and humble because of their acceptance of me into the priesthood.
Since our marriage in 1965 God's work has slowly become more dominate in the lives
of me and my husband Dale, and many things have happened that I feel led to my
ordination.
In 1971 we adopted our oldest child, Shawn and we thanked our Heavenly Father for
this baby we were able to have and to raise. We are so grateful for the
institution of adoption open to those who cannot biologically have children of

-2-

their own. We felt God wanted us to have our children in this way and to raise
them in a Christian home.
In 1974 we adopted a baby girl who made our family complete. When she was almost
a year old we learned she had cerebral palsy and after a period of adjustment to
this startling news/ and our acceptance of it, we realized this was part of God's
plan for our lives.
A great and wonderful experience in my life preceeded the definite outcome of
test::.s and the doctor's diagnosis, and -this experience was a turning point in
my life which brought me closer to my God then I had ever-been before.
I had to take my daughter out of state to a hospital that had equipment to do a
brain scan. The doctors told us this would show if our daughter had cerebral
plasy or hydrocephalus.
We did not know anyone in Portland, where it was decided we would go and we were
going to have to stay overnight. Our Pastor, who at that time was Bill Ball, made
arragements with the Pastor in Portland who was Gary Beebe for me and my daughter
Theresa to stay at their home and they offered to pick. us up at the airport and
transport us where we had to go. I had never had an opportunity to go into the
home ofs§ints outside of the District where we lived and when someone who was a
total stranger to us and lived So far away graciously invited us into their home,
I was huIIJhled.
That was not all though, when_Mrs. Beebe took me to the hospital where Theresa
was to have the test done, the doctor told us they could not put Theresa under
anesthetic, which had to be done before the test could be done. They told me I
could reschedule the test for another time or take her to a local doctor and get
her on antibiotics for a few days until she was over her cold and then come back.
I was distraught with this news and didn't know what I was going to do. The
Beebes were so understandi:ng and loving and did nor. hesitate to say we were welcome
to stay as long as was,, necessary. God's love flowed through that family in
abundance and I felt that love more than an other time in my life. We stayed
four or five days and the Beebes treated us like royalty. I will never forget
their hospitality and love and I am sure this experience made me a more loving
person.
Soon after this I revitalized a cerebral plasy group:c in our town and we shared

-3-

with each other in fund raising and fun as we supported oneanother.
About four years later I got some people together and we organized a horseback
riding program for the handicapped which is still in operation and going strong.
I know God was with us in the·organization process as we met many stumbling
blocks but made our way around them. It is so heartwarming to me to see these
handicapped people enjoying this type of recreation and at the same time be
receiving therapy. We have had our setbacks but when we were able to have
equestrian events in the Special Olympics here in Helena in 1984 and 1985 and
when we were able to send two of our students to the International Special
Olympics in South Bend, Indiana, this year, the thrill and excitement of these
things keep me working to overcome the setbacks.
Another important event in my life was when my husband was called to the priesthood
and was ordained an Elder. I never thought he would be called to the priesthood
and when he was I was again humbled and I felt God's spirit was with me and was
telling me I needed to give Dale all of my support which I did. This brought me
closer to my God and Dale and I both felt God was with us continually and was
helping us to do those things he wanted us to do.
I was at the Labor Day Enrichment Institute in Calgary, Canada earlier this month
and I was asked to serve communion along with five other Priests at the Sunday
morning service. I was overwhelmed to be asked to do this outside my immediate
congregation. This was the largest group of Saint's I had helped serve communion
to and it was a very rewarding experience. It was a very spiritual experience
for me to be able to serve my Lord in this way, especially since it was in a
church so far from home.
Another experience in ministry I had was to go with my husband and serve communion
to a lady in a nursing home. She is unable to get out to come to church so this
meant a lot to me to be able to minister to her in this way.
People in our congregation have accepted me as a Priest very well and I have had
only positive feedback from them. I have been told I looked very nice on the
rostrum, I have received compliments on my talks and have received constructive
criticism a couple of times, but took heed and have tried to use that to better
myself.

-4-

The following two paragraphs tell of negative responses to my calling: We have
an elderly ladey in our congregation who lives in a nursing home. I have tried
to visit her at least once a month or everyother month. I had always told her
to call me or one of the other members and we would come and get her for church.
She never did. I had told her of my call and later of my ordination and when
I visited we always talked about what was going on in the church. One day she
told me how she loved me and she did not want me to take what she had to say
personally but she was very adament in her belief that God had never said women
should serve in the priesthood and since she was as old as she was she was not
going to change her mind. This was devestating to me, but has not changed my
love for her or my visiting her.
Soon after my call to the priesthood was presented to our congregation and they
voted to approve the call, a lady and her son quit coming to church. We knew
they did not feel women should be in the priesthood and that was why they quit
coming. After she had been absent for a few Sundays, I called herto see if I
could talk to her about coming back and when she told me why she wasn't coming
I told her how bad a felt because it was because of my call that she quit coming.
She also told me it was nothing personal but that is how she felt.
Ministry to me means serving my fellowman whether it be by physically helping
them, listening when they want to talk, assisting them in spiritual growth,
helping them with financial problems, being their at times of happiness, joy,
mourning and sickness. In the office of Priest I feel I can do these things in
visiting in the homes of the Saints, in helping with preaching services on Sundays,
and possibly through workshops.
I like to visit and my hope is that as I visit with members I can minister to
their needs and that I will be able to perceive problems they may have and be
able to help them or let them allow someone else to come and help them.
I am going to set up a plan for visiting and hopefully that will help me to carry
out my responsibilities better than just thinking I should do it and then not
getting it done.
My concept of ministry twenty years ago was that I was serving my Lord by
attending church on Sundays and going to Women's meetings once or twice a month.
This concept has changed slowly over the years and the things I have spoken of
in this article have made them change. The loVcl and concern of the members of

-5-

this church has had a great deal to do with this change--not only the members of
our congregation but of the district. I would see some of the district people
two or three times a year and they treated me like I had been a member all my
life and like they had known me for a long time. I loved all of these people
and looked up to them for guidance and direction and soon realized they were
ministering to me.
I also have come to the realization that as I visit other people and do things
for them I am ministering to them. I am so thankful to be able to be a part of
God's great kingdom and to be doing my part to help bring others to know God
and to serve him.

Judy Gardner
1430 Brady Street
Helena, MT 59601

I have had several experiences in the last fifteen years that I feel were leading me up to my calling hourever I did not realize it at the time. I started college in 1964 taking courses just in the Art Department as that was what I wanted at that time. When I advanced to the paint of taking drawing and perspective I began to have the desire to draw something depicting the Holy Trinity. For several years I would start to draw something like three flowers, three leaves an just three anything, I could nat finish any one of the drawings as I would just fade out in my thinking, I would stop and wait but the desire kept returning and again I wuld start to draw but I could not get inspired, After about ten years of this frustration I was attending our Reunion at Stilluster, and after getting my tux granddaughters off to their morning classes I rushed down to the Prayer Service being being held in our "Grove". I sat down and bowed my head trying to bring my thoughts around to the service. I looked up and say the three men of the priesthood, who were to be in charge, with their arns around each others shoulders and their heads bowed in prayer. A very brilliant light surrounded them as I watched, and immediately I had my inspiration for my piece depicting the Holy Trinity. In the quiet of my trailer that afternoon I made the first preliminary drawing and completed it, By this time I was into word sculat ure. Even though my sculpture changed some it still evolves around that experience at Reunion. It took me a year to complete the carving which I carved from a black walnut log. It is the only religious piece I have made and I entered it in the First irts and Architecture Avards held by the world Church in 1986 and was fortunate enough to recieve Top Honor Award, I have since presented the sculpture which is titled, "Peace on Earth through The Holy Trinity', to the World Church to be displayed as they see fit. ashed me when I had been administered to last. I couldn't remember it had been so long, so we retired to a small cabin (open sides) in the woods. sometime. Brother Garland Tickemyer was sent to l'chita, Kansas as his first assign- ment under church appointment, to be our pastor. It save us more light on the calling of Erma Smith as we studied, with the dictionary, to be sure we understood the words correctly. At our reunion this summer (1987) I was ask to assist several times and also to have charge of one of the evening services. The Local pastors were to have charge with their congregations each night. October 1985, I was sitting watching and listening to the first women to be called to the priesthood in the Seattle stake. I had no desire to be called to the priesthood and because of my lack of study and my age, I felt that these would, there would be no call for me. I felt very safe about that. It wasn't that I didn't favor women in the priesthood. It was because I knew that responsibility. As I sat there, the spirit of the of discernment covered me and that I knew that there was to be a call for me. The steward rested on me several times during that service letting me know that there was a call for me. I decided not to share this experience with anyone because I didn't want to make anyone think I was covering a call to the priesthood. As I left the conference, I was on a very high spiritual plane. There was no doubt in my mind that I would expect the call as the month went by and I thought if this experience I had, the spirit would rest in Me and I would feel the sweet spirit of peace and joy. My father and mother had been dedicated to the work of the church and all I had ever knew was work for the land. Lord My father gave me two pieces of advice that really stuck in my mind. When a person is called to the priesthood, he better accept that call and work at it. In later years, he told me to stand behind the Prophet no matter what happened when I first learned if priests if calls for women, I said no way, but then I remembered my dad's words, stand behind the Prophet SAW I said at the prophet comes out with a revelation. I can accept that I prayed about it, and tried to have an open mind. When 156 came out. I felt it was right and I had no trouble accepting it. I felt women had already been doing priesthood work only without the laying of on of hands and without a card. On Thanksgiving Day of 1985 My son Lee was having dinner with me and the subject and the subject of women in the priesthood came up. As we talked, I fell dish is really the experience I had at conference. Lee had very definite ideas about things and I didn't know what he thought about 156 or how you would feel about his mother belonging to the priesthood. I really thought he would have a negative reaction. To my surprise, he was very positive. He made the statement that women could do anything they made up their minds to do one day one of the elders here and Brett Remington came to make a priesthood call. As we talked, he asked me how I felt about women in the priesthood and if I felt there were any embedded Remington who might have a call. I told him yes, I thought there would be some in Remington would be called and I named a few that I thought might be called. left off my name. And he said, How about you? I've been told him of the experience I had had conference. As we talked, I felt that same sweet spirit of peace around me. I was not sure how he felt. But how could he not feel that spirit that filled the room. Days went by and I took temple school classes that were offered, I continued to be on the very high spiritual plane. There seem to be there was even a change in music. As I played for church, I seem to be able to give more expression to my music. I worked on the works worship commission, and the idea seemed to flow so fast of ways we could help the congregation worship a different different way. I realized as we worked in the worship programs, we all were worshiping on Sunday, we worship in a different way. I spoke about this to the rest of the group and they said they felt the same way. One day our presiding elder called me and asked if I was going to be home. I told him yes, I was. He said he would like to come over and see me. After I hung up. I thought this is unusual for him to do. Usually if you want to talk to me, he comes by and he says what he has in mind and he goes away. As I worked around the house, I knew that he was coming about I placed a call. I had no idea what the call was to be but I knew I would try to do the best I could. When Jim finally came he came right to the point and said that there was a call for me to the Office of teacher. He asked me how I felt about accepting a call so I told him of my experience. As we talked he invited me that he wasn't sure how I felt about women in the priesthood. That night I called upon my two sons and my daughter to tell them of my call Lee and Paula were positive but when I talked to Eric, there was a pause before he answered me. My heart sank because I had planned to ask him to ordain me. I felt that maybe he could speak that call. I didn't know if he didn't believe women should be in the priesthood or if it was because of me. Again. I told you the experience I had and he seemed to accept it a little more. I told him I liked him to come to for my ordination, but I knew it was asked me a lot to come clean from Boise Idaho. He said he'd get back to me and let me know. Later I asked her how he felt about the time of my call. He said he felt it really wasn't the night right time for it. But as he thought about it, he became aware it was right. The night we met before the standing high council was an experience I will never forget the candidates sat in a semi circle facing the men of the council. There were quite a few of members of the other conjugate congregation. I knew most of them but as the evening wore on, I felt a landing with these women felt a bonding with these men and women I sat next to a young man from our congregation and who had been called on as a beacon beacon. When it was his turn to speak, he became very emotional. I reached out my head and took his in mind. We had worked together on his music he had been given a God given talent. That was wonderful. Knowing that we are all exceptional people. I wanted to support him in any way I could. As he finished speaking and it was my turn he held on to my hands and give me support. With that support. I was able to talk without breaking down too much. After the meeting was over. The men of the high council came and spoke to us individually. Some put their arms around us and told us they were happy because of our decision to accept our calls. It was nice to hear the Companions pledge to support their husbands or wives in their priesthood. As I sat there and wondered how my husband would have taken the changes that were being made, but knowing him he would have been all for it.

My companion was taken in July 1980. I missed him very much but I feel the there was

more than reason why he was taken. I had always leaned on someone else on my life and I think that this was a time of strengthening for me. I needed to stand on my own two feet and make some decisions for myself. I've known the Lord has been with me all my life, but I feel it felt felt he has especially been with me these last seven years. My ordination was held on Easter Sunday 1986 It was a special time for me because my whole family was there. My daughter Paula sang a song I had picked out and my son Eric was there to ordain me. He had he and Howard Hendricks and lay their hands on my head. The Spirit came on me so strong the tears began to flow. Again, I felt Mike's hand next to mine and support. But first talk is prayer service was an experience of fear and joy. I tried to prepare and I felt I wasn't getting anywhere. I just couldn't seem to put anything together. So I finally went out into the garden and pulled weeds. And as I was on my knees I poured my heart to the Lord. I asked him why I was doing this. And while I was here, I felt so unprepared. I went to prayer service and I gave the talk I had prepared. It went well even if it was quite short. I know the Lord heard my prayers and gave me the words and strength to make that talk. After my ordination. I went through a period of depression I felt I was not doing what I was shouldn't be doing. I'd pray and I'd seem separated from God. When I was at reunion I talked to our state president, Ray Peter and he told me not to be impatient because I'd only been in the priesthood a short time. Now I have had times when I know I'm able to function in my priesthood calling and then it gives and that gives me peace. There's so much I could do but I'm certain the way will be shown to me

My father also learned to weld at this time and did a lot of work welding broken plowshares, which has not had previously been repaired by Smithies. Using the time consumed in way of the forge. That is my background is saying I grew up in depression days Lamoni the brick church had burned when I was a preschooler Sunday school was held over lane, lanes General Store and Lamoni and church services in the Coliseum the movie theater I was baptized in a large galvanized caddy watering tank in the basement of the Colosseum when I was eight years old. In addition to my find Latter Day Saints school teachers my piano teacher wanted dari also was a church member in those depression days, many of things were done by barter. Adult experiences. Ajay, I moved to Oklahoma shortly after we were married and not too long after that I was asked to be a woman leader for the Oklahoma district. Victor ye was a district president at the time and the district was either the whole state or most of it. I did a lot of traveling usually by myself or to speak to different women groups. This one I began when I was 20, about 28 years old, or a little daughter and usually stay with our great grandmother Goodwin but occasionally went with me. In 1970, I was asked to become a member of the Women's Council it was Catherine westwards, last year. Then the council was chained to the women ministry commission with Marjorie tro as chairperson. During the years I was a member of the commission. I was guest speaker at various retreats and workshops on the West Coast and participated in the Commission's 1973 institutes and other such activities. There are two find experiences that occurred in the late 1960s. And in the late 1970s, which helped me in my growth, my husband AJ took an intensive two week course at the American Institute of Family Living when Dr. Phil Papineau was director and was also always reading all kinds of things. He had observed the need in the church had frequent contact with Glen homes who had pioneered counseling in the Los Angeles stake and continued to work advocation only in this field. Also, we started the marriage enrichment program here in Southern California our church's expression of Marriage Encounter the arduous pneus of working together to put down our own experiences and feelings so we could work first as marriage team couple that as a pastoral team couple administering the program conducting the weekend, and all of the other tasks helped me refine some areas of thinking and understanding. I believe all of these experiences helped me to grow on patience and compassion and insight human problems a universal the need for acceptance, the rebelliousness, the scapegoat tactics, the essential loneliness of each person. The desire to become the list goes on and on. And our responsibilities to assist are so limited response of each person to the guidance of the Holy Spirit to him or to her is the most necessary thing in the world. And the hardest to elicit in a continuing basis. The ordination service before talking about the service itself, I think my reaction the day I was called may be of interest I suspect to corroborate the testimony of other women and men and get another witness to the whole presiding elder had taken me aside after the 9:30am worship on October 6 1985. The tell me of my call. My husband had to be gone that afternoon. So I was home alone for a few hours. I felt such a tremendous spirit of peace, of reassurance of confidence of enablement. I felt my mind going toward the kinds of things I would like to see happen in the church community itself and in the geographic church neighborhood, the needs of the people. This was not devoid of realism, realism, however, my own age and other limitations, the abilities and trainings, the time betrayed of others. So it was not a wishing for the moon experience. But the overall mindset I have learned to identify with the Holy Spirit was expressing. I will do the best I can and all I can do for you, Lord, I am sure this is a universal experience for any person who receives the invitation of the Lord to follow in. At the ordination, itself our stake president Jane Nielsen W batter gave the morning message I can't remember what was said other than it was appropriate and brought that five Spirit of God to those of us assembled. I have always deeply appreciated brother batters giftedness and preaching clearly easy to understand personal calling to repentance. I was happy that he could be with us during since I had worked with him and other various activities before a number of years. It was like having an extended family president. What is always conscious of other things too, because of the flak that has resulted in calling women to the priesthood. I was the only woman called in my congregation and one of the first group called in the state. I'm sure that brother batter and his counselors made very sure that they were on hand to support any and to ordain those first women. This would let the members of the church know that they were fully in support of the action taken a 90 day for World Conference. My call to the office of Elder was presented to the Lamoni Stake Conference
on November 23, 1986. Six other priesthood calls for women were presented that day,
representing four Stake congregations. Four calls were to the office of Teacher;
one was to the office of Deacon; one was to the office of Priest. My call came
originally from our pastor, Clair Weddle, at our Stake reunion in August, 1986.
Previous to this time in Lamoni Stake, two women had been ordained to the office
of Elder--Barbara Higdon and Velma Ruch; two to the office of Priest--Lorrie Raiser
and Becky Long; and one to the office of Teacher--Elizabeth Johnson of Chariton,
Iowa. When these first calls just mentioned were presented to the Lamoni Stake
Conference in November, 1985, there was a great deal of opposition to the ordination
of women, Approximately one-sixth of the voting members voted against all of the
women's calls, Stake President, Joe Donald, allowed free discussion, and there
was so much discussion, much of it by those dissenting, that for a while the outcome
seemed doubtful. However, when the final vote was taken, each one was approved by
a large majority. The ordination services for these people were testimonies that
the Holy Spirit was directing this movement of the Church in these latter days.
In contrast to that f1irst meeting where women's calls were presented, the one where
my call and the six others were presented was peaceful and joyful. There were a
few abstentions, but no negative speeches.
The statements accepting the calls were printed in the Stake Bulletin for the
Conference, and each person made an oral statement, My statement printed in the
Bulletin indicates my feeling at the time: "This priesthood call to me is much
like the gesture of Jesus to his special servants as he called, 'Follow me,• It
comes after almost a lifetime of experiences that have led me to this place, this

that there would always be work for me to do if I kept myself prepared and ready.
In recent months I have had an intensification of the power of light and love
and freedom as I have studied for special assignments. I want very m,i\y.h to be an

and purposes of God, our creator,

The Testimony of Raeford Hanson, Teacher
I was called to the office of teacher by my pastor in late
summer of 1984. Since I had been unable to attend World
Conference that year, I did not give him an answer at that time.
I had mixed feelings regarding the calling of women to the
priesthood in Section 156 and I felt I had to be sure. I had
read and listened to what others had said and I prayed earnestly
regarding the call. I knew that "all are called" to serve God
according to the gifts and talents he has given them ''but did
that include priesthood ministry?''
I had always tried to serve in everything I had been
asked to do ever since I was baptized. I had received direction
of the Holy Spirit to do many things I had felt I could not do.
My Patriarchal blessing was a tremendous help at this time
for I was told if I remained faithful, I would do a greater work
for God in the future.
I prayed and waited for some time before giving my answer
to the pastor and the answer came!
Our missionary for our area, Seventy Gilbert Remington,
came unexpectedly to our branch for a brief visit. The first
afternoon, he came to our home to visit my husband and me. He
told us of his own experience in receiving the truthfulness of
women in the priesthood. In their deliberations of the document
in his quorom of Seventy, many of the men were hesitant and
unsure. After much prayer and discussion, they all came together

and voted unaminously. He related many of his own personal
experiences in the past where God had been preparing him for this
time. This helped me to realize that I must not shirk this
responsibility with all that God had blessed me with in the past,
At the local branch meeting, the call was accepted joyfully
by the congregation and their vote of confidence helped me very
much.
A few weeks later at District Conference, I was surprised
to see only two names in the calls to priesthood, A young priest,
Greg Hall, from our branch to the office of elder and my name
to the office of teacher,
On November 17, 1985, plans were made for Ordination Day.
I had asked Brother Remington if he would ordain me and he came.
I was ordained first by he and the pastor, Duane Amos, and Greg
Hall by the pastor and Brother Remington. It was a Pentacostal
experience for Greg and I as well as for others present. The
church was filled, A High Priest from another district came to
me after the service and said he had had reservations about women
in the priesthood but was convinced by the Holy Spirit now that
it was God's wish.
My ministry in the branch and district has been accepted well.
I enjoy sharing in District Retreats, Reunions, and Conferences,
I have taken my place with the Priesthood in the branch who have
been very supportative.
I enjoy home visiting, My husband, who is an Elder, goes
with me and I go with other priesthood when I am asked,
is so much to be done even in a small branch,

There

During my first sermon, I was apprehensive about visitors
who came in, wondering what they would think of a woman speaking.
They would tell me later they were happy to see women in the
priesthood, and that women expressed things differently than
men. I am happy to say, I have not had any negative reactions
to my efforts and I am thankful for that.
I think ministry in general, at least in our area, is much
improved during the last decade. The Faith to Grow Program has
helped both priesthood and members to work together better than
ever before. The Temple School classes are helping people
understand their heritage in the church and also what they can
do to help the church fulfill its mission. With the challenge
to build the Temple, I believe there will be greater dedication
and reconcilation in the lives of people. It is an exciting
time to live in and I'm happy to be a part of it!

September 10, 1987
In response to the request from the Women's Ministries Commission
here are some of my experiences, feelings and story.
My name is Patricia Ann Tesman Hardin. I was born April 28, 1935
in Coleman, MI. and grew up there. I came to Saginaw, MI in 1953
after graduation and have lived here since.
My ordination was approved by the members of the Saginaw Valley
District in July of 1986 during a special District Conference
held at our District Reunion at the Sanford Campgrounds. I
was ordained to the office of Teacher on Oct. 19, 1986. The
next Sunday my husband was ordained to the office of High Priest
at our Regional Conference in Grand Rapids. I am including a
copy of the bulletin of my ·o·rdinat'ion. I felt privileged to be
ordained by High Priest Leonard Young and my husband who would
soon be a High Priest. Len had been our Pastor during 1985 &
1986 while this great change occured in my life. He was always
presenting fresh challenges of service and thought to me and thus
was instrumental in the strengthening of my new faith which was
growing within the new person I was becoming.
Before I explain this 'new life' I would like to give a little
more background on my life.
An historical note:
I am the great granddaughterof A. Vanv~,±enburg whose baptism
and healing is recorded on pages 159 & 160 of INTO THE LATTER
DAY LIGHT by John J. Cornish.
I was blessed by Herbert Case and G. L. DeLapp at Edenville in
Oct. 1935. I was raised in the Church. My father, Leo E. Tesman,
held the office of Teacher when he died in 1951. (I consider it
an honor to begin to serve in the same office at the age of 51;
he died at the age of 42.) He was much loved and respected by
his church, family , neighbors, and fellow-workers. Everyone
knew that 'the Church was Leo's life' to my mother's chagrin.
My mother was a convert. I was 16 and not baptized at the time
my father died. I was too shy to step out in front of people
in that way. Perhaps the call to discipleship was not very
strong at that time. I'm really not sure.
I was not baptized until 1958 when I had been married for four years
and had two children. My husband did not have a church. I came
to realize that I wanted for my family the same quality of life I
had experienced growing up. I wanted a religious base for our
family life and a Church home for our family. I began to study
the Church. I came to the realization that accepting Christ's
gift and being a part of His Church was more important than the
fear of having people look at me.
My husband and I were baptized within three days of each other(Apr. '58)
His is a remarkable story of conversion - a 180 degree turn in
the twentyfour hours following my baptism which he was unable to
attend. At that time this step was so important to me that I

could not wait and had to take it in spite of his apparent dis-
approval. H~~brdained the next year, 1959, to the office of Deacon,

Priest in 1969, Elder in 1973, and served as Pastor of the King
Road branch in Saginaw, MI from 1976 through 1984. He will be
serving as Saginaw Valley Drstr4ct President next year, 1988.

page 2

.I always tried to support him in his ministry by encouraging
him to give all the time he wanted to to his work in the church.
At first we were a team but as the family grew and I .withdrew
he had to serve alone.
In the early years I had all the zeal usually associated
with a new convert. But now I see how I slowly pushed God
out of my life and lost all the zeal. I selfishly refused to
accept God's will for my life in many ways. I went to church
less and.less and withdrew into a shell. I no longer allowed
the Holy Spirit to function in my life. I did as little as
I could get by with without revealing the spiritual wasteland
inside.
In the early '70s my husband often came home from meetings
telling me how many sisters in the church felt like lower class
members, cheated and less accepted because they were denied
the avenue of Priesthood service. I had no desire for that
service for myself. I was happy not to have to face that type
of responsibility. I carried enough guilt already. I was not
even a good member.
In 1984, because of the articles I had read in the Saints
Herald and the talk within the church, I was afraid that this
subject might come up at the next World Conference. I wanted
to be there to hear the discussion if it did. We had never
even been to Independence before. I am so thankful we were
able to go to that Conference. As the proposed section 156
was presented I said, "Oh, no! Not in my church!" I told my
delegate brother-in-law that day, "I wish I were a delegate
too so I could vote against it." I attended all the sessions
and listened to all the debate. When it was read again on
Thursday prior to the vote I KNEW that this was God's will for us,
His Church, at this time and I accepted it completely. I
realized that now I too was under the same onerous responsibility
as my husband had been since he was baptized. I thought about
what this would mean to my two granddaughters. They would grow
up in a different church than I had. They would now grow-"fknowing.
that God would expect them to prepare themselves for the possibility
of priesthood service too, just as the young men of the church
had through the past. Now all males and females were equal in the
avenues of service available to them.
I went home actively supporting the concept of women in the
priesthood. As my sisters in Saginaw were called, I made it
a point to seek them out and offer my support.
This brings me to the year 1985 and the experience that turned
my life around and brought me back to a total acceptance of God's
will for me. I had always known that He was there to help me do,
be, become the best I could be, but I was afraid to accept that
help because I was selfish and did not want to be obligated to
give Him my time freely. My story is recorded in the March/April
1987 issue of the RESTORATION WITNESS. (I am including a reprint.)

(,,~ page 3

Some experiences in this new 'sojourn':
I had never offered a prayer in public, in or out of a service.
Our Pastor Leonard Young, called me in Sept. '85 to ask if I
would take part in an 11:00 a.m. worship service and offer a
prayer of praise. My reply was,"NO, I never have done anything
like that. I can't pray publicly, I don't know how.• After I
hung up I knew I had done the wrong thing. I was not trusting
God and accepting His will. I had every reason to praise Him

and a strong desire to do it in public. I called Len back im-
mediately and accepted. I told him I could not swim but I had

heard that one way to learn is to jump in and so I was jumping
in. As I hung up that time the words of praise began to flood
my mind and the prayer was composed and written before the hour
was past.
In Nov. '85, I was asked to offer the invocation for the combined

service here in Saginaw when the first women in our two congre-
gations were ordained. Although the Spirit was beginning to work

more strongly in my life, I had no idea that I too would be
ordained before the next year was over. But I did feel that if

I were ever called, it would be to the office of Teacher. The-
call was presented to me in April of '86. Len called Bob and I

into the office after the service and shut the door. I wondered
why he was shutting the door when no one else was in the hallway.

I thought he's not going to discuss something secret like priest-
hood calls with his councelor with me here. When he told me

that a priesthood call for me had been approved I thought, 'How
can I refuse when God has done so much for me!' I recognized
the need for this ministry in our branch and I had recognized
my gifts in that direction when studlng the new Priesthood
Guidelines.
I was to be ordained in Oct. '86 after completing the required
classes. To me the most difficult aspect of priesthood was the
challenge of sermons. I did not see this as the primary duty of
the Teacher and I recognized the need of this ministry of the
peacemaker in the branch and had a strong desire to serve in
that capacity. My new faith said that with God's help I could
grow into the 'preaching' phase of service if that was His will.
At our Sept. BLT I was told that my first speaking assignment
would be Dec. 14th. I went home that evening singing "I Am
Trusting You Lord Jesus•. Composition and public speaking had
always been difficult for me. I began to study and jot down

notes, thoughts, ideas, and reference things I read. One beauti-
ful fall morning I started out on an hour's drive alone to an-
other city on a church errand. As I drove through the quiet

counrtyside my soul was full of praise for the beauty of God's
creation, the joy of being alive and in His work. I began to
think about the impending Christmas season and the gift of His
Son. My sermon theme was to be 'Love, Mary's Child Incarnate'.
Words, then sentences, then paragraphs began to form in my mind
as I drove along. I wished I had a tape recorder with me because
a whole sermon was developing in my mind. That evening at home
I wrote it down, remembering almost all of it. I had to work
at it and rewrite it but I felt that the Holy Spirit had indeed
helped me to formulate it. When Dec. 14th arrived I had no fear
nor nervousness as I once would have felt. Instead I felt an

&b page 4

empowering ·spirit during the delivery that made my knees sha.ke.
Once more God demonstrated to me that I need not fear! I am
not alone in this service. I thank Him for another of His miracles.
I can affirm from personal experience that thru God all things
are possible.
The ministry of women has been well received in the Michigan
Region and in my own branch and district. Two women were ordained
in my branch in 1985 and four more including myself in 1986.
There is one branch in the district where it is not accepted.

A member from that branch was at a recent meeting wherein he ex-
pressed a dissenting opinion on the topic under discussion. Some

heated words ensued. I felt impressed to seek him out as soon
after the close of the meeting as possible to demonstrate that
at least one person in the group loved and accepted him. I
sensed that this was within the spirit of my calling. So I
asserted myself in a manner new to me and stopped him and chatted
with him before he could hurry away. Later I realized what had
transpired. He had accepted my ministry whether he realized it
or not; the ministry of a woman. I feel that this illustrates
one way in which the priesthood of women can gradually be
accepted where it is now opposed. It may come about as the
women ordained to the office of Teacher have many small quiet
opportunities to work their ministry of peace. As this becomes
evident to those receiving it the total acceptance of women's
ministry can begin. Is this why a large number of women are being
called and ordained to this office?
As I stated before, priesthood was never something I desired for
myself but I am now willing to serve in any manner Christ wishes
me to. After I accepted this priesthood call I felt a new
strong desire growing in me to bring people to an acceptance
of Christ in their lives and the desire to baptize and nuture
them in their new discipleship. I feel this is another challenge
being presented to me, to study and to learn, to grow and to serve,
and to prepare.
An additional note on my ordination service:
I requested Hymn# 178 "Lord, I Was Blind: I Could Not See" because
it represented what my life used to be and Hymn# 457 "My Lord,
I Know That All My Life" for what my life is becoming.
Our four children are not very active in the church. I feel
that this is my responsibility. It may be because of my inactivity

and attitude as they were growing up. Although we always en-
couraged them to participate but actions speak louder than words.

They were all in attendance for my ordination as well as my only
two grand children, a non-member daughter-in-law and a non-member
soon to be son-in-law. My sister MaryLee Mathews from Lansing and
my inactive brother Richard Tesman from Alma came also. we had
a family dinner after the service in the Church fellowship dining
room. Most of my life I had been more of the Martha; I prepared
the dinner the day before because this day I wanted to be a Mary.
I also invited my mentor Pat Diehl and her husband, the Young
family, E/P Richard Zsymoly, and his guest E/P Ken Cliff,
