"If your pastor came to you and told you that you had been
called to the priesthood, how would you respond?" A rather odd
question to be asked out of the clear blue, wouldn't you say?
And yet this very question was posed to me in two separate
situations during a week!92c'ld session of Calling and Caring Lab I
in January of 1986, almost a month before I was to find out that
I did indeed have a priesthood call to answer.
I was raised in the Lutheran church and felt that I had a
good basis but something had always been missing in my religious
life. Before my husband and I were married, I attended church
with him and found peace, satisfaction, and answers to questions
that I'd had for years. I became a member of the RLDS church in
1

March, 1975, 10 months after we were married. We attended regularly
and were Zion's League leaders for about two years, but that was
the extent of our activity. We ovmed and operated a photography
studio which seemed to keep us pretty busy.
Life has a way of changing when you least expect it, and thus
the years 1980-81 held some real struggles and challenges for us.
we now had two children and a business that was in trouble. We
learned some valuable lessons during this time though. One was
that you really are abundantly blessed when you file and pay your
ti thing, and another was that God doesn't always answer prayers
the way we expect or want but He always answers. We couldn I t save
the business but a good job opportunity came available for my
husband so that we were able to make the transition from one
to the other without a period of unemployment.
Six months later things seemed to be going better. l1y husband
was doing well with his new job and we had most of the accounts
settled from closing the business. The stress level Yor both of
us had lessened considerably, but somehow I still felt unsettled.

I was feeling the need to do something more with my life. I
started praying about it, focusing on what it was that God was
calling me to do, I found that you don't ask God to show you the
way unless you 1

·-re really ready to go because the doors of
opportunity start opening in ways you're not always expecting.
For many years I felt I wanted to be in nursing. When we
closed the business I thought it might be a good time to pursue
this desire, but on closer examination realized we didn't have
the money for tuition~ I also had two small children to care for·,
I had given up the idea, thinking that this was not the right time
when a close friend of mine called. During our conversation she
told me that the sermon that Sunday, which happened to be Heal th
Ministries Sunday, had started her thinking about going to nursing
school and she wanted to know ~ I thought about the idea. I
shared with her what I'd been feeling and the next day we went to
talk with people at a school in Omaha. Because of the financial
loss incured. in closing the business I was eligable for grants
and scholarships, so in June, 1982, I started. nursing school.
It d.id.n I t work out for my friend to start at that time, but I
firmly oelieve that God used her as the catalyst to get me
started on the first step of my journey of service.
I experienced. a great deal of personal growth in the next
four years. I was also feeling God become more the center of my
life. Through many experiences during the time I was in school
I realized that God had led me into nursing, but as I was
finishing my last few months I was feeling a need for further
guidance as to~ I should do now. I, of course, was expecting
the aJ11swer to be related to nursing but God had another area of
service in mind first.
We had a new pastor who was getting '6\'i.'6-priesthood. members

(, 1~ 3
involved in services. He asked me to do one of the prayers for
our Christmas Eve service. I said yes but was petrified. To this
point in my life I had never prayed in public before and wasn't
sure I wanted to now. But because I said yes, God blessed me and
everything was fine. Around that same time I had also been
asked to participate in Calling and Caring Lab I. It seemed
that more and more I was being asked to take a more active role
in the congregation.
When I first heard that Section 156 had been presented, the

thought went through my mind that I was going to need to re-
evaluate and get rny life in order 'oecause I might be asked to

myself for being so bold as to think God might consider me for

The Omaha-Council Bluffs Stake Conference was scheduled

Since my family are non-members I was concerned how they

The next communfon Sunday I was scheduled to serve. I was

to serve communion, I knew that all eyes were on my every move
and I felt very small. But God held me up and the response of
the people afterwards was more loving and supportive than it had

A few months later I was asked to help prepare the emblems
and serve the priesthood. I had both a positive and a negative

struggling with women in the priesthood but I a.idn' t know it at
the time. He was so helpful in explaining what to do and we
shared a little in conversation after the service that seemed so
positive, I was shocked to find out that he was having difficulty

is done with a great feeling of inadequacy and a great love
for my Heavenly Father who has blessed me so richly.

Essex County presented.

an excellent Deacon and a great role model to follow. It was
easy to have him for my mentor. But, I know he found it difficult
sometimes to let go of the office of Deacon. It is very different
when you have the responsibility to move out and do the Deaconing.

because God answers prayers';

r I

fer Sunday morning at 10:00 A.M., September 7, 1986, at the Lake Charles, Louisiana Branch, There were two High Priests

My name is Janie Ladnier Hicks and I have attended the bay you Labatt hurt, Alabama branch all of my life. I'm 20 years old. I'm married to a wonderful man named Steve Hicks, and I'm in my last year at university at South University of South Alabama. I've been very involved in the church since I was old enough to attend youth meetings. I played the piano for church help type the bulletins and served as a music director during my early teenage years. My brother and I went to every service they had when we were in high school and thoroughly enjoyed meeting with the saints. I was informed to my call to the Office of priest in August 1986 by our pastor elder rusty Borge. I felt very unworthy but I was very eager to serve the Lord and whatever way I could. At the time, I was informed on my calling. My husband had only been a member of the church for three years. When we heard about section 156 We were both excited and knew it was the Lord. However, I knew he did not understand the calling process or the responsibilities of the priesthood and probably never expected me to be called. As the days passed, the more I thought about my calling, I began to become confused and afraid. Even though I had a good marriage, I began to think that my call would cause problems in my marriage. The next week was terrible. I felt unhappy, depressed and scared to tell my husband that these feelings were completely opposite to the ones I had experienced when I was a formed of my calling. I prayed about my mixed feelings frequently and read my patriarchal blessing. These things enlightened my mind and I told Steve about my calling. I soon began to realize that the confusion I had experienced was from the devil, that only piece of copper come from the Lord and confusion comes from the devil. Steve took the news fine and told me that he would support me whatever I decided to do, I felt better but I was still a little afraid. However, I was looking forward to being ordained in November and went to the women's retreat at Bluff springs campgrounds at the end of September 1986 Some ladies there knew I had been called to the priesthood, but I'm not sharing my feelings or personal experiences about my column with anyone. During the commandment service that Sunday a young lady who I had admired that weekend but never really talked to named Cindy Kistler stood and told me that the Lord loved me very much. He knew what I was going through and he would be with me as I moved forward. From then on, I knew that my column was from God, and since then I have not been afraid to proceed with my pastoral responsibilities. I was ordained in November 2 1986. The Biola batho branch had been very supportive and receptive of my ministry. I have had the opportunity to serve communion deliver sermons baptize a 12 year old young lady named Tanya special and do and to do many other things in my branch and community. As a result, I have grown closer to the Lord and my fellow human beings. I'm young and still have a lot of my life ahead left ahead of me with the support of the Lord, my family and friends I will continue to grow and develop the talents of blessings as the Lord has given me with joy in my heart.

My call to the office of Elder came through Lamoni Stake. I
was ordained on February 24, 1986, by Apostle William T. Higdon
and Presiding Evangelist, Duane Couey.
My husband, William T. Higdon, is an Apostle.
As I look back over my life from the vantage point of almost
fifty-seven years, I am aware of the presence of the Holy
Spirit. I have been richly blessed with the kind of enabling
support that has given me unusual opportunities. I was nurtured
in a home that had very little money, but was rich in love. My
parents tell me that they felt a special stewardship for me
because they recognized unusual giftedness. Their determination
that my giftedness should be developed and the strong influence
of the concept of stewardship of energy and talent which the
Church taught me, combined to give my life direction; I can never
remember a time when I did not have a strong sense of calling. I
would have described it in different ways at various times in my
life, but it always consisted of a feeling of having been
dedicated by a power outside myself.
That feeling did not arise from specific spiritual
experiences. In fact, all my life I have longed for the
spectacular kinds of encounters with divinity which many other
people report. The Lord has not worked with me in that way. My
Creator has guided my steps through a very still small voice

1

which, because I felt it presumptuous to identify as God's
spirit, I have sometimes called intuition.
At great sacrifice, my parents insisted that I participate
in Orioles and Zions League and the life of the Englewood
Congregation in Independence. In those activities, I encountered
people who made an important contribution to my development.
Even in grade school I had a strong sense of the need to develop
my intellectual capacity and that sense has stayed with me
throughout life. Again at great personal sacrifice, my parents
saw to it that I attended Graceland College and I found there
role models, new ideas and Bill. I was serious about another
young man when Bill came into the picture and I have often
thought how different my life would have been and probably how
much less opportunity I would have had if my choice of a husband
had been different. The strong attraction which existed
immediately between Bill and me was unmistakable and made my
choice easy. My marriage has been one of the richest blessings
of my life.
Because we were blessed with three healthy, outgoing and
independent children, I was never confronted with a choice
between my personal development and their well-being. If I had
been, there would have been no question as to where my primary
responsibility lay. Of course, it resided with them, but that
was not the case and we were able to have a very rich family life
while I pursued at the same time my own education and
professional activities.
The opportunities that our family has had for service have

2

been great. Even though it would not have been our choice, Bill

was called to the Office of High Priest and served as self-
sustaining district president following the death of the church

appointee in the Houston area. This responsibility took him away
from our family every weekend and that was not an easy thing. At
the same time, I had an opportunity to teach at a predominately
black university and had a number of important experiences there
which enriched my understanding of injustice and prejudice. The
opportunity to go to Graceland on the faculty came to both of us
and when Bill was invited to become President, I was able to
continue to teach, a very unorthodox arrangement, but one which
worked out successfully.
One of the most difficult decisions that Bill and I had to
make was his response to the call to enter full-time Church
appointment as a member of the Council of Twelve Apostles. He
felt very comfortable and successful in higher education and had
expected that his career would continue in that field. I knew
that the call to the Council would mean long separations and
exposure of Bill to personal danger. I had often given thanks
during earlier years that Bill was not a church appointee because
I was close enough to the Church to see deprivation which
appointee wives faced with that identical challenge. My worst
fears were realized when Bill's first assignment was an extensive
foreign field. I was not sure that I could sustain the
separation and the Lord had chosen not to give me any assurance
either of the divinity of Bill's call or of my ability to play my
role. During those first years when he was away so much, I was

3

7;2-<f
sustained by very challenging opportunities at Park College and
by internal strength which I can only attribute to the presence
of God's spirit. I had been wanting to write a book on preaching
for the Church for many years, but had simply not found the time
with an active career and three children. Our children were in
college by this time and I made the conscious decision to spend
my weekends working on my book. I remember thinking I could sit
home and feel sorry for myself or I could finally write my book.
And that's what I did.
I had thought when we moved into our home in Independence
that this would be the last stop; that I would continue to work
for Park College until retirement; that Bill would continue to
serve as an Apostle until his retirement. I was completely
unprepared for the invitation from the Board of Trustees to
interview for the presidency of Graceland College. As I look
back on that experience, I realize that prior to its happening
the thought had crossed my mind on several occasions that I
perhaps had the skills to do the job, but thought that it was one
of those opportunities which would simply not ever come to me.
Bill and I talked about it very realistically knowing that if I
were to accept, it would create a new dimension of separation and
personal stress. We agreed that it was something I needed to do.
We have been sustained in our personal deprivation during the
past three years. There have been times when I felt that the
burden was simply too heavy to bear with the added problem of
maintaining separate homes. But each time I have felt that way,
something has happened to strengthen me and give me the courage

4

to go on.
The Lord has never worked with me in ways I would have
chosen. If I had been given an unmistakable spiritual experience
which guaranteed for me strength and support and assured me of my
ability to handle the various challenges which have been mine,
I've always thought I would have been able to meet those
challenges with greater confidence and greater tranquility, but
that has not been the way the Lord has worked with me. Many
times in my life I have moved into an unknown; unsure of my
ability to meet the challenge and unsure that God's spirit would
be with me. I don't know why God chooses this way for me, and I
don't know why I am continually pursued by doubt in my ability to
do a job and lack of confidence in God's presence. The serenity
is not a gift that I have been given or have been able to
cultivate and I long for it and ask God's forgiveness for the
inability.
I believe that God gifts human beings for the purpose of
enriching the human community. We are called on to respond by
creating opportunities for that development and taking advantage
of them. My criticism of the past has been that many women did
not feel that principle applied to them and repressed their
giftedness, that repression being a sacrifice on the altar of
supporting other members of their family. I think we all owe
support and nurture to other people, but we also owe it to
ourselves and we do not have the right to neglect God-given
gifts. How we manage both the nurture of others and the nurture
of ourselves is perhaps one of the most difficult challenges of

5

life.
I remember very clearly my shock at an experience I had as a
young adult. Bill and I attended a weekend retreat and the guest
minister there was a person of academic attainment and brilliance
whom we both admired very much. He had a stimulating and
challenging presentation to the group about the stewardship of
giftedness and the need of the world for the gifts of those
people. I was so turned on that I went up to talk with him
afterwards because what he said squared exactly with what I
thought the Church taught about the stewardship of giftedness.
Instead of having my feeling confirmed by him, he poured serious
cold water on my whole concept. He let me know that he didn't
think that a married woman with children had any right to be
thinking along the lines that he had just outlined in his talk.
I realized that he hadn't been talking to the whole group, only
to the men and unmarried women in the group. I simply could not
accept that limitation, I knew of no basis for it anywhere. I
felt disillusioned and rebellious at the same time. His position
could not be right in the light of the teachings of the
Restoration. I could not accept that they were "for men only,"
My Ordination Service was held at Graceland College in the
evening. There were hymns and the charge was given by Brother
Couey. A college student read the words of my hymn, "Creation
Flows Unceasingly" as a statement from me. Velma Ruch was
ordained by J. C. Stewart and Bruce Graybill to the office of
Elder in the same service.
I did not favor the ordination of women when I first heard

6

the reading of Section 156. I felt that it would throw the
Church into turmoil which would set us back in evangelism by
creating inner disunity. That however, was only a minor basis
for my objection. I felt that as a movement we had never come to
terms theologically with the role of priesthood. My criticism
through the years has been that we put priesthood and potential
priesthood on a pedestal and make of the laity second-class
citizens. It seemed to me that we needed to engage in the hard
disciplines of sorting out the job descriptions for the
unordained before we added to the ranks of priesthood. My
efforts had been to push wherever I had a platform for that
objective. I felt that ordaining women would take pressure off
of the need to do that kind of analysis. As I have lived with
the reality of 156 for the last three years, I have changed my
position in the following ways. I think, as painful as it is,
the turmoil in the Church is probably a necessary sifting. Many
persons who have been negative and against any change for years
and years have selected themselves out of the fellowship. This
probably has needed to happen as their presence may have held
back the growth of their unit and in the process driven away
positive people who simply became fed up with the obstruction and
the negativism.
As to my second objection, I believe the kind of analysis
and discipline I was calling for would never have taken place
without this development. I'm not sure it will now, but I think
we are coming closer to being forced to do it as an institution
than we ever would have been if things would have stayed the

7

7) ✓ V
same. We, of course, have a long way to go in getting used to
the idea, but I believe there is some hope for a careful analysis
that would lead to the use of the gifts of all who belong to our
fellowship.
I have never felt that women shouldn't be ordained. In
fact, I have used every opportunity presented to me to hold up to
women and their stewardship of gifts including their capacity to
minister and to try to get them to understand that they are not
second-class persons in the eyes of God because they were not
eligible for ordination. To have excluded a whole group from the
possibility of ordination has spoken more loudly to the
inferiority of women than all of the protestations to the
contrary which the leadership of the Church has voiced.
I have felt strongly over the years that women needed to be
challenged to be the best they could be, even without ordination.
Because of that view I had some difficulty in accepting my own
call. I had felt a strong call to ministry as long as I can
remember and I have tried very consciously to direct that urge
into channels that were available to me and at the same time keep
on the pressure for an acknowledgment by the institution that
women's gifts were not in any way inferior to men's. Ironically,
I feared that accepting priesthood office would prevent me from
being able to speak with authority to that large group of women
who have absorbed and accepted their second-class status. I
finally accepted my call with the expectation that I would be
able to continue that ministry. I have found my opportunities
for ministry enlarged by the presence of the ordination.

8

1 J -1
I have had a number of experiences in which I felt empowered
in a way that I have never experienced before. One of those
experiences which stands out in my mind took place in the late
fall of 1986. The Dean of Students at Graceland College called

me in the middle of the night to inform me that a life-
threatening situation had occurred in the dorm. A student, who

was a known asthmatic, had a serious attack. The medical team
had been unable to find a heartbeat or register a blood pressure.
When I arrived, the young woman was receiving CPR, the ambulance
was on the way, but the doctor shook her head and said, ''I don't
think she's going to make it." The Dean of Students and I and
several close friends of the young women followed the ambulance
to the hospital and kept vigil for several hours while the
medical team attempted to stabilize their patient. Eventually,
she was stable and then we began to worry about brain damage.
She was finally conscious enough to ask for administration and
Rick Bunch, the campus Pastor, anointed and I confirmed. The
prayer of confirmation flowed out of me freely, which is an
unusual experience for me because I find public prayer to be one
of the more difficult things that I do. I found myself praying
for her mother, who was on the West Coast, and with whom we had
been in telephone contact, for her, and for her friends and
expressing gratitude for the great blessing which we had
experienced. (The college physician felt that her recovery was
miraculous.) When I finished the prayer, I leaned over the bed
and kissed her and tucked in the covers that had fallen off one
shoulder. On the way back to the campus, Rick Bunch and I

9

1J-/I>
discussed the experience and he expressed great appreciation for
my action at the end of the prayer. He felt it was an
appropriate thing and said a man would not have felt free under
most circumstances to have done what I did.
I hope my action has provided a model for him. I know he
has told the story in other places and I hope that it will free
male members of the priesthood to express greater warmth and
appropriate affection.
The other experience I would like to recount is the week I
spent at the Lost Angeles Stake Reunion number two, in the Summer

of 1987. I was one of the guest ministers and my responsi-
bilities included preaching one evening, teaching the Temple

School class on Preaching, and being available for other ministry
that might be needed. I participated with Paul Lucero in
administration and bore my testimony at one of the fellowship
services, delivered one of the evening sermons and gave the
Sending Forth at the consecration service on the final morning.
I have been on the staff of reunions before. My responsibility
has been to teach. I felt singularly enabled at this reunion.
My sermon was very enthusiastically received by children as well
as adults. After it was over, one little girl came up and said,
"That was the best sermon I ever heard." I felt that the power
of God was walking with me that whole week. I came back from the
reunion utterly exhausted, but feeling that God's spirit had
worked through me.
I have always had to fight an ego problem. I have wanted to
be the best, I think trying to compensate for that feeling of

10

second-classness. My reaction to that week was almost as though
I was an outside observer, watching God's spirit use me to meet
the needs of many people who were there. I had no sense that
what I accomplished was to my credit. I have never had that
feeling with such strength as I did that week.
I am a veteran of negative responses. For many years, I
have tried to say "yes" to opportunities for service and as a
result of that, I have been walked out on, I have been
criticized, and I have been seen as pushing for the ordination of
women. Perhaps that is the effect of my effort, but that was not
my intention. When my call came before the Stake Conference, the
Stake President permitted those in opposition to the ordination
of women to present their position. For about an hour, several
held forth on why 156 was not the will of the Lord. Some of them
were my good friends; they were very careful to say that there
was nothing personal about me in the position they were taking.
I felt a terrible burden during that hour. I thought I was
hardened to that sort of thing because of my past experiences,
but I found I wasn't. I empathize with pain of the persons who
were speaking and the silent ones whom they represented, but at
the same time it became very clear to me that, their
protestations to the contrary, not withstanding, their objection
were personal because part of who I am is my femaleness, whatever
that means. Over the years I have tried to develop in my own
feeling that that does not mean a great deal to me. My humanness
is what I have focused on. But I know that to most people, their
maleness or their femaleness is their primary part of identity,

11

1 )_ / (?-
so I have had to deal with that rejection and I'm happy to say
that the bitterness which has been part of my reaction over the
years to the position of women in the Church is completely gone.
That night at the Stake Conference was a kind of purging of that
bitterness.
Since my ordination, I would have to say that my ministry
has been very well received. I have traveled a lot for
Graceland College and have frequently been asked to stay over
Sundays and preach. I have been invited to priesthood meetings
in various jurisdictions and asked to talk about my experiences.
One of the high points of my experience was to be on the staff of
a women's retreat held at Kirtland Temple. I participated in a
communion service in the Temple and the congregation was ninety
percent women and the response of those women to those of us who
had been ordained was wonderful. There was an outpouring of the
knowledge that women had ministerial gifts which had been held in
suspension for all of the life of everyone of us there.
I perceive ministry to be an attempt to allow God's spirit
to use the minister to meet the needs of other people. It is
putting the needs of other people ahead of one's personal needs.
It is being committed to sharing the good news of the gospel of
Jesus Christ with others. I believe any person who is committed
can offer that ministry. The primary distinction among persons
is not ordained or unordained, but committed or uncommitted.
Since I have never held any other office of priesthood, I don't
know how being an Elder is different. What I do know is that I
have felt a power since my ordination that I did not feel before.

12

That surprises me because, frankly, I have seen myself as an
empowered person without portfolio. I felt I had done the things
that an Elder does and I had felt empowered in those activities.
I have a sense now of an accompanying spirit which I simply
did not have before, which has made what I do easier in a sense.
I have less feeling of struggle and a greater feeling of joyous
acceptance of the problems that I am confronted with.

13

My life has been one dependent on my Lord since my early childhood. I wish I could say I had I came from a loving Christian family but nothing could be farther from the truth or not for early association I had with Jesus Christ through my Sunday School teachers my life would have been without hope. I know that only through God. Grace my life has been guided and guarded it has developed according to His plan as I continued to come to Him in prayer when I needed assistance. I continue to lean on that strength to this day. Perhaps the preparation for my calling to the Office of Deacon started about 1973 or 1974. I had attended a women's retreat in Nauvoo, Illinois, the theme was talked walking in other fellows moccasins. She told one of the sessions was given by Florence Orth. It was based on her experience of becoming a widow. She told her her husband's lung illness and the preparation she had to make. She gave a vivid account of the things women need to do. For many women. It comes as quite a shock when they must take over and move from a position of dependence to one of independence. She stressed the importance of a will things that should and should not be in a safety deposit boxes, etc. One of the things she impressed upon my mind was the importance of knowing your own going your way financially. She said every woman should have some money in an account in your name only. She said Money enjoyed accounts are frozen for time at the time of of death of either joint owner. The same is true of your safety deposit box. I've always tried to be alert or fine tuned myself into the Lord's wavelength. This information was something I did not know before and I felt this message directed to me as I have a small income of my own. I immediately opened the account in my name and began setting away a little at a time in 1975. At my husband's persuasion, I went into business with another party, this business business was really to promote their hobby of tropical fish

the partnership simply did not work out. I have closed the shop so soon would have closed the sub show soon would have meant heavy losses for both of us. I bought the partners interest and prayed really prayed. The operation far exceeded anything I had ever done before. There were employees to be paid further withholding form sales tax form stocks to be ordered inventories to take cetera. I soon learned that it does no good to kick against the pricks or feel sorry for the mess I'd gotten myself into. With God's help. I was determined to make the best of the situation. I would learn all I could to become the best at the business and learn I did I attended seminars any place I could find them this took me places I had never been before Chicago, St. Louis Memphis and even the Waikiki Aquarium in Honolulu, Hawaii. I started water quality filters, chemicals, habitats, etc. I read everything I could get my hands on I picked salesman's brains, I learned the areas where these exotic creatures came from freshwater brackish lakes. Or oceans so they can better meet their needs when they arrived at my shop after a long trip from air. I learned their names and could say sudo tropes zebra as if I had known them all my life I had to learn new things in order to know what to order most of the life fish I came into O'Hare airport area and I ordered by phone. The only reason I mentioned this past of my life is because I am tested. I can testify that when you wake up, make up your mind to do something. Nothing is impossible with God's help. I now know that the strength of me for things that were not yet to come November 1978 I sat during the funeral service for my sister in law I realized how quickly life goes by I thought that he I thought here I am waiting for my time actually life on something as frivolous as this business. I could no longer take part in women's retreats. It was even hard to arrange time off to to go to a monthly meeting. We had just purchased property to build a new church and I wanted to be a part of that. I was homesick for close attention I had with my brothers and sisters in our little mission. I wanted my life back. I wanted to enjoy living nothing big just a little let me out of this structured life. When you pray for something you had better mean it I was serious and the Lord heard my prayers within six months I had sold my location. Notice I did not say I had sold the business, just the location there was not even time to dispose of the inventory. So I took it home and sold it off one sold it out of my home a little at a time. How could I have known that the Ozark airlines would announce shortly after that they would no longer serve Quincy, Illinois. I didn't know but I do know that it would have done what it would have done to my business. Thank God for His perfect timing. We broke ground for a new church in June 1980. How grateful I was for the money I had in my own name only how thankful I was for the business and experience I had and for the fact that the running of our household it always felt on my shoulders. There were many times when I had resented that responsibility. Now I was glad at least I knew where things were located. In my case there would have been no time to prepare the Florence or prayer like Florence Orth had thank God I attended the women's retreat and acted on the information. It was difficult time, a time where everything had to be attended to immediately at a time when you're thinking it's impaired by grief and physical exhaustion. So I just said take your time don't act in haste I moved slowly through the maze. Eventually I realized I needed to broaden my financial knowledge. My name is Joyce Hornberger. I am a member of the Reorganized C11urch of Jesus

Christ of Latter Day Saints. I have attended the Livermore, California con-
gregation for the last 7 years. (This is in the San Francisco Stake)

I'd like to back up for a moment, and tell you a little about myself. I am
from a RlllS church family. I have been blessed with parents that taught me the
story of the church. I have always felt that I was a good "church-goer", but I
was more comfortable not having any responsibilites within the congregation.
My husband and I lived most of our lives in Pennsylvania. We were happy to be
within driving distance of our families. In 1980 my life took a dramatic change.
My husband accepted a job in California, and we made this move.
I felt very comfortable with the saints at the Livermore church. It was a
small congregation, and a feeling of warmth engulfed us that first Sunday.
A short time later, I found it difficult to say "no" to request of getting
involved. The more I did, the more confident I became.
When Section 156 was first past, I had mixed feelings. It was hard to imagine
the church taking such a change. However, I made it a subject of prayer, and
soon felt it to be of God.
I must admit though, that as I thought of the women that would be of great service
for the Lord, never once did I envision myself in that role. I was comfortable
where I was at.

When the pastor, Dennis McAlister, told me of the call to teacher, my first re-
action was to say "Oh no"! But as my brother continued to share I felt the spirit

comforting me, and I knew that the words he spoke were the words of my Heavenly
Father.

say.
My husband, Earl, and I have a son and a daughter. Both the children are adults

and are church members. My husband is not a member, but he is often my in-
spiration.

daughter was not home that night, and I wanted to tell them all at the same time.

said that I just lacked the confidence, and I know this has always been one of

me to be,
In July, 1986, at reunion, Elmer was the Director and I was incharge
of registration and whatever else came along. During the commitment service,
Elmer and I were spoken to under the influence of the spirit. This was my
first experience of such an event and I was not sure what to make of it.
I knew I was filled with the Holy spirit and I am not sure what all was said,

(1)

but I knew I had a work to do with Elmer in the Church and refused to
acknowledge anything beyond that. My relationship with Elmer had become
strained and that was upper most on my mind and this new enlightment was
about all I could handle at that time. Others who were present at the
time, said that they believed a call was going to come forth for me. That
was something I was not prepared to accept at that time.
In October, 1986, I received a call from the District President, Cleo
Boswell, informing me that Elmer, who was away on a business trip in
California, was called to the office of Elder and that I was called to
the office of Priest. I was not too surprised to hear of Elmer's call,
but I was shocked to find the floor was still underneath me when he told me
of my call. I had been taught from childhood that priesthood calls were
of God and I needed some time to have God reveal this to me. When I
called Elmer to inform him of the District President's call, his reaction
was about the same as mine. As Mission President in Flagstaff, he had
called other men and a women to priesthood, so he treated this call the
same way and gave me some helpful advice on how to prepare myself in trying
to make my decision as whether or not to accept the call. I had no problem
from the beginning with women being called because I felt that calls were
of God not man, so who are we to decide if it is appropriate for women
to be called if God is doing the calling. To me it was as simple as that.
No other discussion seemed necessary.
I was glad I had the next few days alone in the house because I could
fast and meditate without having to go to a corner of the house to do so.
The television was not turned on for the next three days and I read
scriptures, Patriarchal Blessing, Priesthood Manual and sang hymns. On the
third day, Saturday morning, I rode into the national forest and sat in my
car, because of the rain, and again sang hymns and read scriptures, While
reading my Patriarchal Blessing, given to me by Patriarch Blair McClain
shortly after my marriage to Elmer, it was as though I was reading it for the
first time. The words seem to come out of the page and said to me "do not
be unwilling to accept responsibility, but do the work that you are called
to do." I knew then that there was no doubt that God had been preparing me
for this all my life and different episodes in my past life began appearing
before me that lead me to this point. I had no other choice but to respond
positively to this call and I also needed to enhance my spiritual life by
furthering my education both spiritually and academically. This was
definitely a new enlightenment because after graduating from Business College
in 1979, I had no desire, what so ever, to take another class.

(2)

My call was presented to the Arizona District in October of 1986.
There were some at the conference who wanted a secret ballot, but this was
voted on and turned down. It was stated that a show of hands showed the
candidate that they were willing to support each individual in their call.
There were a few that voted against all women who were presented. One was
a dear friend who had been my acquaintance since first arriving in Arizona.
She came to me after the conference and told me she did not vote for my
call, but it was not anything personal. I gave her a hug and said it was
ok. My oldest son, who sat in the back with those who voted against all
women, got caught up in it and did not vote one way or the other, He
said he was undecided about how he felt. My youngest son did vote for my
call and has given his full support. The rest of my family have not given
an opinion one way or the other and live out of state.
After completing my required temple classes I was ordained in my home
congregation in Flagstaff in January, 1987, by my brother-in-law, Patriarch
Truman Horton who came from Michigan to assist my husband in my ordination.
With him came his wife, Joann and my mother-in-law, Thelma Horton. I was
very pleased to have my mother-in-law there because she has been a,·very
special lady in my life. Although she had voiced disapproval in the past
about women being called, her approval of my call was important although
not necessary to my decision. It was very special to have Truman Horton
perform both ordinations, Elmer's and mine, because he was also the one
who performed our marriage ceremony ten years earlier. I felt the holy
spirit fill the room during both ordinations and the same was voiced by
many others who were present. I knew for certainty that I had been ordained
by God to do his work.
I have now taken several temple classes and have enrolled in college in
hopes of pursuing a degree in counseling. I have taken a psychology class
and a speech class and have signed up this year for three more classes. I
hope I can handle it with my work schedule and priesthood responsibilities.
I have about twelve families I do housekeeping for.
I want to be able to minister to the broken hearted, drug abusers,
elderly, etc. I have been involved in a lot of emotional pain in my life
and I want to bring others who are suffering to the hope of a better life
in following Jesus Christ.

(3)

Being in a small mission, I have had the opportunity to serve in many
capacities of my call, One of my greatest concerns when I accepted the call
was that I felt I needed to prepare for a memorial service and mentioned
this to Truman Horton when he was at my home for the ordination. I received
his advice and counsel. As I suspected, a close friend past away when I was
up next to be in charge of the service for that particular week. He was of
another faith and had a funeral service with his church in another state
where he was to be buried, He attended the RLDS church with his member
spouse on many occasions and had made many friends. I felt good to be part
of that service and felt it went very well. I have given two sermons and
felt good about those also. I reoeived some very special compliments from
those in attendence. One senior lady said, flthats the best sermon I heard
in a long time." I will cherish that compliment for a long time because it
gives me the courage to go on with my responsibilities. I have assisted
many times in S'Qnday service and served communion several times. I have
had the opportunity to be incharge of two prayer services and preside at a
couple of worship services. So far I have had only one person refuse
communion. It has been a blessing to be in a congregation that is accepting
of women priesthood. I believe both the congregation and the women priesthood have been blessed because of this. There is one other woman priesthood
and another woman will soon be ordained.
I know women have served in their own way in the past, but not in the
same way you can as a priesthood member. I for one was not willing to
assist in services prior to my call. With my call, became a feeling that'
I could do this because God called me to do it and I had better be about
his business, realizing there must be a talent in me that I had not taped
into yet. My spiritual life has changed dramatically and I will have to
say, so has by personal life.
I am looking forward to serving in the church in my new role as Priest,
and I am unwrapping talents that have been buried, I feel like Moses, who
did not know he had the ability or was worthy of the task he was given to
fulfill. I know I was called to this office, being a sinful person, but
because of faith and the grace of God, I know He is forever forgiving.
This call erased any doubt I might have had that this is true. I will
continue to serve my Lord and Master, knowing I will stumble and fall along

(4)

the way, but confident that he will pick me up and carry me along the rough
spots. I know I will encounter those who are not approving of my call and
hope I can still minister to their needs and love them just as they are.

PERSONAL TESTIMONY of DORIS JENKINS - Ordained to the Office of Teacher, Nov. 30/86
I was born on a farm near the small country town of Oyen, Alberta, on October 4, 1921. My
father, Ignatius (Nate) Shaw, a gentle, wonderful person whom I remember well, died of
leukaemia on October 1, 1931, and was buried on my 10th birthday. It seemed like the end
of the world for my mother and her seven small girls. I was second to the oldest. There
was no future for us on the farm following this tragedy, so we auctioned everything we could,
and moved to Calgary, where the nearest branch of the church was located. I was baptized
there on November 16, 1934.
During my younger years I didn't always want to serve and sometimes even wished I hadn't
known about the church, so I wouldn't feel guilty. But there was always that still small voice
that wouldn't leave me alone, and finally I was able to realize that God really cared about me

and wanted something from me in return. When I reflect on my call to the priesthood, some-
thing deep inside reminds me that God has been calling me all of my life. At each wrong

turn I took, He was there, calling me back.
As I grew older I came to appreciate and marvel at the stamina, determination and courage,
coupled with a faith that never wavered as my mother selflessly and lovingly disciplined and
cared for her children. Stella (McLean) Shaw was a very special lady, and I know in my heart
that the credit for my active participation in the church today, is largely hers.
On September 4, 1947, I was married to B. Charles Jenkins, whom I have loved for over
forty years. • We have four beautiful grown children and five darling grandchildren. Our
oldest daughter, Wendie Anderson is Chief Nutritionist & Dietitian for Armour Research.
April Ely owns and manages her own business, Life Unlimited Health Center. Hilary Charles
is a Special Agent for the F. B. I. and Rhett James is an architect and builder. Charles has

been a very steadying influence in my life and we have shared many rich experiences to-
gether. He has been a devoted scientist as long as I have known him, working primarily with

the improvement of the exciting and relatively new grain, Triticale. He has served in the
ministry as Priest from December 8, 1947 to August 2, 1953 when he was called to the office
of Elder. On May 22, 1962 he was ordained by Roy Cheville to the office of High Priest,
where he serves to the present time. Surely the richness he has brought to my life has much
to do with my spiritual growth and my clearly perceived need to share the joy, the hope, the
dream of Zion, with those whose lives touch mine.
When Revelation 156 was presented, my heart sang, as the doors to the future flung wide!
I envisioned talented, dedicated young women in our church taking their place beside those
who had served so long unaccompanied. What wonderful new possibilities sharing together
would offer! I never in my wildest dreams thought of one as old as me participating in this

way, and I was unable to respond for quite some time. It was on my mind and in my prayers
constantly and finally I knew I must accept. How could I refuse my God, who had given me
my life twice?* I made a solemn new committment and it has been difficult for me in some
respects. I am deeply grieved over the loss of some of our branch members who were close
to me and have distanced themselves because they interpret God's plan for His Kingdom
differently from those who accept 156. They were present at the Monterey Branch Business
Meeting on January 29, 1986 when my call to the office of Teacher was presented and accepted
by a majority; and not only voted negatively, but spoke negatively to the motion. And again
at the Central California District Business Meeting at Happy Valley Conference Grounds,
July 13, 1986 where my call was again approved, two of my very old friends came once more
to voice their disapproval. This elder and his wife have since set up services in their own
home where other dissenting members meet separately, and do not participate in the Monterey
Branch, a grievious loss!
Joseph III proclaimed the inherent worth of all persons, and I have loved and admired him for
his deep insights ever since I became acquainted with him through his writings, and history's
portrait of him. And Wallace B. --I know him personally through numerous contacts, and my
love and respect for him is boundless. At a High Priests and Spouses Retreat at Happy Valley
September 26-28, 1986, he shared his personal testimony on his struggle with D&C Sec. 156
and I deeply appreciate his courage and integrity and am grateful to God for the strength and
power extended to him in this special time of need,
The theme on November 30, 1986, the day of my Ordination Service was "Joy: Shepherds
Faith Made Manifest." The beautiful' music at this service ministers to me to this day and
always will. The Hymn #106 "How Shall We Come Before You Now" by Geoff Spencer, opened
the service. A solo, #362 "A Diligent and Grateful Heart," by Raymond Gunn, was sung by
Susan Cline just previous to District President John E. Wiley's statement to the ordinand (me)
and the congregation. His remarks were tied in with the first Sunday of Advent and the coming
celebration of the birth of Christ, a new adventure; and the new adventure that my new
responsibilities will bring--a very sobering and thoughtful and challenging discourse. #409
"Touch Me Lord With Thy Spirit Eternal" voiced the prayer that was on my heart as Susan
and David Cline sang this stirring hymn. The prayer of Ordination was ?ffered by John
Wiley, assisted by my husband, Charles Jenkins and pastor David Cline'. I felt joy, humility
and a little sadness--a tear or two--so much to do, and so little time to do it, I knew my
course

• See enclosed copy of "PERSONAL TESTIMONY" recorded January 30, 1984.
See also copy of "AN ENCOUNTER WITH GOD" recorded in February, 1970.

was set, and there was no turning back! The service was concluded with the rousing Hymn
#398 "Let us give Praise to the God of Creation" by Geoff Spencer, one of my favorite authors
in "Hymns of the Saints." I see and hear the Lord Jesus in Apostle Spencer, and am grateful
for the profound influence bis ministry has had on my life.
In the months that have elapsed since my ordination I am continually aware that God is
stretching my soul, raising my consciousness and expectations, and rousing me from
complacency into a deep and careful search for what I really believe, where my loyalties
lie,
The extra study involved in my new committment has been a wonderful discipline, which I had
neglected, and has opened up so many new avenues of thought. I am delighted with all the

exciting material our church is offering, especially the Temple School Classes and the out-
standing articles in the Herald. The increased knowledge that comes with increased study

gives me a sense of freedom not experienced before. I hope I am becoming n'lore open to the
workings of God in my life. Certainly, I am becoming more involved as I listen to the call to
be more obedient, more loving and more compassionate. The knowledge that God loves me
motivates me to be God's person for others, for His church, His world. I sense a need to
nurture· those in our congregation and community who do not share the hope I have, and to
assist in bearing their burdens.
In the first sermon (March 29, 1987) I was asked to give, I made it clear that I felt I was not
called to preach. I feel that I am called to spend what time I have left working "in those basic,
stabilizing, reinforcing, nurturing rrunistries that provide the undergirding health of the
congregation." (words from a sermon by Geoff Spencer). We can work our fingers to the bone
preparing beautiful services and wonderful sermons--we need these, but we also need someone
to watch over those who are too lonely or too distressed to even come to hear a sermon. As
I experience God's nearness I experience inner peace, but I also experience an obligation to be
God's hands--an agent of God's power and love. And I know that this is a call to-effortful
caring, to a life of service, and a life of sacrifice, indeed a call to be a parent to the human
race.
When Charles and I visited our daughter, Wendie and family in Phoeniyz, early in April
of this year, I was prompted to call on an old friend from the days when we lived in Canada,
now living in nearby Sun City. I was aware that he was in very deep trouble, the details of
which I did not know, nor do I yet know. But my heart went out to him when I learned that he
was too distressed even to attend church. I spent several hours just listening to him pour
out his anguish, and I sensed the healing and peace that came to him from God, as I put my
arms around him when we parted. I felt that God used me to listen, and I need to remember
this experience when I see others very alone and withdrawn. It is a good discipline for me

to be still and listen. I learned that nothing is sometimes a good thing to do, and always a
clever thing to say.
I was asked by our District Women's Leader, Arla Martindale, to give a class on "Health"
at Women's Retreat on May 2, 1987. I was somewhat hesitant knowing that many learned women
in this field would be in attendance, and then I realized I could share my own personal
pilgrimage in search of health. I went back to my early childhood and recounted the events
in my life and also an experience I had in 1970 which without a doubt took me along a path
that led me, without conscious choice on my own part, into studying and experimenting
with nutrition.
I knew I had lost something very precious--my health, and that somewhere out there, I could
find it if I was disciplined and patient enough, because of God's promises. My life at this
point became a quest--a treasure hunt! and the first clue led me into a study of nutrition. I
had a number of questions, and my doctor could not answer them for me. For fifteen years
I pursued each clue I came across, and as one clue led to another, I FOUND THE TREASURE!
My problem is sharing it with the people I am closest to--my church family. A number of
years ago when Dick Mumper was Church School Director in our branch, I was asked to teach
a series of classes on nutrition, which I did, but I felt that my efforts were being received
more as entertainment by the listeners, than anything else. I guess I experienced a bit of
rejection, and I admit I am still a little nervous to share some of things I have discovered
because they are so simple, so straightforward, they are almost unbelievable. At this point
in my life I enjoy a wonderful sense of well-being and good health, and I am humbly grateful
to God for this.
In summing up, I am very deeply awed by the trust God has placed in me, in His call to
service. Our church offers the opportunity to women who want to make a difference in the
world, offers the right and the responsibility to serve in an arena where their talents and
abilities can have full scope. I am indeed grateful for this. I feel that the HOLY SPIRIT is
leading women to a new intensity of participation in church life on all levels. It is a special
delight to me to see so many women responding affirmatively. When the pain. precipitated
by D&C Section 156 subsides, we will experience a peace, a strength, a tremenduous inner
growth which is always "the beautiful child" born of struggle. I believe we will know joy
greater than anything experienced before in the history of our church.
I feel that the church is really mine! I am happy and eager to serve with God's priesthood
in a church where there is no status, no class structure, where there are believers united
in faith and hope and love for one another.

Doris (Shaw) Jenkins
418A Cayuga Street
Salinas, CA 93901 (408) 422-2486

World Church (General Church, in those days) provided outstanding ministry

from Eastern Canada. In 1948 Youth Camp was converted to a full-scale
Reunion Camp. Youth camps were later re-introduced on church owned property,
Hills of Peace, after I married and went to California.
I have served as Historian for Monterey Branch for the years 1967, 1968 and
1969, and was again appointed Historian in 1980 and have served in this office
to the present time.
I have been branch Recorder for the years 1985 to present.
I have been branch Secretary for the years 1984 to present.
On July 23, 1987 I was appointed Branch President of Monterey Branch, to the
end of this year, by District President, John E. Wiley. Our elected Pastor,
David Cline, left the branch to take up World Church Appointment, July 22,
1987.

Doris (Shaw) Jenkins

PERSONAL TESTIMONY Recorded Jan. 30, 1984
One day late in August, 1927 I was gathering eggs on the chicken roost. My mother had
filled a heavy iron pot with stones and water so that young chicks could drink without
danger of falling in and drowning. As I stepped off the roost, holding eggs in my apron,
I slipped and fell into the iron pot. The eggs were broken and my ankle was severely
sprained. By evening it was so swollen I could not put weight on it. My grandfather,
Charles McLean, was called to administer to me.
For a week my mother tried everything to reduce the swelling, but it continued to get worse
until it was swollen from ankle to knee, at which point I was taken to the Oyen hospital,
owned by Dr. Swartzlander, an inept doctor who employed a young doctor named Barney
MacPhai_l, who was out of town at the time. Dr. Swartzlander lanced my ankle to relieve
the pres.sure caused by infection, but it was Dr. MacPhail who, upon his return, diagnosed
the prol\]em as acute osteomyelitis of the tibia, and operated immediately. In a second
surgery;the infected bone was scraped and maggots placed in the wound to devour any decayed
matter remaining, but the bone was not salvageable and was later removed from ankle to
knee. The severed bone was placed in a solution of alcohol and sent to the University Hospital
in Edmonton, Alberta for examination.
I well remember sitting for several hours a day in the hospital lobby with my leg soaking in
a pail of some sterile solution. I was the only child in that small country hospital, and the
lobby was a much more interesting place than my room. I could observe all the hospital
activity .and talk to everyone as they came and went. I received a great deal of love and
attention from the staff and other patients, and I can't recall ever feeling unhappy or alone.
Christm,as was the highlight of my five months' stay in the hospital. Early that morning I
was carried from my room to the lobby in time to see Santa's black boot just as the door
closed behind him. I was so excited I couldn't speak--and the presents he left--there was
everything a small child could wish for, besides gifts from the entire staff. To this day I
have a book "Chuckles" from Miss McPhee, the head nurse, and a teddy bear "Beady" from
Miss Jodewine. Both are priceless treasures!
Sometime after the fourth surgery, a new bone miraculously replaced the diseased bone,
but didn't grow at the same rate as the outer fibula, so my ankle grew curved and when I
was able to walk with crutches, I walked on the outside of my foot, making the curvature
worse. When I was full grown my right leg was 2 1/2 inches shorter than my left, and I
wore laced boots, one with a 2 1/2 inch sole, and a brace on my leg from knee to ankle.
The brace was two metal bars fastened together with leather straps, and fitted into holes
drilled in the heel of my boot.
In my 23rd year I went to an orthopaedic specialist in Calgary, Alberta--Dr. Gordon
Townsend, to see if there wasn't something to be done about the ugly boots and brace. He
advised amputation. I was horrified, and cried myself to sleep that night, but somehow
got up my courage to go ahead with his suggestion, and I have never looked back. He very
skilfully removed the kneecap and fastened it to the end of the femur bone, saving skin
from the front of my lower leg to cover the kneecap and leaving a neat scar at the back of
the stump. Forty years have gone by and I have never had to have any repair work or
subsequent surgery on this leg. Dr. Townsend was a craftsman in his field.
I should mention that while I was in the Oyen hospital osteomyelitis, which is carried in
the bloodstream, broke out in my right arm just above the elbow, but was discovered
immediately by Dr. MacPhail. The affected bone was scraped away and the arm healed.
Two years later (age 8) when everything was seemingly under control, my left forearm
became swollen and I was taken to the Junior Red Cross Children's hospital in Calgary
and attended by a bone specialist, Dr. Deane. Dr. MacPhail had left Oyen and taken up private
practice in Kindersley, Saskatchewan. My left forearm is crooked as a result of one bone
being affected and not the other.

At eleven years of age, I was again stricken with the disease, This time in my left shoulder
and I was to be sent back to Dr. Deane in Calgary. Dr. Swartzlander had sold his little
hospital soon after Dr. MacPhail left and the new doctor, whose name I don't recall, is the
one who had recommended Dr. Deane three years earlier. The night before I was to leave,
my grandfather again laid his hands upon my head after annointing me with oil, and prayed
in the name of Jesus Christ for a healing. I was very young to be sure, but can vividly
remember the wonderful feeling of peace and calm that came over me as he prayed.
I did not realize the full impact of the wonderful blessing I received at that time, but it was
brought rather sharply into focus when by chance in 1954 when we were living in Saskatoon
a news item concerning the Saskatchewan Rough-Riders mentioned that Dr. Barney MacPhail
had trea;ted one of the player's crushed kneecap. The name, Barney MacPhail was instantly
familiar and unusual enough that I just couldn't rest until I found out for sure if he was the
young doctor who had operated on my leg when I was almost six years old.
I phoned his office and asked if I might have an appointment just to talk to the doctor, as an
old friend. I did not remember him to recognize him and of course he couldn't possibly
recognize me, so he looked at my quizzically when I was shown into his office, I asked him
if he remembered a little girl named Doris Shaw, whom he treated for osteomyelitis in the
Oyen hospital. He said he did, indeed, and when I said "I am that little girl" he said
"Absolutely impossible!" It was when I asked him why that he told me that osteomyelitis
was not curable that many years ago, and that anyone who had it had recurrence after
recurrence until it got into a vital bone such as the scull or back and death was the result.
I said "Suppose I tell you that I remember that Dr. MacPhail came often to the hospital on
a Saturday morning--his day off, and took me riding on a sled drawn by his big German
shepherd dog." He remembered too! and shaking his head in disbelief said "It is nothing
short of a miracle!" I told him I had been healed, and he said "I'll have to accept that--
there simply isn't any other answer." There were tears in his eyes when he told me why
he took me out Saturdays to enjoy the outdoors--the snow. He knew I wasn't going to live
very long. This was a very sobering moment for me. I knew full well from that instant
on that my life was not my own.
Sometimes it was difficult for me to understand why God spared my.life, and what He
expected of me in return. I have often experienced guilt when I failed to measure up to
what I imagined He expected, and this has been hurtful. As I grew older I was able to
lay my guilt aside and have experienced a deep sense of being loved and forgiven. God
has blessed me richly in other special ways--through my beloved Charles and four
beautiful children. I will praise His Name as long as I live.

- Doris (Shaw) Jenkins

AN ENCOUNTER WITH GOD

On Monday evening, February 9, 1970, I was admitted to the hospital to have

minor surgery, I hoped, and because I was somewhat apprehensive I asked for admin-
istration, My fears proved well grounded because when I recovered from the anaesthetic

I knew that a radical mastectomy, not minor surgery, had been necessary, For a night
and a day I was too ill to care about anything, but on Thursday evening the realization
of my utter helplessness and wretchedness crept slowly into my consciousness and I knew
despair as I have never experienced it before. Those who are close to me know that in
my early life I won a battle against osteomyelitis that cost me much surgery in both arms
and the loss of one leg, but surely God had deserted me now because this in addition was
just too much to bear. I drifted into total oblivion after a merciful injection of medication
that evening, but was awakened by my own sobbing sometime during the night; and the
awareness of the futility of life was heavy on my soul and my heart cried out to God in
anguish because my body was so hurt and broken. Then out of the blackness, in clear
and gentle tones, an audible voice said to me: "Of what consequence is your body? It is
your spirit that matters: and a promise is given to you that your spirit will never be
broken, and you will have power to comfort bruised and broken spirits." The tears
flowed down my face in sweet release and a wondrous joy filled my being. I do not
believe in God any more- -I know Him! and I realize that if I had been granted the blessing
I asked from administration I would not have heard His voice. I cannot feel even a 1.ittle
sad that my body is not whole, and because of this encounter with God, I know the joy that
was in David's heart when he wrote the 23rd Psalm. I have walked through the valley of
the shadow of death into green pastures beside still waters. My cup is full and overflowing
and because I have walked in green pastures I am now able to walk in pastures not so green
where my calling is to lift others from despair.

Doris I. Jenkins

I am a convert to this church. I joined the church in 1960 while still a
college student at Central Michigan University, in Mt. Pleasant, Michigan. I
joined the church not believing in the authority of the priesthood. My husband
baptised me, he was a priest at the time. He told me my confirmation prayer would
be straight from God to me. I laughted at such a preposterious idea. I did not
believe God used priesthood in such a manner.
When the two elders placed their hands on my head to say the confirmation
prayer, I heard 4-5 words of the first sentence, Then the elder said those exact
words and completed the sentence. Again I heard 4-5 words and again the elder said
those exact words and completed the sentence. This continued through out the entire
prayer. After the experience I was confused, I did not know what had happened. I
assumed one elder had told the other elder what to say. My spiritual growth wasn't
such that I could discern what had happened, One year later as I beca~e more and
more troubled by what had happened I went to the assisting elder and told him what
I had heard and asked him what had happened. He said, "God works in mysteries ways
Barb". At that moment the holy spirit flooded my understanding, and with tears in
my eyes I stood in awe of God's greatness, and the authority of His priesthood.
Because of this experience, I was a worker in the church. My husband served
as a priest. Our children were born, and blessed and the church was a very large
part of our lives, Our family life was centered around the church, and we were
content and happy, We were growing as a family, and we were growing spiritually.
During these years we lived in several different locations. My husband took graduate
studies in several different universities in different states. After completing
most of his graduate work he took a job at Ferris State College, in Big Rapids,
Michigan teaching physics.
We took on any and all the jobs that needed to be done in the small branch of
our new college town. NO was not a word in our vocabulary. We were beginning to
confuse uchurch work" with lfGodts work". Fortunatly, a year later, another church

-2-

family moved into our branch. They too were educated but somewhat more mature in
their church envolment, They were both 10-15 years older then my husband and me,
and had learned more copeing skills. They were just as dedicated to God---but they
taught us that it was not good for us, our family, or the church to burn ourselves
out on every job that needed to be done. They taught us that iR a little branch
of 20-30 active members everyone is needed, and some jobs may have to go undone.
This lesson saved our family and our marriage. We were still very active and
completely commited to God and the church, but with more realistic objectives.
In 1970 I started graduate school and took a class called Sexism in America.
The first day in the class I told the professor and my fellow classmates that no one
discriminated against me, By the end of the class my conciousness level was raised
and I was MAD. I was informed and verbal. Some people even said later to me that
they thought I was abrasive, They didn't tell me this until some years later, when
they no longer felt that way!
This was a very difficult time in my life, But God was there. I had to know
if God discriminated against women or if only my church discriminated, I could
accept the discrimination from the church, but not from God, If God did discriminate
then I had no God to worship!!! This was very serious business---and I had to know.
I prayed constantly for the answer, I did consider withdrawing from any organization
that discriminated against me. When I told our pastor this, he responded with "I'm
considering that too Barb". When he said that, I knew what a tremendous responsibility
I had. God was working in my life, and God was working in the church. I agonized
over the whole thing, I had to know if God discriminated against women! I continued
to pray for an answer.
One Saturday night I stayed up very late preparing my children's Sunday school
class material for the next morning. I went to bed and neglected to put my hair
up on rollers. In 1972 that's what women did. The next morning when I got up, I

-3-

went into the bathroom and looked into the mirror, My hair was a mess. In my
thinking I began to scold myself for my poor stewardship. And as I was doing this,
and looking into the mirror a thought came to me that was not my own. The thought
wa;§; "You' re going to be asked to go up front today''.. I responded with "No way Lord!"
The thought came again with such force it could not be denied.
After teaching my Sunday school class, I went into the sanctury and sat
down in a pew towards the back of the church. A few minutes later our pastor came
up behind me, put his hand on my shoulder, and asked me to go up front with him
that morning, Because of my earlier experience I got up and followed him. This
was unthinkable in 1972. When I got upfront I was to read a scripture. I looked
down at the words and didn't know if I could read, I had earned a Master's degree
in Family Life, but was so conditioned by my culture, and my church I wondered if
I could read from "holy ground" at 11 a. m.
After that experience I knew God did not discriminate against women, but my
church, and my culture, and my society certainly did. I lived with an emotional
rage inside me. I spoke out in support of women where ever I was, so many of my
sisters were enemys to there own sex. I carried this rage until 1979. By then I
was too tired to hold on to it any longer. I turned it over to God, and felt it
just melt away, I still spoke up in behalf of women, I still was assertive, but I
no longer carried the burden.
I have wondered over the years why God gave me that experience to know I'd
be asked to go up front in church that Sunday morning. I believe God gave me the
experience for three reasons:
1, To save my soul,
2. To make me supportive of women and the ministry they can give.
3. To prepare me to go up front, (I would have said no, if asked,
without that experience, I would have wanted and expected
my sisters to go up front.)

I have always needed the ministry my sisters have to give to me. I have
wanted that same rich ministry for my husband and family. The only place I received
that ministry was a:Womem's Retreats, My husband and children did not go to women's

-4-

retreats. They missed that ministry! Perhaps I was selfish, because I always
looked at what they were missing. I never looked at myself to give priesthood
ministry, nor did I expect to be called to the priesthood.
I have been assured siuce 1972 that God does not discriminate, and I have
looked forward to when my sisters would be called. I never expected it to be in
my lifetime. I rejoyce that God has let me see this change take place.
Today my best friend (female) is pastor of our congregation. She is a
wonderful administator. She does administration work for a college for a living.
I serve as a councilor to our District President. Because I am not teaching at the
present time, I have time to listen to the people of our district.
I have found my Masters Degree in Family Life very helpful in the ministry
I have to share with others. I have been called into the lives of many people since
I was ordained August 1986. I feel the awesome responsibility, because of my early
experience in 1960 teaching me the authority of this priesthood. I have seen lives
changed, as the holy spirit works in me and through me.
My husband and I are both elders, and often work as a team, involved in
bringing Christ into the lives of all people.
We have done our first wedding together, even the preparation was a celebration.
Our material was done in a dialogue form.
Next summer we will travel with Reed Holmes on his tour of Isreal and Egyup.
We are excited about our life---our church---and our God, and we pray daily for
those suffering the emotional turmoil and rage I have experienced in the past. We
have faith that God can see them through their difficult times, just as God saw me
through my troubled years. God fills our every need if we are open to his spirit.

Please do not use the following material for any publication while my Mother-in-law

is still living, it could cause her pain.
Our immediate family is open and supportive to Section 156. My husband's two
brothers are not supportive, they have not left the church but they both attend a
different church then they had been attending. My Mother-in-law struggles with a
critical nature toward women priesthood members. And yet she is seeking me out for
my acceptance of her. We have had a good relationship for nearly 30 years, and a
short time after my ordination we experienced two difficult times. My husband and I
administrated to her at our Reunion this summer, (I did the prayer), and we all
feel a special healing and forgiveness took place in each of our lives, and our
relationship with each other. She and her husband have transferred to our branch,
50 miles away from their home. We await God and the holy spirit, to continue to
work in our lives~
