1.        I was ordained to the office of priest in Maquoketa, Iowa by Ken Renyolds, assisted by Robert Taylor and Roger Reid. My ordination was approved by Waterloo-Clinton district (Iowa) in about (?) December, 1986. My ordination was February 8, 1987.
2.        My husband is a non-member, and attends no religious functions. I respect his choice, and try never to push.
3.        1973 was a turning point in my life. Before that time, church was not an important fact in my life.
I enjoy and am involved with horses. In this area all trail rides were and are held on Sundays. So, l had decided to put God/Church “on a shelf” while I broke a colt. I refused three of four times when asked to teach Sunday School. I felt church was fine, IF it was convenient, but I wanted absolutely no commitments. Nothing to take my horse time. After my last refusal to teach, l was preparing for a final/morning worship, it was never delivered. On a Thursday I took a break from worship preparation studies to work with my colt. I can’t remember how, but my colt threw me, I lay for some time unconscious, I’m not sure how long. It was as if someone was turning my awareness off and on, like a light switch. My husband was running an electric saw, I woke up and shouted at him for help several times before falling back into the darkness. He couldn’t hear me over the noisy saw at first, but eventually he heard me calling and noticed, the colt with an empty saddle. He told me, later, he came to help me up and helped me walk the acre or so back to the truck. (I can only remember bits and pieces)
I sat upright in the front seat of the truck and he drove me 12 miles to the hospital emergency room. X-rays revealed a fractured vertebra! Two or three times a nurse came in to make sure the feelings were still in my legs and feet. Dr. Rask told me to lay completely flat or else it would sever the spinal cord! How on earth could I have walked up to the truck, climbed into it, and rode sitting up 12 jolting miles, then to find it dangerous, just to roll over, sounds far-fetched.
I spent 6 weeks in a brace, and have fully recovered, thank God. The realization that God is still in control and is with me, even when I wasn’t listening, hit me like a ton of bricks. This was repeated to me in my Patriarchal Blessing.
I haven't missed a Sunday since, for reasons of spending time with horses. I taught Sunday School, the very next Sunday, and still teach.
Mv horse story has a happy ending, too. I had to sell Joker, my colt, he was uncontro1able. I wanted a Quarter Horse in the worst way, but only had enough money to buy a cheaper anima1, $500. (at the time what I wanted was going for $1,000 - $1,500) By way of a flat tire, I stumbled on to a Quarter Horse, that was for sale for only $500. Exactly what I wanted. The owners were even offered more money, for the horse, but chose me to buy and own their animal. This horse is my best friend, he was a gift.
After I had become more into the church, I was spoken to, from the pulpit. A visiting priest stopped in the middle of the sermon, he looked me squarely in the face, an told me using my first name, “The Lord is pleased with you!”
I was shocked and puzzled. How could this be? It sounded like a priesthood call, or more so an awareness of it. This was before women were called to priesthood. (74-75) I’ve thought it over many times, and disregarded it, until 1984. I think I was told about 10 years ago, to prepare. 
The last call that led me to the service of the Lord was a funeral. My Father-in-law died the day after Thanksgiving, 1980. As I said before, my spouse has no desire for religion, neither does his family. They were trying to find a minister to speak at the funeral, out of the yellow pages. I asked to please choose a minister from the R.L.D.S. church, if they have no preference. The funeral was about 100 miles away from my home branch. The small world that it is, when the priest came to my house, I found I had babysat his grandchildren years ago. 
We all sat down and began to discuss what should be said at the funeral. Priest Symonds asked if there was a favorite scripture anyone wanted him to use. I spoke up, and asked for Matt 10:26-27. “Are not two sparrows sold for a farthing. And one of them shall not fall on the ground without our father knoweth it…Ye are of more value than many sparrows.” When he read the scripture my Mother-in-law broke into tears, so did the rest of the family. Everyone except my husband, stared at me in wonder. I was totally baffled, and questioned what I said, and why this scripture was a trigger to bring a flood of tears. It took the family a while to explain it, to me. My Mother-in-Law woke up the day of her husband’s death, and went out on the back porch. Laying on the porch was a dead sparrow! She said she knew the minute she saw it that “something was going to happen, something bad.” I had no knowledge of it, but it came from the heart. That His message was comfort. It was a forerunner of my calling.
4.        My ordination service was a time for rejoicing, as we in Maquoketa have had no calls or baptisms for over 10 years. There were besides myself three other women and one man, ordained, to various offices. I was ordered by Ken Renyolds. He gave a short sermon also, also. He is an excellent and gifted speaker. It was uplifting. In my ordination, he asked for a blessing for me, in my weakest areas—courage, and to recognize the gifts God gave me. I feel I’m not best at public speaking.
5.        I felt that ordination of women was coming but never dreamt it would be so soon. I felt it was unfair, before section 156, of my creator, to make me female, I felt if only could have been male life would have been easier and maybe I could serve. Of course, I jumped ahead of the plan and now I see women in the Priesthood is completing the priesthood. Like a person with one hand can live and function almost normally, but with both hands we can get a whole lot more accomplished. Women can (my opinion) go someplace that men can’t, and can reach people that are unattainable to men alone.
A view that should not have been, but was, tells women to “keep silent” in meeting places, it’s changing. Worldwide women are emerging. They are being recognized as people with brains, too! Women’s work and ideas are becoming more valuable, and the world is recognizing they can be leaders, and are needed.
It is a shame to look at the past, and see the talents and abilities that were unused because they were given to a female. Section 24 to Emma Smith, is thrilling to read to me.
6.        My ordination was Feb. 8, 1987. In June, only five months later my cousin asked me to officiate at his wedding. I was scared because it was to be a large formal wedding, and at first, or so I thought, for me. I had another couple unexpectedly ask me to perform a wedding quietly, and quickly, just a few weeks before my cousin’s. (I was a second marriage for both.) That gave me experience in counseling and helped me figure out the paperwork. Everything came out fine. 
The real shocker was just before his wedding when I had the last counsel meeting with him and his bride, he poured out his heart. He expressed to me his hurts and angers of the wrong doings that the people around have done. The confession jolted me for I certainly was not expecting it. I know he felt relieved sharing with someone. I feel I was sent, and as hard as it was, just to listen, I believe I was given strength to stay and hear him out. I also believe this was the reason, (the confession) that I was chosen to officiate at the wedding.
I feel that one of my gifts is the talent of listening, and counseling one on one as I am a good person to confess to. I have had other confessions stemming from drug abuse, and other unfair problems. It touches me if I can help others, figure out their problems. It’s the one “natural High,” I love the most. 
Another experience is that since my ordination “things” fall into place. Generally speaking, I’ve been succeeding in getting some of the things I’ve always wanted. I don’t think of them as rewards, but stumbling blocks are melting away. As the phrase “all the things work together for good to them that praise the Lord.” I believe it. 
I’ve been chosen to find money and valuables. On my horse-back rides, I ride on trails where folks come from all over to rent and ride horses, the last three times in a row, I’ve found money.
The first find was a billfold full of important papers and $159. I traced down the owner and returned it. The next time it was cash, not much, but I did try to return it, unsuccessfully. I couldn’t find the person, so I like to share my finds in the collection plate, at church. The last find was another billfold. I opened it to make positive identification. (some of the trail riders are not the honest kind). I tracked the man down and was able to return it. Again, there was a good deal of cash involved here, and other valuables. The man was grateful, and insisted on giving me a reward, then apologized because he couldn’t afford more!
I think I was lucky and chosen to find these valuables because I try to return everything others lose. And all belongs to God, He wants us to be honest. I thank God for choosing me. It feels so good to help others.
7.        Very mixed are the feelings of others around me. A man I’ve known for many years who holds the same office as myself, priest, was negative. For many years, he didn’t even attend church, I saw him drinking, too. He was unactive. I’m not sure why, but after my calling he became active once more. I think it was hard for him to accept women into the priesthood. He was a special person, to me because he baptized me. I am pleased to see him active again, but hope it’s an urge to serve, not a jealous drive.
Others around me are negative, my Grandmother, was upset by my ordination. A friend of my Grandmothers, who wasn’t long for this world, helped explain to her how wonderful it is that women’s’ roles are changing in religion. The friend helped her to accept my ordination, somewhat. She still feels that men should be the leaders of the church. My Grandmother is a Baptist. She doesn’t care much for my church, but loves me.
I find that older people (50s & 60s) are having more trouble accepting women, than are the people 40 and under. The people I found who accept it the easiest are the people under 25, and me, in my experiences are more accepting. I have some problems, because of the title, Priest. Some persons confuse the duties of our priesthoods’ Priest, with those of the Catholics’ title of priest. This causes a lot of questions, and at times opens a can of worms, being a priest and a woman. I try to use these opportunities to my advantage, but old habits and beliefs are hard to break. Many are confused, but at least they are thinking so the traditional dogma, may be challenged. 
I spent a day last summer in Nauvoo, Ill. I went to see “The City of Joseph,” the play. I enjoyed it, and spent some time questioning some of the Mormon Ladies who work in Nauvoo. That is where I found the most opposition of women’s changing roles. The Mormons are dead set against it. I felt that they see it as a threat. I also visited with some of the tour guides who work in Nauvoo, that are members of the R.L.D.S. Church. They are, at times, being harassed by the Mormon Priesthood members. This was more directed to the young women than the young men. I feel a change in the way we are treating each other as churches. As for myself, I’m trying to prepare for the challenges and the questions between the churches as far as women’s relationship with God.
On the positive side, I find the devoted members of my church branch could not be more supportive. They all have been wonderful. I haven’t heard one negative word toward me as a person or a woman. The pastor has been great help, and it doing a tremendous job overall.
My own family is mediocre. My husband doesn’t care to be involved at all. He does, however, support me with help in physical needs, transportation, helping with chores, etc., which I was surprised at. My daughter was baptized but follows in his footsteps as far as religion. It is the only thing she has in common with him, they don’t get along, with each other at all. I am asked not mention that I am a Priest, when I’m in public with either one of them. I don’t UNLESS asked, because I don’t want to “embarrass” my family. I don’t like to push, it’s not my style, or the church’s. I do talk about being a priest, if anyone asks or the opportunity presents itself, However, I feel talking about Jesus/God is more meaningful.
My second family is a bit more supportive. Mom & Dad are proud, and urged me to accept the call. So proud, I have to remind myself and them sometimes, it is not a position to have pride in, but to serve in humility. I have to caution myself when I’m around them not to let it go to my head.
My brothers and sister are so-so. They think that church isn’t important to them, all baptized, none active. So, they compromise, and say “Gee, that’s nice,” again I don’t push, but I won’t turn away, either.
I do question, how it would be if only more of my family would be active and supportive. I think I am more tempted and have to drive myself harder than others whos’ families are attending and are together. I am very alone, but I stubbornly cling to my faith. I have to be strong, maybe that keeps me going.
Outside of the church, I’ve had more negative response, even poking fun, than positive. But this is new to many people, so I hope I will learn and responses will improve, with time. This is new to me, too, perhaps male priesthood members have equally had the struggle. I question this. My favorite verse that helps in negative time, is “to be in the world—not of it.”
8.        Ministry is working for God, to serve in whatever method I can. To not be above the lowest person, nor to be beneath the greatest. I think kindness, and compassion are essential. Clean living shows a good example, to practice what you preach. We have to have a feeling of well-being and joy, to draw other to share in God’s love. To also accept persons of all religious beliefs, and respect their choices. Above all to love Jesus Christ, and do His will.
My office, Priest, cane as an astonishment to me, I thought I’d be a Teacher (SOMEDAY). As I am poor at spelling, and feel it reflects in public speaking. I’m shy and have a lot of stage fright. I have only given four sermons, and two weddings so far, much to my delight I’ve done “O.K.,” I’m sure I’ve had some divine inspiration or could not have done it.
Our branch in Maquoketa, was in great need for priesthood members. Off and on for the past 10 to 14 years we’ve only had one active Priesthood member, and he lived 40 miles away. The best part of my office is getting in touch with people, all people. 
Truly the Lord has chosen the right people for the right job.
9.        A good deal as changed for me during the class “Introduction to the Scriptures,” taught by Bob Taylor. The program was excellent, and gave me new understanding of humankinds’ strive to reach God. I came to see people from all over wiew, and lifted some of the veils where we tend to keep God enclosed. 
Another high point for me was a youth trip (to Nauvoo) around six years ago. I met the couple in charge, for the first time, Roger & Barb Reid. They lived about 65 miles away from me, in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. I was impressed by the way they were tremendous spiritual leaders. They were very caring, about the young people, and were in love the each other. I was sad to see the weekend draw to an end, because I knew I would mis these beautiful people, and had a longing to know Roger & Barb, better.
I was even jealous, our only Priesthood lived so far away, how we needed a leader in Maquoketa, like Roger. So, I made it a point in prayer, asking for a family life Roger’s, and praying for Priesthood leadership to come and live in Maquoketa.
A year or two later, my prayers were answered. We got a family like Roger’s, exactly like Roger’s. His job was transferred from Cedar Rapids to Maquoketa!! I was overjoyed, still am. To have a prayer answered, so exactly is realizing God’s awareness, and His concern for me.
10.        I feel growth has been a steady upward climb. If I allow it. I know also, I have had many struggles and heavy burdens I’ve brought to myself, so I know when God’s concern is for me. I realize Priesthood members are human, too. This world is misleading, tempting place, that constant prayer is the only way I can make it, through.

ORAL HISTORY GIFT

OF
LOIS G. KAZYAKA

August 30, 1987
My name is Lois Gayle (Richards) Kazyaka. I was baptized at
the age of thirteen (13) in the Everett, Washington.
I have always felt close to God even before coming to the
R.L.D.S. Church - even when a child I always felt God was calling
me.
When I joined the church in 1956 I was upset that priesthood
was just for men - for I had been attending another church where
women were also ministers and were allowed to participate in
worship services besides playing a musical instrument or singing.
They were allowed leadership roles. I joined the R.L.D.S. Church
with all of my being.
I married John P. Kazyaka In 1963, he was a non-member. We
were married on a Friday evening - on Saturday afternoon he was
baptized - Sunday he was confirmed and on Monday, he was spoken
to under the influence of the Holy Spirit. We were both in the
Navy, stationed at Betheda, Maryland.
John and I joined hands in serving God - God has always been
first in our lives. We have three grown children: William Benson,
Carrie Lynn Beitz and Joseph Theodore.
When Carrie was small I started to question once again why I
was not al lowed to function as a minister in the Church, but as I
looked at our children, I was concerned as to what to tell our
daugther. I finally came to the decision that this was a man-made

policy and some day it would change. I trusted God to use me when
and where He wanted. I would be ready and willing to follow.
In November, just before the time of my ordination, John

was ordained an elder, serving also as a deacon and priest. How-
ever, that summer prior Katy Gardner, my friend, asked me what

would I do if I was told that there was a call to priesthood. I
told her I would pray, fast and need a confirming testimony -
Katy left, went home to Colorado, and I started to pray about
priesthood once again. Later that year, during the last worship
service of the Junior High Camp I was directing, I was told by
a staff member through inspiration of the Holy Spirit to continue
to walk in faith and not be afraid of the changes taking place in
my life. It was then, I knew there was a calling.
The night before our pastor came to inform me of the call
John and I were talking and I was telling him what office I was
best suited for - that of priest or teacher. The following night
Ron, our pastor, inform me of the calling to priest. My heart
swelled with joy, I realized that from the very beginning, God
had prepared a way for me.
It was scary - my name was the first women in the Clairemont
7
BranchAin the San Diego District. I was scared about the people's
reaction to the calling. I told both conferences that it didn't
matter which way they voted, I was still going to serve Jesus
Christ - both conferences voted unanimously in support of my
calling.
I have preached, counseled, served the Lord's Supper and
participated in each ordination of three other women in our

congregation in some way or another and including laying on of
hands in the ordination of Carol Beitz to the office of deacon
as assistant to the spokesman. The most memorable experience was
service communion side by side with my husband.
My prayer is that I may never view any aspect of ministry as
routine or common-place, but that I may always go forward in fear
and trembling to serve my Lord and Master.
There have been times, when my judgement was called into
question and in those times, my testimony was that I did what
Christ would have done and know deep inside of me, with the help
of the Holy Spirit, the truthfulness of that statement.
For years, I have struggled with our stance on Communion,
and even now as a priest the struggle is even greater. But I
know thru this kind of struggle, the most ministry is given, and
change takes place. However, I will always serve my Lord, doing
what or where I am called.
I struggle for adequate words to express my innermost
feelings and find it very hard to say anymore .

Elder Seth Kieffer, my Pastor and my father, told me
September 24, 1986, that I had been called to the Priesthood.
'!he Arkansas-Memphis District voted and approved my call at
camp Powderfork in Bald Knob, Arkansas. Our Branch at
Fisher, Arkansas, approved my call also. On November 2, 1986,
Elder Martin Passmaore from the Jonesboro::Branch and my father
ordained me to the office of Priest.
My ordination was a joyous experience. Onw that I will
never forget. I could feel the Holy Spirit, and I knew this
was a call frt'.om GOd. My brother, Mike Kieffer, was ordained
thll!,t day also to the office of Priest. He had received his
call earlier. We both were permitted to serve communion that
1

day. Being able to serve my family the emblems is an experience
I'll mever forget also. I never thought I would have tJ-eopportunity
to serve God in the ministry to such an extent as I have.
The day of my ordination I felt joy, love, peace and fear,
because this was the beginning of a life time committment to
God and tocminister to others. I was and still a'ii excited
about being in the Priesthood. There is so much work that needs
to be done and I feel such a big responsiblity knowing God has
called me to do this job. I will try to do my best for Him,
and with His help and guidance, hopefully I can spread the
gospel.
There have been two people who have quit coming to church,
because I have been ordained to the priesthood. We have tried to
talk to them, but they will not listen or even try to understand.
I have known these people all my life, so it hurts knowing they
do not accept me. All I can do now is pray for them and all the
others who are against women in the priesthood. I have supported
Section 156 fr0m the beginning. Lfel t that one day I would be
in the priesthood however I did not know it would be so soom.
When my father received my call, he called other Elders and
a High Priest asking them to pray about it, because he wanted to

My parents have always taken me to cJmrch so I have been
raised knowing about God, My father has been assistant Pastor

In 1985, I had an experience that became a turning point

It was a very tramatic experience;. but I totally turned my life

Since that experience I have been like a sponge absorbing

The week before I ,,as to be ordained Satan really tried to
cause me to have doubts and feel confused. I had to pray and study

brought me closer to my Lord. I have a different perspective of'
things now. It is amazing the changes I have gone through since
my ordination. •
Our branch is small so I am,able to preach, teach, serve
commumion, be in charge of' Prayer services and do whatever needs
to be done. I am thankful I have the opporunity to serve God
in all the many ways I can in our branch.
Being more attuned to the Holy Spirit, I have been able to
feel and experience it more. At a women's retreat in Racine,
Missouri, our district was in charge of' one of' the worship services.
As I stood to do my part the feeling of' the Holy Spirit was over
powering. I have never had such a wonderful experience. Also,
when I I have talked to individuals who are •;troubled, the Holy
Spirit has guided my thoughts so that I could help that person.
When I'm giving a sermon I can feel God's presence and
guidance. But in order for me to have come this far, I have
had to depend on God, and study and pr?';)' harder than ever before.
I have only just begun my ministry. I know that I will
have many experiences in the future. I want to serve to the
best of my ability. With GOd 's help, I can. Since I am

forward to serve my Lord.

In retrospect, I have found eight ways in which the Lord r,repared me to
accept not only Section 156 of the Doctrine and Covenants and the
ordination of women in the church, but also the eventual calling and
acceptance of priesthood responsibilities in my office as Teacher in
the Aaronic Priesthood, He cultivated, watered and fertilized my
potential through: a church questionaire; a strong testimony of the·
divinity of the Prophet, Wallace B, Smith; the recognized presence and
ministry of the Holy Spirit; going back to college to earn a Bachelor's
Degree; the leadings of the Spirit through my own prayers; the testimony
of our regional administrator; the pages of our daily newspaper; and
finally, the respect and trust engendered by the priesthood members
through whom the call was processed. What follows are the details of
each of these methods as seen from my perspective.
A questionaire was sent to members from the World Church prior to 156
in which there was a question relating to acceptance of ordination of
women. My answer was that I did not feel that it would ever come about
in the church, but if it did come through the prophet as the mind and
will of God, I would accept it,
When the Hymns of the Saints was being compiled, several sets of words
were sen-c to me to work on for hymn settings. One was what is now
Hymn #205, "My Children Hear Ye Him, My Word," As I read and reread those
words, the Lord's Spirit enveloped me and I became aware that they were,
in fact, the Lord's words and an expression which had come through the
presidency of the church, So sure was I that Bro. Wallace B. had been the
author through whom the words came, that when the hymn first appeared in
the Herald and·Bro. Tyree's name was listed as author, I felt there would
be a correction in the next issue. Shortly after that it was time for
World Conference and Bro, Tyree was named to be in the First Presidency
as· counselor to the Prophet. I felt that, for some reason, the Lord had
given me-a testimony regarding the divinity of this calling. Even though
Bro. 'Tyree and I have never met, I wanted him to know of the experience I
had had, In my letter to him, I added that this was also a testimony to
Jllt1 of the validity of Bro. Wallace's office since he was the one through
wl'lbm Bro. Tyree's call had come. Having been present at the conference
when Wallace was ordained prophet f'lnd president of the church, and having
received a strong testimony of the Spirit at that time concerning his
calling, I could not understand why I needed additional evidence of his
being the Lord's chosen prophet.
At the World Conference,when 156 was presented to those of us who were
delegates other than priesthood, I sat in the front row center as Brother
Sheehy read the document, '!'he only way to describe my feelings is to
compare them to what it must be like to experience an earthquake. My
whole world had moved unexpectedly, and I was without my own strength as
a resource, As always in the past, the Lord was present at the very
intersection of my need. His sweet and gentle spirit was like a soft
blanket that prevailed throughout the cham~er. I was reminded of my own
words, that if this ever came thrttugh the prophet, I would accept it.
The sudden knowledge that God really does love me and all females as much
as he loves and cares about male persons, was surprising and thrilling,

Lancaster
I don't ever remember consciously forming the thought, but buried deeply
inside me must have been the suspicion that God might be partial to men.
That notion was completely dispelled at that time.
Later, whenever I heard persons express doubts about the prophet, I was
able to share my testimony about the Spirit's witness to me in that
regard. It was amazing to me then and still fills me with awe and wonder
that God in all His magnificence could reach into my life and provide me
with the necessary preparation to accept what He knew would be so difficult
for me.
The Lord has motivated me through many persons. One such was my oldest
daughter, Kathy. When Kathy was a sophmore in college, she not only
suggested I go back to college to get my degree in music, she insisted on
it. With the encouragement through the years of our second daughter, Libby,
and the patience and support of my husband, Arnold, I graduated summa eu,~
laude from Jr. College and then magna cumlaude with my Bachelor's Degree
in Music Education, May of 1986. I do believe, in all h11-mility, that this was all part of the Lord's plan for me to be able to be of better service,
It was during my senior year at Lambuth (Methodist) College that I began
4uestioning my husband, Arnold, who was by this time Tennessee-Kentucky
D,P,, about why our pastor at Jacks Creek, Tennessee, Elder Dwight Jones,
was not working on calls for two women in the branch - Shirley Bailey and
JoAnn Jones. Their calls seemed so obvious to me that I aould not understand what seemed like procrastination. He wisely put me off without telling me
their calls were already being processed. Something I had no inkling of at
all was that a call was also being processed for me, JoAnn and I had
talked many times throughout the years of how much need there was for the
office of teacher to be in place and functioning in the church in view of
the many troubled persons in our day, That is the office to which we were
both called and ordained,
The time came for my senior piano recital and I felt much in need of special ministry. As I prayed throughout the day while at school, I felt
impressed to 0 ask for angelic ministry to be present because I felt such a
desire to bring music ministry to my teachers and friends and loved ones who had done so much for me,and knew my own resources were insufficient,
It was very soon after that on a Sunday after the 11: 00 A.M, service that
our young pastor called me into the church kitchen, As he handed me a
letter to read, I felt the Lord's Spirit in great degree. To my amazement
it was a letter regarding my calling to the office of Teacher. I have no accurate recollection of what I said, but I'm sure I expressed my great
surprise and that I had no evidence at all of any calling for myself.
Dwight very patiently told me to pray about it and I agreed to do so.
Our new RA Bob Bruch, was in the district at the time, wi,th my husband, _t;:r- nold, When they returned to our home that evening, I reported to them what
they both already knew. They told me of their confirming evidence. Bob's
was before he even met me, but this was still not enough for me.

Lancaster

Monday evening we drove to McKenzie Branch where Bob was the speaker.
In his talk he told of his own first calling to the Aaronic priesthood
and of how he had remembered praying for the ministry of angels shortly
before being told of his call. For him that was confirming evidence
because that priesthood holds the keys of that ministry. Bob or noone
else could have known of my private prayer before my recital. It was still
not enough, however.
During that week or two, I did pray often asking for evidence of my own.
Arnold called to my attention how many times persons had come to me for
advice and counsel, of how I had been functioning as an arbitrator as a
Teacher would. He told me also of the confirming testimonies of the
former DP, Dale Galimore, and his counselor, George Lemonds. This wes
still not enough, I felt.
During a very busy school da,,, I opened the Jackson Sun, our daily
newspaper, and my eyes fell on the headline of a full page article written
about me and another non-traditional student who had returned to school
late in ife. Over a large picture of me studying for exams, in bold face
type, the quote read, "I have always wanted to be a teacher." "What a
coincidence," I thought, but at the same time my mind raced to the
testimonies of all the priesthood members involved in my call,and I asked
myself, "Just how much evidence do you want?"
lV',y thoughts continued, "If anyone of these men, whose spiritual integrity
is beyond question, had stood in a prayer service and said, 'Thus saith
the Spirit, ' and called me by name and told me I was called to the office
of Teacher, I would have accepted it." Of course, that's what I had been
expecting - to hear the Lord's voice once again as I had heard it years
before, speaking through one of His servants to me. After much apologizing
to the Lord, I accepted the call and began my Temple School course.
At our Tennessee Kentucky Reunion of 1986, provision was made for both
Shirley Bailey's and my ordinations so that our family members from afar
could attend. At the district business meeting on the first Sunday of
reunion, there was a group of Saints from one br;mch who came to try to
block confirmation for the cells of the four women from our district, lut
they were unsucessfu.l.
That reunion Thursday evening, July 3, 1986, I was the first woman from our
district to be ordained a member of the priesthood. EL~, Bob Bruch, was the
spokesman, assisted by my husband HP and DP, Arnold Lancaster, Sr,, along
with Elder Kenneth Hardee (our daughter Libby's husband), and our two sons,
Elder Ohris Lancaster, Sr., and Arnold Lancaster, who was then a Priest.
Arnold, Jr.•s call to the office of Elder was acted upon with the ssme group
of calls. At the very beginning of the service, as Shirley and I sat on
the front row waiting to have priesthood conferred upon us, I felt the
unmistakeable presence of Err.ma and Joseph beside us and a great sense of
lineal descendency of priesthood heritage from those days of the restoration
of authority. lv!y personal response after ordination follows:

I am nothing in and of myself, but
Where there is hunger, let me be one of the servants, serving the food prepared and blessed
by the Master Chef,

serving the Great Physician.

repairing the breech.

Lancaster

and abilities from all of us misfits, as well as the skillful,

Wnere there is longing for the Kingdom, I would be one of the

If I can remember that I have been hired to be one of the servants of the Master Servant of the universe,
If I can remember to always let him give the direction, set the pace, determine the values, and find the

Since ordination, there have been other confirming evidences for me.
On one occasion in our home branch, Ja~ks Creek, I asked our deacon in
charge of Sunday offerings if I might help receive the offering during
the 11:00 A, M, service because I have had strong feelings about the
service of offerings for some time. My feelings are that this is one of
the principles of the Kingdom in which we can reflect the personality of
our heavenly father, He is a giving person, and it is through our giving
as vocation - not avocation - that we can become his coworkers. While,
in no way, we can approach the gifts of redemption and reclamation He has
given, still it is through life lived as a stewardship response that we
can witness to the actuality of His gifts. It is a high privelege for
those who receive any offerings, from the body of Christ assembled, on
behalf of our Lord. In this spirit, I participated with my husband, Arnold,
who was asked to assist also, After the service, one of the sisters in
our congregation who had had some problem with ordination of women, came to
me and said that as we proceeded down the aisle, she could feel the Lord's
Spirit in great abu_~dance. She added that she felt I had been inspired to
ask to help.
On two other occasions after I was the speaker for the 11: 00 A,M. service,
women whom I knew had expressed strong opposition to the concept of
women in the priesthood came to me and hugged me and said what a good spirit
had been there.
Whenever I have been asked to participate,as a member of the priesthood,
in any service at church or as counsel, there has been a greater degree of
the Lord's Spirit than I had ever experienced before ordination. This has
helped me to understand that there is a definite purpose for ordination,
At one time, I was under the impression that even without priesthood, a
person who really served the Lord with full purpose of heart could gi-we
as much as a person who had had the added mantle of priesthood. Now
I recognize that priesthood is not just a pat on the head in approval for
the desire of a person wanting to be of service to the Lord, While

priesthood is definitely servant ministry, it is also the Lord's designa-
tion of that person to do a specific function for which there is needed

His commission and approval. It is the difference between doing the job
your own way rather than recognizing the Lord's right to be in charge of
what he knows needs to be done. The concept of servant ministry is a
real delight to me. The fact that some priesthood members had looked at
their priesthood as meaning they were somehow better than others, always
disturbed me. There was a time when I accepted that as a fact ~~thout
question, but I am most thankful the Lord has spoken through 156 on the
issue to make clear that self-aggrandizement has no place among the Lord's
workers.
Thank you for the privelege of giving expression to what means so much
to me.

Sincerely,
Ardith Lancaster, Teacher

Having grown up in the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter Day Saints, I was very content with the role I played as
wife to an elder-pastor; busy in the women's department and
involved in the music, both locally and district wide. I don't
remember having actually been taught that only men could be in
the priesthood but grew up accepting this way of thinking. I had
plenty to do to keep me busy without the worry of being or not
being in the priesthood.
I had deep doubts when talk began about women being ordained
and I was definitely against it. I made the remark that before I
would ever accept women in the priesthood it would have to come
through the prophet and then the Lord and I would have a talk.
One of the more important experiences that happened in my
life was around 1971. We, as a family, decided to go to a
reunion other than the one we normally attended at Racine,
Missouri. We chose to go to Guthrie Grove, Iowa. It had been on
my mind for sometime that I had always accepted the fact that
this was God's church but I didn't have my own testimony of this.
I had made this a matter of prayer over the past few months. One
night, while at this reunion, I was awakened three times by a
voice that said, "There is a God and this is his church." I felt
at this time a greater responsibility to do the Lord's work and
as my children grew older I tried to become more involved.
Another experience I had was on a Witnessing Week-End. The
persofi in charge asked me to stand and tell how my life with God
related c with my family life. As I stood I couldn't say anything
and a voice inside of me spoke to me. It told me I did not have
a real relationship with my God because I was bound to my
children. I was too involved seeing that they might be what I
expected of them. I was told I would have to give my children
their freedom to make their own choices before I would be free to
make the choice of truly serving God. This was a shock to me
because I felt I was doing so much for Hirn. I am so thankful for
the love of God and that he cares enough for me to chastise me
when it is needed.
I have tried to grow closer to the Lord these past few years
and tried to see my service in a more "serving" way. I realize
now I did my "church work" because I enjoyed doing it and
probably for that reason only.

me. I really wasn't sure what it was but I sensed the Lord was
working in my life and I attempted to respond to that feeling. I

think if I did something. As I read my Patriarchal Blessing I

TESTIMONY OF SHIRLEY LEEDER

My ordination was approved by members of the Wiarton, Ontario, Canada
Branch, and the Owen Sound District, in Ontario, Canada. Approval was
given in November, 1985. I was ordained in the Wiarton Branch in Wiarton,
Ontario, on April 27, 1986, to the office of Elder. High Priest John
Bradley was spokesman, assisted by Elder Steven Leeder (son) and EvangelistPatriarch Alma J. V. Leeder (husband).
In 1965, I was driving on a highway in Ontario and was involved
in a car-truck accident. I came out of the accident with only a cut
on my head. Shortly afterward, I was spoken to through the pastor, a
high priest, who said my life had been spared because God had further
work for me to do. Much earlier than that, I had a dream where I was
at a meeting in a small building, similar to small community halls common
in rural areas. In this dream, the person who was to speak did not arrive,
and I was asked to speak. I did not know what this meant, but felt that
I needed to do more studying to prepare myself. Since then, many different
opportunities have come to improve myself and broaden my outlook and
understanding.
When I was invited in 1984 to become a member of the Women's Ministries
Commission, I prayed to know whether I should accept this responsibility.
I was reminded of the dream, and this time recognized that the building
I had seen before was the one-room rural school that I had attended as
a child. At that time it was made known to me that God can use people
from the humblest of beginnings if they but trust Him, and have faith
in Him and are willing to serve Him. At the time of the priesthood call,
I was again reminded of this and felt that by accepting the call I could
more fully be of service to God and for god's people.
I have always believed that women have talents and abilities that
can be used for helping others. I believe that being a member of the
priesthood allows these talents and abilities to be further enhanced.
One area of ministry has been assisting at administration for the
sick. The first two times that I did this, each of the persons said
they had a confirmation of my call. These have been good experiences.
Another good experience was offering a pastoral prayer at a communion
service.
People have responded pas iti ve ly to my ca 11 i ng. Non-member friends
have been supportive. On the negative side, two people have refused
to partake of the communion emblems. On the positive, supplying funeral
ministry to non-members was a good experience. Also a mixture of positive
and negative--a certain sister feels she cannot accept women in the ministry,
but after a talk I had given at the morning worship service, she said
she didn't reject my ministry.
My idea of ministry is to be doing what Christ would have doneextending kindness to people, comforting people, making people feel good
about themselves, showing Christ's love to others by being understanding
and trying to inspire trust and faith in people. Ministry is to try
to show people how to know God better and to have a desire to align their
lives with God.

As requested by the women's ministry commission I Mabel Bernice Levitt would like to share my testimony regarding my ordination to the Office of priests. I've been a member of this church since I was eight years old and I've always had a desire to serve God in whatever way he would have me serve. I felt very humble when my call to the priesthood came through our pastor and my husband husband, elder Alvin, love it. The calls for our branch were processed through our usual channels, my name and that of another woman in our branch after submission to the district, regional and world church officials. were presented to our congregation and approved unanimously, the complete support of our small congregation was very meaningful to me. Following the branch approval, our calls were presented at the district fall conference in Edmonton, Alberta. This was a spiritual experience for me as several spoke in favor of my call among them our two sons and our youngest daughter. The support and encouragement they portrayed was most humbling and very important to me, as a mother and a servant of God, my ordination and that of my friend and co worker, Lillian Perkins took place in our home branch, the 11 o'clock service, march 1 1987. Lillian was ordained by her husband and their son and I was ordained by our son. Although Brian Levitt, assisted by my husband, elder Alvin love it. I was very happy that our five of our children and their spouses and children were able to attend to the service although our eldest daughter and her husband and family also our youngest son in law, were unable to attend Lily and I shared her spousal service with Lillian's little granddaughter, Chanda Elaine Larsson as it was her blessing service as well. Our small church was almost full with our families and many guests. I've never had a problem accepting section 156 and the revelation regarding the ordination of women. My regret is that some church members have not been able to accept this revelation and the ensuing problems have caused this dissension and alienation within the church as a whole I pray that we as a collective group will be able to heal this hurt in order to get on with the work of the Lord. I am blessed when I was spoken to under the spirit regarding my call to the priesthood the council came through an elderly elder in our congregation who wholeheartedly supported the revelation regarding the ordination of women in Section 156 He had been given the up the knowledge prior to the revelation that the ordination of women would eventually occur. The acceptance portrayed by our elderly brothers was that this was the will of God was very reassuring indeed. Our congregation has been very supportive and encouraging to us as ordained women. Our ministry to prior to the ordination includes similar duties to that of the priest except for preaching and performing ordinances. We have taught Sunday school and Vacation Bible School for many years and have accompanied our husbands on priesthood calls to the homes of the members and to the sick and shut ins therefore, I believe the transition to ordained priesthood has not been as traumatic for our members. The only negative response I have encountered was a minor thing. At our family camp this summer I was sitting at the picnic table visiting with a group of people when one brother who was visiting from the independence area directed a question to the men around the table asking how many priesthood were represented. When I also said I held the office of priests members Mark was completely ignored. This did not really bother me, but I do hope the day will come when the ministry of all people will be accepted by the membership. However, I intend to continue in service mindful of the scriptures that all are called according to the gifts of God unto them, that I may laboring together with God for the accomplishment of the work entrusted to all. TESTIMONY OF MY ORDINATION by Sheran Suzanne Long On Friday evening, March 31, 1984, I was driving alone from Warrensburg, Missouri, to Independence, Missouri, a trip of about 50 miles. Because of a great fear of water and no where to learn to swim when I was a child. I still did not know how to swim, and I had a great desire to do it. I have been in charge of have been well attended, and the people have responded with excellent participation. One Communion Sunday we had visitors, a man and his wife, attend our service. That was my first time of serving communion, and these people were sitting where I served. Not knowing whether or not they were church members, I offered the bread to them, and they both shook their heads and refused it. I assumed they were not members of the church; however, the following Wednesday evening, I discovered that they were mem- berg and had been for many years. I don't know why they would not take communion that day, but I assumed it was because I served them, and they could not accept my priesthood authority, That has been my only negative response to my call; and even though I felt badly about it at first, I realize that all people have to decide for themselves what their response will be. All of my life I have desired to serve the Lord in what- ever way I can. There have always been many opportunities to bring ministry to others, but since my ordination to the office of elder, I feel very deeply the responsibility of ministering for Jesus Christ and representing him in whatever I do. This is what ministry is all about--being a servant for the Lord.

presiding elder, Jim Estes, approached me regarding some church

activity, when he asked if he could visit with me a moment, a

The Omaha-Council Bluffs Stake approved my ordination, and I was ordained to the office of Teacher on March 25rd 1986 by L. Dean Carper (Patriarch-Evangelist] and Larry Oiler CElder] in my home church in Shenandoah, Ia. 1 am marpied to Gene Maher who also holds the office of Teacher. Over the last 15 years, I have felt a special closeness to my church family and they have been very supportive to our family. As we have shared together in worship, I have felt God's Spirit there many times. In the past few years, when asked to serve in any way, I felt I should try. I even dared to teach an adult class which is very scary for me. My experience was very uplifting and I received far more than I gave. As we heard more and more about Section 156 and the controversies about it, I did have some mixed emotions about the truth of it. I read the many articles in the Saint's Herald, and I had such a good feeling about the testimonies given by other women. I rationalized in my mind that God could use women as well as men to do his work. But when our pastor, John Rounds, and his family visited our home in November, 1985 and related to me that I was called to the Priesthood, I was stunned. I knew he was serious, but I didn't want him to be. Thoughts raced through my mind of several of my sisters at church that I thought God could use in His priesthood, but not me. Finally, my reply was that I would think and pray about it. As diligent as I know how, I did that in the days following, but I didn't receive a confirmation of it. There were those in the priesthood who assured me that my call was from the Lord. David Harrington told me that he had a dream that I was called to the office of Ruth. Then he said, "I realized there is no such office," but as he "thought about the book of Ruth, and how she was a faithful servant, then he could relate it to my calling." Paul Hansen, who I respect as a true servant of the Lord, assured me that my calling was true, and he had known about it for some time. He told me not to be too concerned that I had not had confirmation of it. Because of the testimony of those two good brethren, I wanted to believe it was true, but I still had those times when I felt so unworthy. Even so, I made the decision to accept the call. Then shortly before Christmas, there was a special communion service. As I stood to tell my church family that I had been called to the priesthood, God's Spirit was so strong within me that I felt I must be doing his will. Two of the Temple School courses that were required, I did through home study. As I prepared the course about the office of teacher, I felt the Holy spirit so many times as I read the scriptures pertaining to it. It was a meaningful experience for me. The day of my ordination was truly a humbling experience. After the service, a young man that I hadn't met

before, congratulated me, and told me that Secion 156 had caused him some concern, but today as the elders placed their hands on my head, he was filled with God's Spirit and would doubt no more. His was another of my brother's testimonies that strengthened me, and I was thankful he had shared it. The summer following my ordination, Wallace B. Smith was the honored guest at our reunion. It was my privelege to be on the rostrum with Brother Smith as the featured speaker. I was to have the invocation and I was very nervous and feeling inadequate. I had allowed it to make me uncomfortable, but as we metfor prayer behind the sancuary and joined hands, I felt what a warm, humble person he is. And as God has promised each one of us, when it came time for my prayer, the words were there accompanied by a calm and the warmth of His Spirit. My church family have been very supportive of me through prayer and encouragement since my ordination. I have never felt any negative reactions there. I believe ministry means serving and meeting people's needs through prayer and the guidance of the Holy Spirit. The teacher's duties are to watch over the church which means to me to be mindful of their needs, promote peace when there is contention and to urage particoation in church ativities. Before I was called to this office, I didn't fully realize the importance of praying for those people who have responsibilities. The strength that comes frem other's prayers never ceases to amaze me. Recently I felt very unworthy of my calling and because of the diligent prayers of one of my sisters in Christ, that weakness was turned to strength and an added witness of the truth of God's promises. We are given weakness that we may be humble, but His glorious promise is that He will make weak things become strong unto them. I submit to you, when God calls you, say you can, and your reward will be sheer joy. Your Sister in Christ, Mary Maher

The year 1986 was one of the most difficult of my life. On Friday and Saturday April
25th to 26th, I attended my first temple school class and was delighted with the quality of the
class material and the teaching of it. Here was real vitality, the kind that generated excitement for
further learning.
The following week, after a thorough examination by a specialist in preliminary diseases,
my husband was told he had cancer in his right lung. A bronchoscopy was scheduled for three
days later, but shortly after we returned home Bill started spitting up blood. Terrified I called the
doctor and was told to get him to the hospital as soon as possible. The bronchoscopy was done at
the hospital early the next week after the hemorrhaging was stopped. The doctor never reached
the offending lung cancer; He found cancer cells in circling the lower extremity of the trachea.
Inoperable, he told us. I tissue samples showed the cancer to be one which would not respond to
chemotherapy. We were referred to a radiation specialist and the daily treatments were started
before will left the hospital. Thus began three months of five-days-a-week 112-mile round trips
to the Springfield Radiological Grm__, an affiliate of St. John's Regional Health Center in
Springfield, MO.
The doctors were candid about Will's chance for survival and we appreciated their
honesty. We were told that the radiation treatments were merely buying time, but we assumed
that that meant at least a year or so. Bill died August 7th at home, as he had wished, as I sat
holding his hand.
On September 17th, which would have been bills 70th birthday, I received a call from my
pastor.
“Evelyn, you have been called to the office of priest,” Elder Maurice Rice told me. “Are
you willing to accept the call? I need your answer as soon as possible so that your name can be
presented for confirmation at the district conference on September 21.”
I had taken the call in the bedroom and now sat down abruptly on the side of the bed. My head
reeled with the implications of what I had just heard. I knew I could not give an immediate
answer.
Maurice waited and I finally said, “I cannot say yes to this now. I will call you in on
Friday.”
There had been no one uncertainty for me about the validity of Doctrine and Covenants
156. I had known for a number of years that eventually women would be called to the
priesthood—but not, I was sure, in my lifetime. And now a call for me? At my age?
My telephone line was busy the rest of that evening. I called my daughter, Jo, first, “Go for it,
Mother,” Jo said quietly. “You have been preparing for this all your life.”
My sons Norman and Matthew were more cautious in their approval. They gave positive
response to the call but were concerned that I might assume a too-heavy load. both were feeling
protective of me in the absence of their father. My sister and brother agreed to pray.

Dear Carol,
I will try to answer your questions and give my testimony. My call came through Peter
Harter, who was the pastor of the Central Congregation in Springfield, MO, the one I attend. It
was approved by the District President Lee Roy Palmer, and Regional Administrator Robert P
Brooch, and I think Apostle Paul Booth. It was voted on in a special business meeting of the
Congregation and at the district Conference in September 1986.
I had to take some Temple School courses before being ordained. One was the ministry
of the Deacon, the office I was called to. I took it as a home study. I took one on Restoration
history taught by Elwen and Peggy Vest, and an introduction to scripture taught by Peggy Vest. I
was ordained November 23rd, 1986. Under the hands of Glen and Charlcie Yeoman, husband
and wife both elders and friends of mine.
My ordination was very special. The church was full. Another lady, and a man was
ordained to the office of elder the same day, and a baby was blessed. The children's choir sang,
and the adult choir sang.
Patriarch John Smelser spoke, and the Regional Administrator Robert P. Brooch Gave the
Charge to us, calling each one by name and talking individually to us, about our call, our duties
and responsibilities and our rights and privileges. I felt like he was speaking under the influence
of the Holy Spirit.
My husband Ralph de Marshall was and still is a Deacon. He is my third husband, the
second one was not a member when we were married. He was baptized nine months later and
was ordained a Deacon about one year later. My first husband was a member of the church when
we were married. I was not. I was baptized several years later. He died in 1960 and I married
Francis J. Winn in 1965. He died in 1978 and I married Ralph in 1983.
It is hard to separate the experience of the last 20 years from the experience of a life of
over 71 years. I think that everything in my life has had a bearing on my calling. For instance, I
grew up on a cotton farm in Mississippi, and had I stayed there I never would have heard of the
church, and had I not met Alexander Tousley and went to Independence, MO and married him
three years after leaving home, I still may not have come in contact with the church. In 1975, my
husband at the time and my youngest adopted daughter and myself moved to Springfield, MO.
We didn't know anyone here, but I felt led by the Lord to come here. We lived in Phoenix, AZ
where I had taken my daughter and moved there because of her health in 1962, after my oldest
adopted daughter got married. I moved back to Independence after my husband died and my
daughter got married, and I worked at Resthaven for a while. I had a good friend down here. She
would stay at my place when in Independence, and I came to see her once in a while and in 1982
she asked me to come down to meet someone. It was Ralph. He had lost his wife a year before
and was lonely, so was I. Three months later we were married, and I moved back down here.
In July of 85, I became one of the solicitors of the central congregation.
when section 156 first came before the conference I was shocked. I thought it was a big mistake.
But as I prayed and studied about it, I came to know that as God's chosen prophet Wallace B
Smith knew more about the will of God than I could ever hope to, so I told my husband and his
children (all of which were very much against 156 the kids still are) that I was going to stick by
the Prophet. My husband is as strong for it now as I am. I prayed for some time that if I was to be
called I would serve if I knew it was God's will. When I was called, I was expecting it. I now
think the calling of women is the beginning of the fulfillment of Acts 2-17 & 18.
My first experience in speaking I was very nervous; I never was very good at talking
before a group of people. I worked on it for weeks, and prayed about it too. I was so sure I would

be so nervous that I would never get through it, but when I got up there that all left me and I was
calm, several people told me I did fine, but I am not so sure. I know that if the Lord had not been
with me, I could not have done it at all. I have not been scheduled to speak again. I have given
the offetory talk several times, and each time the Lord has helped me. Each time I put in time of
study and prayer too. I trust and depend on the Lord, but I think he expects me to do what I can
to prepare myself for service too. I have helped prepare the Emblems for communion. One time I
made a mistake, and the head Deacon took time to show me how it should be done. I was
grateful to him.
For the most part, the response to my attempts to serve has been positive in what is left of
the congregation after those that could not accept Section 156 left. Most of them had left before
any women were called in our congregation. Two other ladies and myself were the first ones
called in our congregation. One thing I have noticed that I have not been asked but once, to help
take up the offeren. When I have been Deacon in charge, I get others to help me, and once in a
while, when my husband is in charge and he can't find men to do it, he will say “I guess you will
have to help me today.” Once I asked him why he never asks me to do it, and he said he did
when there was no man to do it. I think that is a put down. He is always saying that I was called
to take his place. I don't think one person can take another's place. I think I have a place of my
own to fill.
I think that Ministry is that which helps people to worship God. There are many ways one
can minister, a good choir or soloist can, a good speaker can bring ministry, but it takes more
than these to make for a good, worshipful experience. The place needs to be clean, quiet,
comfortable, and a well-organized program where everything goes smoothly. That's where the
Deacon comes in, to see to it that everything is in readiness for the service, but that is not all a
Deacon can do. They can do anything that doesn't involve performing the Sacraments.
I have become more interested in helping other people, especially elderly people, and in
visiting the Saints, encouraging them to come to church, to file an accounting, or just to be a
friend to them. Let them know that I and the church cares for them. 10 years ago, I was too timid
and shy to do that unless I knew the person well. Several months ago, I felt that I wanted to do
something for people outside of the church, but I didn't know what or where to start. Then one
day, I heard that they needed people to deliver meals to the shutends at the senior citizen center. I
thought “that is something I can do,” so since then my husband and I have done that two or three
days a week. Those people have become very dear to us.
Sincerely,
Eva Rae Marshall

A PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF MY ORDINATION EXPERIENCE

Submitted by Rebecca Jansma McCanse
Rockford, Illinois Branch Wisconsin District
October 26, 1987

A PERSONAL TESTIMONY OFMY ORDINATION EXPERIENCE
My call to the office of teacher was made known to me in December of
1985 by the pastor of my home congregation, Elder John Herman. The
following month it was presented to the Rockford, Illinois branch for
approval and subsequently to the Wisconsin District. I was ordained
on August 10, 1986 in Rockford. The service was conducted by Elder
J.R. Longsdorf and the prayer of ordination was given by Elder John
Herman who was assisted by Patriarch-Evangelist (then Elder) Richard
Brock.
The priesthood call which I received was not entirely unexpected for me. From the time I was young I felt the importance of serving the
Lord (although I have not always acted on that conviction). I was
raised in the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints
and my mother's family has a long history in the Restoration movement.
My father, although not a member, has been a friend and supporter of
the church. Church membership has always constituted an important part
of my personal identity. For many years I have felt that God had a calling on my life. I viewed marriage, family, career and church activity as important and
necessary parts of my life but I knew that, for me, this couldn't be
the ''whole picture'' if I were to truly find fulfillment. I was never
able to envision exactly what form this call would take, but I have
always had an awareness of it and an anticipation that, in God's time,
it would become apparent.
Looking back over the past 17 years (since 1970) I can identify a number of events and experiences, along with this sense of calling,
which have led me to my c~rrent convictions and life situation. One of the first experiences I ever had in which I felt the hand of God
moving strongly in my personal life was when He opened the way for me
to attend Graceland College. I very much wished to be a student at
Graceland but, due to personal feelings and financial restrictions,
my father would not allow me to even submit an application. Realizing
I could not change his mind, I put the matter in the Lord's hands.
After being accepted at another college and receiving a good scholarship I assumed that my academic future was set. However, due to a
series of unexpected events, I decided to broach the subject with my
father one last time. To my total astonishment he had had a complete
change of heart and encouraged me to apply to Graceland. I am convinced
that God intervened on my behalf to bring this about. I believe that my two years at Graceland probably made a significant difference in my life. At the time I graduated from high school I was
leaving a very unsatisfactory church experience. If I had gone on to
a college where there was no church and no church friends (which would
have been the case at the college where I had originally been accepted)
I think I would have been very susceptible to other influences and
probably would have begun the process of severing my ties to the church.
My religious and social experiences at Graceland helped me to define
and internalize my beliefs. Prior to this, although I had a commitment
to the church, it was basically a reflection of my mother's commitment
and not truly my own. Constant association with people who held moral
and spiritual convictions similar to my own was a
influence which drew m~ very positive closer to Christ and to the church.

-2-

The next event which had an important effect on my life was transferring
to the University of Arizona and there meeting the man who would become
my husband. Again, because the events leading to this meeting were so
extraordinary I have always felt that the Lord was involved us together. My husband was (and is) a church in bringing
anticipated marrying a church member,. Becausevnumbers are so few, Jil'rmber. I had never really
relatively speaking, I felt that that was an unrealistic expectation.
From the beginning of our courtship an important part of our "connectedness" was based in our shared commitment o the church and to Christ.
While I realize that it is entirely possible to maintain a commitment
to the church while being married to a non-member, I am aware from
observing my mother's experience that it is much more difficult to do
so and can be frustrating and disappointing. I believe that being
married to a man who shares my convictions
freedom to participate in church activities, to accept responsibilities, has allowed me much greater
and to take advantage of opportunities to grow in the church.
A year after our marriage my husband and I
husband became involved in setting up a business and this necessitated moved to Illinois. My
long hours of work six to seven days a week. We also lived 35 miles
from the nearest branch of our church. Due to these factors, our
activity level at church decreased shar,J.y during this time. Our participation was limited to semi-regular attendance of Sunday morning worship
services. Although·my husband's work situation eventually
church activity level did not change. We continued in this manner for easEdup, our
about six years. At that time some friends from Oklahoma (David and
Marsha Barlow) invited us to go to Reunion with them. We accepted and
had a very positive experience. I left with a strong desire to become
more involved in the church. Be~se I had had years of piano lessons as a child, I decided that I would offer to be available to play for
church. This was not an easy decision to make because I had a tremendous
fear of playing in front of people. Although I had resolved to call the
music director as soon as I got home, I found myself rationalizing
("I should practice for a while first", etc.) and deciding to put it
off for a few weeks or months. Before the week was over, however, the music director, phoned me and said that while I was gone she had learned
from my mother-in-law that I knew how to play the piano and asked if I
would consider playing for church! The Lord gave me the courage I
needed to accept this responsibility and from that point on the "snowba 11 effect" took over. I eventually became music di rector, worship
commissioner, adult Sunday School teacher, book steward, choir director,
young adult leader, etc. The results of my Reunion experience were two-fold. First, it brought me back into active participation in church life. Second, it opened the
door to a degree of participation which I had never experienced, or
felt capable of, before. I suddenly realized that I was (or should be)
a responsible adult and that if something needed to be done in the
church that I shouldn't always expect someone else to do it. It was,
afterall, my church home and, therefore, more my responsibility. As I became involved and accepted new challenges I was transformed from a quiet, timid, uninvolved follower to a person whom others considered
willing and capable and could be looked to for leadership in various
capacities. For me this was a rather remarkable change because I had
always been a very shy person and had usually tried to tion which might draw attention to myself. Through service to the Lord, avoid any situa-

talents and abilities which I never even realized I possessed have
been discovered and enhanced. This has given
freedom which I never before experienced. It has allowed me to grow me~sense of personal
in ways which I didn't dream possible. I have found a tremendous amount

All of these experiences, and others as well, have worked together
to prepare me for service to the Lord within the framework of the
priesthood. While these events were taking place I was simultaneously experiencing
a "raised consciousness" concerning the role of women in our society.
I.,_ grew up in a conservative traditional family in which the male was the bread-winner, decision-maker and authority figure. I believed
that women had very few options available for life choices. Although
I was not in 100 percent agreement with all aspects of the women's
movement, the feminist philosophy spoke to me in very powerful and
relevant ways. I became aware of the potential which women possessed
and how little of that potential was being realized due to cultural
restrictions. It both saddened and angered me. At this time I began to
question the exclusion of women from the priesthood. I knew many women
who had the capability and desire to serve the Lord as fully as possible,
yet, because of their gender, were restricted in the capacity in which
they were allowed to serve. Although this did not seem fair, I felt
that the rightness or wrongness of this situation was not really mine
to decide. I believed that the church was under the direction of the
Lord and it was up to Him to decide who would serve in His priesthood.
Therefore, it was with great joy that I learned that Section 156 had
been presented and approved and that women would be ordained. I felt
very sorry that this created divisiveness in the church but I believed that it was of God. I believe that women have a great deal to offer
and the church should not set limitations on it. I am convinced that
through the combined gifts and aptitudes of men and women a much richer
ministry will result. Even though I had hoped that one day women would be allowed in the priesthood, I had never actually envisioned myself in that
Within days after Section 156 was approved, however, I knew beyond a role,.
doubt that Someday I would be called to the office of teacher. When
the call was made known to me one and a half years later it did not
czyme as a surprise. As the branch president presented the call to me he shared that he
had been aware of the call for more than a year. He kept this to himself,
however, feeling that it would be wise to allow the branch time to
adjust to the idea of women in the priesthood before being confronted

friends, he needed strong confirmation from others concerning this call
to verify that it was indeed of God and not simply his own personal

Confirmation did not come immediately, but when it came, it came all at once. Within a very brief period of time a number of priesthood
members and others spoke to him concerning my call, including one man
who had had a very difficult time accepting the ordination of women.
The confirmation which was the most meaningful to me came from a man
I barely knew. I had attended the Wisconsin District Reunion the summer

-4-

of 1985 and the person camped next to us was Richard Brock. He later
told me that one evening early in the week he had felt compelled to
visit my husband and I. At that time he had a sense of my calling to
a priesthood ministry. He shared this with Apostle Howard Sheehy, Jr.
(who was the guest minister at Reunion) and they agreed to make it a
matter of prayer. Later in the week they both confirmed that they
believed it was a call of God and each of them felt it was to the
office of teacher. Brother Sheehy counselled Richard not to share this
with my branch president until he felt led to do so. Several months
later he did inform the pastor of his experience and it came at a time
when the pastor was actively praying and waiting for confirmation.
This confirmation was very important to me because it did not come
from a long-time friend or personal acquaintance. It assured me that
the call truly was of God.
As I have mentioned, I had anticipated my call to the priesthood.
When it actually came, however, I was quite overwhelmed by it. It was
a humbling experience and I felt very unprepared to take on that
responsibility. I found it somewhat difficult to say "yes" and asked
for time to th!ink about it--to get used to the idea and to be certain that it was a commitment I could make in good conscience at that point
in my life. All the while, however, I knew in my heart that I could
not and would not say "no". If Godl wanted to use me in that capacity and believed that I was prepared to serve, then I had to believe that
He would also give me the ability to serve.
To-me the most amazing facet of this experience was that God had caIIted me by my name. Although I had experienced God's movement in my life-on previous occasions, somehow it had never been quite as personal as this. To realize that God knew me inside and out--He knew my mind,
my heart, my failings and my dreams--and found me acceptable was a
revelation of God's love and grace extended to me, as an individual,
that I had never encountered to this degree before. My reaction to
this was both elation and a deep sense of unworthiness. The fact that the call was to the office of teacher was also not
unexpected. As I began to study the duties and responsibilities of the
teacher I realized that this office was perfectly suited to my personality and disposition. I felt that, to a certain extent, I had already
been functioning in that capacity. I tend to find it easy to empathize
with others and I often seem to have an intuitive understanding of
what is going on in a person's personal life. My educational background
in psychology and sociology has also been of great benefit in helping
me understand and carry out the responsibilities of a teacher. The idea
of being a "peacemaker" particularly appeals to me. Harmony in interpersonal relationships certainly must be one of the main foundations
of a Zionic condition and being an instrument to promote this seems to
me to be a very worthwhile role to aspire to. At this point I have no
desire or aspirations to serve in any other priesthood office. I think
that the teacher can provide a very beautiful and much needed ministry
to the congregation and it seems that this is a ministry which has not
always been fully understood or utilized in the past. It is my hope
to learn to be the best teacher I can possibly be and to function in
that office with authority and competence.
When my call was presented to the Rockford branch for approval it

about support at the District level. I was the first woman in the

-5-

District to receive a priesthood call so I didn't know what to anticiate. Again, there was some opposition but it was by and far outweighed

and used and not just tolerated in the Wisconsin District. Knowing

who touched me most deeply, however, were the elderly women who spoke

me, tears in their eyes, and embraced me and expressed their happiness
for me. While this was a joyful experience, it also saddened me a

My ordination service took place in August of 1986. My son had
recently completed pre-baptismal classes so we combined his baptismal
service with my ordination so that out-of-town family members could
attend both. It also was meaningful to me that together my son and I
would be commiting our lives to the Lord. The speaker, Elder J .. R. Longsdorf, focused on the theme of service and commitment and made the message a very personal one for myself, my
son and our family. Iw.the,.prayer of,.ord,inatfon stti:Jcl<0 b}' r:was particularly the,,statemerit that-this" caU had been with me and that preparation to serve in this office had begun at the time of pll my I ife
my birth. This certainly was at least a partial explanation of the
sense of calling which I have experienced most of my life. The entire
service was beautiful and sharing it with family and friends who shared
my joy made it very special. I have been quite fortunate in that the response I have received to my calling has been very positive. My husband, who holds the office of
priest, has been extremely supportive. Both of my parents were thrilled
when they learned of my priesthood call. My husband's parents were

experiences and for this I feel very grateful. The major change in my church activities since my ordination is that
I have been regularly scheduled to participate in worship services. In

-6-

been minister-in-charge on a few occasions. I have approached these
opportunities with a tremendous burden of responsibility and an

ne~s that when I am at the pulpit representing the church that I aware-
should, as much as possible, be speaking for God and not expressing my

own personal beliefs and opinions. This has caused me to do a great
deal of studying and soul-searching and this, in itself, has been

to prepare a sermon, it is something I look forward to because it is

Another opportunity which I have had since my ordination is to be a
counsellor to the branch president. This has been an interesting
experience because it has allowed me to see what is involved the
