As I mentioned previously I feel that, prior to my ordination, I had
in many ways already been functioning in the capacity of teacher.
Therefore, much of what I do as a teacher (i.e., home ministry, providing emotional support, offering friendship, etc.) is not new, but
it has taken on a somewhat different perspective now. I see these
activities as part of the responsibility of my priesthood office and
so I try to approach them more conscientiously.
Ministry has a very broad meaning for me. Put in the simplest terms, to me ministry is sharing the love of Christ. This can be done in a wide variety of ways. It may be in a structured manner such as giving
a sermon or teaching a Sunday School class, or it may be informally by
being a friend, offering a listening ear, or giving emotional support.
Ministry means being a servant to the church and a servant to individuals (both members and non-members) in whatever capacity seems appropriate and needed. The ministry of the teacher, it seems to me, should be particularly on a personal level. The main concern of the teacher should be the persons who constitute the body of the church. This means being aware
of their personal lives and interrelationships. A teacher needs to possess sensitivity and empathy and to make her/himself open and

able to others. A teacher should be a friend in the truest sense of avail-
the word.

In the past decade my concept of ministry has changed in two signi- ficant ways. I used to equate ministry with activity or ''doing''. While
activity is certainly an important aspect of ministry, ministry must
encompass much more than merely being involved in church activities.
I would say that ministry is, above all, an attitude. This attitude involves a life commitment to Christ and to serving others. It also
involves allowing yourself to be used in quiet, unperceived ways as well as in those ways which are more apparent. It is possible for a
person to be very active in church and yet lack a Christ-centered commitment or a true concern for people. Therefore, I believe that this
attitude--this willingness to open up your life to Christ and to
others--must be the core of ministry. Also, I have come to the opinion that a minister should be a minister
for Christ first, and then a minister for the church. The most important mission a min6ter ' can have is to convert individuals to Christ. Conversion to the faith of the Saints' church is secondary (although
that would be great, too!). In fact, there do exist church members

-7-

who have not truly experienced a conversion to Christ and are in need

tremendous amount to learn and to understand. I know there is much that
I could do that I don't do and much that I do that I could do better.
Sometimes this is discouraging, but mostly it is a challenge and an
adventure. As I study and pray and learn from the ministry of others
I can see myself growing and maturing in my own understanding. There
are times when it seems that life would be much easier if I didn't
have the responsibility of a priesthood office l?iut then when I attend
an event such as World Conference or the
and see the quality and dedication of the people who constitute this receni Living Faith conference
church, I feel very blessed to be a member of the Saints' church and
a part of the priesthood.

My ordination was approved by de Moines State Conference in October of 1985. I was ordained in January 19 1986 by my uncle Dwayne field and my husband Thomas mccluer, to the opposite of priest. At the time I was called, My husband was called to the Office of Elder he had been serving in the office of priest. In 1970. I was still in college when my chosen field was not working out the way I'd hoped. I quit college and started my family, a man my family, the center of my life and put their wants and needs first. I was active for a few years when my boys were small and my husband was not a member. I started attending regularly and teaching Sunday school when the boys were about four or five. Soon my husband started going occasionally. Then there was a chance for my husband to change jobs the changement selling our house and moving to a small town with no our LDS church, while waiting for a house to sell. My husband started cottage meetings, he was baptized and we decided we would try whatever work God had for us to do. We found a house on an acreage I thought was what we needed. And while praying about it, I was told we would not be moving. The reason I was given was that I would bury myself in the country working for my self and my family and church would not be attended. I was also told he had waited too long for me to to come as far as I had and I must not slide back because there was work for me to do. We didn't move. My husband quit that job and found work in Des Moines again. I also went to work for the first time since I was married. which put me around a lot of different people every day. We became more involved in church and at Northwest and my husband was ordained as a priest and my ordination service for wards and counting myself and the pastor was set aside. My husband and another man ordained to the Office of Elder I was named to the Office of priests and the pastor's wife was ordained to the Office of Deacon ordination prayer answered some of the worries I came away assured that the Lord would give me the strength they needed to do the work he had for me. I did don't think my views on women's ordination have changed. I felt it was right when I heard about it. I didn't feel it would include me. The only change is I felt more impatient with those who still refuse to accept it for their denying themselves and others so as much the First Communion Sunday after my ordination, I was asked to serve communion. There was another woman serving and a woman up front that day, I was concerned because I knew some were against women in the President's are more undecided. As I served I prayed and I two or three hesitated, but no one refused the bread and no one hesitated when I served with the wine. This spring My husband was asked to be Pastor Perry I Indiana he asked me to serve communion the first Sunday, we were there again. I knew there were those against and those undecided in the congregation. The one man president that day who was undecided was seated where I would be serving. I could see the struggle he was having and the expression on his face when he realized I was seated. He was seated where I will be serving. As with the first time at Northwest Indiana, I said short, a short prayer and left it with God. The man accepted the bread, the wine and me. Most people have responded positively lead to me since my call there have been no direct personal attacks those who are strongly against women in the priesthood have seemed to try to pretend I don't exist. I tried to make a point of speaking to them, so at least they have to acknowledge my presence. After my first sermon, I was hugged and encouraged by many in the congregation since I am not a public speaker and I'm very uncomfortable in front of a group. This was very important to me. My father is a non member. Our relationship has been strained for years. But when my daughter was born four years ago, things seem to be getting better than my call came and there is that distance between us again. He has never talked about it, but I don't think he knows exactly how to react to me. I see my ministry at this time as a quiet ministry listening and helping where I can I teach Sunday school and take more of a serving role in worship services rather than a leading role. I think the last few years I have come to see ministry as a day to day ongoing thing, a lifestyle not just something that is done when asked or just on Sunday, from the front of the sanctuary or even just by a priesthood. Ministry is seeing and meeting the needs of another person.

One of the first, in a lifetime of treasured blessings, was the opportunity
to be raised in a family who taught me to love my God and my church. From rny
earliest recollection, they provided the opportunities and the encouragement to
learn and grow and to develop the gifts within me. I never questioned or doubted
that "all are called according to the gifts of God unto them."
I have always felt there was a need for me to share the talents and gifts
God had entrusted to my care, and for which he has asked me to be accountable.
Thus, when my call to the office of priest was made known to me by David Ettinger
in late summer of 1986, I accepted it as yet another aspect of stewardship to be
explored and developed. The 1986 Fall Conference of the Omaha-Council Bluffs
Stake approved this call,and on November 16, 1986, I was set apart by spokesman
David Ettinger, assisted by Tim Arnold, before the witness of the Riverside Congregation of Council Bluffs, Iowa.
I attended Graceland College from 1971 to 1975, earning a B.A. in elementary
education. It was during this exciting time of growth and self-awareness, that
I had the opportunity to be an active participant in the "Graceland Functional
Stake." I served as a member of the Worship Commission with several others from
all over the states. We combined our rich heritages and backgrounds with the
excited enthusiasm of our youth, and studied and explored the elements of worship.
We had at our disposal, a wealth of talented and gifted young men and women, the
wisdom of beloved faculty and sponsors, and the opportunity to create unique and
meaningful worship settings in and through which we could meet and come to know
our God. This opportunity was open to all, male or female, student or faculty,
member or non-member, and through the grace of God, all searched ... all learned ...
and all became.

1

_/

Upon graduation from Graceland, I moved to Council Bluffs, Iowa, and was
warmly accepted into the congregational family of Riverside. They were open
and receptive to my offerings and were not afraid to explore new and varied
opportunities in which, and through which,to worship. Thus, my spiritual life
continued to develop and flow much as it had while at Graceland.
In March 1978, I suffered a critical head injury in a sledding accident. I
regained consciousness after lying in a coma four days. The injury resulted in
an extensive portion of the left side of my brain being severely bruised. The
bruised area became a mass of scar tissue. By medical definition, I should
never have survived the accident. Because of the love and prayers of the Saints
both in the Omaha-Council Bluffs area as<well as those of friends and family
across the country, I not only survived it, God allowed me to experience personally a vital, meaningful understanding and awareness of the power to become.
The next two years were filled with many growing experiences and realizations,
but, of all I learned from and through them, there is one particular realization
I value above all. Many of us spend our lives, and our life's efforts, trying to
reclaim lost ground. We give our attention and efforts to returning to a certain
time or point in life when we felt we were at our best. Others delay ... they put
things off until a certain set of priorities or circumstances have been met. I
was wasting time attempting to return to who and what I had been when the accident occurred, thinking I could then complete the recovery process. I finally
realized what I needed to do,was use what I had at my command,to be my very best
for the moment, with the understanding that the opportunity to become even better
as I went on, always awaited me.
During the period of time I was considering accepting my call, this concept

2

was brought to my attention once again in a somewhat different light through
the sermon of a close friend. It focused on the idea that God is the center of

our existance. If we accept Him and place Him first in our lives, all other con-
cerns and conflicts we face 1will be resolved through Him. To have refused the

invitation to do God's will through this calling, would have been to deny the
beauty and purpose of all that had brought me to this point in time. I saw it
as an opportunity to be accountable for my life and its purpose. To have turned
from this call would have been the same as turning from the God who had created
me, loved me, guided and prepared me through all those other experience in my life.
My ordination service was a beautiful confirmation, challenge and sending
forth into this new avenue of service. It is our practice at Riverside 1 to open
our worship services with a hymn sing to direct our attention in preparation for
that which is to come in the hour ahead. I requested four hymns which acknowledged the power of God as our source of inspiration and strength, and confirmed,
in song, the commitment I was making to the task of accepting others, caring for
them, and moving out with them in the process of becoming. The verses of "My
Shepherd Will Supply My Need", "Help Us Accept Each Other", 11

0 Holy Dove", and
"All to Jesus I Surrender", not only spoke to the intent of the commitment I was
making, but offered me hope and assurance that I would not be alone as I moved
out in my response to God's invitation to serve Him and assist with His work.
My father, mother, and grandmother journeyed from Penn:;Jylvania to join my
sister, who resides in Omaha, and me for the service of ordination. We were
also joined by a cousin and two of his children from Lamoni1

and another cousin
from Independence. Each of these individuals played an important role in my
early development, and each had been and remains an important influence in my

3

testimony of the Church.
A man who, at the time of my accident, had come each day to the hospital to
offer encouragement and support, was in attendance with his wife. I had shared
meaningful growing experiences with all involved in the service that morning,
except the speaker for the hour. The ministry of music was offered by a friend
who wasn't even aware of his gift of song until a few short years ago. I coaxed
him into singing with me to begin, and through those early attempts we discovered
a rich and vibrant gift of song. I asked him to share the song he did the first
time he sang solo .... "Be Still and Know That I am God." As his voice filled the
church, I was touched by the memory of the experiences shared, which had brought
both of us to this particular point in our lives, and was moved by the lyrics
and the counsel they offered.
Tim Arnold who presided that day, as well as assisted David during the actual
ordination, had been a member of the sledding party the day I suffered the accident. He had immediately knelt in the cold snow and administered to me, asking
God to bless me and care for me. He has offered that same prayer for me many
times through the years which have followed. The gentleman who brought the message
that morning was a stranger to me, but his words of challenge and support were
exe11plary of the bond we share in Christ through the church community.
I chose David to offer my ordination prayer for several reasons.
worked together often for the growth and development of the congregation.
We had
We had
planned and executed many stake and congregational endeavors. We shared a kindred
spirit of hope and expectation for the church,and respected and valued the talents
each had to contribute to the church's purpose and cause. We were seated together
at World Conference the afternoon conference delegates accepted Section 156. I
remember turning to him and remarking, "We just made history," to which he replied

4

" ... and they'll hold us responsible for the rest of our lives." It seemed only
fitting that as I accepted this responsibility, I be set apart by the servant

through which God's call had been extended, and one who had been such an influ-
ential catalyst in my spiritual growth.

David spoke of how much it was a privilege and pleasure to be witness to
this ordination, and the joy that came from seeing and understanding the beauty
of God's plan of priesthood. He noted how, through the years, through the urgings
of the good spirit, my parents, family and friends had ministered to me in preparation for this time. Throughout this time, I grew and matured in my responses to
my family, to the opportunities I experienced at Graceland, but most specifically,
through my response to my congregation. Because of these experiences throughout
my life, I now, of my own free will, accepted God's call. A call was being
extended to them as well, as they were asked to support my efforts in these new
avenues of ministry. The beauty of God's plan of priesthood is understood when
we realize some are called to specific responsibilities, but all are called to
give service unto their God.
I have always believed women have valuable ministry to offer the church and
its members. At first I was ready and willing to support other women in this
endeavor, but felt no need for a priesthood call to be fullfilled in the service
I was giving. I've come to realize it has nothing to do with "my" need or desire.
I was called to respond to the needs of others by my God, to lend my services to
His cause, not for my glory or prestige, but for His glory and His honor.
I f:iJnd the greatest negative factor I face is myself. I have no difficulty
loving the lovable ... those who allow me to create and grow ... those who understand
and share similar priorities in their outlook on life.

5

My biggest frustration, and the point where I continue to fall short of my
purpose, is in my work environment. I am a senior underwriter for a leading

insurance company. A good portion of my time is spent in training and develop-
ment of new underwriters and keeping experienced underwriters updated on

procedures.
The corporate philosophy promotes motivation ... self-actualization ... and the
need to do better and promote oneself, but corporate structure often seems
closed to their own philosophy. My greatest struggle lies in attempting to
carry out Company dictates.
I often find these dictates in opposition to my.own priorities,and have
difficulty motivating my people to carry out instructions I don't see as being
valid myself. I'm struggling to learn how to compromise without denying my
values and perspectives,or my belief that within each of us is the potential
to become something more.or something better than we are at any given point in I I
time.
I have been grateful for the congregation's response to,and acceptance of
my call. I have not been faced with the turmoil and dissention others have had
to deal with as we move into this new dimension of ministry. I called home to
share the news with my parents and after conversing with other family members,
finally managed to get them on the extensions simultaneously. "I've some good
news to share," I began. "I've been called to the office of Priest." ...... The
silence lengthened. "Mom? ... Dad? ... " My mother was the first to respond,
voicing her support and encouragement. The silence continued. "Dad?" ... ''Wayne?"
prompted my mother. "I suppose they figure if the men can't do it right, the
women can." Never hesitating, my mother calmly said, "Maybe they figure they
can do it better ... together." We had a very meaningful conversation when I

6

) pb - 1

returned home some time later. We discussed events which had led to this time
of change in the life of the Church, and I shared with them the opportunities
which were now before me. It was a time of meaningful growth for each of us.
As I stated earlier, they had taught me to love my God and my Church ... they were
now being called to support me in my response to both.
Following the birth of my brother's second child, it was my privilege to
return home to share in Danielle's blessing. Having been asked to share some
thoughts to focus the congregation's attention on the blessing, I brought my remarks
to a close indicating it was my prayer that Danielle's blessing might be a challenge to each of us. That we would grow in our awareness and understanding of
our call to be agents of influence and example in each other's lives. That the
bonding process of God's love would strengthen and sustain us as we strive for
new and better dreams each day of our lives, and realize the potential that
lies within each of us to achieve these dreams.
The beloved congregation who were my first chwrch family, received me with
the same love and acceptance they had shown me in the days of my childhood. I
hope each one present realized the influence their lives had had on me then, but,
more importantly have now ... as I continue to draw from those past experiences
and from the heritage they created in many of my present endeavors.
As I grow in my awareness and understanding of God's presence in my life,
I'm finding that He calls us to new life through the Christ. This new life calls
us to be creative, to make intelligent choices by learning to use the gift of our
agency wisely,as we minister to those around us, and to be accountable for our
response to His presence in our lives.
He has blessed each of us with gifts and talents and has asked us to be

accountable for their develoμnent and use. He has need of all our potential, not
just a select few. He invites all of us to be actively engaged in His work.
I pray that with each new day that awaits me, I will choose to reach out in
faith to those around me, and share with them the good news of the new life which
can be ours through Jesus Christ.

When the subject of the ordination of women began to be an issue, I was
not actively for it. However, I kept an open mind and the thought of
ever leaving the church because of it never crossed my mind. Some of my
friends were wholeheartedly in favor of the issue as well as some of the
women's rights movements. I have worked all my life and have never been
discriminated against seriously because I was a women. I had no quarrel
with the differences of men and women. I felt quite comfortable with
only men holding the priesthood. I never felt deprived because I could
not hold an office. At various times, I served as Women's Leader,
organist, church school teache, secretary/treasurer and most recently
as the singles advocate. In two congregations I was an unofficial
"pastor's counselor". A long time ago, a close friend of mine who was
an elder, suggested that I should be in the priesthood and I dismissed
the idea as being absurd.
As time went on and the discussion continued regarding priesthood for

-I

women, my Father and I came to the conclusion that it might be conceivable.
However, there would have to be a new revelation. I was personally
acquainted with several women who I thought would be prime candidates for
priesthood office.
When Section 156 was presented and approved, I accepted it, although I
still had a few reservations. Since I do believe in continuing revelations,
I would have to come to grips with it. God has always worked with me
through the testimonies of others. Very few timeshave I had the experience
with the Holy Spirit as others have had. This time was no exception. I was
very moved by the testimonies of others regarding the rightness of the
document. I was touched by the testimonies of the senior Saints regarding
this.If I believed anything of what I had been taught about our doctrine and
the workings of God and Christ, how could I not accept this too? However,
while it may be right for some women, I knew that I would not have to be
concerned because I would never be a candidate. I was divorced, did not
have a college education and felt extremely unqualified.
Since my divorce, I felt deprived because I had not obtained an education.
When the Temple school courses became available in field study in our
District, I took many of them. I began to learn a lot. For one Distrit~t
Conference, I signed up for a counseling class. It was canceled and The
Role of Pastor was substituted. I did not feel it was right for me to take
the class, but our pastor insisted that I take to course. Of course, I was
glad I did as it increased my understanding of priesthood. At that time,
our pastor had submitted my name as a being called to the priesthood even
though I knew nothing about it.
In 1986, my son David Mellendorf was pastor of the South Bend congregation
and I was Women's Leader. It was not unusual for he and his counselors to
request meeting with me. In June of that year, my son asked me to meet
with he and High Priest Dean Cox. At that moment, I knew exactly what he
was going to tell me and in my mind I said, ''Oh no, Lord, not me.'' They
did tell me that Dean had received the call while he was still pastor and
the call was confirmed by Dean as well as my son and High Priest John Drader.
I was told to pray about it and let them know my decision. I knew in my
heart right then that if I truly believed in God and Jesus Christ that there
could be only one decision. I still felt very unworthy and could think of

Section 156 is a thrilling new dimension in the ministry of Christ. My
present attitude surprises some people who have known me all of my life, for I
have joyfully lived out the traditional role of women. My only time of employment outside the home has been the period of time that my husband was a full.-
time student at Graceland. I was a part-time student, homemaker and bread winner
during those years.
When we moved to Florida both of us became active in the Pensacola Branch.
He served as a priest, then as an elder for about twenty years. He was brought
up as the member of another church. My dad baptized him during our first year of
marriage. We have been married for thirty-nine years. Our finest experience
was when I was ordained under his hands and those of Evangelist-Patriarch
Donald J. Baldwin.
Another wonderful experience that we shared was a few months ago. He baptized one of our grandsons, I another. He confirmed both of them. We anticipate
baptizing others.
Through the years I have been contented and productive in the church, home
and community. I developed as many useful skills as possible and made my contribution through them. One that has opened many doors is my work in youth camping
and leadership in the women's department. I have written for several publications
within and outside the church. I also wrote a book about camping, "Nature Lore
Tastes Good". Intrestenly enough, the book has enabled me to share my testimony
of Christ with the community. I was brought up in the cradle of the Restoration
movement in the Deep South, Lone Star. In fact the first church in this area
was established there in my fore parents home. They were William and Martha Booker.
From my earliest years I was steeped in the message of Christ to his people.
Many offspring of William and Martha have remained faithful to the gospel.
A large number served in the priesthood. My dad and brother-in-law were branch
presidents at Lone Star. Both brothers, two brothers-in-law, four nephews
youngest sister are functioning in the priesthood.

I was called to the Office of Elder Christmas Day 1985. My call was approved in the congregation of Greenville, Michigan,

then by the Grand Rapids district of the Michigan region. I was ordained on May 18 1986 by my husband Roger Meyer. He was assisted by Patriarch Barry and Bowman and my cousin Tom banks. My husband was an elder at the time and also our pastor.

The call to the priesthood was no surprise to me and to the people who know me. I've been very involved in the church since I was a very young child prayer has been an integral part of my life in a special way. I've also wanted to come wanted to be where the scenes are. This has taken me into leadership roles also since I was very young since graduating from high school in 1989 71. I've worked at many youth camps taught classes at reunions in Michigan, Oregon and Washington attended five youth leadership training camps and work on nearly 30 witnessing weekends. Everywhere I've worked, I've been involved in leadership and every situation I found myself in has brought me closer to my heavenly Father. In the time leading up to my ordination, I was very concerned that the people who did not yet understand section 156 would have their own testimony. I spent a great deal of time in prayer for the people of my congregation and district at time as time to close for the service. I felt a great need for others to have an experience. I thought perhaps this could happen somehow in the service. I was the very front was the first woman called in my district and another woman in my congregation and I were to be ordained the same day. We knew there were many people coming for the service My prayer for others took me to the point of offering to the Lord to give others my strength, my faith and let me carry their burden. The Lord answered this prayer, all during the service. I felt no presence of the Spirit at all. In fact, I was bored with the sermon, which spoke to us to the service would never be overall. Others tell me I shook so hard that my dress flutter this was a service that everyone said had a tremendous outpouring of the Spirit that the sermon was magnificant most importantly, many people shared with Liz, the other ordained, and with me how their heart was changed during that service that they also had testimony of each of our calls. The Lord gave me strength, my strength to someone who needed it. But when Roger put his hands on my head and began to pray, the Spirit touched me as well. Some of the phrase in that in that prayer were identical to the ones in my patriarchal blessing. This is added testimony to me. The only other elders able to act be active in my congregation are very busy patriarch and my husband who is now a seventh tree. These two men have gone quite often in January and February of 1987. They were gone several weekends that left me the only elder available due to some illnesses among other priesthood. Also two different Sundays I was left to preach sir communion sang in choir and played the Oregon for the service took community out to sick in the afternoon and had several administrations. At one point that winter I had prayer several service on service on Wednesday, preach through Sundays and took care of the things listed above. The Holy Spirit was gracious. I never lacked for words or energy also the congregation began to see me as a leader and allowed me to pastor them. This was very important growth for now I am the pastor for 1988 I have been very richly blessed. I've been many rich experiences in preaching and administration to the SEC in particular, others have responded well to me and have allowed me to serve the first summer I was ordained I was asked to share a testimony about my call it the opening service of the reunion. Following this, a woman came to me and said that I was just awful. I had no right to be in the priesthood and that I should give back my priesthood card. And go on as before she felt this quite strongly. I just smiled and said I would like to share my testimony with her sometimes, if she would allow this. There hasn't been much else negative said to me. Ministry is nearly everything I do or should be I realize now that I'm the priesthood people watch everything I say and do and hold us to it. That makes me everything. I'm called to care for God's children in a way that's needed. That means I go out on administrations late at night I take a meal into someone that is sick care for someone's children. I clean the church, teach, preach administer the sacraments of the church and do everything that needs to be done. Several years ago, I thought ministry was when you were upfront or went to visit or had someone from church over for dinner or when we're on staff at a camp, but I've had a growing awareness of the ministry. It is never ending and not always what I what I feel like it some of the significant points of growth may have been following the crisis we've been through. My husband had cancer all through him, underwent surgery and chemotherapy. The first year we were married here nearly died four times that year and the doctors give God all the credit for his life. We adopted two girls and the agony that followed it was necessary to give back one of the girls wanting to have children so badly and not being able to was a tremendous growth. My husband had two heart attacks and Emergency Bypass Surgery when he was only 37 years old. He should have died then also but he didn't this past year he was called Norgate seven tree. This past year he was called and ordained a 70. The past five years of his pastor ship all of these were points of tremendous growth and struggle. I wish there was time to share the story of each situation and the blessing we received. God has been so very good to us. I want to help others find the joy in Jesus Christ. I've learned how to carry heavy burdens. I've tried to help lighten others loads

Christ of Latter Day Saints, on March 16, 1987, at Bluff

the call of women to the various priesthood offices in the
church,

should call to be able to serve in the different priesthood

near my 70th birthday, I didn't expect it for me. I had no
great spiritual experience that assured me of a call
only came as our pastor, Elder Myron Salter, met with me in
- that
private to discuss my call, As he told me of his assurance of

strongly as a revelation of truth that I had to express it to
Brother Myron, As I did, he said, "But there are 2 other
women called." I was very pleased with that fact, but I knew
and trusted what God had revealed to me, that in some way I
was to '~laze the trail" for others. I never asked or knew
until my name was brought before our congregation for approval
that I was the only one who accepted the call.
Before my call I had been arising at 5 AM to study, pray,
and meditate for an hour or more and had enjoyed this way
starting the day. After the call I pursued my study with
of

even greater determination and joy, and took advantage
Temple School courses that were offered in our area and at
of the

be able to follow the leadings of God's Spirit however
would have me to serve Him and His Church,

Like Nephi, I can begin my testimony by saying that I was born of goodly
parents and from infancy was taught the gospel and to love my Lord. I had
the privilege of attending an active congregation in England where the
ministry of all people, regardless of sex or age, was accepted. I was
nurtured by the Saints and encouraged to serve. I had always had a desire to
do what I thought the Lord ~ted me to do, and this desire undergirded my
actions during most of my life, although= I have failed at times.
In 1962, I went to the United States in order to attend Graceland College
where I met the man who was to become my husl:Bnd. When Meredith and I were
married in 1963, we made a commitment to serve the Lord through the organization of the church, and both of us have had many opportunities to do so.
I have taught Sunday s!}fool, organized Vacation Church Schools and Junior
camps and been a branch and district women's leader. We lived and servled
in Iowa, Arizona and Kansas before moving to the Federal Republic of Germany
in 1981. I never saw a need for ordination in my life. I did not desire it.
I had lots to do as a non-ordained person and never lacked for opportunities
to express and use my gifts and talents.
One comment which Meredith and I had made early in our married life was
seriously challenged, however, in 1984. We had said that no matter what
happened in the church--through all its problems and challenges--if we
were in doubt about the path to take, we would follow the prophet. I had
never expected i that~he proμ1et would ever bring inspired counsel to the
church which would open up the sacrament of ordination to women.
Meredith had the privilege of attending most of the 1984 World Conference
while I stayed at home in Germany with our three children. When he returned
home, he brought with him a copy of the document which had become Section 156
of the Doctrine and Covenants. My heart burned within me as I read the counsel
regarding the temple and the responsibility of the priesthood, but it

plummeted into my shoes when I read paragraph nine. How could the Lord
change his mind? Why had he decided to change the status quo? These were
some of the questions which were going through my mind. For several days
after reading this revelation, I wept as I worked around the house. I
2

would be doing dishes, making beds or vacuuming the rugs with tears streaming
down my cheeks. I drove the car to work with the tears flowing,a nd in the
middle of watching a television program, I would feel the tears begin to
flow. Why? Why? Why, Lord?
After a couple of weeks, I realized that my concern over one section in
the Doctrine and Covenants was hindering me from bringing the service to my
family and congregation that I should have been bringing. And so I made a
pact with the Lord. I was going to leave the whole problem in his hands.
There was much to be done, and I needed to be doing my share. So, until
He decided to call me to the priesthood (if He should ever decide to do so)
I was not going to worry about it anymore. I was going to continue to do
~tever I could do. But if God ever wanted me to accept a priesthood call,
He was going to have to give me an unshakeable testimony of its validity.
To say that I did not worry about it again is not exactly true. I
continued to pray for a testimony from time to time, but when the testimony
did not come, I would lay& the matter aside and continue to fulfill my
responsibilities to the best of my capabilities.
One day early in August of 1986, I was driving home from work and was
thinking again about the ordi':11tion of women. As I prayed for a testimony,
a sweet, calm and peaceful Spirit entered the car and made me aware that if
I should be called to the priesthood it would be all right to accept the call.
Three weeks later, Jerry Shelton, the pastor of our Stuttgart congregation,

3
came to me and told me that I had been called to the office of priest. I
askea/£or a few days to consider my response, and after discussing it with
my family and praying as fervently as iμave ever prayed about anything, I
told him that I would accept the call. But where was my unshakeable
testimony? Did I have to take a step in faith without reassurance? The
reassurance, when it came, was always a peaceful, calm Spirit.
My call was approved by our small German-American group in Stuttgart
and by the participants at our military retreat in Be=htesgaden, Germany.
On October 4th, 1986, I was ordained by my husband and my uncle, Harold
Woodhouse. Both men had held the office of elder for many years. The
service was a beautiful experience. High Priest Hendrik Campier of the
Netherlands gave the charge to Jim Graham and myself (Jim was also ordained
to the office of priest a~this time,) and the ordination prayer by Meredith
was a moving and yet challenging statement. But still I had not received
my dream, vision or "hand-writing-on-the-wall" experience.
I had received a letter from Patria=h Edgar Holmes of England. He
had been the pastor of my home congregation, Sutton-in-Ashfield, during most
of my growing-up years. In this letter, he told me that I would receive
assurance of my call as I reached out in ministry. So I entered a new
avenue of service. I believe that I have been a minister for many years.
When a person serves the physical, emotional or spiritual needs of another
as a follower of our Heavenly Father, he or she is a minister. But now
other areas of ministry had been opened to me. I could now offer to others
some of the ordinances of the church. Within a short period of time, I had
opportunities to minister as a priest in Germany, the Netherlands and in
England. Each time, I felt the same calm and peaceful Spirit which I had
experienced in the car.

4
Several weeks later, I was reading my patriarchal blessing, when I read
again instruction which had been given to me as a teenager--that when I
do those things which are in accordance with God's will, I will have the
peace that passeth all understanding. This has been my experience. I have
served cormmmion to a group of less than a dozen people in Germany; I have
spoken in the Netherlands and in England to groups of over 200; I have
ministered in English and in German. On each occasion, the Spirit of peace
and calm was my companion.
In the fall of 1988, I had the privilege of being the spokesperson in
the ordination of Sister Thea Sofke to the office of teacher and of Brother
Henry Schlenker to the office of deacon. These are experiences which I will
always treasure. I felt a tremendous responsibility because the Church was
being committed, through my actions, to bestow priesthood authority on my
sister and brother. I do believe, however, that I was used as an instrument
by my Heavenly Father to bestow his authority on them to act in accordance
with the rights, responsibilities and privileges of their respective offices.
I know that some members of the Church are still struggling with the
idea of ordaining women. There are members of my family who continue to
struggle, and I can empathize with them. It is hard to leave behind the
traditions which we have held and cherished for so long, but when those
traditions are not in line with God's will, they must be changed.
I am thankful to my Heavenly Father that instead of giving me the one
"earthshaking" experience which I desired (and which I~ have tried to use
to convince others of the validity of my call) that he gives me a continuing
testimony as I serve through his Spirit which accompanies my efforts.

I was born into a family who embraced the Anglican faith. I was the
middle child, having an older brother and a younger sister. I was baptized
shortly after birth into that faith. However, confirmation wasn't allowed
before 12 years of age, and so I had to wait for that. Actually, since
there was a lot of confusion in my family years later, and a resultant
separation of my parents, I was about 14 or 15 years old before I was
confirmed in the Anglican Church. I thoroughly enjoyed going to Church,
and remember many times when my mother and I would go to the evening
service, just the two of us, and I would lay my head on the fur collar
of her coat. It was such a peaceful feeling and with the candles glowing
on the alter, it was a beautiful experience. I began to develop a strong
faith in God.
When the time came to be married some years later, my husband and I were
married in the Anglican Church, and I felt that even though he was R.L.D.S.
that I could win him over and make him see that "my" church was the right
one to attend. We started out our marriage by alternating Churches; one
week we would go to mine, and the next week we would go to his, but he
quickly got tired of this, and since he didn't like so much getting up
and down in the services at my Church, or so many litanies, he stopped
going, and rather than be separated on Sunday mornings, I began going to
his Church. I had attended his Church with him several times while we were
dating, but I thought it was so small and so plain, although I did enjoy
the friendliness of the people.
We had many discussions after the services concerning what had been said
in the sermons etc., especially where "revelation" was concerned, because I
believed that it was impossible for God to speak directly to individuals
in our day and age. I did however, believe in the influence of the Holy
Spirit. As I studied and searched for things that could disprove the
R.L.D.S. Church to myself and my husband, I began to see more things that
were right with it, and eventually, I had to face up to the fact that I had
to make a choice. This was very difficult because I loved my Church, and
did not want to let go of it. When we were married almost three years, we
attended an R.L.D.S. Missionary Series with Seventy Al Pelletier, which
was held in the Park Theatre in Chatham, Ontario. In the preceding year
my husband had come through a serious bout with peritonitis where he had

During this period of time, my personal life seemed to go into upheaval
and for a period of two or three years I struggled with anxiety and depression which were related to an unresolved and unexpressed grief over the
break up of our family life when I was 11 or 12 years of age, and my mother's
subsequent death at the age of 49. It began at the end of my pregnancy for
our second child, a daughter, and doctors seemed unable to give me any
answers or much help. It turned out later that I had some problems with hypoglycemia which aggravated the situation. There were days when I could not
function in my duties of housewife and mother without going to the Lord in
fervent prayer several times a day. He was my help and my strength, and I
was able to go about my tasks so that no one (except my husband, of course),
knew how much I was struggling.
I appealed to the Lord to renew a right spirit within me, and that if he
would do this I would serve Him for the rest of my days in whatever way I
could to the best of my ability. I really felt that I was already doing
this, but I was bargaining for my life, and I was very serious. I read
the Scriptures diligently and tried to fill my mind and soul with every
positive thing that I could find concerning love, faith and the strength of
the Lord. He revealed these things to me through the choir which I was

be able to bring a strength and faith and joy to the ministry which will
I will

I believe that Ministry is basically being a friend and helping those
who express needs in specific ways, and endeavouring to perceive
unexpressed needs. I also believe that Ministry is setting an example
before the world of the way Christ told us to live. I believe that Ministry
is trying to bring out the best in people, whether in attitude or talent,
and endeavouring to bring these things to the worship of God. I believe
that Ministry is endeavouring to engender co-operation and love in the
congregation and also in the priesthood, so that jealousy and suspicion
are kept to a minimum, and we are all able to grow in an atmosphere of trust
and comraderie. I believe that Ministry is encouraging people and lifting
them to greater heights of worship through teaching them prayer and the
results prayer can have in their lives if they could be sincere and consistent. I believe that Ministry is bringing us all to the Lord, through
rep.entance and desire to grow in a new and better way.
I believe that the specific office of Priest which I hold demands that I
get as close to the people of the congregation as possible, and encourage
them to pray and to surrender their lives and the circumstances of their
lives to the Lord, so that his Holy Spirit may reveal to them the ways
in which they too can be effective ministers through using their talents
and gifts for the benefit of all people. I believe that it calls me to
preach, teach, expound and exhort the things that I perceive to be of
the Lord, through study and prayer, as a means of encouraging all of us

WHAT A PRIVlLEGE TO LOVE THE LORD

Testimony by Elaine (nee Carson) Olson

Although the period from 1970 to now has been one of
tremendous growth for me, it would be difficult to pinpoint
anything in the past seventeen years that has attributed
more to my ordination than what happened in the first decade
of my life, or the second, the third or the fourth.
Naturally the first decade began with my birth into a

poverty-stricken family, in a pioneering prairie agricult-
ural area of Saskatchewan. Our life revolved around the

church. Except for the mundane facts of life: Dad earning
the livelihood; Mom laboring endlessly to keep house, sew
the clothes, seed and weed the ganlen and direct the lives
of her family, the church was our life.
Walking to school, combatting epidemics of flu and
diphtheria, practicing for Christmas concerts, playing ball,
and initiating our own entertainment in a world devoid of
radio, television, electricity or modern plumbing, seemed
to be only side effects as we rotated around the church,
the centrifugal force that moulded us.
Visiting our relatives and friends, our conversations,
and our play were church-oriented. We played Sunday School,
we played baptism, we held mini-preaching services and
funerals. Every little chicken, kitten or puppy that died
received the honor of a burial with an attending service.
We always opened with "Jesus Loves Me", included "Bring Them
In" somewhere on the agenda, and closed with "God Save the
King".

\

I v1 -2-

As Uncle John and Aunt Nellie Tomlinson, our pastor and
his wife, visited with us; or the missionaries came to see
us, we children listened wide-eyed and open-eared to e~ery
word of testimony. To me these were God's men. They were
my heroes and, early in life, at least by age six or seven,
I longed to be one of them. I wanted to carry God's banner

like Moroni; I wanted to be sensitive to God's Spirit call-
ing me to go here or go there as Apostle J. F. Curtts testi-
fied so many times. I wanted to open missions like Paul and

Brother J.J. Cornish. I wanted to preach and to teach. I
wanted to be God's man!
But - I was a girl! Mom and Dad brought forth five
strong, healthy, wrestling, whistling, outdoor, freckle-
~aced boys. When it was my turn, they changed the pattern
and I came out a child of a different slant. "Sit like a
lady", 111:eep your skirt down", "Don't wrestle with the
boys", "Brush your hair", and "Don't shout". My sister
Alice was my constant companion, and Renie a sometimes
reluctant tag-a-long. I'm sure we coached baby brother Ken
on our version of ministry and admonished hir,1 to "prepare".
In my deepest longings I envied my brothers.
Early in my second decade there developed a pattern of
life by which I live: an issue; a prayer about the issue;
a definite answer or a caution to wait and continually pray.
It began with a lost pen, an award given to me for having
made the most progress at school in Grade Three. I was
devastated when I lost it and, day after day, week after week,

1
I / o 1 -3-

through the winter months, I prayed, "Please help me find

patches of snow holding out, I suggested to Alice that'we
walk home from school on the road followed by my father as
he drove us to and from school in the winter. Usually we
took a diagonal short cut through the pastureo We walked
the long way home and, on a small patch of snow beside the
winter track, I found my pen.
What joy, what thankfulness, and what a lasting boost to
my faith that God hears our prayers!
The first and foremost issue of my life was the desire
to serve as a minister. I had stopped asking God to change
me into a boy, but even at that tender age I knew effective

hard at school. I heard about Graceland and a new issue for

occurred to me how impossible that would be from where we

This was a long term target indeed, covering my life
from about Grade IV to high school graduation in 1936, In

Mom and Dad left the hopeless farming situation to go to the

all those growing children to the wicked city".
The move was "God's mysterious way" to help answer my

Merlin was a priest when we were married and was called

Our son Gary and his wife Joan have two children and they

God knows the concerns we felt as we allowed them to grow

I have always enjoyed relatively good health. Far from
~eing athletic, as a youth I had played soccer, softball,

"last straw" that broke my health.
At any rate, teaching school, family responsibilities,

I ended up in the hospital with what was diagnosed at thAt

''Thou art priv11ged to live in momentous times, in the day of
preparation, spoken of by prophets of old, for the preparation
of His people is now on, and you can have a part with that great
people if you shall desire to advance the interests of his work
in every way,'' and that has been my desire. But I never dreamed
it would involve a call to the priesthood.
But one morning, the telephone rang. It was our pastor Charles
(Chuck) Tripp. He said he would like to come and talk with me that
morning. I told him that I would be home and invited him to come.
After I hung up the telephone, a little feeling of uneasiness came
to me, but I thought surely not, not at my age, seventy-nine.

So when he walked in with some papers and booklets, the uneasy feel-
diminished as I thought, ''He must have come to talk about Junior

Church and children's activities. 11
I invited him to sit at the dining room table where he could
spread out his pap~s. Then he told me,-told me that he had had a
beautiful experience one night in which it had been made known to
him that I was called to the office of teacher, and that this call
had been processed and had received administrative approval

and clearance. I was totally unprepared. I had had no previous in-
timation of this, except for that feeling of uneasiness.

But as Chuck opened one of the booklets that he had brought,
which I recognized as the booklet we had studied at a district
workshop, Guideljoe.s f.□ r f:x.te.s.t.trn.od, and began to read to me the
specific duties of the office of teacher and came to the sentence,

''The teacher 1s an advocate of full participation, healthy relation-
shios, and development of a healing, redeeming attitude among the

Saints,'' the Lord let me know that He knew that these were things
I would feel comfortable with and have joy in doing. So I told
Chuck that I would accept the call and the responsibilities that
go with this office, trusting that the Lord would be with me, for
it is still my desire to advance His work in every way.
Chuck told me of the three Temple School courses that were
required for preparation for ordination. As I studied these,

pa rt i cul ar l y the courses on The Te ache r and In tr oduc t iQ...n j;_o f,l_i nis t_i::_y,
I was made aware in a way I had never sensed before, the burden of
responsibility that all these years had been put on the shoulders
of the men of the priesthood. It was similar to the awareness that Arnold Dvcrn
came to me when my husband 4 passed away thirty years ago and the
responsibility for a family of five sons fell on my shoulders. I
never fully appreciated how he felt. Similarly, I realized that
now God want~d women to share the burden of this responsiblilty.
In a small measure, as the wife of a priesthood member does, I had
shared his burden as he was called first to the office of priest,

and then elder. I will never forget the day when Apostle Ches-
worth told him that he was called to the office of patriarch. He

was fifty one years old. It was the year before he passed away.
At that time there were only eighty five patr~archs in the entire
church and he was awed to think that he, a convert to the church,

should be called to this office, especial1⁄2when he ca~e to real-
ize that there had not been a patriarch from this area since Hyrum

Smith. I remember how I shared this awe with him, wondering if
this was the way Mary felt when she was called to be the mother
of Jesus. And I had the privilege of typing for him the beautiful
blessings that were given through him.
As I thought about this call that had come to me, my mind
went back to the World Conference, two years before, when I came

early to the Auditorium for the first of the three Communion Ser-
vices. I sat in the row just behind the priesthood and as they

rose to participate in serving the emblems, I can remember think-
ing, "I am glad that there Olre no women among them. Mem give a

strength and dignity to priesthood that women can not give''
Then two days later, on Tuesday morning, President Sheehy

read the Inspired Document, now known as Section 156, that con-
tained the words, "In regard to the auestion of who shall be

called to share the burdens and resconsibilities of priesthood
in my church .... do not wonder that some women of the church are
being called to priesthood responsibilities. This is in harmony
with my will.'' It came, should I say, as a shock. You could feel
the stunned silence. When the leader of the caucus, who was a
womanJ was asked to open the assembly, she made the remark, ''Per
haps you feel as I do, that you would like to stop right here
and go home and spend time alone just between you and God.''

As I sat there thinking, I remembered that whenever the subject of ordination of women had come up, I always said, "If it

thought perhaps the need for ministry is going to be so great that

PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF ORDINATION

by

Patricia A. Page

Compiled for:
History Commission
RLDS Church
Independence, MO
August 16, 1987

On September 15, 1985, the Portland Metropole, Northwest
Pacific Region, met to consider three calls of women to the
priesthood. Each call was unanimously approved.
I was ordained to the Office of Elder on November 17,
1985 by Evangelist Patriarch Donald Balsiger and by my
husband, Seventy Greg Page. This took place at the Portland
Metropole in Portland, Oregon.
The ordination service was attended by persons from many
parts of Oregon and southwest Washington. There were those
who brought groups of children and youth to witness this
historical event. One local network in Portland sent a news
crew to interview the ordinands and other key people of the
church, and to videotape the service. There was excitement
and joyous anticipation in the air.
The theme of the service was "Touch Me Lord." The
opening hymn was Hymn #58, "Now is This Moment." Elder
George Hansen gave the invocation. The congregation then
sang a hymn of confession, Hymn #107, "The Love of God,"
which was followed by a congregational statement of
affirmation. Elder Robert Dixon shared the charge to the
ordinands, after which Hymn #363, "According to the Gifts
that God" was sung.
Following this hymn of ordination:
Katherine Gregory was ordained to the office of Elder by
Evangelist Patriarch Richard Hawes, Elder Chet Gregory
and Elder Oral Snively;
Christie Ostendorp was ordained to the office of Elder
by Elder John Berger and Elder Robert Dixon;

2

Patricia Page was ordained to the office
Evangelist Patriarch Donald Balsiger and
Page.

of Elder by
Seventy Greg
I experienced a loving, reassuring Spirit present during each
ordination. I had never before felt such a powerful yet
peaceful Spirit as I was being ordained.
Cathy Boyer provided the Ministry of Music following the
ordinations. Then the Portland Metropole President, Seventy
Greg Page presented each ordinand a rose and a Priesthood
Manual. After the presentation, the congregation read in
unison the following:
"The Spirit of the Lord God is with us. We have been
anointed to speak good tidings to the meek, to bind up
the broken-hearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives;
to give beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning,
the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness;
that we might be called the children of God."
Elder John Berger asked the congregation to take a moment to
write personal notes of support to the ordinands. These were
collected and given to the ordinands after the service. The
service ended with Hymn #467 "Lord of All Nations" and the
benediction by Elder Chet Gregory.
A reception followed the service to give individuals an
opportunity to share with the ordinands and their families
and to celebrate this historical occasion.
My ordination will always be a cherished experience.
The congregation was united in loving support, and their
excitement and enthusiasm are still carried within me.
As I consider life experiences which led me to my
ordination, I acknowledge a rich family heritage that goes
back many generations in the church. I went to Graceland

3

College where I met and married my husband, Greg. I found
him to be a companion who was strong in the faith and deeply
committed to the church. We combined our commitments and
have kept the church and the Living Christ a high priority in
our lives. We have served in a variety of capacities over
the years, and in June 1977 we accepted world church
appointment.
I felt led to change careers and develop the gifts and
skills God has given me. I left an upwardly-mobile position
in a large corporation where I was being groomed for
management, and I went back to school. I finished my
Master's degree in counseling and stretched in each
counseling position I have filled.
I learned to invite God's Spirit to assist me in every
work situation I faced. I have been confronted with my own
brokenness and need for healing and recovery. I have learned
to trust that the Living Christ is always there with me.
It was during the time that I went back to school that I
began to deal with women's issues in the world and in the

church. I recall going through a painful consciousness-
raising period of my life. It was during this time that I

began to question the exclusion of women from priesthood in
our church. I became acquainted with ordained women
ministers from some of the area churches. I experienced the
beautiful ministry they gave, which made me grieve even more
about our church's exclusion of women from priesthood.
I was very pleased when the church implemented the Faith

4

to Grow Program, for it facilitated an increased involvement
of all members in very significant ways. Yet, this did not
resolve my growing concern regarding female role models of
ministry in the church.
As I made it a matter of prayer, I became increasingly
aware that God was no respecter of persons and that I felt
reassurance that women were being prepared even then for this
expanded ministry. It never occurred to me that I was one of
those women. In hindsight, I realize it was too scary for me
to think I was being called.
I never considered myself as a trailblazer. I was
always grateful that I never lived in the early pioneer days.
I would have never moved west. I saw myself as one who stood
behind others giving them support and encouragement. I was
the cheerleader, but never the player. I was not one to go
through a newly-opened door first.
I attended the 1984 World Conference and was sitting in
the conference chamber when the document (which is now
Section 156 of the Doctrine and Covenants) was read to the
delegates. A hush swept over the delegates as President
Sheehy began reading. All of a sudden, I felt surrounded by
a calmness I had never previously experienced. Then I heard
the words that restated the restoration affirmation
statement: ''All are called ... ," and that some women were
being called to priesthood responsibilities. I sensed that
God was very close. I remember thinking, "Oh, Dear God, I've
been so angry.'' I sensed a response of grace and

5

understanding. I spent the week of Conference celebrating
with others who had been waiting for this new and exciting
direction of the church.
After the 1984 World Conference, I dedicated my efforts
to working with persons in my area who were struggling with
this change. I felt that my role was one of facilitating
reconciliation and preparing the way for the first women who
would be called in our area. It never occurred to me that I
would be one of those women.
Several months after the 1984 World Conference, I began
receiving impressions that I was being called to the
priesthood. My first response was to discount these
impressions and dismiss them. As these impressions continued
and became more specific, I began to protest to God that this
was some major mistake. After all, didn't God realize that I
did not have the courage nor the capability to blaze those
new trails and create new models of ministry. I was more
than happy to support other women--stronger women.
My protest increased as I sat in on discussions by
church members who stated things like: "the first women
ordained will be crucified!" or "the first women to be
ordained will have to face rejection and persecution."
One day I was sitting in my living room reading, when I
was tole by that still small voice to go read through my
great, great grandmother's papers which my grandfather had
sent me several months before. I went to the drawer where I
kept her papers and as I looked through them, I was startled

6

to find a poem I hadn't seen the first time I read her
papers. The poem was titled, "Gethsemane":
"The garden lies, strive as you may,
You cannot miss it on your way;
All paths that have been, or shall be,
Pass somewhere through Gethsemane.
''All those who journey, soon or late,
Must pass within the garden gate,
Must kneel alone in darkness there,
And battle with some fierce despair.
"God, pity those who cannot say,
'Not mine, but Thine,' who only pray,
'Let this cup pass,' and do not see
The purpose in Gethsemane."
I realized that I was battling with a fierce despair--all my
fears surrounding the call to priesthood. It felt too heavy.
But I could no longer deny that God was calling me to the
office of Elder. After rereading the poem, I prayed, asking
for God's forgiveness, and asking for God's help, because I
knew I could not do it without God. Again, I experienced a
beautiful spirit of peace and reassurance.
It was in August of 1985 that I was told of my call. I
accepted without reservation. Two of my dear friends were
also being called to the office of Elder. We spent the next
few months together preparing for ordination on November 17,
1985.
About three weeks after my ordination, I performed a
wedding for two dear friends whom I had introduced. It was
such a privilege to participate with them in this capacity.
Several weeks after my ordination, I flew to Kansas City
to participate in a baby blessing for my newest baby niece.

7

This event was special for many reasons. I was returning to
the congregation of my youth, Mission Road (formerly Prairie
Village Congregation). My grandfather, Francis J. Raw
assisted me. Grandpa was dying of cancer, but even though he
was very weak, he was determined to share in this holy
ordinance with me. My uncle, Howard Vaughan, also assisted.
This was such a joyous occasion for me and my family.
The Portland Metropole seemed eager to receive the
ministry of newly-ordained women. We three were asked to do
several administrations. The ordinance of administration was
one that felt very heavy to me, in terms of feeling
responsible for the outcome. With each new experience, the
Spirit helped me let this burden go, and my understanding
grew regarding this ordinance.
Perhaps the most important learning experience was when
I flew back again to Kansas City to see my grandfather who
was dying of cancer. He was semi-conscious and in pain. My
aunt asked if I would administer to Grandpa, assisted by my
uncle. I heard myself say 'yes' and at the same time panic
flooded my entire being. How could I give ministry at a time
when I was in need of comfort? I was broken-hearted to see
my grandfather so emaciated and struggling with every breath.
I wanted to run away or just become invisible. My inner
dialogue with God was one of pleading--please God, get me out
of this--I can't do this!
I looked around the room and saw members of my family,
each experiencing their own stage of grief. I was still

8

trying to figure a way out, when I realized my uncle was
offering the prayer of anointment. I felt weak and thought I
would faint. Then I said a quick, silent prayer to God:
"Dear God, I can't do this, so you will have to take over!''
As I placed my hands on my grandfather's head, the
weight of panic lifted immediately. I was filled with a
spirit of peace. I heard my voice pray for my grandfather.
I knew that God was preparing a place for him and that the
family was comforted by the reassurance that Grandpa would
feel no more pain or discomfort. I realized that I was given
this opportunity to connect with my grandfather on a deep
spiritual level. I was able to tell him what a privilege it
had been to know him and I thanked him for the rich heritage
he passed on to us. I was able to say good-bye.
The Spirit ministered to me as well as to my grandfather
and other family members. Grandpa died two days later.
I learned that I need to get myself out of the way so
God's spirit can provide ministry. I needed to let go of a
performance anxiety. I needed to "let go, and let God."
The Portland Metropole was a very supportive
congregation. They had unanimously approved the calls and
continued to be enthusiastic about "their women elders.'' I
never experienced any negative responses while ministering
there.
My husband and I received word that we were to be
reassigned to the San Diego District six months after my
ordination. We started learning that the San Diego area was

9

struggling over women in the priesthood. My old fears of
rejection surfaced. I wondered what would happen to my
ministry after we moved.
After we moved and settled in, I was approached by the
pastor of the branch we had transferred to, and he stated
that he would like to get me involved immediately. I was
pleased and somewhat relieved until he started warning me
that there might be some who would refuse to support my
ministry. I was introduced to my new branch by giving the
offertory statement and prayer. After the worship service,
several members came up to welcome me and share their
support. I was aware of others who avoided me, but this did
not bother me like I thought it would.
The first time I served communion in my new branch, only
one person got up and left. Again, I was surprised by my
response. I did not feel rejected--I felt compassion for the
person who left, for I discerned her pain and confusion.
At this same communion service, I recall serving a
husband and wife who were deeply moved by being served for
the first time by a woman. They held each others' hands and
looked up at me with such radiant smiles.
I remember preaching at another branch. I felt somewhat
concerned about the level of receptivity. When I stood to
begin the sermon, I felt a heavy weight of anxiety resting on
my shoulders. I read the opening scripture, and as I looked
around the congregation, I made eye contact with a young
girl. At first, I was startled by the look on her face--but

10

I realized that this look was one of immense joy as she
listened and observed me. I sensed affirmation and support
from this little girl, and my weight of anxiety lifted. God
ministered to me through this beautiful girl. As I reflected
back over this experience, I became more aware of how my
ministry was creating affirming role models for girls and
women.
God has sustained me throughout my life. Whenever I
have faced new challenges, God has taught me to surrender my
fears over to Him. My fears are replaced by peaceful
reassurance that I am not alone.
I see ministry as the expression of God's spirit within
one's transformed life. Ministry essentially is a process of
enabling others to move closer to God, to more fully
experience the transforming love of God. While priesthood is
one form of ministry, the practice of ministry is not limited
to priesthood. The restoration affirmation statement, "All
are called ... ",informs this belief.
As an elder, I sense that an important part of my
calling is to give guidance to a member's spiritual growth
and maturing faith in Christ. This includes awakening hope
in others, empowering all members into discipleship,
assisting persons to discover their unique gifts and talents,
and creating new opportunities for members to express their
giftedness within the Body. Ultimately, ministry must focus
on assisting God to reclaim all of us as children of God.
It is a privilege for me to have the opportunity to walk

11

through that newly-opened door and blaze new trails. This
"great and marvelous work" continues to emerge and evolve.
God continues to call me into unknown possibilities. I am
able to respond to the call, because I know without a doubt
that God is already there, creating new opportunities of
servant ministry. I dedicate my life in service to the
Living Christ, who calls each of us to walk with Him.

Carol Anway,
I relate the many ways my life has its’ fast steps to the calling to priesthood would be writing my
autobiography.
My association with the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints has been the
whole of my life.
My mother belonged long before I was born, but that is another story in itself. She was widowed
(I was told) with eight children and devoted her efforts to instilling in us the the importance of
using our lives in the service of Christ and to always remember the commitments we made to
Him. Her unwavering devotion, lane of Christ and his Gospel, and her many sacrifices for us was
an inspiration to develop our giftedness to serve both church and community.
I have had the opportunity to serve in the church as teacher, musician, resource person in Crafts
and use such skills at workshops, camps, reunions, and in the community.
I was widowed after six years of marriage to a wonderful Christian (man of my faith) who
encouraged me in my church work. H_____ my children my time was used to support myself in
church and community activities. I worked for 16 years as recreation instructor and director in an
institution for the mentally retarded. I enjoyed my work with these special persons and continue
to be involved as needs arise.
After retirement in 1972, I had the opportunity to go with a church health tam to Honduras and
was trained as a dental assistant. I have sown slides and spoken about my experience there and
the church involvement there.
I have had many experiences in the church that tested my commitment to serve the Lord; many
are my testimonies of the joys and sorrows that strengthened my faith in a loving, caring God. It
has not always been easy for the black members in the white congregation. An experience of
many years ago when our family was asked to leave the church for a period of time as some felt
we were impeding the branch’s growth and expansion, left me devastated. We tried without
success to establish a mission. When asked to return (we were not the problem) and with
apologies from the congregation for the humiliation we suffered, we returned with the courage
given to us by the Holy Spirit through Jesus Christ, and his dear Father.
Serving in the church wherever needed and being a representative in the community has been
both a source of challenge and happiness.
It was with an indescribable shock of surprise when I was approached by our pastor, George
Check, and told I was called to the office of elder. I questioned the need for me at 77 to be called
when there were those so much younger without the handicap of impaired vision that could serve
and too, I was already very busy in church work.
I pondered the call feeling unqualified, too old, very tired and yes, full of wonderment if I could
fill this commitment to expanded service to my Lord. After many hours of prayer and

medittaiton, I accepted the challenfe, knowing God would prepare the way efore to give of my
service, Many have told me that this call is from God and their testimonies with my own has
strengthened me.
I still tremble within at the immensity of what God expects of me and you know he will never
leave me alone. I will do my very best, my privilege will be serving my God with the help of the

At present, I am counselor to the pastor, Church school teacher, musician, and invalued in the
