This is the testimony a test of Lillian Ida Perkins. My husband Don and I were baptized as members of the organized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints. In 1953. Ron was ordained to the Office of Deacon priests and then an elder and has served as pastor several times. Our family attended the Bridgestone branch and participated in worked in several departments for about 30 years. We lived in our farm and raised a family of six children and six foster children. In 1979, he retired from the farm and moved to Wainwright Alberta, where we attended the Wainwright branch. We are active in most of the apartments and John has served as pastor since here too. I have often heard people talk about the women being called to the priesthood, and I guess I tried to ignore it. I had no desire to stand behind the pulpit and preach. I also knew that this was part of all offices. I do. I did do a lot of leadership work which put me behind the pulpit, but I felt unworthy to stand there and represent His holy priesthood. When I attended the 1978 conference, there was a lot of talk about women in the ministry. I wasn't sure just where I stood on the subject, but when I heard whatever I heard or read of the Scripture, all are called, I would get the feeling that I had greater work to do the subject of women in the ministry kept coming. Up and around here in Alberta district. I came to the conclusion that if women were to be ordained, there would have to be a revelation from God. When the revelation did come in 1984 I did not I did accept it. My thoughts were how wonderful for all the women that were educated and had the ability for this. I still didn't really want it for me, and I felt I was doing all I could. We spent the winter of 1985 at Nobu as guides for the Joseph Smith historical center and was making plans to go back again in October for another winter to guide God kept promising me Then one day as I was meditating, I saw myself standing with a group of women. We were standing behind a group of priesthood men. My husband, John and the pastor Alvin, were there too. As I looked forward, I could see a bright light shining down on the men some of this light did filter through to us. It was made known to me that this guy was the power for his holy priesthood, and that these men were of His holy priesthood. Then there was a pause. Then the men became restless and started to move about then I found myself and some of that women in amongst the men standing in the light and I both spoke to me and said, and now you can do your work. I knew it would be a while before I would receive my call. So we continued with our plans and went back to Nobu for more winter. My call came through in September 1986 To the office of priest. My call and Mabel Levitz call to a priest came before the Wainwright branch in October 1986. Then in the on the 25th enough to have October in 1986 or calls came before the Alberta district office at the Kensington branch in Edmonton, Alberta. We were both our date in March 1 1987. At the Wainwright branch, our oldest son Kenneth and his family from Iowa were able to attend my ordination or other five children Harold, Ronald, Carolyn, Lorraine and Cheryl, their companions and all of their children were there, all 21 of them. In the earlier part of their service, our 13th and youngest grandchild, Shonda Elaine Larsen daughter Oh Cheryl and Dwight Larsen was blessed by her grandfather elder John Perkins, assisted by Pat the pastor elder Alvin Levitt. I was ordained at the office of praise by my husband elder John Perkins, assisted by our son priest Harold Perkins. Mabel Levitt was ordained by their son elder Ryan Levitt, assisted by her husband elder Alvin Levitt. After the ordination, our chairs were pulled back from the priesthood and as I stood to sing the closing hymn, I felt a comfortable feeling of being up there. Following the service of family and a few close friends had a potluck dinner in our church dining room, which included a decorated cake for me for my ordination. One for Shonda for her blessing and two more cakes for two birthdays. Since my ordination. I've taken my turn at preaching with five of us in the priesthood. My turn comes every five weeks. I must add with God's guidance. I have been very comfortable at carrying out the duties of my office. I have certainly had God's help and enlightenment as I study and prepare for my sermons also as I work in other departments I feel I am giving more ministry than I did before. I had an experience this summer I would like to relate. I was at a meeting where A call came from the people for a vote to accept the young lady to the priesthood. Some people spoke against the call when the time came to vote something about it all. I hesitated because I questioned the office that she was called to. I wondered if I could accept my ministry from her that I remembered the testimony the pastor related before he had started to promise or call that I felt very much impressed to vote for her that she was young it would be able to minister to people that I would not ever reach or see the majority did not did vote for her. There was a few people within the church that had been cold or unfeeling to me since my ordination. But the majority has certainly given me respect and support. Our friends and especially our family have been very supportive to John and I as we work for the cause of the kingdom.

PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF SUSAN PERKINS

I was informed of my priesthood call in November of 1985. I was
very much surprised, pleased and confused all at once. I had had
trouble accepting the revelation Section 156, calling women to the
priesthood. I, myself, had put our male priesthood members up on a
pedestal; a little higher than the general congregation, mainly out of
respect and admiration. I did not feel that women would be accepted
the same way and would destroy the reverent atmosphere surrounding our
present priesthood. I realized that this matter must be studied and
prayed about continually in order to search out and find the answers I
needed before I could accept my calling. After 3 months of searching,
studying and praying, with much joy, I accepted my call to the office
of Priest,
I had come to realize that I had been performing in a priesthood
capacity already for years but without an official title. My way of
thinking took an about turn but there still was the odd "nagging"
thought; prompted by Satan, of course!
The Ribstone Congregation voted on and accepted unanimously my call
to the office of Priest on February, 1986.
The Alberta District unanimously approved my call at the Alberta
District Meeting on April 27, 1986.
I was ordained Sunday, April 27, 1987 by Apostle Phillip Caswell,
assisted by Elders Eldon Dallyn and Pastor Wayne Skinner at a 2:00 pm
special ordination service during our churches' "Homecoming Weekend",
This was indeed a joyous occasion for me as all of my large family
were in attendance; many of them non-members and the rest inactive.
This was a special moment for me and they shared this with me.

l '

I truly felt the Lords' spirit with me as I spoke to the
congregation previous to the ordination itself. As I was ordained, I
was filled with the holy spirit and tears of joy and peace flowed
freely down my face. As I looked out into the congregation, I saw
that they witnessed the same feeling and were deeply moved.
I had prepared myself for this day, both physically and
spiritually. I had fasted all day and studied. I went home to fast
and pray while the congregation feasted on a "Homecoming Dinner"
previous to the ordination servce. When I returned to the church
after dinner, I was in complete peace with my Lord, ready to
officially accept my call to servanthood.
My ordination service was made very special for me through the
ministry of music, which is my special field. The organist was a
special friend within the church and there were several solos by other
0
church friends'. My youngest sister and her husband, professional
musicians and active in the Edmonton congregation, had written and
sang a special song for the occasion. This was a beautiful tribute to
God and filled the church with his presence. The Regional
Administrator delivered the message and was filled with many words of
wisdom and encouragement as well as a challenge to the congregation to
uphold and support this calling.
My family, 21 altogether, hosted a family supper and get together
following the service in celebration of his event.
I have been married since July, 1969 and my husband held the office
of Deacon at the time of my ordination. He was ordained in February
of the previous year. He was in full support of my calling and was
expecting it sooner than I was.

I was always active in the church as a child and a teenager. I had
attended almost every Sunday and had been baptized at the age of 8
years. At the age of 13 years, I became pianist of our branch; having
played for my first funeral service at the tender age of 13 years on
an organ that I had no experience on. Many times, during my teenage
years, I had £el t the Lord walking with me and at times, holding my
hand when I needed an understanding companion. I knew very surely of
his presence in my life and in the lives of others. I helped with
Vacation Church School, music, and attended youth camps and rallies.
I was married at age 18 in 1969 and moved away from our home town
for 5 years. During this time we were inactive in the church. We
returned to our home town in 1974 and were warmly welcomed by our home
congregation. Before long, I had accepted the music director positon
and was the regular and only organist at church every Sunday right up
until present day. This was a strain at times as my two boys were
very young (1 and 3 years) at the time. I also played an active part
in organizing services, congregational activities, youth leadership,
and was a member of the worship commission.
I was responsible for developing a Junior (age 6-11) and an older
youth group (12-18) within our branch and for encouraging our Regional
Administrator to start a Junior Camp for 8-11 year olds.
I was so busy accepting responsibility and "doing", that I did
not realize that someday this would lead to a call to the ministry.
Others just accepted my calling as a normal occurance and were not
surprised at my calling.

My views and feelings are favorable towards women in the
priesthood, although I have seen a change for the worse in the
priesthood structure of our branch. Communication came to a stand
still. The priesthood, men and women, all felt that nothing was ever
discussed with them before a decision was announced on Sunday. We
were never informed of any service schedule changes, etc. I feel that
this had no reflection on women in the preisthood, only that our
Pastor was worn out and in return, the priesthood became inactive due
to the fact that they never knew what was going on.
I have only one doubt about womens' ordination and that was when
our Pastor called his wife to the office of Elder. I thought I had my
firm convictions on this until I had to start counselling others as to
the righteousness of this calling. She was a very knowledgeable
person but not a spiritual person. The congregation could not see how
she could transmit the spirit of God when she, herself, could not feel
it strongly. She had also caused some very hard feelings within some
members of the branch as she had a very sharp tongue. This caused
much contension in our branch which was a very difficult situation.
As with every new event, it takes time to accept and time for ill
feelings to heal. So I strongly encouraged our members to pray about
it and God would direct them.
I, myself, have thoroughly enjoyed my responsibility to servanthood
and have found there has developed a special bond between nyself and
other ordained women of our branch. It seems as though we know we're
all heading in the same direction with the same goals in mind.
I have had good and bad experiences in my ministry since I was
ordained. I was full of great plans when I was first called but soon
found out that I did not have time for a great deal extra to do.

I found, as a priest, that setting up a visiting schedule was not
as easy as you think, ~. people know you are an ordained priest and
when you phone ahead for a visit, they hesitate, After a time period
though they realize that you are still the same person as before and
can sit and chat like a 0

normal person'. I was hurt at first by the

evasion responses but time changed all that.
I had an older lady phone me up at work and reprimand me severely
because I had been ordained for 6 months and should have had visited
every home at least once by then. I have a full time job, a growing
family, live and work on a farm, am community active and could not
have visited 105 church members' homes in 6 months! I still haven't.
Our Pastor and his wife worked with a mother and her two children
for several months and finally had them committed to baptism, The
Pastor phoned me and said that the little girl wanted me to baptize
her, so I agreed, It was a terrifically stormy afternoon at the lake
and no one attended the baptism except those involved. We had to rush
into the service due to the electrical storm and the spirit was
definitely not present. All concerned did not have a good feeling
about it, We returned home to find that lightning had struck our
house, causing much damage to appliances, aerials, etc. I was very
upset, feeling that God was trying to tell us something. The family
was not confirmed because 2 weeks after their baptism they left our
church and joined the Penecostal church as it was more spiritually
active, which was what they were looking for. I was not disappointed
or surprised but was certainly left with an uneasy feeling.
I have had several good experiences since my ordination,
particularly with counselling. It is such a good feeling to know you
have helped someone or just being able to take time to talk to them or
be a good listener!

I had one astounding personal experience. I had been less than
healthy for about 3 months as I had a blood immunity problem caused by
being worn out, physically and mentally. I had resigned my job as
music director and worship commission chairman as I needed rest and
had to reduce the amount of stress in my life. In April of this year
(1987), I discovered by accident, a lump in my breast. This lump grew
in size and became very alarming. After 2 weeks, I went to the doctor
and was immediately referred to the cancer clinic in Edmonton.
Naturally, I had my share of bad moments when I worried intensely, and
physically cried to the Lord for help and ask why this was happening
to me. Day after day, my entire life would unfold before my eyes:
how things could have been and what would happen if it was cancer. I
aged a lot in the two weeks before my appointment at the cancer
clinic. I felt very strongly that I needed God's special blessing
before my appointment, so I phoned my favorite elder, long-time friend
and former Pastor of years ago to come and administer to me. We both
prepared ourselves for this and had 2 other elders assisting. The
moment they laid their hands upon my head, I felt the Lord's presence
go through my body like an electric shock and tears of supreme peace
flowed down my cheeks. From that moment on, I knew I could face
whatever I had to: I WAS PREPARED.
The morning I was to go to Edmonton, I awoke and discovered the
lump was gone. A miracle had occurred overnight! I kept my
appointment finding out that something had been there but nothing was
malignant. I rejoiced in the fact that I had truly been the recipient
of one of God's mighty miracles.

As a priesthood member, I have been called upon for many functions.
I have offered prayers at many community functions and visited the
sick. My most satisfaction has come from counselling; knowing I can
help others. My interests are especially with the older youth of our
church and with the ministry of music. I find that at this time our
older youth have been lost to the church and I feel a special
responsibility towards them as we were the ones that started them off
in youth group years ago but the subsequent leaders seem to be
uninterested. I feel there is a special ministry required in that
area. I have presided over several services since my ordination and,
as time goes by, the task becomes much easier. My favorite, being
communion services. I get a special feeling when I serve communion to
others. It feels as if I am truly presenting each member with a
portion of Gods' spirit. The first time I can remember feeling the
presence of the Lord's spirit was at a communion service when I was
just a child. The tasks and opportunites ahead of me are endless, but
my time is so very limited as is my physical strength.
Just a very short time ago, the elder who was to speak did not show
up and the congregation just sat there and waited and waited,
listening to the prelude music. As I sat there, I realized that all
these people came to church expecting something and should not go away
empty, and being the only priest present I discussed it with the 2
deacons present and decided to hold a prayer servce. One of our youth
had been in a bad accident and had requested prayers so I used that as
the starting point. I was totally unprepared for anything so I was
grateful that the Lord was with me as I conducted that service. It
was certainly a growing experience for me.

My views toward Section 156 have changed very little since
ordination as I see many qualified women being officially called to
servant hood. There are many I would like to see called and haven't
been and some who have been called that I have doubted their calling
but supported it in faith.
I feel that I have been fully supported in my priesthood calling
and accepted by all. The members don't hesitate to call on me or give
me words of encouragement. I feel that I am seldom called upon to
assist during preaching services except when another woman is
preaching. The men automatically always line up 2 other men to
assist. I find this an unintentional negative response to female
priesthood.
My feeling towards ministry is serving and helping others and
administering the word of God. A brief definition is being a
'servant' of God. That is exactly how I feel about my ministry to
others at this time. As a priest, I feel it is my duty to assist in
serving communion, preside when the elder is absent, and home
visiting. For myself, it is hard to have a steadfast home visiting
program but visiting others, not only church members, has certainly
become a priority in my life. I have participated in presiding over
services but do not want it to become so routine that I no longer find
it enjoyable. With my music responsibilites to the branch, it is very
easy to take on too much at once. I feel I was specifically chosen
for this office because I find it easy to love others and reach out
and touch their lives. I find it a great talent to be able to share
the spirit of God with all those around me.

During the last decade of my life, the most significant personal
growth has been one of faith. I used to worry about everything.
Being a farmer, it nearly drove me to emotional ruin as nature
controlled everything in our life. Material things seemed to take
first place in our lives. As our faith grew stonger and stronger,
these things seemed to be not so important. I had stated once, that I
would give everything up to go and work for the church in an under
prvileged country. Material things are not so important. I do not
worry about the farm so much as I have come to accept things as what
will be, will be and we can't change that. God will take care of us.
I do not know what God has in store for me in the futrue but as
long as I live the way He wants me to and continue to do his will, I
feel that I will be blessed. I know that my ministy will not be easy
at times but the good times will outweigh the bad. I am grateful that
I have had this opportunity to share my testimony with others.

Before the World Confernece of 1984, I believed that women would be
called to the Priesthood. I did not have this preminition because I felt

it was true and of God, but because I felt the prophet was under tremend-
ous pressures by those who so openly "campaigned" for women in the priesthood. I was devastated when Section 156 was approved. My thinking was

that the prophet had betrayed me. If he had been given a true revelation,
I pondered, Why did he present the document with the stipulation that the
entire document had to be accepted or not any portion of it at all. Why
had he not trusted God's Spirit to touch the people?
The next two years were filled with spiritual darkness for me. I
prayed and cried to God for enlightenment about Section 156. Each time
I tried to open my mind to His will a flood of angry thoughts would cascade
my mind and heart. I was so bitter that I could not forgive God, the
prophet and those who had wanted it so badly. The idea of leaving the
church entered my mind. Were would I go? I knew I could never return
to the Catholic church of my youth nor could I imagine being spiritually
fed in a community church after experiencing the Restoration Gospel. The
message I received from God when I prayed was that this was His church
and He was in charge. In my frustrated state, I decided to wait upon
the Lord to bring His wrath upon His wayward Saints and try to worship
as well as I could.
Women were being called to the priesthood in the Seattle Stake for
the first time and their calls were to presented at our Stake Conference.
ihe night before conference, I couldn't decide whether I should stay at
home the next day, go and vote against all the womens' calls, or go and
not vote at all. I prayed through the long and sleepless night asking
God for guidance. No answer came until the early hours of the morning.
Feeling completely alone and rejected, the Spirit of God rested upon
me. His voice rerrinded me of the many times I had raised my hand in support
of priesthood calls of men when I did not have a personal testimony of
them. I voted because of the Holy Spirit that was present as the call
was being presented and the confidence I had in the process in which priesthood calls are handled. The Lord told me to go to conference and be
intuned to His Holy Spirit. If any of the women had anything within their
lives where they could not serve Him as a priesthood member, then I was
to vote agairi&t 1llem;

-1-

During the presentation of the calls, I experienced God's loving and
gentle Spirit when each name was presented and as each candidate bore

their testimony. I could not deny the Spirit I felt, so I voted for every-
one. With my submission to God's will came His assurance that He meant

for women to serve Him in this special capacity. I also realized I had
been angry at the prophet and others for actng in a human way-just as
I had acted toward God in my rebellion-and I was able to forgive them.
I was overjoyed to be in the mind and heart of our Lord once again and
fully accepted Section 156 as revelation from God.
I appreciated the eagerness of women to serve in the priesthood capacity
but had no desire myself to have the responsibilities of priesthood.
I had always felt God had allowed me to minister, so when my ca!l to the
office of Elder came through my husband, Francis, I was confused. My
husband was filled with excitement and joy. Because the call came through
my husband, the Stake High Council needed additional time for prayful
consideration and when Francis had their approval, he shared the call
with me. I told him I didn't want or need a priesthood call to serve God.
He replied that I may not need the calling but the people needed the ministry
they would receive from me as a priesthood member. He asked me to pray
about it, study and listen to God. I wanted to do the Lord's will and
not my own as I had done when struggling over Section 156. As I prayed
and searched, God brought to my mind the experience of the birth of my
youngest child, Catherine. I was forty years of age when I became pregnant.

My thoughts and lifestyle were geared toward taking care of my four teen-
agers and a baby didn't fit into my plans. When Catherine was born, she

was like a magnet, drawing us closer to each other and to God. I remembered
that I enjoyed a loving response with God whenever I permitted Him to
be my guide and didn't struggle with my will over His. I decided he desired
to bless me as he had done before, so I accepted the call on faith. I
knew He would give me confirmation in His own time.
At the closing service of Spring Conference, the Spirit of God rested
upon me and filled my soul. He told me I was where I was suppose to be.
My acceptance of the office of Elder was His intent for me and I would
fulfill a great need to minister to those around me If i continued in
faith and followed His standards. I knew then that He created me for
this particular time and place to share the Gospel of Jesus Christ. I

-2-

had always wanted to walk in His stead and let His life be the life that
others see. I praised God that He turned a small amount of faith into
a great knowledge of truth.
The Saints accepted my call to the office of Elder at the Seattle
Stake Conference on October 19, 1986. It was not an unanimous vote, but
all were in spiritual order. The people who voted against me, voted a~ai~st
every woman whose name was presented for approval. I understood their
feelings because of the struggle I had had. I was ordained on February
15, 1987 at Highland Park Congregation in Seattle, Washington. My husband,
Elder Francis Porter, Sr. and my eldest son, Seventy Francis Jr., ordained
me. Many friends and family were present. It was special to have my
side of the f~mily present because none of my relatives are church members.
My greatest joy, so far, as a priesthood member was assisting my son,
Francis Jr., in the blessing of a baby. I felt God's great love and desire
to be an intricate part of all our lives. I knew that the love of the
parents, grandparents, family and friends was nothing compared to God's
love and plan for this little precious girl.
Presenting sermons and being in charge of prayer services has required
much more emotional energy than I had anticipated. I had always enjoyed
a beautiful spirit in the telling of stories and acting in plays. I wasn't
prepared for the different emotion priesthood responsibility would have
on the presentation of a sermon or being in charge of a service.
Most of the people are supportive and loving when they share their
appreciation for my efforts to serve. Those who don't accept Section 156
are very distant in their actions and attitudes. Some people who were
good friends with me before I was ordained, have very little to say to
me now. I have empathy for them because I had felt betrayed by those
who were supportive of women in the priesthood at one time too.
I perceive my ministry will grow and become a more natural part of
my life. At times, I really have to stop and think about being an elder.
I try to determine what God intends for me to accomplish. I know that
my ministry will be uniquely designed for me. God wants me to touch others
through the same spirit of love that He has administered to me. I'm excited!
A little frightened perhaps, but always hopeful that I will measure up
to God's plans for me. My plans have led into ways of pain and anger.
Other people have ideas about what my ministry should be and their plans
are often colored by their personal feelings for me. God, my Creator,
knows me and desires for me to do His will; for His glory; my salvation;
and the salvation of those who respond to my ministry.

It was not until I met my husband, Greg, in 1973 that I first
heard of the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints.
As our relationship grew, so did my curiosity regarding his church.
One Sunday I went to church with him. The building was small and
simple, but the warmth and love of the Saints there was most
abundant. I was deeply moved as we all sang praises to God. I had
been raised Presbyterian and had been blessed with many good
growing spiritual experiences as a child and teenager in that church.
As I grew older, however, my attendance slacked off. I went
through a normal type "questioning" stage and found little use for
organized religion. My faith, though shaken, was not altogether gone,
however.
After an unsuccessful previous marriage of five years to an
agnostic1, meeting a guy so interested in God and his church was
inspiring to me. In 1974, Greg, then a Deacon, and I were married
and became regular church attendees. It was then that I began
receiving regular cottage meetings. Several months later, at a family
retreat, I was spoken to by God through our Seventy and told it was
time to put aside my questions and reach out in faith to be baptised.
This was certainly an important event in my live. I was baptised
and confirmed and became a strong "convert".
For thirteen years now I have been a Priesthood Wife, first for
two years as the wife of a deacon and since then as the wife of an
elder. I have always considered my role as "Priesthood Wife" to be
important and have tried to be supportive of my husband's ministry.
I olten accompanied him when he visited the sick and when he took
communion to the shut-ins. On occasion our three children would go
~ along - a blessing to us all. During his four years as Presiding
Elder of the Ft. Lauderdale Branch ,I tried to serve and help him in as
many ways as possible.
So, when Section 156 was presented to the World Conference
for attention (I received this news by telephone), I was shaken.
Surely the Lord knew we Saints were not ready for that yet. I
fought with myself all week. I had never suffered from feelings of
inadequacy because of my sex and I had always realized that women
were vital in the success of their husband's ministry and had gifts
themselves to offer. But I felt that as a people, we were still too
immature to accept this "equal" role of Priesthood.

After much prayer, however, and after hearing the testimonies
of our Conference Delegates upon their arrival home, I realized that
God knew just exactly what He was doing and that we, as a people,
could grow to understand and accept His word. In faith, I believed
that since He gave this revelation to us, that indeed it was His will at
this time.
One experience I had which added testimony to this occurred
at our annual Regional Women's Retreat at Deerhaven in October of
1986. It was at our closing communion service that two women
elders, recently ordained, served the emblems for the first time. The
service was carefully planned so that we went up to the table to be
served by either of the two women or by our Regional Administrator.
This allowed any woman who struggled with acceptance of Section
156 to still take communion. It was a "moment in history" for us,
and as I walked up to receive the emblems from my sister whom I
had been very close to, I could see Christ in her eyes and I was filled
with assurance and faith.
A month later when my pastor called me aside privately to
talk, I knew what he was going to say. For months I had felt God's
gentle but constant nudging as He prepared me for this time in my
life., when I had been called to the office of Priest. I had some
reservations, simply because I am the mother of three children and
keep so busy with my family responsiblities. But, again, I felt God
telling me to reach out in faith and that He would show me the ways
I can serve Him now even at this time as a Priest. And so, I was
ordained on March 8, 1987, at the Ft. Lauderdale branch within the
South Florida District. I was ordained by Seventy Clayton Condit and
assisted by my husband, Elder Greg Powell, and his father, Elder
Elbert Powell.
The ordination service was very meaningful to me and to my
two sisters who were also ordained at this same time. It was
planned carefully to include our favorite hymns and to involve those
persons especially close to us. Our sister, Elder Jo Ann Condit, came
from Phoenix, Arizona (along with her hasband Clayton) to give our
ordination charge. She had been a member of our branch for 13-14
years and knew us well. As a recent o&inee, she was really able to
relate to us and to feed us with encouragement, with loving
admonition, and with the understanding of one woman to another.
Fortunately, most everyone in our branch and the vast majority in
our District were extremely supportive and the many who came to

share in our ordination moved me greatly. My actual ordination was
a humbling experience and a special blessing to me as I felt God's
spirit fill me with abundance.
A few weeks later, I wrote to my childhood girl friend. We
have remained close throughout the years although we've seen each
other only a few times. I shared my ordination service with her and
explained about Section 156 and what the roles of the Priesthood are
in our faith. Soon afterwards, I received a letter from her. She had
had a dream just days before receiving my letter. In this dream, she
saw me up in the front of a large church, one which was unfamiliar
to her. This church was filled.with people. She couldn't see what
was happening to me but knew it was something very important.
The dream was very vivid to her and she couldn't shake it. When
my letter arrived, she was thrilled and then understood the dream.
Janie's testimony has given me additional assurance and strength.
Since my ordination, I have come to appreciate the Priesthood
even more. I have felt the heaviness of responsiblities along with
the blessings of service. I have only been ordained now for a few
months, so my opportunities to minister as a Priest have been
limited. However, one special experience for me occurred when I
was asked to deliver the Baptismal Charge recently for two young
boys. I felt blessed in being able to give this kind of pulpit ministry
and felt a positive response from the congregation. I have also
experienced the joy of my sisters and brothers as I have had the
priviledge of seving them communtion. God has been prodding me to
study more and I am trying to heed to this, although I find it
difficult. I have so much yet to learn. In January I shall become our
Christian Education Directf08 and I am hoping to make some
improvements to our dwindling church school attendance. I hope
that as a Priest concerned with ministry of the family, I can
encourage more families to make church school part of their lives.
There are many families I need to visit. Recently, our branch held a
weekend workshop on home visiting which I felt was most
beneficial.
We are fortunate here that most of our people are very
supportive of women in the Priesthood. At both the branch and
district business meetings during which time our calls were
presented, many stood to testify of the validity of our calls. Again, at
the Ordination Service, so many people throughout the district made
a special effort to attend. Just remembering all those hugs fills me

with great warmth even now. As I have ministered from the Pulpit,
people have responded in a positive way for the most part which has
given me encouragement, for it is an awesome responsibility.
I have noticed that we women are being used "most cautiously"
in many avenues. The fear of offending, of rushing into things, is still
present. I am patient in this regard. Those few in our branch who
are opposed to Section 156 have done so quietly. Others in the
district are more obvious in their opposition and have even left their
home congregations, but have not totally "shunned" me. I know they
must hurt terribly and must feel very confused and alone. I pray
that somehow they will hear the voice of God soon.
Ministry, to me, means enabling people to draw closer to God
and to grow in their relationship with the Christ. We all can minister
and I have always tried to use my gifts to do this as best as I can. As
a Priest, however, a responsibility comes with this ministry. Not only
do I have special specific duties and ordinances in which to minister,
but I must become more prayerful and more sensitive to others in
order to know their needs and wherdI can help. This is a heavy ' responsibility. Today, the pace of life is so very fast that church
attendance, personal, and family Christian growth suffer. Financial
stress, materialism, divor(e, sexual promiscuity, two full-time
working parents, and drugs are taking a toll on everyone. Good
personal and family relationships with God and each other are vital
to overcome the obsticles of our times. I feel the office of Priest has
a special role ( and responsiblity) to minister to families in an attempt
to bring Christ into their every day lives. As a mother, wife, and
Priest, it is my responsiblity to do this - even in my own home.
The message of our church today is so very important. People
everywhere need Christ in their lives and need to grow, continually,
in their relationship with Him. Many know of Christ but have not
made that personal contact, that personal commitment, that is
needed to bring peace and joy and meaning to their lives and the
lives of others. I am happy to be an instrument of this message of
hope and pray that my ministry will be a blessing to others.

I, Donna Marie (Miller) Pratt was born on May 6, .1933 in Keota, IA
to humble but Godly parents. I was taught the basic truths of the
gospel, to be a good person, to treat others as I would wish to be
treated, to keep a promise (your word is as good as your bond), to
stand for those things that are good and true, to respect my elders,
to be kind to children and all of God's creation. But I thought
that there was something more ...
I met and married a member of the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ
of Latter Day Saints and shortly after the birth of our first child
I was lead to join the Church. One of the more important things that
helped me to make this decision was the quality of the lives of the
people that I knew were members. I thought, "If this many good
people believe in something, it must be right".
I have worked in the Church Office since before the Stake was
organized in 1969. During this period of time I have had the opportunity
to work closely with a number of fine priesthood men. My call came
through my pastor, Drew Denton, who is also an appointee. It's always
been a joy for me to do the Lord's work and I feel fortunate that I
have had the chance to work where I have for such a long period of time.
My call was presented and approved by the Des Moines Stake Conference on
May 18, 1986. Brother Denton's testimony of my call was" "Donna's
devotion to the church evidences itself in countless hours of effort on
behalf of the church. Time and again I have been impressed with the
thought that she should be called to the office of Elder". My response
to the call was: "I humbly accept my call to the office of Elder. I have
felt for some time that I could be of greater service to God and to you,
the people. With study and prayer on my part, with your support and
prayers and with the help of God, I feel that I will be capable of

offering that service".

My husband, Harlan E. Pratt, was an Elder at the time of my call and I was
ordained by him on November 16, 1986 at my local congregation, Central in
Des Moines, IA. He was assisted by his first cousin, Eugene H. Potter.
Bishop Roy Leamon, Des Moines Stake Bishop, gave the sermon on that day.
I thought this was particularly appropriate since I have worked with the
Stake Bishopric all of the years that I have worked for Des Moines Stake.
He spoke on "Our Stewardship" and how there is a work for all. I chose
as one of the hymns, "I have Found The Glorious Gospel". This has always
been very meaningful to me since I came in contact with the Church.
The service was simple, but a very moving experience for me. I had asked
Harlan to ordain me but was surprised when he so readily accepted. In spite
of his size (he played football at Graceland and Drake University) he is a
very gentle man with deep emotions. It was a beautiful experience for
both of us. He spoke of our years together (we have been married for 33
years) and of the kind of person that I am and his confidence in me that
I would take my call seriously and prepare for the tasks which were ahead
of me. I co~ldn't have asked for a more appropriate setting, or for
anyone else to have done the ordination. Gene Potter gave a charge to
the ordinand. He has known me closely for most of my married life. He
brought in some of the personal things from my early days in the church.
No one could have done it better.
I always felt strongly that I wouldn't worry about whether or not
there were women in the priesthood. If the Lord wants us, he would
let us know. I never felt that my ministry was limited before. I
guess I sense a greater responsibility in my ministry and that now
I too must share the load.

Sometimes I can hardly believe how fortunate I am for having found
God's Church restored here on the earth, along with all of the beautiful
people that are trying in their own way to bring about Zion in our day.
My heart is sad at times, when I know that there are some people struggling
and often bitter about the direction in which the Church is going. They
profess to be Christian's but don't have the charity to forgive someone
that doesn't think as they do. Working here in the office, I have occasion
to know and to talk with some of them. I know that their hearts are hurting
too, for they are good people but at this time, in this place, they "see
through a glass darkly". I pray that the Lord will make me an instrument
in his hands so that I can by example soften their hearts.

The Waterloo-Clinton District Conference of the North Central Region approved my call to the office of Priest in Cedar Rapids, Iowa on Saturday September 27, 1986. My husband Roger L. Reid has held the office of High Priest since 1973 and he is also the presiding elder of the Maquoketa congregation. There were five (5) priesthood calls fom Maquokets and after we had all taken the necessary classes a special ordination service was held on Sunday morning February 8, 1987. Perhaps if I used one word to describe my feelings that day it would be peace. Sure therewere concerns, fears, excitment, but mainly I felt good about my calling, and my ability to do Gods work. with five ordinations the whole service was one of dedication and service to God. Seventy Robert Taylor was spokesman assisted by High Priest Ken Reynolds and High Priest Roger Reid. It was a beautiful day to share with family and friends both in worship and with a potluck and visiting afterwords. One year at world Conference someone tried to make a motion from the floor in regard to women in the priesthood. My decision was formed at that time. when Godwas ready then I would follow Him.

When section 156 was presented two years later, at first I simply felt good about it. Then on the following Sunday as I read the document to my brother out in New York, I felt the Spirit of Confirmation so strong. I knew I would never have doubts that it was Gods will. I feel very fortunate that my immediate family and local church family have supported me completely in my call. I know there are those in our district who are against 156, but so far I've been accepted as my self. So far I have not used my priesthood calling in a district activity where another might express anger or walk out. My prayer is that if or when this happens that God will continue to be with me and help me to understand. 1/1. v I feel so very blessed by the support of my family and the Ma- quokets congregation when my fears of being inadequate come through such as when I have to preach, so many seem to know and understand and give me verbal as well as prayerful support. I feel that support and am able to do what ever is necessary. Its very hard for me to start with 1970 so bear with me, and I'11 be breif. I was born in 1939. In '43 I had a ruptured appendix and almost died. My parents, both R.L.D.S. members called for the Elders. A healing was given that left even the doctors amazed. My folks have shared many times throughout my life of that experience and witnessed of there faith over and over.

I attended Graceland and met my husband & married in 1960. In 1966 & 67 we adopted our children. My Husbands priesthood calls were a source of reaffirming Gods continued love and guidance as we have both grown from his calls. Our knowledge of God and His love have helped us have patience and a growing understanding of others and there needs. It seems that many of our spiritual experiences have come with trials or times of testing as some would say, I guess because we reach out to God more at that time. In the fall of 1967 I had kidney failure and ended up on dialysis. I spent the next seven and one-hald years of a kidney machine. Those years carried many ups and downs. Our children were young and I felt guilty because I was so limited in my physical strength. It took many years before I realized that we were a closer family and spent more time together, one on one with the children than the average family. Also facing death each time I was hooked up to the dialysis machine I came to depend on friends, My loving husband but mostly on my Heavenly Fathers for strength to carry me through. He taught me that anyone can SMILE and in so doing feel better. In 1974 we moved to Iowa and began to seriously consider a kidney transplant. After many tests with members of my family the call

came that my youngest sister Kris matched me like an identical twin. The day before the transplant, Kris and I spent alot of time together just sharing. Then that evening, knowing that many of ours friends were at prayer service and remembering us in pray we also headed to the hospital chapel, We talked quietly for a while, sang eone of our favorite hymns then had prayer together le both felt Gods reasuring love so strong that we both went to bed and were able to sleep the whole night through. On June 5, 1975 I received a healthy functing kidney. After 12 years Kris and I are still doing Great. We have moved many times over the years, and been able to share our lives with so many special people. Friends who have given us so much so that we also might give. For the past three years I've been doing volunteed work, such as meals on wheels, and also I've helped teach a class sponsored by dept. of mental health. I really have felt that the children in my class have grown and have helped my grown. Ministry is giving of myself to others. Giving in love and understanding so that they also might come to love both them- selves and others. My call to the office of Priest is to continue as I have been doing but enlarging my own understanding and continue using and developing my abilities.

periences since ordination have been growing.) The confirmation

QUESTIONNAIRE RE: ORDINATION

1. Ordination approved by Omaha/Council Bluffs Stake
Conference held September 28, 1987. I was ordained October 19,
1987 to the office of Priest by my father, Paul F. Hansen, High
Priest and my husband, John L. Rounds, Elder at the Shenandoah,
Iowa church.
2. My husband was an Elder at the time of my ordination; at
the present time he is a High Priest.
3. We had lived in several stakes and in many different
congregations and each time we moved we were allowed to assume
new and more responsible positions. I especially was allowed
to "try my hand" at a wide variety of jobs. I felt that if I
tried new things that God would help me and each time I grew as
a result of being willing to risk. Everything from teaching
Sunday School classes, being on a stake women's council,
singing, both in choir and solo, being on or heading up the
worship commission, organist/pianist, being involved in youth
camping, (counselor, cook, teacher), planning adult retreats,
and planning special services for congregations. I was able to
go to World Conferences, Worship 81 and each time I learned
anew what it was like to be a member, actively supporting my
church; wherever that was and learning new ways to help growth
occur.
4. Enclosed is a copy of the bulletin for my ordination. It
was a beautiful day for me. Very exciting, a spiritual high
and filled with such love and concern from friends, relatives
and my home congregation. Everyone was so supportive and
couldn't have been any more helpful. My family, sisters, aunts
and uncles and cousins had come from great distances to be
present and that was especially moving for me. Also, my bosses
and their families were there as well as the lady I work with
who were all nonmembers. That was very exciting for me to
share that with them. My husband and I had planned the outline
for the whole service and we sang for it as our gift to the
congregation. The spirit of God was there in abundance. Many
people after the service commented on what a beautiful spirit
there was. We had a potluck for family and friends to continue
sharing the fellowship of all present.

It was extremely satisfying, and one of great joy
that my father and husband were able to ordain me. My father
has only daughters (5) and no sons and I had always felt badly
that there would be no one to carry on in his footsteps in a
priesthood responsibility.

TESTIMONY OF IVENA ROUSH
September 10, 1987

My call to the priesthood did not come as a surprise to me, although
it certainly was not something I aspired to. If it had simply been up
to the members of the church to decide whether women should be in the
priesthood or not, I probably would have voted against it. I have always
felt that women are as capable as men of filling any position, but it
seemed that there is so much ministry that can be given outside the
priesthood that the ordination of women might not be necessary. I did
feel that something was needed to motivate the priesthood cen to take
their responsibilities more seriously and to improve the quality of

ministry they have to offer. Section 156 spe~ks to both of these con-
cerns. When the message came through the prophet that it was in harmony

with God's will that women should also be ordained, I felt that now
God was giving direction. It was no longer_i~ the members of the
church making this decision, but we were being led by the Holy Spirit
and I was quite comfortable with the acceptance of that revelation, ·;
although its implications for me personally did not immediately occur
to me.
The Church has always been the central focus in my life and I an
happiest when I am involved in making its programs work and seeing the
effects of Christ1s love in peoples lives. Over the past several years I

have been given many opportunities to use my gifts in a variety of ser-
vices in our local congregation and in the Stake and District. Being

willing to give the time to be in leadership discussions and planning
sessions has given me the opportunity to develop some skills and insights
which I feel must have been preparing me for the call to specialized
ministry which was to come.
In the two years after the conference of 1984, several people
suggested to me that I would be called to the priesthood. My response
was, "No, that calling is for other women. I can serve to full capacity
without ordination?" However, when the Pastor called to schedule a
visit with me, I knew immediately that he was going to talk to me about

-

Ol
0

accepting a call to the priesthood. Later, others shared their testimony
that they had been aware of a call for me even before Section 156 was
given.
My ordiantion to the office of Preist was approved by the Loveland,
Colorado Branch on October 5, 1986 and by the Northern Colorado District
on October 18, 1986. I felt a wonderful spirit of support in both
instances.
My ordination service took place January 11, 1987 at the Loveland
church. I was ordained by my husband, Bishop Richard Roush, and Elder
Charles Millner. My preparation for the ordination service was doubly
intense because a young man in the Branch, whose call to the office of
Deacon was approved at, the same time as mine, had asked that I ordain
him following my ordination. So we shared the service.
The order of worship for the ordination service followed the one
suggested in the Herald for use in November of 1985 when the first
women were ordained. The theme "Touch Me Lord" was very meaningful to
me and as Randy (the other ordinand) and I read the affirmation I felt
very humble and realized how much we would need the touch of that Spirit

if we were to fulfill our new call to ministry. I was also most appre-
ciative 0r the message that Patriarch-Evangelist David Shupe brought

emphasizing the meaning of calling in the lives of all people. The
entire service spoke to me in a very meaningful way from the special
people who participated to the words and beauty of the music which were
presented in love.

So far my ministry has not been much different than before my or-
dination except that I am used more often in worship services to offer

prayers, give offering statements, etc. and I have helped serve communion
on a regular basis. Most of the things I do aren't that much different
than the things I was doing before, but there is a difference in me. I
find that my concern for others has deepened and I am much more sensitive
to their feelings and responses.

When I was ordained several families who could not accept the ordin-
ation of women, had already left the congregation but there were several

who still had not dedided how they felt and were working through their
doubts while still supporting the congregation. The first Sunday that
I served communion two of these families·weresitting on my aisle. The
Spirit was present during the service and the importance of what was

b

happening uppermost in our minds. All went well and everyone shared
in partaking of the emblems. After the service those who had been
wavering expressed their appreciation that I served them and that they
now felt the validity of my role in the priesthood. A similar experience
occurred the next month when I served communion to another family who
had some problems accepting section 156.
I have not had opportunity yet to minister in many of the ways
which the office of Priest calls for, so many challenges yet lie ahead
as I develop my ministry. I feel that ministry involves serving people
in all areas of life where I can bring my talents to fill a need. Letting
them know how Christ can be the stablizing influence that is so needed
in our world--that God loves them--, needs to be the reason for ministry.
Thus far my main avenues of ministry since being ordained have been through
fuller participation in the worship services, but I sense a great need
in our branch for family ministry. It is my desire to develop visiting
skills and become acquainted with the Saints in their homes in order to
perceive the needs there and minister to them,

During the actual laying on of hands for ordination, I found my mind
wandering at first with irritating concerns. Was the microphone going to work?
Did my daughter remember to turn the tape recorder on? This mind-wandering is
one of my frustrating faults. Quickly, I realized my distractions and turned
myself over to the matter at hand. After all, I was giving myself to God again,

in a new way and responsibility. If I was really serious about this, for good-
ness sake, I'd better attend to what was happening.

Never in my life have I experienced what I experienced that evening. As
Brother Ken Fisher began praying for me, I listened to his words and felt the
beautiful warmth of the Holy Spirit touch me as it never had before. I actually
felt warmth, very intense warmth, begin at the top of my head and, as the
ordination prayer continued, so this warmth continued to flood slowly from my
head downward through my body until it touched all the way to my toes.

•

Gael Lynn Self - 3

After the prayer was over, and I contemplated what had happened, I thought,
if I try to explain this, someone will think I've been having a hot flash. Yet,
no hot flash ever started at the tip of my head--nor reached my toes.
Having the support of my family and loved ones in this new responsibility
has been a very important plus in my life. However, there are some families in
my congregation who have not accepted the concept of women in the priesthood--
let alone "me" as someone to minister in their lives. This has made me very
sad in many in~tances, because some of these people I've known all of my church
life. However, even though I have felt the sting of their rejection when they
have literally turned their backs to me, ignored me, not spoken to me, said
angry things about me, none of these things has made me reconsider for a moment
my decision to respond to the calling of my Heavenly Father. r, do have to say,
though, that I have never had anyone verbally abuse me face to face for my choice
and I'm grateful that I haven't had to experience that.
Since my ordination, I have been uplifted many times by the acceptance of
most of our membership. One young mother, on a Connnunion Sunday, said to me,
"If you had been serving connnunion, I would have moved to the side of the
church where you were serving." What a kind expression of support. I did
tell her, however, that it was not the responsibility of the Teacher to serve
conununion, but that I was so grateful for her thoughts.
I have never been overly comfortable praying in public, but I have been
amazed so many times since my ordination how easy it has been for me to stand
up in front of people and pray, and how often my thoughts in my prayers seem to
be more organized and more easily expressed. It has surely been the working of
the Holy Spirit in making this opportunity to serve in this way so much smoother.

It has only been a few short months since my ordination. My opportunities
for public responsibility in ministry have been mostly in offering prayers during
our Sunday worship services. However, I find many ways to work one-on-one with
people, talk to them, show my love and concern for them. It is very easy for
me to encourage people in their attendance, reach out to children and young
people to make them feel a part of activities, try to find areas where individuals
can serve. I do not mind being in the background.
My definition of ministry at this moment seems very simple--I see ministry
as the responsibility and the opportunity to bring the message and presence of
Jesus Christ into the lives of people. As a Teacher, I realize that my ministry
is most specifically to be to the members of my congregation, and I like that.
Just because people have been baptized and are now members of the church doesn't
mean that they are in constant communication with God and feel His presence in
their lives all the time. Members need to be nurtured and renewed and refreshed
over and over again as they strive to keep close to their Heavenly Father. I feel
blessed that I have been given the task of "watching over" my congregation.
My professional training has been that of an elementary school teacher
although I only worked for a short period of time in the public schools.
However, I have used my training many, many years with church school, women's
activities, camps, etc. The experiences in this area have helped me become
more prepared for the ministry I can offer as a member of the priesthood.
I am so grateful that I feel no questions or hesitancy about the rightness

of my being called into the priesthood. In the past six months, as our congrega-
tion has struggled W:ith acceptance of Section 156, many of us have recognized

continuous instances of confirmation of the rightness of this direction. Our
worship services, while becoming reduced in numbers, have been richly blessed by
the Holy Spirit time and time again. That is, perhaps, one of the greatest
confirmations.

Even since my husband, & I were married, I've been somewhat involved in our Church. We were married in April 1854 and I became a member in Nov. 1554. I pay somewhat involved in church because my husband wasn't involved and we would go to church hit and miss for 10 yrs. When the family started arriving, I felt they should be in Sunday School so I would drop them off & with them up every Sunday for about 2 yrs. At that time I thought it was rechculous to just drop them off, so I stayed and would leave  in the middle of class to go home I pick up my husband. My husband was a policeman, and was using his job as an excuse for not coming to church earlier. He would come for 11:00 service though. Then in 196, I was on the verge of a nervous breakdown with family problems, my husband's strange hours, and 4 children to take care of, when one night I had an experience  with the Lord I'll never ever forget. He told me to call our pastor and ask for my patriarchal blessing. Since it was 3:30 or 4.00 a.m. I felt it was too early to call so I tried to forget about it until morning. However, the Lord had other ideas. I dialed the pastors number and couldn't put thetshone down until I had completed the call. When he answered the phone I told him why I was calling and he told me he knew already. This was my first experience of many. After that I was involved as a teacher in S.S. and bringing in the chair. I he over the year. about every possible position in church work except Administration offices. The one job I thorsughly enjoyed was Zions League Leader appr. My husband & I had that for 10 yrs. It almost broke my heart to have to give up that pb. I can see now that the Lord was – leading my life even then. About a year later I had to have concer surgery. All during and after the surgery I never had any descomfort unsually associated with breast cancer. I was on a natural high for most of my stay in the Inspital. I was cheering up people who were coming to their me. Even the hospital personal could understand it. Of course I would tell them it was the Lord's work not not me. Since I then I have been visiting masectomy patients in the hospital to help them “adjust to their surgery. I knew within myself that I had a calling long before 156 ever came into being I was hit head on with 156, when I some of my husband's relatives who it loved very much over the years and wouldn't speak to me because I didn't believe the same way they did about women in the priesthood. I still hadn't heard of my calling yet. I could only I speak of our branch, but I could see our mens participation in their priesthood offices dwindling. It became apparent to me then that God had no choice but to call women since the men werent doing their jobs. Even when some of our representatives for the conference where 156 was passed, came back and weren't sure whether this was of God or not. I found myself to telling them that if James 1:5 worked for Joseph Smith, why not try it for yourself too. Then came the day about 6 mos. later when our pastor said he had something he wanted to talk to me about after church. I knew exactly what he wanted to see me about. The only thing I wasn't sure about was the office. He told me I had been called to the office of Teacher, but I wouldn't be ordained. until I complete the required courses. After my courses were completed, I was ordained in Nov. 1986, It was the most beautiful service I have seen for a while (male or female). There was another woman ordained the same time to the office of Elder so I had her assist in my ordination too. I hadn't had a very good relationship with my mother before she died, but on the day of my ordination she started at the back of the sanctuary and was escorted by two angels up to the front where I was being ordained. She smiled at me as if to say, everything is alright. My husband holds the office of Teacher also a my son hold the office of Deacon. My son didn't have a problem with my calling, and my husband says he believes in women in the priesthood, but for other husbands wives not his. I can see where God can use each of our talents to His advantage. We now have three teachers at our branch. Each one has a different type of minister to give. My husband has a way to bring people together and get them to do different things in the church. No one has been able to get them to do these things. My friend Esther's gift is teaching scripture to the people. I have a gift of being able to feel at ease talking to the strangers I meet at the hospitals I visit. Each one of us are doing our jobs, but in different ways. The only thing I felt real bad about was my inlaws didn't come to my ordination. Some old friends I hadnt seen many years came though so I guess it compensated for it. Some day they'll come to a better understanding of "Women in Priesthood" I never thought I was serving in my office since being ordained, but I am. My husband & I were asked to work at a camp this summer. As is a habit of mine, I asked God ahead of time to send some people to me. I worked as an assistant to the Cook and I noticed this one give who I always saw sort of staring at me. finally one day she came into the kitchen and started talking to me. Some of the things she talked about were some very personal things about her father (an alcoholic) that was bothering her. She was dead serious when she became concerned about where her father went. when he was killed in an accident. I did what I could to measure her, Then when I arrived home from that camp, I found that the mother of the little boy I babysit for said she felt very comfortable talking to me. Some of the things she talked about were problems. she a her husband were having and were tearing them apart. Again, I did what I could to help her. I do these things so automatically that I really don't realize I'm doing them. I find myself praying quite a lot before I do anything. I have only had 2 negative experiences. Both of them were when I had asked for Administration and they forgot all about it. The one time was after I had been ordained a while. When I realized what had happened, I called for the ministering angels for myself. I knew I could call for them for some one else, but not for myself. I am finding that if I am willing to allow God to use me, I never have to worry about how things will be done. We have at our branch a part of our service we call Sharing Time. sometimes I'm not real sure whether I should talk about a certain subject or not. I usually hesitate. before long though I'm lifted out of my seat by a power not of my own to talk about it whatever it may be. I hope this is brief enough. I could go on. My not so silent partner & I have been through a lot together.

4J.. I am a member of the Shawnee Drive Congregation of the Kansas City Stake and have
been an active member of that congregation for 25 years. I was baptised into the
church at the age of 8 through the Taberville, Missouri branch.
I was married at the age of 16 and have 3 children and have been married for 271⁄2 years.
My call to Elder was approved at the Kansas City Stake Conference of October 19, 1986.
I was ordained on November 9, 1986 at Shawnee Drive. High Priest Bruce Rider and
Patriarch Ival Scholtz ordained me. The speaker that morning was Margaret Athey who
was one of two of the first women to be ordained in our stake.
My husband is a member of the church but was inactive at the time of my call. Also,
two of my three children were inactive due to bad experiences with people in the church
and loss of two babies in my sons marriage.
I have always felt that God has blessed me and been with me through my life and I knew
that He would bless me in my call so I prayed that my ordination service be to His glory
and that it be a special experience for my family members and the congregation.
I believe it was. My family were tearful and seemed to make some changes immediately in
their lives. Deneice, my oldest child, had been seeking God in her life in many ways
and after the ordination service seemed to be somewhat changed and anxious to help me
and support me in my service. She and her roommate have been attending church more

PERSONAL TESTIMONY OF ELDER JANET NORRIS SMITH September 1987
As a young girl, my family and I attended church reunions each August
at Kirtland, Ohio. The experiences in the Temple were a high point in
my religious life and provided a source of strength from which I can
still draw.
I was usually rather quiet and silent in groups of people whom I did
not know well. So it was a surprise to me that during a special young
people's prayer and testimony service, I sto'od with others in the
upper auditorium of the Temple and found the words to express my ~
desire to serve God in whatever ways He might find for me. ♦
Little did I realize, or could I or others comprehend, the events
which would lead to my returning to the Kirtland Temple in October,
1986 for the Women's Institute. Nor could I foresee that my life
choices would lead to and permit me to accept a priesthood call to the
office of elder which would also give me the opportunity to become one
of the first women to serve communion in the Temple.
I was baptized at the age of 12 and almost immediately began teaching
a Church School class at Nelsonville, Ohio. There were few people to
help in this small Southeastern Ohio branch where I received my early
church training. But when the Church School Director asked my father,
who was the pastor, if I could teach the kindergarten-primary age
children, he first said no, I needed to be in a class myself to
continue my learning. Then he revised his thinking and said yes,
because, in order to teach, I would have to know the material which
would require study and preparation on my part.
Throughout my years in the church, I have served in many offices and
activities. My interest and abilities in music have permitted me to
serve as music and choir director, pianist and after taking organ
lessons at Graceland College, as organist. I became a professional
teacher and used these skills in teaching and directing Vacation
Church Schools, in continuing teaching church school classes of all
ages, and in serving as District Christian Education Commissioner. I
am now working with the Upper Ohio Region as a core instructor to
train teachers for Temple School classes.
I enjoy planning worship services and special programs and have served
as branch Worship Commissioner since 1984 when our Faith to Grow
Commissions were first established. I have been on the branch budget
committee for seven years, serving as chairperson for three years.
Most of my adult and married life has been spent in the Lancaster,
Ohio congregation of which my husband, Don, is pastor. My priesthood
call was initiated through him after much deliberation and prayer
because of the responsibility placed on him to present this call for
his wife. The Lancaster Branch approved my call on February 9, 1986,
followed by Columbus District approval on June 8, 1986.

- 1 -

shar~d with me their feelings about my ministry. Two women in
particular who have been dear family friends for many years and have
known me throughout my life, have provided continued support. One

Another woman shared with me that, although at first she had doubts
about women serving in the priesthood, she was convinced of the
trut~fulne~s of ~ection 156_in June 1985 when I was in charge of a
sp~cial Children s Day service. I had planned the worship service
using young people and women, two of whom have received calls to the
Aaronic priesthood but have not yet been ordained.
My own thoughts concerning a possible call were feelings of joy and
anticipation mixed with concern and humility. Even as it was
impressed upon me that I would be called to the office of elder, I
tried to change God's mind or at least attempt to think about the
responsibilities of teacher or priest as they would relate to me. But
each time I tried, those offices were erased from my mind, and only
the office of elder remained.
I knew when Regional President, Tom Mountenay, called and asked to
come to my home to talk with me that his purpose was to inform me of
my priesthood call and that it would be to the office of elder. With
his responsibilities as pastor, my husband has always maintained
silence concerning calls and other church matters until they can be
made public. Because of Don's strict adherence to this procedure and
also because in the case of a wife's call, she should be given time
and opportunity to make her own decision, I felt that I should not and
could not talk with him during my early searching for confirmation. I
also had no idea that he had initiated my calling many, many months
before.
There was never any doubt in my mind that if I were called, I would
accept. Not only had I promised to serve God wherever and however
possible, but my parents, Delbert and Helen Norris, had devoted their
lives to Him and had spent time and energy in teaching me, through the
example of the way they lived, the basic principles of the gospel. I
did know, however, that the way would not be easy. We have persons in
our congregation who cannot accept the giftedness of women as it
relates to priesthood. Some are very outspoken, some ignore those who
are called, and some have refused to attend services either all the
time, or when I am scheduled to speak.
But on the other hand, I have received strong support from many
people. I have seen the attitudes of some chan~e and_I have ~e~n able

I was v~ry pleased and happy to have five people from my office attend
the ordination service. I had invited the staff to attend when I
shared my calling with them several weeks previously. It was a joyful occasion to have three women and two men from my co-workers attend.
Another man sent me a very lovely note of congratulations and

Part of my testimony must also include my mother's testimony as well.
My mother had never enjoyed good health and during her early twenties
had a kidney removed. About twenty-five years later she had surgery
on the remaining one and made such a rapid recovery that even her
doctors were amazed. Mother was a convert to the church and lived her
seventy-seven years serving faithfully in many ways.
After she and my dad were married, they wanted a family so my sister
was born. Her doctor then told my parents that it was too dangerous
for her to have any more children. However, they did not want to rear
an only child, so four years later I was born. With all her physical
problems, I know that she did not expect to have a long life. She
often said that her prayer was that she would live long enough to rear
her daughters and give them a sound foundation in the church.
Both my sister and I have enjoyed good health throughout our lives.
Except for an emergency appendectomy twenty-seven years ago, and a few
other minor problems, I have not had any illnesses and usually do not
even suffer from flu or common viruses.
One evening, about four years ago, I was waiting in the·church foyer
for the start of our Women's Department meeting. I had just completed
my annual physical examination that day, and was quietly sitting alone
for a few minutes with no particular thoughts in mind. And then it
was impressed upon me very strongly that God had blessed me with good
health because my mother had never enjoyed that same blessing. That
feeling of being so blessed was to return to me many times within the
next few years. It was only about three months later when I developed
a back problem requiring medication and treatment, but which has not
affected me in any major way.
However, nine days after my ordination, I discovered a lump in my
breast. I had had a complete physical less than four months before
with no indication of any problem. But there was no mistake, a
sizable lump was present now. I immediately contacted my family
physician who then referred me to a surgeon.
A biopsy was scheduled as soon as possible. I was administered to and
found a source of strength therein as well as from my experie~ce four
years ago of realizing my blessing of lifelong good health. I also
kept thinking that certainly there was more to my service as an elder·
than the few days or weeks that I had experienced so far. And
throughout the days before surgery, Don, my sister and family, my
children, and my church friends were so supportive. I had much to be
thankful for.

But wednesday afternoon when we returned from the second day of tests,

Throughout my experience, I maintained that three things were
important in my life - my faith in God, the support of my family and
friends, and my sense of humor. Although there were moments of
sadness and depression, and there were tears and questions, I still
felt that the blessing of health would be mine.
On Thursday morning following administration and before I could even
wake up and get ready for the day's test, the hospital technician
called me to say that the CAT scan had to be postponed because the

Another result of the administration was that I knew the surgery as

every activity possible. We were present at conferences, homecomings,
missionary meetings, Zion's League get-togethers, and all regularly
scheduled branch activities on Sunday mornings, Sunday evenings and
Wednesday nights. Our home was open to the missionaries who came to
minister in our area and I have many wonderful memories of listening
to men such as George Lewis, Virgil Billings, John Blackstock, Lou
Zonker and Merle Guthrie. Their lives and testimonies, as well as
those of many others, have had a great impact on my life.
During the early years of marriage, I remember the guidance and
support given me by Brother Frank Fry who was working with the
Columbus, Ohio District. It was he who shared with me the information
that my husband would be called to the office of elder. Although I
did not share that information with anyone, it gave me the insight I
needed to encourage and support Don as he considered the
responsibilities which might be his if he accepted that call.
I had been happy to give support and help to my father, and then to my
husband, in their priesthood calls. About five years ago, Don, who
was counselor to the district president, was asked to attend a
conference at Kirtland in place of our DP. As we worshiped in the
Temple, both of us felt the spirit of God working with us toward the
realization that there were other ways in which we could serve. At
the time I felt that my role would be that of support to Don in his
calling to another priesthood office. So as I walked back up the
driveway one day, carrying the mail, I knew as soon as I saw the
letter from Independence, that he had received his call. After I
handed the letter to Don, he read it slowly and then humbly handed it
back for me to read. In October of 1983 he was ordained to the office
of High Priest.
