l's?- i~

I am st ill happy to support him in his calling, and he gives me the
same st rong support and encouragement. Within these past years of
working together, I have come to a greater realization of the worth of
;:e~y person in the sight of God and the importance of helping each
_in a place to serve. The office of member is still the most
i~portant in the overall plan of things. As I branched out into
different responsibilities, I saw more and more needs to be met. So
when my own priesthood call came, I felt that it was an extension of
wh~t I ~as already doing. The greatest difference was in relation to
being given the authority to preside over the sacraments.
Althou~h_I have always been aware of the importance of the sacrament
of administration in my own life and those close to me I did not
realiz~ that the elders were called on so frequently b~ so many for so
many different reasons. Within a year's time, I have participated in
numerous administrations as a result of physical and health problems,
of stress from loss of a companion through death, or of depression due
to unemployment, financial needs and personal problems.
I see my own ministry as providing healing, not only as a result of
administration for problems whatever their cause, but also to bring
our branch closer together so that God can work through us to expand
our service to all. I want to be able to perceive needs wherever and
whenever they arise and to meet those needs, either through my efforts
or through providing a source of help from someone else who can better
serve them. I realize that I cannot meet everyone's needs, and that I
must depend on God's direction to guide me in finding the most
effective means of helping others. I also realize that some people
will not respond to me, either because I am a woman or because of
differences in our outlook and experiences.
Some people have not been willing to acknowledge my calling, and will
not even give me the opportunity to minister to them. At the business
meeting, during the presentation of my name for priesthood support,
there were several who voted against me and others who spoke out
against calls of any women to the priesthood. I feel that I have been
especially blessed in feeling that these actions were not against me
personally. Instead, I have felt a sadness for those who will not
accept each of us for what we are and can offer. I have also
appreciated several who said to me that they did not believe in or who
had not yet been fully convinced of women's place in the priesthood,
but who continue to attend services and support me in many ways.
Their openness and willingness to look beyond their own point of view
have been a testimony to me.
One of the great joys in sharing priesthood with my companion has been
the opportunity to serve together on many occasions. I can empathize
·th him so much more because I, too, have "been there". Although he
;~st still maintain silence under some conditions as they relate tQ
d · · trative and confidential matters, I feel that I can more freely a m1n1s h' 'd • relate some of my own insights to him for is consi eration.

• There is a special feeling present as we share together in

administering the sacraments. As we place our hands together on the
head of the one receiving administration, or receive good news from
the recipient as a follow-up; as we participate together in a worship
service; or as we plan for the priesthood and commissioners meetings,
I know that God is there, providing us both with insight and strength
to accomplish His work.
In July we shared a very special occasion when Don and I officiated at
the wedding of our step-grandaughter. The wedding was held in our
church and it was an opportunity to minister to those in attendance
because our children were the only RLDS church members present with
us.
We are continuing to work together for outreach, for strengthening our
present membership, and for enriching the worship and fellowship
experiences provided. I feel a more personal responsibility to each
member as a result of my own ordination. I can also see that in the
future I may be called upon to assume administrative duties for which
I have professional training and experience. I know all things are
possible through God so I am limited only by my own perceptions, and,
in part, by those who are willing to accept my ministry.
One of my personal goals at this time is to devote more time to study
and to meditation. I have always been a person to enjoy being
involved in things all the time but I sense a need for quiet time, for
more prayer and contemplation, and to be open for receiving direction
from God as He works within my life.
My experiences through the Temple School Learning Center during the
past year have provided opportunities for me to begin achieving this
goal. I enjoy learning so I have taken courses from the original
School of the Restoration and then from Temple School even before I
had any idea of ordination. It was simply to help me grow and be more
qualified to serve in any way I could.
With the establishment of the Regional Center at Lancaster, I can
teach and I can take more classes during the times I am not assigned
teaching responsibilities.
My first class to teach was The Elder, and the men in the class were
all elders, Great! And I had been an elder for all of two months!
But it was a beautiful experience and the spirit of God rested upon
each one of us as we shared together. I was so graciously accepted
that it was difficult to end the class and see them leave.
In August, I helped teach Priesthood of Women to the high priests and
spouses meeting in Bellaire, West Virginia. God's people are so
loving that it is a joy to be with such a group and to work with them.
And I always find that I receive much more than I can ever give. I
appreciated the response and individuality of each one present. What
a marvelous way to get acquainted or better acquainted with those
present, and to share with the Bellaire branch in the Sunday morning
worship service.

- 11 -

Women in the priesthood need a support system even as the men have
always needed it. One of the ways which we have been strengthened is
through a winter retreat and slumber(less!) party at the home of one
of the other women elders in the Columbus District. Under the
leadership of Elder Bonnie Glandon of South Church, Columbus, nine
women who had accepted Aaronic priesthood calls, met for an evening
and morning sharing session. Besides Bonnie and myself, there were
two other priesthood members present; Elder Judy Cross, who is pastor
of the Worthington Branch, and Teacher Nora Guilkey, also from South
Branch, Columbus. We worshipped together, we shared our testimonies
and our concerns, we cried and hugged, we ate and talked, and we
prayed about the roles we were to assume in God's kingdom. What
potential there is for providing strength to each other - priesthood
or member - in like occasions.
There is always risk in taking on new responsibilities and in opening
oneself to new learning. Yet I have found that God is always present
to help me over the rough places and to show me a better way if I am
willing to see, to hear and to feel. I know no other way to grow. I
must be concerned with others and recognize from where they are
coming. So it has been my aim to take things slowly within the branch
with the prayer that my ministry might be seen as an extension of what
I have been doing throughout my years of service in the church. I
have attempted to give the nonsupportive element some time to adjust
slowly to the changes. Yet I know that there is a time when they must
decide for themselves the extent to which they can accept my ministry
and that of the two other women who will shortly be ordained.
As I review my first year of priesthood responsibility, the feelings
of joy and anticipation, concern and humility are still present. To
them, I have added an all-encompassing gratitude and love for the
opportunity to extend my service, and for the people with whom I come
in contact daily.
The words of two hymns - one from the past and one from the present -
join together my thoughts of commitment and service. The traditional
recessional hymn at the Kirtland reunions was "Unto God, Who Knows Our
Every Weakness". The third verse is:
"Lord, accept the humble consecration
Of our lives, our talents to thy cause,
Till thy word is preached in every nation
And each one has a knowledge of thy laws."
To those words I add the present-day thoughts expressed by my young
friend, Ken McLaughlin, now pastor of South Church, Columbus, in his
hymn, "Lord, Who Views All People Precious":
"We accept the great commission.
May we, strengthened with your power,

Join with Christ in common purpose-
Serving nobly in this hour." AMEN

"If they ever ordain women in my church, I'll leave it!" Words out of
my mouth one day in 1970 or 1971. We were sitting at lunch at the table in my
office at Kearny Mesa Convalescent Hospital, in San Diego, California, were I
was the Social Activities Director. At the table with me were my right hand
helper, volunteer and best friend, Lois Kazyaka (who is also a member of the
Clairemont Congregation where I attend), and Margi (Dillinger) Herrick, R.P.T.
Margi had just mentioned a woman minister in a local church. Margi was and
and still is active in a Methodist church in La Jolla. A fleeting, unexplainable,
wierd feeling impressed the moment in my mind indelibly. I passed it off as
a reaction to such a dogmatic statement.
As time passed, my friend Lois and I had many good talks about many things,
both 1n and out of the church. We didn't always see eye to eye, but that was
all right because we were both exploring, questioning, and testing. No one

ever tried to sway my thinking one way or another, but .long before the presen-
tation of what is now Doctrine and Covenants 156, both my husband, Seventy

G. Wayne Smith, and I knew that women should and would be included in the ranks
of priesthood. I felt we had been "taught by the Spirit."
In my mind I came to understand that "male" and "female" were physical
differences for earthly procreation. In the spirit there are no color, ethnic,
nor sexist designations. I also became aware that ministry is not confined
to priesthood, although the responsibility for the administering the ordinances
and sacraments 1s. Any individual who cares for another can be used by God
to give ministry. "These signs shall follow them that believe. . " (Mark 16: 16).
I have also become aware of the gulf that sometimes separates God's truth from
human traditions. I also realize that, as I look back upon the fallicies of
my earlier understandings (such as the opening statement above) I can also
look forward to an even more accurate understanding of God's nature and will
in the future as I prayerfully study the inspired writings of His ministers.

- 2 -

I have discovered. that God is much bigger than any human understanding.
We can not enclose Him in a box of our own understanding and making. I believe
th at if we free Him in our own minds to work in whatever way He sees best
that the job of '' • . w1.nn1.ng the world" would be accomplished more effectively.
On October 19, 1986,at the regular Fall branch business meeting, my call
to the office of Teacher was approved. On the same day, in the evening, at the
Fall District Conference my call was approved by unanimous vote of the Conference.
My call was presented by Elder Ron Beitz. I felt the presence of the Holy Spirit
at those times that I gave my acceptance publicly.
I was ordained in the Clairemont Congregation on January 25, 1987 under
the hands of my husband, Seventy G. Wayne Smith, and two of our daughters -
Elders Barbara Gardner of Casper, Wyoming, and Cheryl Saur of Carrollton, Texas.
Our only son, David, and his wife Connie, their three daughters, and our other
daughter, Cynthis Chesnutt, of Davenport, Iowa formed a family choir. We sang
my favorite hymn, "Now In This Moment". (No. 58 in Hymns of the Saints). Our
new pastor, John Kazyaka presided, his wife Lois, a Priest, gave the charge to
the congregation. District president Ed Towers gave the charge to the ordinand,
Elder Barbara Gardner did'the,offertory and Elder Cheiyl Saur gave a reading.

I had been ill most of the month following Christmas so I was still feel-
ing a little "disconnected", but my feeling of pride for our children overshadowed

anything for myself. It was the first time 1.n almost ten years that all four
children had been together. It was a happy and joyful day!
I've had no negative reactions to my ministry. - It seems that my ordination
freed me to act on the insights I have into people's needs as I observe and visit
them. At a Women's Retreat in the Spring of 1986 I was given the Gift of Prophecy,
apparently in preparation for the ministry of a Teacher. Sometimes, in fear
and trembling, I approach s.omeone with a message but I have never beem rejected.
only received with warmth and love.

the love and support we recieved from our fellow saints.
Our calls were approved unanimously by both the congregation

the testimonies of reassurance and support offered by people
from various congregations, some of whom I barely knew.
Three of us were ordained on Easter morning, 1~d7, one

TESTIMONY
OF
BEVERLY A. SPECHT

I would like to bear my testimony of what was the major factor in my turning my life over
to God. I have been a lifelong member of this church, and over the years have held many
offices, taught church school, etc. I considered myself a good member, a good Christian,
(I thought), a firm believer. But as I look back, I can see where I wasn't as dedicated
as I should have been, or as I have been since my parents died. My parents were wonderful
loving, Christian people, and members of the church. My dad was as elder. They set a good
example for me in everyway, but especially in dying, My mother suffered greatly, but never
complained. She was dying of cancer and she was an inspiration to everyone, especially her
fanily. Mom got down to forty-one pounds and looked pitiful, but her faith never wavered.

I'll never forget that last day. Our children and three yearold grandson, my dad, my hus-
band and I were all there. She knew why! She said, 'I'm so gald to see everyone. She was

looking forward to death, a release from pain. After everyone else left, dad and I sat
wth her and held her hands. She was drowning in her own fluid and it was not an easy death.
Still she did not complain. She told us she loved us. She could no longer get her breath
and we tried to make her more comfortable. All of a sudden, she looked up in the room and
said, 'Mother!' My mother was sixty-nine years old and her mother had died when she was
fourteen years old. With that, she was gone, and we knew that something wonderful had
happened. Her mother had come to take her home. I will never forget this. I knew then
that there truly was a God and that we do go on living. I needed that testimony in my life
to strengthen me. God knew I needed this.
The second great experience came after my fathers death. My father had Alzheimers disease,
and to me it seemed worse than my mothers illness, because he could no longer communicate
with me. I was devastated. When I could no longer take care of him, the doctor said he
must go into a nursing home. I didn't know anything could hurt so much. I was desolate.
My wonderful, loving father couldn't even feed himself. His final present to me was Christ-
( nas Day, just two weeks before he died, He hadn't been able to speak for months, and as I
sat with him that Christmas Day, he looked at me and said, 'I love you, honey.' The most
wonderful present he could possibly have given me. He was dying with pneumonia, and the
doctor said, 'I am not giving him anything to make him well. Your father does not want to
live in the conditon he is in,' I knew that the doctor was right. He had known dad before
this terrible thing had happened to him. Yet when he died, I sat there watching him and
thinking, I have killed my father because I agreed to this. I have never known despair
such as this. For two weeks after dad died, I was an emotional wreck and I prayed, 'God,
you have to help me.' Then one night, I had this beautiful dream. My father was lying in
the coffin and there was a blanket over him. All of a sudden, he threw the blanket off,
threw his leg over the side of the coffin and jumped out. There was my dad - the way I
remembered him. Tall and handsome, with a big smile on his face. He said, "I'm fine,
honey.' I woke up the next morning, peaceful and happy. The Lord was still with me.
I knew after section 156 was passed, that someday I would be in the priesthood, I didn't
know it would come as soon as it did though. When our pastor said he wanted to talk to
me, I knew right away what he was going to say. He then told me how he had been awakened
three times during the night with the message that my husband, who was a deacon, was called

to be a priest, and I was called to the office of teacher. Bill and I were ordained Nov-
ember 30th, 1987. We were ordained at our home branch in Clinton, Iowa. I was ordained

by our pastor, Elder Howard Brown and assisted by Patriarch Clarence M. Beil. Bill was
ordained by Clarence and assisted by Howard.

( For years before section 156, I had thou~,t that women should be silent, but after my
parent~ deaths, I started studying seriously and preparing myself, I knew I wanted to
be a witness for Jesus Christ, I knew that God had work for me to do, Before my parents
deaths, I couldn't even get up in prayer meeting because I was too shy to speak in front
of people. I know this change in me came abo11t because of the Holy Spirit working in my
life. I have preached several times siiice r was ordained, I have borne my testimony many
times and want to tell everyone how they can have this great joy in their life.
Since I have been ordaind, I study more and I find I can talk more openly to people about
my beliefs. One of the nicest experiences I have had, was after I had preached on Sunday
morning. A dear friend, who I was sure wouldn't come because she does not accept women
priesthood members, not only came to hear me, but after the service, threw her arms around
me and told me she loved me. I cherish this memory, because I know that even if we do not
agree on section 156, we can still love one another the way Christ wants us to.
Doctrine & Covenants section 17:lla starts out, 'The teacher's duty is to watch over the
church always, and be with, and strengthen them, etc. I want to help strengthen peoples
belief in God, make Him more real to them.
I pray that I will be a witness to everyone I am with, by the way I live, and that my love
for God will show by the way I love people. I thank God that He has given me this wonderful
opportunity, and I pray that I will be worthy of my calling.

In March, 1986 I was called to the office of Teacher. On Nov. 30, 1986
I was voted on by the Branch members and ordained by Lillian Dunlap and
E~len McGuire after I had completed my Temple School Classess. I was widowed before my calling, my husband had died in Ireland in Nov. 1984.
We were on tour there, and he had a massive heart attack, and died within
a few hours.
On my way home from Ireland I was very concerned about my future life and
I was given to understand that God had a work for me to do and later on
a'.ter the funeral and everything was cared for,a messenger talked to me one night and told me I was going to be called to the priesthood and to
think about it, I was very concerned because I was 70 years old but I
was given to understand that age meant nothing to God. I started to go to
Temple School classes to prepare for my calling, when my Pastor called on
me to tell me about my calling, Itold him I knew all about it ahd would
accept it. He was very pleased.
My odrination service was very spiritual, and uplifting and encouraged me
to work and spread the gospel and help~dthers. When I first heard of Section
156, I felt it was a divine revelation and .badly needed and hoped I could
be a part of it and I am and I feel it is working well and very much
needed.
I made a call on a dear sister who was not well and who had been a dear
friend and when she answered the door, she told me my calling was not
divine but by Satan and not to work in my calling. I couldn't talk to her
so I told her I loved her and I left. Since that time she has apolized to
me and she feels good toward amd and we both love each other.
Others have responded very nicely to me, some were a little cool at
first but now all is well and 3 families have trturned to church and
I am still working with others. I have been called to come and visit them
and I feel God is helping me a lot in my efforts to work. I pray a lot
about it and I feel led by the spirit, when there were times I didn't know
what to say, I was directed by God and I was amazed at my anwers. Now it
is easier for me to talk to people and to offer prayers and take a more
active part when called upon. I do feel the Temple School calsses are
very helpful and educational and I feel I am much closer to God and his
plan of Salvation. I wish I was younger in years but I will endeavor to
do a1l I can now.

Leona M. Staples

In August 1986, my pastor, Ross Wood, made an appointment
to come and visit me along with our Dlstrlct President, Neil
McLean. I felt sure that they were coming to present a
priesthood call to me. I was dismayed for I had not wanted
to be called to the priesthood. However, I was not really
surprised because I knew that I had been doing a lot of the
so-called "priesthood work" for years. I felt that I had
done so much of that kind of work and had accomplished so
much without being ordained that I was content to continue in
this manner. I felt that my ministry in our branch was not
only accepted, but welcomed and I felt fortunate that women
in our branch were used and appreciated without ordination.
I was asked to accept the call to the office of elder and to
discuss it with my husband, Bill, who had been an elder for
many years. The men assured me that I had the right to ask
for assurance of my call. This I have done, but do not feel
that I have received this assurance - at least not the
unquestionable assurance that I hope for.
My husband was delighted with my call. So, with his support,
and knowing the great need we have for priesthood in
our branch, I said I would accept the call.
Another woman from our branch, Helen Sevis, was called to the
office of teacher at the same time. We were the first women
to be called in the Niagara Falls branch, and along with
Martha Grigsby of Brantford, the first to be called in the
Niagara District.
Our branch business meeting was held in September 1986. It
had been announced that priesthood calls would be presented,
but not whose. When the calls were presented and accepted by
Helen and me, one elder who was a counsellor to the branch
president got up and said that he felt that as a counsellor,
these calls should have been discussed with him prior to
their presentation. The pastor said that it was his
understanding that he was to discuss them with no one except
the District President. The elder then stated that he had
nothing against Helen and me personally but that he did not
approve of women's ordination in general. When it came to
the vote, he, his wife and daughter voted against it but
they were the only ones. After the meeting he assured Helen
and me again that he did not have anything against us.
Our District Conference was to be held in Brantford, which is
the branch in our district furthest from Niagara Falls.
Ordinarily very few people from our branch attend district
conference, especially when it is far away. However, the
women from our branch wanted to attend this time to support

I

was touched by their support and I invited them ~11 to
t

ordination of women, W.B.Smith and Section 156, They had
caused so much trouble in the branch that they found it
a~rnost impossible to elect a branch president. When one was
finally elected, he cancelled the rest of the election
closed the meeting and notified Apostle Austin who '
dis?rga~ized t~e branch. The letter notifying 1

the branch of

been read at the morning service the day of the conference.
Consequently, many, including the dissenters, did not attend
the conference. Therefore, there was no public opposition
when our calls were presented. There was, however, a great
deal of support, some very moving testimonies and a good
spirit.
We were very anxious to proceed with the ordinations after
they had been approved because two of our elders, including
the pastor, go south for the winter, leaving my husband and
one deacon to keep the branch going. Everyone wanted the
ordinations to take place before they left. However, we had
one problem. I had not taken the required Temple School
courses. In the back of my mind I had thought that since
they must be completed before one could be ordained, if I did
not take them I could not be ordained. Therefore, I had
consciously neglected to take them. Two of the courses were
being offered locally so I took them and immediately wrote to
Independence to enroll in the third course by correspondence.
The ordination date was set for November 9, 1986 and I waited
impatiently for the course to come. It took almost two
months to arrive and never did arrive until the week after
the ordination. This caused a great deal of anxiety because
the Regional President would not relent and approve my
ordination until the day before it was to take place. He
finally gave permission on condition that I complete the
third course as soon as possible, which I did.
As Worship Chairman, I was responsible for planning the
ordination service so I planned it just as I wanted it.
we sang "Now In This Moment" which seems appropriate for many
occasions but I did feel we were writing a story not heard
before. our District President spoke and used a clipping
from a 1959 Saints' Herald which gave a very modern
interpretation of Jesus' regard for women. We all though~
that it had been written recently and everyone was surprised
to learn it had been printed in the Herald for Moth:rs' Day
1959. After the singing of Hymn 363, Helen was ordained to

Bill Stoner with Ross as the spokesman. I planned for Helen to be ordained first lest anyone think that I wanted to be
t~e first to be ordained. Then I was ordained by my husband Bill, Ross Wood and Neil McLean. Following the ordinations my daughter and another girl sang a duet. They also plnned a
corsage on Helen and me. Then the pastor, Ross Wood spoke.
He mentioned how God had worked with people over the years to
bring them to a new way of thinking, such as of slavery and
pol~gamy. ~oth Neil and Ross were inspired and it was a very
moving service. The service closed with "Send Me Forth, o
Blessed Master". After the service we had a lunch downstairs
and one of the women had made and decorated a cake with
"God Bless Helen and Shirley".
I had not thought it proper to invite people to my ordination
service until that very morning. Then I called my brother
and one friend. They both came. I know that others were
disappointed that they did not know about it. However, I was
surprised how many who had heard about did come, some from
two hundred miles away. It was a very exciting day. I was
on a high comparable to my graduation from university which I
had just accomplished a year earlier. The only other thing to
compare it with is the wedding I officiated at this summer,
which was also a wonderful experience.
As for the events which led up to my ordination I can only
say that I have been actively involved in the work of the
church all my life. I have held almost every branch and
district office. I am a school teacher by profession and
in later years a school librarian. I still work half days as
a school librarian and English teacher. I met my husband at
reunion and we have been married thirty three years. We have
two children almost grown but still at home. My husband
has been branch president many times and I have assisted him
in many ways including visiting, correspondence and planning.
About ten years ago we had two contractual assignees in our
branch for eighteen months. They studied our needs
and developed a system for sharing the work load of our
branch. Up until that time no one ever wanted to be pastor
because it meant so much work. The system they devised was
almost identical to the commission system. They were very
gratified when the commission system was introduced because
it showed they were on the right track. So the Niagara
Falls branch had been on the commission system for a couple
of years before it became church policy. At first I
was Christian Education chairman but have been Worship
Chairman now for many years. I arrange all the speakers
for the branch, plan all the services, schedule the speakers,
presiders organists and special music. I type the bulletin
and keep track of all the announcements that should go on it.
over the years I have done a great deal of visiting. I try
to keep track of all our members and offer as much care as I

can. Consequently, the people of our branch depend on me a
great deal. If anyone in the branch wants to know anything
or needs any help, they call me. Hy mother lived with us for
eleven years and she could not believe the calls I received.
In 1975 I decided to go to University to get my B.A. I took
only one course at a time but eventually graduated in 1985,
majoring in Philosophy. One of my courses was Religion and
Philosophy and I had an essay to write on a topic of my
choice. I had been interested in something my professor had
taught us about Wisdom being personified as a woman in the
Bible. I decided to write my essay on the gender of God.
The research I did was very enlightening. I was able to work
through intellectually a lot of thoughts that it would be
necessary to come to terms with in order to accept
priesthood, although my call was unknown to me at the time.
When my essay was completed my husband showed it to our
Regional President. He asked me to send it to the Herald,
which I did. It was not printed in the Herald but it was
printed in Restoration Studies III. Writing this essay
certainly did a lot for me in preparing me intellectually to
accept the ordination of women and myself in particular.
However, I was still emotionally unprepared for my own
ordination. I accepted the calling because I had been doing
the work all my life and because our branch was so
desperately in need of priesthood, but it was hard for me to
feel right emotionally about being a priesthood member. I am
gradually getting used to it and feel more comfortable with
it now that almost a year has gone by.
Soon after my ordination the other elders left for the winter
leaving my husband and me to carry on the branch. In their
absence I was quite comfortable in serving communion, but did
avoid serving the one woman who had voted against my
ordination. Bill and I arranged that he would serve
whichever side she sat on, just to avoid trouble.
The first thing I had to do as an elder was administer to the
sick. We had so many sick last winter and Bill and I would
go together and one of us would anoint and the other confirm.
I was pretty nervous about it but think I can do it now. I
administered to a sister who had just had surgery not long
ago and she was very appreciative. Shortly after I was
ordained we were asked to bless a baby. I held the baby and
Bill offered the prayer. It wasn't long until we had another
baby blessing and this time I was the spokesperson. I had
spent a lot of time thinking about the prayer, as well as
working with the parents. They were unwed, very young, not
living together but well supported by friends and by her
family. The service went very well and it was a good
experience. The next baby blessing I was again asked to
offer the prayer. I thought it would be easier because this
baby was very tiny and I thought he would sleep quietly.
However, he wanted to be fed and screamed loudly all through

the prayer. It was a little disconcerting but I managed to
keep my wits about me and did the best I could. I think
after that experience I could do anything.
Then I was asked to officiate at the wedding of my niece. In
order to do this I had to complete the Marriage Ministry
Seminar offered by the Ontario Region and then apply to the
Ontario Government for a license to marry. The marriage took
place on August 15, 1987 in the Mississauga Branch. It was
an exciting and thrilling experience for me. My husband Bill
could have officiated but he ls such an excellent
organist that he is always asked to play for weddings. We
look forward to being asked again to serve as a wedding team.
I believe that this was the first wedding of our church
in Ontario to be solemnized by a woman. The groom and his

joy to the wedding. There were only a few Latter Day
Saints at the wedding but they all came up to me afterwards

One of my concerns for the wedding was what it would be

about delegating responsibility but it is easier said than
done. Sometimes there are just not enough people to do it
and sometimes they are just unwilling to the point of
refusal. Those of us who truly care about the church just
keep trying to do more and more. I care for a family, run a
household, work, am involved in the community and go to night
school as well as what I do for the church and there are no
spare minutes in my day. However, the rewards are great. My
husband and I are looking forward to the day when our
children are on their own and one or both of us can retire
and have more time to spend on the work of the church. We do
not see ourselves leaving the branch for extended periods of
time but continuing to work with the saints and community of
Niag~ra Falls to establish that Zionic condition which is
our dream and goal. We hope and pray that the Lord will
grant us many years of good health that we may do this.

Testimony of Nancy Strom

I. Which jurisdictions approved your ordination by vote of the
members? When? When and where were you ordained, and ~y whom
(all persons involved)? To what office?
My priesthood call was initiated through my husband, Elder

Gene Strom, who was the presiding elder of the Casper Con-
gregation at the time. This call was approved through Jack

Rees, former District President of The Wyoming District.
Then the call was channeled through the West Central Stake's
Region. All of this was done during the later part of 1985.
The call was approved by vote on the congregational level in
the spring of 1986. (The congregational meeting was difficult
for us three ladies that were accepting priesthood calls,
as the congregation wasn't unified over Section 156. Personal

comments were made that were quite painful. The three can-
idates chose to sit in the front row, so they couldn't see

the hands when it came to voting time. The calls were approv-
ed, and to this day I still don't know what the count was,

and who voted for or against. We were just thrilled and
thankful to be approved.) These same calls with others were
voted on and approved at the Fall District Conference. (The

support and spirit at conference was so warm; it was a re-
freshing surprise for me especially after the experience we

had had on the local level.)
My ordination took place at th~~ same Fall District Conference
held in September of 1986. Elder Earl Saxton (Cheyenne), Elder
Zane Geis (Laramie), and my husband, Elder Gene Strom officiated
my ordination to the office of priest.

My husband held the priesthood office of elder at the time of

3. What were some important life experiences and events since

My husband, Gene, and I were married in March of 1970 in
Chadron, Nebraska. I was a member of the Lutheran church at
the time. When dating we had visited the church, and I found

the people warm and caring. The Book of Mormon and The Doc-
trine and Covenants threatened me however, and I looked upon

the church as an appendage of the Utah Mormons. Before we

were married I had told Gene I would go to church occassion-
ally, but felt uncomfortable at joining.ln the summer of 1971

the elder of our two daughters was born. We were so thrilled
with the birth, although these were difficult times. And now
with the responsibility of parenthood the division of religion
grew more apparent.

Describe briefly any changes in your views and feeling about
women's ordination from the time you first heard about Section
156 to the time you were ordained, and since your ordination.
When I first heard about Section 156, I wasn't surprised at all.
However, my emotions were mixed. I was excited about the new
document, and I felt that it was true. But I also felt a feeling
of heaviness, for I knew that this excitement would not be shared
by everyone. The changes would be difficult for many thus effecting
all of us.
I didn't realize these changes would so dramatically impact my
own personal life. When I was called into the priesthood in the
spring of 1986, I faced varied responses. Through it all I didn't
change my feelings of acceptance or excitement over the document,
although non-supportive and even rude reactions made it rather
difficult at times. I had to keep telling myself,"Don't take this
personally, Nancy.w Often I would only take it one day at a time;
I would hope that it would be different after my ordination.
After my ordination Section 156 became alive for me. I praised
God that I had made it through all the procedures, approvals, and
personal responses, and now I was just hopping at the chance to
be used in any way that I would be asked. I always felt happy to
be of service when asked to make a home visit, serve communion,
preach a sermon, share a testimony, read a scripture, usher, say
a prayer, or take up the offering. I remember promising God that
I will never say no when asked to help, if at all possible. There
are still times that traditional think~ng or negative responses
cause happenings that still hurt. ( Fairly recently a lady left
just before communion was served, when she saw that I was going
to help bless the emblems)

6. Tell briefly of two Qr three of your experiences Jn ministry
since you were ordained.
The first experience in ministry that was a highlight to me
was in serving the communion. Shortly after I was ordained
I got to help serve the emblems. Communion has always been
a special ordinance to me, and I have always looked forward
to partaking of the bread and the wine. Now I was given the
opportunity to serve in a congregation of people I had loved
for years. Many of who were elderly, and that I felt a close
bond to. Others there were children I had seen grow up since
infancy. I felt such a strong feeling of humility to be able
to offer these emblems of Jesus, to these wonderful people
that represented his body, also. The emblems representing the
blood and the body that had been sacrificed through the love
of our Savior. The people representing the body of Christ,
many times serving through sacrifice, to the community and to
one another. I remember looking into the eyes of many as they
reached for the bread or the wine. The love I felt for those
people that day was over-powering, and I cried, as I served.
This experience made me think;"if we as humans can feel such
a power of love through God's Spirit, can we even comprehend
the magnitude of God's Love?"
Another highlight I have experienced is in making priesthood
visits ... to go into one's home to -listen, to share, and to
express the hope of Christ. On a couple of occassions I have
asked Clifford Miller (Fort Collins) to accompany me on these
visits. Clifford is retired and is in his eighties; Cliff is
a source of priceless experience and enthusiam. I look upon
him as a teacher, for I have learned a great deal from him.
He radiates with the love he feels for God and all of God's

children. I sit in awe when we go and visit people. The sensi-
tivity he has and the timing he demonstrates never ceases to

amaze me. He seems to be able to give what is needed,encourage-
ment, hope, or humor. His main concern is to be there to serve

and to meet a need. The prayers he offers are appropriate,
and most timely. Knowing and sharing with Clifford has been
quite a blessing for me.
The last experience I would like to share is on one of the sermons
I recently gave. I have only given four sermons since my ordination,
so it is relatively a new experience for me. The sermon topic
was on Unity, so diligently for weeks I searched for appropriate
scriptures, poems, and testimonies. This was my first sermon
in my new home of Fort Collins, and being the only priesthood

statement for at least two weeks. Then I struggled with the

house-cat) if they would sit still long enough. I had the message
down to a science so I thought. I was so excited to share the
Word. It would be easy as pie, for I had prayed, prepared and
practiced. So I was ready, or so I thought. That Sunday morning

I like to think that, someday, my great granddaughters and
their peers may read this. For their sakes I'm glad to have
this opportunity to relate how it felt being one of the
first women in the priesthood. I hope they will allow me to
change my opinions on several church related topics between
the time I write this and the time they read it. My views
have changed radically in the past ten years and I suspect
they will change again. I know I am in a time of
transition and in the process of questioning all my previous
beliefs. I wish I had kept a diary because already many of
us, including myself, take women in the priesthood for
granted. So this may not sound "hot off the press".
At the risk of being egotistical, pedantic or
excommunicated, here goes:
I have been active in the church all my life. I started
playing the organ and teaching church school in my early
teens. Since then I have been choir member, branch and
district music director and Christian Ed. director. I have
been Skylark and Orioles leader and responsible for Junior
Church. I have written Junior Church and Christian Ed.
material. More recently I have been Commission Head for
Pastoral Care and Zionic Outreach. I was the second woman in
our branch to be counsellor to the branch president. I was
for Brian Gibson and am now for my husband. Have I
impressed anyone yet?
Living in Africa for two years (August 1973 to August 1975)
separated us from the R.L.D.S. Church but introduced me to
other Christians. At first we attended the Church of the
Nazarene because all the American accents made us feel at
home. It wasn't long, however, before we tried the
Methodist Church and were warmly welcomed and quickly made
friends with several of the English families attending, who

were more often Baptists or Presbyterians rather than
Methodists.

//'

Looking back I think my belief in belonging to "the one true
church" began to wobble then when we attended Wednesday
night study and fellowships in this warm, ecumenical
setting. I felt as close to these people as I felt to
church members and shared with them under what I felt to be
the power of the Holy Spirit. I remember thinking they
would make excellent R.L.D.S.I
But in one thing we kept apart. We did not partake of their
commumion. I think our attitude disturbed them and they may
even have felt insulted. They couldnt understand why we
participated in some ways and not this. I usually played the
organ. Vic sometimes assisted in planning a service ( in 5
minutes when the national minister didn't show up) and once
or twice they even allowed the "reformed Mormon" to speak.
Vic said later that he was getting to the point where he
would have partaken of their communion just before we
returned to Canada.
I very much respected their ministers, who became close
friends but it was not until a few years later that I was
bashed over the head with our concept of the authority of
the priesthood.
Vic and I attended a Marriage Encounter presented by the
Anglican Church. The weekend ended with a communion
service. I agonized whether or not to partake but when the
minister came around to me I refused it. As I watched him
continue around the circle I was overcome by a terrible
sense of shame.It brought tears to my eyes as it still does
when I recount it. What right had I, I thought, to deny that
man's years of ministry. When he and his wife came around
the cirle again,to wish us farewell, I apologized to him and
he offered a prayer of blessing.
Since that time Vic and I have been among the instigators of
resolutions for open communion at World Conference.
For several years prior to 1984 I tried to promote, within
our branch and district, the idea of women in the
priesthood. I talked about it to other women mostly but to
men also because I thought people should get used to the
idea. I was considered by some to be "bucking for
priesthood".
At the same time I was involved in discussions about the
role of priesthood: why do we have it, what ls the
difference between a priesthood member and any other devoted
member. I thought of the people who had nurtured and
influenced me for good in the church and most of them were
women.

f.~ould not see why men who actively participated in branch
ite were usually called to the priesthood but women were
no • If this was the way it should be in God's estimation th en God must regard women as second class citizens. That'
thought was not compatible with my concept of God.
Be~ause I considered women and men equally eligible for
priesthood and when I thought of women who had devoted their
lives to serving our branch, I asked myself, why have
priesthood at all. Women have done nicely without lt for
ages.
In January or February, 1984, before Section 156, Bud Sheehy
of the First Presidency was in our home for a meeting with
District officers. I remember pressing my views about
priesthood upon the poor man and feeling extremely
disappointed as he reviewed some of the commonly held
opinions, not necessarily his, of the problems that would be
created having women in the priesthood. I recall two: what
if a woman were called to a higher office than her husband;
how could a man and a woman priesthood member go priesthood
visiting together, alone in the same car. To say the least,
compared with feeling like a second class citizen, neither
problem weighed heavily with me. Regarding the latter, I
had been travelling to meetings alone with other women's
priesthood husbands for years. Most women, I thought,
would risk either problem!
When I heard Section 156 read at World Conference two or
three months later, I was very pleasantly surprised. I
remember sitting in my delegate seat silently saying,"Thank
you, God."
It's difficult to remember thoughts and feelings I had after
that. I know I thought that having the right to be called
was what really mattered, not being called. I think I was
expecting to be called, as were other people expecting me to
be. Nothing happened for well over a year.
r received my call from Branch President Brian Gibson ln

When the calls were presented at the district business

I received my call from Branch President Brian Gibson in

When the calls were presented at the district business
meeting there were no votes against. Again, there were
several' supporting statements. Two non-resident women in
the district were approved at the same meeting.

Even though my husband and I planned the ordination service,

I remember very little about it a year later. My step-
mother, Verlyn Wood gave the scripture lesson. What I do

remember was the pleasure of assisting Vic in ordaining our
sixteen year old son, Jared, to the office of priest
immediately following my ordination.

I was surprised at my reaction to my call. I was not at all
eager to accept. By that time my feeling was that I did not
want to be separated from my church sisters in this way.
Women had always worked together in the church on an equal
footing and now hierarchy was being imposed. I still
thought it right and fair for women to hold priesthood if
men did (if we must have priesthood, then both sexes must
belong) but I did not want to be singled out in that way. I
was very disappointed when my friend and cohort in church
activities was not called when I was. I could see other
women in the branch whom I thought had as much or more
reason to be called than I had. On the other hand, I wanted
women in the priesthood to start to balance things out. I
risk sounding self-righteous by saying I decided to accept
the call as a duty. I couldn't expect other women to isolate
themselves from their sisters and not do so myself.·
Part of my thinking on this matter was influenced by
sessions I had attended in the early eighties when the World
Council of Churches was held in Vancouver. Women priesthood
from other denominations spoke on such topics as women
priesthood doing things differently than men priesthood.
They said that men have always formed hierarchies while
women are more process oriented: if there is a job to be
done, women get together and do it.
I knew from previous experience that there was often
frustration involved when I was in meetings directed by men
and where most of the attenders were men. I hadn't realized
before the wee sessions that men and women solve problems
differently and consider different things important.
Now r related this to priesthood, which was developed in a
male-dominated society. I guess that is why I question our
hierarchy of different priesthood offices.
For a while I took it upon myself to educate men and women
in church meetings that we do things differently. The men
tried to bear with me in a kindly way but obviously couldn't
see there was a problem. Some of the women knew right away
what I was saying, not all.

/VI- 5
: ~onsider myself lucky having the men in our district that
hav:v: 1 to associate with in branch and district meetings. I . ways felt accepted and appreciated by a large maJority of th w h d em. e ave delt together with good humour
an mutual respect. Even the chauvinists are friendly.
Having decided to work within the system, 1 must admit 1 did
look forward to some firsts: particlpatlng in the
sacr~ments, which were the only things 1 hadn't done
previ?usly, it seemed. Participating in administrations for
the s1~k have been positive experiences. Other than
~ommun1on, the only other sacrament or ordinance 1 have been
1nvo~ved in is the blessing of a child, which was very well
received by the congregation at Reunion. If 1 am serving
communion I try to avoid serving on the side that has people
I know to be opposed to women priesthood.
This does not always work. Once, in a different
congregation where I was the first woman to serve (as I was
in my home congregation), a woman refused communion
altogether because I was serving, even though she was being
served by a male District President. On my side, I saw one
women look at me, look at the tray, nod her head in a
resolute manner and take the bread. A few rows behind, her
daughter was smiling and winking at me with pleasure at
being served for the first time by a woman. An older man
turned his back on me with a decided "NO" and almost assumed
the prenatal position. He died shortly after. In my own
congregation I was not able to avoid serving a woman whom I
thought objected to my priesthood and was pleased to see her
accept with no evidence of displeasure.
I am still waiting for some couple to ask me to marry them.
I don't think my perception of ministry and priesthood is
common at this time in the church. I have been influenced
by my husband's explorations in theology. I compare his
intellectual approach with my father's emotional approach.
I recall previous personal experiences where I thought I was
being influenced by the Holy Spirit. I try to examine those
experiences to find another interpretation. I cannot accept
my first interpretation without some doubt but neither can I
disregard those experiences.
I still cling to the concept of a God. I am busy
rearranging my beliefs about the trinity, scripture,the
church and Christianity. And, of course, priesthood.
At the moment I feel secure in my belief that there ls no
harm in questioning anything that comes to mind. Beneath
all doubt there seems to be an awareness of God as
accepting, loving and understanding.

I attempt to minister with a vague assumption that God can
use me if it benefits someone else. Hy intentions are
honourable. My desire is to help others find joy in life by
finding a sense of self worth, friends and a decent standard
of living. I also wish them an awareness of beauty.
Those are some of the things I personally have and value. I
didn't lose any of these when I lost my belle£ in the one
true church or the authority of priesthood.
I believe my authority as a priesthood member was given to
me by the church body and I try to work within the
parameters of that respected, caring fellowship in such a
way as will benefit its members. I think I'm lucky I was
born into a church that allows for differences of belief.
I feel called of God in the sense that, from all I have
learned so far, I am trying to follow His/Her/Its (I wish
there were an appropriate word!) general intention of good
for all and all for good.
My goodness, this sounds pompous.

Sincerely,

rilyn Suddaby, Elder

that I never dreamed I would be part of just a few years earlier.

At the time of my ordination, my husband was an elder and he was

called me to the priesthood. The day the priesthood cal 1 ings
were announced in our congregation was a very exciting and

was 40 years old I taught the main class at a youth camp. This was
the first time I felt I had done anything on my own that I felt was

really worthwhile. I sat down and cried for the joy of accomplish-
ment but I also cried for remorse for all the years that were al-
ready gone.

I have always felt women had talents and abilities to give but
from my upbringing, felt it was to be to "help the man" in his duties.
I have even in my younger years known several women that I felt had
real leadership and priesthood abilities. However, our culture
would not allow that.
As the church began to explore the question of women and the
priesthood I watched with interest. I became frustrated with the
slowness with which the problem was being addressed. I began to
notice the incompetence of some of the priesthood and their lack of

preparation. I saw young women leave the church when they were con-
tinually assigned to "cook" and/or "take care of the kitchen." I

became aware of the sexist language that was being used. I would
find myself leaving a worship service so angry I wished I had not
attended. It came to the point where I found it very difficult to
attend the Sacrament of the Lord's Supper because of the attitude of
the men involved and my feelings.

In May of 1986, our pastor called and said he wanted to talk
with me after prayer service the next evening. I knew at once what
he wanted. I had a call to the priesthood.
I really did not want priesthood for myself. I had felt for
several years that women should have priesthood and felt I would
hold it sometime, but not this soon. However, I prayed and thought
about my call and decided I would accept. One of the main reasons
was because I had encouraged so many others to accept their calls
and I felt I could not turn down this responsibility.
One of the problems I had were my parents, who were very much
against the ordination of women. My father was on the Stake High
Council at the time my ordination was brought up. I asked my pastor
about my fathers reaction. There seemed to be no problem as far as
he could tell. My father was able to accept my call and was very
pleased and has been very supportive. My mother, on the other hand,
has had a very hard time. She was not necessarily against my call,
but was against the ordination of any woman. She did attend my
ordination however and is able to accept my ministry, but it is still
very difficult for her. I have tried to be very tender with her
and her feelings and yet I have let her know exactly how I feel
the subject. We have sort of agreed to disagree. She is still
struggling and is being more accepting every day.

I was ordained by Nancy Gorden, the first woman ordained in Des

Moines Stake. I asked Nancy for several reasons, one being the ex-
perience I had had when she was ordained. I was also very impressed

with her growth and ability over the few months since her ordination.
I had known Nancy for several years, but now saw her in a different
light.

I wanted to have Kay Ewing (who had been ordained the month be-
fore) to assist Nancy, but felt having two women might hinder my

ability to be able to minister to some people in the future. So, I
asked David Phipps, a well loved Patriarch to assist, so people who
had problems with women in the priesthood would feel I was not to much
of a threat to them.
I was ordained· on Sunday, August 24, 1986 at the West Des Moines
Congregation in Des Moines, Iowa. I don't really remember to much
about the service. As a worship planner, I do remember feeling the
service went very well, but I really don't remember much except the
time while Nancy and Dave were ordaining me. That period of time is
still very vivid to me, but the rest of the service is sort of a blur.
I was very pleased that there were some 40 people from other
congregations there to support me. Three of these were dear friends
from Independence - Marge, Dick and Karen Troeh. I had sent notes
to close friends around the church telling them of my ordination and
inviting them to attend if they could and if not, to remember me on

that day. I received many wonderful notes and letters of love, en-
couragement and celebration from these friends. After the service,

there was a pot luck dinner for the congregation and visiting friends.
It was a time of shared love and celebration.

In September of that year, I was asked to be the Pastor for the
Des Moines Stake Women's Retreat. This was the first time a woman
was able to do this and I was very pleased. By this time we had
several female priesthood in the Stake. I asked Nancy Gorden, an
Elder to be in charge of a prayer and testimony service on Saturday
morning,and I presided over the Sacrament of the Lord's Supper on
Sunday morning. Our cooks at the retreat were men. I asked them
not to join us for either service because we wanted the women to
have the experience of being able to worship as women alone. The
Sunday morning Sacrament service was outstanding for me. I spent a
lot of time in meditation and prayer on that service. I so wanted it
to be special for those women in attendance, and so it was. The
Holy Spirit was there in great abundance. As I read the prayer of
blessing on the bread - I cried. I had never really paid attention
to what it said. I cried for what I was able to ask;God to do for
those women, As I served the bread, I looked at each women. Several
of them met my eyes and I say joy and tears there. One woman gave me
a great big wonderful smile. Another newly ordained Elder, Delores
Versteegh, blessed and served the wine. She also was blessed by her
experience. At the end of the service there was some time to share
with each other. Many spoke of the significance of what we had done
and of the special blessing they felt. Several of these were women
from a congregation that had no women called to the priesthood (as
of this day they still do not). But, they thought our experience
was very special. And so it was.
As we sang the closing hymn of that service, the Holy Spirit
permeated my body in waves, over and over again. I knew then for

I

sure I did have a call and that I did have talents and abilities that
God wanted me to use in this way.

I have preached a few times and have enjoyed the challenge. I
took the Temple School class on Preaching and found it to be one of
the hardest most challenging classes I have ever had. When 1 start
a sermon or talk, I think about it awhile and then start to write
some things down. I always get "bogged" down and frustrated in the
middle of it and wonder what in the world I think I am doing, why
I ever accepted the responsibility to talk and why I ever accepted
my priesthood call. As I keep struggling it all seems to fall into
place and then it is a great joy to see the ministry I am able to
give.

my being a part of the service would keep her from worshiping as she

this woman. When I got up to speak she put her arm up on the pew

Before to long however, I noticed her eyes on me and before I fin-
ished I could see she was crying. I was very glad that she had been

My first sermon (by myself) was given at a small congregation in
our Stake. It was a holiday weekend and several of the congregation
were not there. However, when the worship service started I noticed
the group was even smaller than it had been for Church School. I
was told later that 11 people went home because I was preaching. It

really did not bother me, I was just sorry they were not able to wor-
ship that morning. In the congregation, however, there were about

eight people sitting together that I did not know. Their expressions
told me they were not at all happy I was speaking. They had the
attitude o:f "ok, let's see just what you can do." Part way thru
my sermon I noticed their eyes were glued on me and several were
sitting on the edge o:f their seat. After the service their comments
told me they had been ministered to. I was very happy they were able
to worship that morning with me.
I feel my ministry on the most part has been well received. My
congregation is very supportive, As a congregation, we try to
involve everyone in the activities (classes, worship, social events,

etc.) -- ordained, member, children and even non-members. If the
person is willing to participate, we are pleased to have them. My
husband and son are very supportive of me as I am of them. We are
all three Elders now and it is great fun to work together. While
Paul was home this summer, Jamie was in charge of a service where Paul
and I shared the preaching.
One of the experiences I have had has brought again to me very
strongly the need of women in the priesthood. I became acquainted
with a young woman who had been raped. She has had a very hard time
with this experience. Because of the rape, she has a very difficult
time relating to men of any kind. Some time after the rape she
asked to be administered to. There were four Elders in the house at
the time, two· men·and two women. She asked the women to administer
to her. I feel this helped ease her pain and hurt. We as women
could identify with her feelings. Even tho the men were very kind
and supportive of her, they could never really share her feelings as
another woman could. Since this young woman lives in another city,
I have not had a lot of interaction with her, but have shared with
her when I was there and have written to her. This has been a
special experience for me and was very glad I was a priesthood member
and a female so I could help her thru this experience.
I am very pleased with the participation in the life of the
church that I see women making today. I am very pleased with the

push for Temple School classes and the general upgrading of the priest-
hood both for men and women. I have always felt if a person accepts

a responsibility, they should carry it out to the best of their
ability. r see this beginning to happen more and more. I am also

I am sorry to see the hurt and confusion that there is in the

Being a priesthood member has not always been easy. I have
thoroughly enjoyed my ministry this year. Just the fact that I am
ordained has given me a different status in some peoples thinking.
I don't really understand it but am thankful for it because I find
people give me more credibility now and listen to my ideas more
readily. This also, however, puts a great deal more responsibility
on me and what I say and do.
I feel the ordination of women has been good for the church.
I rejoice as men and women share together. I rejoice as I see
women giving the tender loving ministry that they are able to give.
r rejoice as I watch women grow in wisdom and stature as they find
new ways to use their talents and abilities.
May God continue to bless us as we strive to help each person
develop and share their talents and abilities for the help and care
of those around us.

For a moment, I think I blanked out the reaction of my parents,
si st er and brother. My story would not be complete without sharing
that part of my life. They have been part of a segment of the church
which has separated themselves from the world church. They are
incorporated in another church called the Restored Church of Jesus
Christ. Over a ten year period, along with others they began pulling
away as a result of administrative decisions not to their liking and
later over doctrinal differences that arose due to the growing and
prophetic church. Loving these people as I do, for giving me my
heritage in the restoration, for happy memories of family times of
years gone by, I have grieved over certain barriers which do exist
between us. We continue to talk, share, write and visit as opportunity
permits. But I do experience a great sense of loss of closeness we
once shared. The group about which I have just written totally reject
Section 156 of the Doctrine and Covenants. When I told my folks over
the phone about my call,my sister was angry and said something unkind.
My mother added that she felt the same way but she was not harsh about
it. I did not expect them to be supportive and did Lnot dwell on
their reaction. My father said nothing. We were very close in the
old days. He is proud of my being an educator professionally. He
thought that should be sufficient for me within the church- teaching.
My first assignment as a priest was to read the prayers of the
bread and wine during a communion service. The words were very
full of meaning for me as always and I had to struggle with my emotions.
One man did not take th~, communion that day. It was known he did not
support Section 156. Nothing was said, however. If my participation
was the problem, I felt he needed more time to accept what was now the

September 14, 1987
The first time I heard about Section 156 and women to be in the
prie st hood was at a Wednesday evening prayer service at my local
branch. This was during the week of World Conference, 1984. Our
District President, also a member of our branch, was at Conference
and had called to let a member know how things went. At the prayer
service, the announcement was made concerning the revelation and its
passage. My first thoughts were something like, "Oh boy, now what
are we going to do? This is really going to split the church. What
am I going to do? Who am I going to follow - the Conference action
or the beliefs of our local priesthood? What do I believe?" The
Spirit was very strong at that service. There was much concern and
many of the prayers and testimonies centered on the news of 156.
After the service, my grandmother (without her, no one in our large
family would be in the church today) came up to me and said, "I think
you're going to be in the priesthood one day." My thoughts were,
"Oh, sure, Grandma! Me?" I did not feel any inclinations toward being
a priesthood member and it was not something I desired. I thought that
women in the priesthood would not reach our Pacific Northwest Region
for quite some time. Surely all those appointee and apostles' wives
will get called first.
In the spring of 1985, two women in our district were called to
priest. They were women I highly respected but felt awe that they
were called right in my own district. "What if what Grandma said
came true?" I still had no feelings toward myself being in the
priesthood. In November of 1985 I was downstairs babysitting at our
branch during a choir practice. Our pastor came downstairs and said
he wanted to talk to me. I had no idea what he wanted to say, and I
was shocked when he said I had been called to teacher. He said to
think about it and let him know later. I did not tell anyone, not
even my husband, for a long time. This was something very personal.
I felt overwhelmed. I did not know what to do. I began praying and
meditating a lot. I did not receive any clear "answer." Later, I
told my husband and he was very supportive. He had just been called
to elder. He shared with me the rightness of my call. I needed
more time. Over the next 2 weeks, the Spirit came to me in small,
quiet ways and I began to feel the call was something God wanted me
to do. I accepted the call, but not without a lot of worry and

priesthood would mean in that person's life. There also came the
overwhelming conviction that I would be called to priesthood.
Since that experience at the 1984 Conference the women who
came to my mind have all been ordained. I, too, am now a member
of the priesthood. I was called to the office of elder in May,
1986. The call was accepted by the Audubon Terrace Branch in
Spokane, Washington, in .July. Spokane District accepted the call
in September. And in October I was ordained.
The two years before my call and ordination were difficult
ones. I had felt God's call with great strength for over a year
before it wa·3 presented to me, It was as if a terrific force was
pulling at me, telling me "there wa,3 a task I should be doing.
Yet I had no real way of knowing what the task was or how God

expected me to respond it. I spent much time :-'.i-ri self-
examination: examining my motive:3, my ft~eling:s, my belie 0

fs, my
intuitions. At times I questioned whether there re·ally l,{d::3 a.

..,; /r/1- 2

call or whether it was only ego which made me think so. During
this period I also gave considerable thought to the positions of
close friends within the church who did not believe that Wallace
B. Smith was speaking prophetically when he presented Doctrine
and Covenants 156. I knew my friends felt sure that their
viewpoints were right, and yet such opposite viewpoints could not
both be correc"t. I did not want to close my mind to light and
direction from the Lord concerning the matter, so I prayed often
and tried to remain open to the witness of the Spirit.
Confirmation came again and again that Sec.tion 156 was indeed
God's will for his church. My experience at the '84 World
Conference was also a source of strength, and I continued to feel
that the Lord was calling me to ministry through priesthood. At
first I had no indication of the office to which I would be
called. Then the conviction grew that it was to the office of
elder. I also received a strong conviction that I would be
asked to serve as a counselor in our branch presidency the
following year. This later came about.
During this period there were other women in my district and
my family who received calls. I prayed about these calls, too,
and through the witness of the Holy Spirit it was continually
affirmed that the Lord was moving at this time and in this way to
bring about his purposes for the church and the world.
The time prior to receiving my priesthood call was painful
for me because of the intensity of the call and my struggle to be
sure that what I was feeling was actually from God. It was a
great relief when the call was finally presented to me. Only
then could I find peace within myself. That peace has continued
since.
Three other women were called.and o.r.dained at the same time
as I was. Each of us had different reactions to our calls, but
all of us received confirmation of their divinity. Special
rapport and understanding developed among the four of us, and we
were able to share our feelings and our reactions concerning the
new events taking place in our lives. There was a feeling of
rightness about what was happening and a sense of gratitude to be
called to serve in our own special ways. But there was also
apprehe~sion because of the risk involved in being the first
women to receive priesthood calls in our branch. It was a source
of strength ta know that we did not stand alone in this. We were
together and this encouraged us.
Our ordination took place during the 11:00 hour on October
12, 1986. The Southside Branch joined us for the service.
Evangelist/Patriarch Bill Williams gave the challenge to the
ordinands. My notes show four points of advice which he gave us:
1. magnify your calling (increase its significance) 2. focus
on individuals in your ministry 3. give a reasonable service
4. trust God rather than self. Bill also encouraged us with
these closing words: "The task before you is not as great as the
power behind you." These words have helped me deal with feelings
of inadequacy. When I feel unqualified or unequal to a task, I
remind myself that it is the Lord who has called me to do it.
Through ordination he has given me the power to accomplish
everything he asks me to do,

.. / V1' 3
Thos~ ord>l.ined with me at this service were Donna Lewis and
D~ddie Williams. to the office of priest, and Connie Read to the office of teacher. I was ordained by my husband, High Priest
Keith Townsend. He was assisted by Elder Robert Nelson, pastor at
Audubon Terrace. A special moment for me came after my ordination
when Keith presented me with his bottle of consecrated oil to use
in my ministry. Audubon Terrace Branch held a reception for the
ordinands following the service. This gave us an opportunity to
share with the congregation in a time of fellowship. Many
statements of s~pport were extended to us.
Not all members of our group were supportive, however. The
apprehension which I and the other women ordinands at Audubon
Terrace felt proved to be well-founded. When our calls were made
known, several people transferred their membership to another
branch. These members were all close friends. They said such
things as, "It's nothing personal." But I felt it was personal
in that they must believe I have been deceived. They must think
I am acting contrary to God's wishes and without his authority.
My ministry has been accepted well by the remaining members
at Audubon. I have served actively this year as counselor to the
pastor. I have been involved in these sacraments: communion,
administrations, three confirmations, one baptism, one blessing
of a baby. Several times during the past year I made this
comment: "Every day is a new adventure!" There have been
continual opportunities to give ministry in ways that were new to
me, and I have found that participation as an elder is not at all
the same as participation as a member. Participating in acts of
worship such as communion is quite different when the
responsibility of priesthood has been added. It is amazing how
many times we witness ministry and fail to notice the many
details involved and remember how these are done. Then, too, the
challenge of new priesthood seems greater for women than for men
in that we have no role models or traditions upon which to
pattern our ministry. I am not content to simply mirror the
dress and mannerisms of male priesthood members. I want my
ministry to be an expression of my personhood and that means
there will be elements of the feminine within it. The problem is
also compounded by the idea which I and many other women
priesthood members have that everyone is watching us and
expecting us to do everything perfectly. The thought has been
expressed that when new male priesthood members do something
wrong the fault is attributed to inexperience. Sut when new
female priesthood members make a mistake it is because women
really shouldn't be trying to do the job of a man. While this
attitude is certainly not that of all persons, it is expressed
often enough· (sometimes unconsciously) that many women priesthood
members do feel pressure to live up to the high expectations of
others. In my ministry I have tried to not feel intimidated by
thoughts of being on display, by thoughts that everyone is
watching to see if I will make a mistake, But the thoughts have
been there.
Another aspect of priesthood that makes it hard for women is
that many of us already have a full schedule of congregational
service. It can be difficult to integrate new responsibilities
