n Christian Education, music ministry and worship
planning/presentation. These ministries are all vital to the
life of our branch and cannot be neglected. Yet at times I have
been expected to set them aside because of rostrum ministry.
- In contrast to these negative aspects, serving in the
priesthood has also been exciting and rewarding for me. The
power that was conferred through ordination has touched other
areas of my life. And the experience which l have gained in my
previous church work brings a fresh insight into my new ministry.
I am enjoying drawing upon my background in working with children
and incorporating this into the usually adult-directed ministry
of an elder.
Another positive aspect of priesthood membership has been
association with other priesthood members ip a new and different
relationship. At Audubon Terrace we have 18 active prLesthood
members. Among these are a retired church appointee, two
Evangelist/Patriarchs, one High Priest, and the District Bishop's
Agent. These persons, as well as all other priesthood members,
have been exceptionally supportive and accepting of my ministry.
The first time I stood to preside over a priesthood meeting, I
was keenly aware of my lack of experience in comparison to most
of those who were there. Yet I also knew that these persons
wanted me to succeed. It helps me know that they are willing to
do whatever they can to help me function successfully in my
priesthood responsibilities.
In two weeks our branch will hold its election of officers
for the coming year. The pastor intends to nominate me as
presiding elder. I feel that God has given me some skills which
can be used as an administrator, and I am willing to serve in
this way if that is what the people of the congregation desire.
But I also realize that I am inexperienced in spiritual
leadership. It gives me confidence to know that if I am chosen
by the people to serve as their pastor, I have many strong
leaders upon whom I can call for help and support.
Many persons have been supportive of my call and ordination,
but I appreciate especially that which I have received from my
husband and family. I grew up in a family of three girls. My
mother <Alice Little of Raymond, Washington) never expected to
have children holding priesthood office, so my ordination was a
source of great joy to her. Since that time a sister has also
been called as a teacher. We rejoice in this new avenue of
ministry which has opened within our family.
My husband, too, has been a special support to ue, both
before and after my ordination. As District President he
processed my call when it was submitted. He has also served as a
wise and loving mentor and adviser in my ministry. One of the
most meaningful aspects of priesthood for me has been the chance
to share with Keith in giving ministry. One week after my
ordination a weekend retreat was held for the women of Spokane
District. On Sunday morning Keith and I served together at a
fellowship service, at the closing service of dedication, and in
two administrations. We have shared since then in several more
administrations. We have worked together in planning and

presenting ma~y Sunday morning and mid-week services. We have
also given a· team.·sermon. An especially glad time of sharing was
the blessing of a gra.ndson and the confirmation of a
granddaughter. Our shared ministry has added another dimension
to an already ~ich relationship. I thank God for this new way of
expressing mutual companionship.
Support has come also from both of my children and their
spouses who live in Vancouver, Washington. They are a1i members
of the priesthood: Kris Townsend, elder; his wife, JoAnn,
teacher; Kerry Amos, deacon; and her husband, Dennis, priest. My
ordination completed a family circle of ministers and has brought
further opportunity for shared ministry within our family.
My experiences as a member of the priesthood have assured me.
that the Lord is calling all persons to assist him in his work.
Whether ordained or unordained, there is a task for each one. I
have heard the Lord's call to me. I cannot say no to him.

Dear Carol Anway enclosed the testimony of Telma Pearl Turen on August 13th, 1986 following prayer service I had an appointment with our pastor David Shall in his office. When he told me the Lord was calling me into his holy priesthood office of Priest, I was completely overwhelmed with His spirit of love and humility that the Lord would call “even me into His ministry. My answer came quickly “who could refuse the Lord”? I feel this to be a great responsibility, a great opportunity and I pray for God’s guidance to carry out His desires in service to my fellow-man.
After taking the courses repscived for this office by correspondence (as I am employed part time and couldn’t attend classes), My call was accepted t the fall state conference of the Omaha-CO. Bluffs State on Sept. 28, 1986. (My call was verified by others of the priesthood also). I was ordained at CO. Bluffs Central on Oct. 30, 1986 by my son-in-law High Priest Walter J. Conway of Jefferson City, MO, assisted by Elder Oliver Edris of C.B. Cong? It was a beautiful service and many I’m sure felt the Holy Spirit present. As related in Myrl Dempsey’s testimony, her nephew Elder James Birkby who was participating the service saw personages in the balcony who he identified as Myrl’s family with 2 personages in the back of them who he did not recognize who I feel was my mother, Sarah E. McWilliams (now deceased) a charter member of the Lincoln, NE Congregationand my husband Stephan W. Turpen (also deceased, 1970)(Grandson of M.M. Turpen, an early missionary).

(My husband did not hold the priesthood bt when living in MPIs, MN in 1940s he assisted the Bishop’s agent and also taught a Stewardship Class in the Jr. church of which I was Director, having been appt. To the job when Sr. Lydia Wight moved away.)
(We also hold Church services in our hme after moving to Mankate, MA. once a month as the weather allowe, and Patriarch James A. Thomas from Mpls came down and ministered to as many as 25 people gathered there, many whom had not attended church for years.)

On april 3, 1984, Myrl Dempsey and I were sitting high in the rear balcony f the conference chamber when the document was read, I can honestly say I felt a burning in my bosom and felt the spirit of the Lord was in the message as it came and I have never doubted it since. Sorry, I sometimes lose my patience with people have here-to-for have accepted all revelations, but not this one.

From the years 1964-1974 I as secretary for four appointee pastors to the CB congregation, Elders Victor Witte and Hal Collins, Robert Wood, and Almer Shere. At the same time and for 18 years was recorded for the CO. Bluffs Congregation. I directed the children’s choir for 3 years, I sing in the adult choir and have played violin solos when asked. 

Myri Dempsey and I started visiting the church five or six years ago. Visiting 2 hospitals and four rest homes almost every week, and to some individual homes. We take the communion of the rest homes also, and have served the communion in the morning service, I asked the prayer on the wine, served the PH and handed out trays last communion Sunday. We have also given prayers in both morning worship and prayer services on one occasion when taking the communion to an elderly sister, I took alone Ruth Blane. She is a converted Jewish lady who lives in the retirement home where I am employed. She was baptized over 2 years ago and as her testimony at her baptism, she  sang “He Touched Me” (she was formerly an opera singer in california) and truly everyone at this service was touched. So I was relating this experience to the sister I was serving communion to, and she asked Ruth if she would sing it for her which she did, and we were all blessed once again. Just this week we visited a recently widowed sister and I took along a tale of Harry Black’s, “My Story, My Song”, it seemed to really minister to her, as she hasn’t been able to attend for some time. When making visits in the homes or hospitals we ask if they would like a prayer, and so, since we have been ordained we feel a greater freedom in our ministry and are blessed by the Holy Spirit. 

I feel visiting is my calling, and I find it very rewarding and also humbling to be serving my master. I pray I may continue to be blessed as I attempt to serve when and wherever I am asked.

MY TESTIMONY

Reta Mae Vandel
In processing some of the priesthood calls from our area,
there was a delay due to the absence of some of the World
Church leaders. When the approval came, it was necessary
for the ones called to be contacted for acceptance shortly
before the September District Conference of 1986, subject
to approval by the branch. Both the Springfield District
and the Lebanon Branch voted to approve the calls presented.
There was a great degree of the Holy Spirit felt among the
people as a number of women and several men humbly expressed
their testimonies of willingness to accept the calls and to
seek to serve. It was truly strengthening to me to have my
good companion and my dear sister stand with me, in support,
as I accepted the call to the office of priest. Though
feeling quite inadequate, I felt that with God's help, I
could accept this challenge and would seek to do my best to
fulfill my calling.
We had felt directed to move to Marshfield, Missouri, from
Houston, Texas, in May of 198J. It also seemed God's will
for us to participate in the services of the Lebanon Branch,
which we have been happy to do. One evening in early May of
1986, a prayer service was held in our home. Our Branch

President had been fasting and praying earnestly, we under-
stood, and I felt he was struggling with priesthood calls.

He testified in part of spiritual experiences he had received.
I could sense the Spirit he felt so strongly that under that
power, I knew that should a call come for me, I'd need to
accept.
On November JO, 1986, I was ordained under the hands of
Elder Marvin Rice, who was our Branch President, assisted
by my husband, Evangelist-Patriarch Roy Vandel. There was a
record attendance at the Lebanon Branch on the day of the
ordinations. It was a beautiful service! My ordination was
especially meaningful to me; but I felt very moved, also, when
Evelyn Maples was ordained to the office of priest and Judy
Rice to the office of teacher. As Elder Peggy Vest placed her
hands upon Judy's head, and her words flowed in earnest prayer,
a "special" confirmation of the ministry of women came to me.
I have been amazed that my feelings have changed so much in
the last decade! (Hopefully, I have grown some.)

The 1970's were rather turbulent for our family in various
ways. The Church and its teachings had always held top
priority for my husband and me, and we had tried to instill
this in the lives of our children. Our sons who were both
in the priesthood no longer seemed responsive to their calls,
and this brought us grave concern. Also, reading and hearing

of possible changes in the teachings of the church were some-
what confusing and disturbing to me. Feeling I might hinder

my husband's patriarchal ministry, I felt it was needful for
me to remain silent regarding any doubts or fears I might
have. Church history became very important to me, and I sat
up late many nights studying; but I seemed to become even
more confused and fearful at times. Finally, almost in
desperation, I wrote to the Church Historian. Recognizing
my urgent need for help, Brother Howard very promptly
answered some of my queries, gave me food for thought,and
guidance in seeking some assurances for myself. He suggested
that he thought it might be well to share my concerns and
feelings with my husband. This proved to be good advice, as
he was able to understand and give some counsel, too.
When I first heard about Section 156, my concern was mainly
that it might be very divisive. During the discussion period
prior to the vote on the Conference floor in 1984, there was
a calm, gentle spirit that caused me to feel that I could
accept the document as being true. Further confirmation of
this came at the 1986 Conference in even greater measure.
Participation in Temple School Classes, prayer services,
Reunions, and experiences in my own attempts at ministry
have all helped greatly. These have brought both a sense
of humility and of fulfillment.
Most of the people in our branch have responded in a very
positive way to women's ministry. I felt that the Holy
Spirit assisted in my first sermon, especially, and in

serving of the emblems. The people were loving and support-
ive. Some of my friends in other places have yet to feel

confirmation regarding Section 156, and they have asked
for testimony about my call. I have attempted to comply
in this regard.
My definition of ministry is that of servanthood. It has
always been my desire to serve others. As the work of the
priest deals directly with families, it is my true desire
to assist in bringing the love of God and Christ into the
lives of people.

I felt very fortunate to be among those present in the Conference
~' Chamber during the 1984 World Conference when President Sheehy
read the document we now know as Section 156. I have heard other
documents presented but never an experience like this -- even those

who had a negative reaction to the content must have felt the un-
usual tenseness or tension in the room.

My great-grandfather was the Tom Angell mentioned in church history
as helping establish the church in England so my mother grew up in
the church. My father did not belong to any church but was friendly
toward the R.L.D.S. so that's where we attended and I was baptized
when nine years old. The Newton branch was very small (100 or less),
no church building and no kids my age so as a teenager I wanted to
be with my friends and attended Methodist Youth Fellowship.
At the age of 19 I married a non-member and my indifference toward

my own church continued into my early twenties. My parents encour-
aged us to have our baby girl blessed and we did but our response

ended there. A couple years later our baby boy was blessed -- and
that was that. When our daughter was about 31⁄2 years old my parents
asked if they could start taking her to Sunday School. We said yes.
At some point after that routine got to be established we decided
we should not expect them to take her, that was our job -- so we all
started going to Sunday School.
The branch was meeting in an old, deserted country school building
and there was an old piano. Mary Kirlin was playing the hymns and
felt she wasn't able to do an acceptable job. She begged me to play,
although I hadn't had lessons or practiced for years. She suggested
I could pick the two hymns and have all week to practice and just
play for Sunday School. So began my first step of serving although
we were not even staying for church yet.
Vivian Sorensen came to town a couple years later. My father and
my husband joined the church and both were called to the office of
Elder. In the meantime the branch had moved to meet in the Union
Hall. My father was elected to serve as pastor which continued for
some 14 years. So my experience around priesthood responsibilities
began in 1959.
During the next few years I was trained to print (on Hectograph)
the weekly bulletin, we held Vacation Church School at our house,
served as Women's Leader, we took our turn having Prayer Service
in our home, and of course, by now was playing the piano for all
services.

L

The perfect family -- until 1962. My husband fell in love with a
gal at work, gave up his Boy Scout work, his Eldership and his

family. So I went back to work as a secretary at the Maytag Com-
pany and after resisting finally agreed to a divorce. Out of that

experience and seeking strength for my life I took the children to

Reunion. Our first time, and since no one else from Newton was go-
ing we didn't know anyone until we got there -- than we made friends

fast. I have gone every year since.
There is an interesting story about how I met my first husband's
second wife, we became friends because of Christ's presence in our
lives,she joined the church and served as Women's Leader for our
branch. That was the same time I was District Women's Leader.

Aleta Runkle came to a retreat, stayed at my house and was so im-
pressed with Phyllis and I being friends in spite of the circum-
stances, she planned to share our story as she traveled about.

Five years after our first Reunion (1967) I met a wonderful guy

during Reunion, John Decker, a Deacon in the church. We were mar-
ried that fall. During our 14 years of marriage I learned some more

about priesthood -- this time from the Deacon's standpoint.
During this period the children got married, established their

homes here in town, and started their families. My daughter, hus-
band and three girls are active in the congregation. My son's

two children by his first marriage are members and semi-active,
and his step-daughter is a member and fairly active -- he is not.
John developed cancer, lived four years following diagnosis and
surgery, and died in November 1980. Sorely missed not just by his
family but by the congregation. He had been Bishop's Agent and with
his passing I stepped into this role.
In 1982 I married a fine Luthern fellow and that brings us back
to that morning in the Conference Chamber as the document was

read. My reaction was very positive but I felt what I was hear-
ing ~ould have a lot more meaning for gals younger than myself

~nd in years to come -- did not imagine that at my age it would
involve me personally. And that was fine. I have been allowed
a_get deal of liberty over the years in participating in many

d~ffere~t assignments in the church and could very happily con-
tinued in that way for the rest of my life. Priesthood was not necessary for me to feel of service.

At some point during the spring of 1985 I started receiving a
message that said "If you are called to the priesthood, it will

It,./? Is_)

be ;o the office of Elder." Some time would pass, I would
decide I was misunderstanding something -- but later the message
would come again. I never mentioned it to anyone. After all it
could have been my imagination and my ego.
I had only had this type of experience twice before in my
life. Once (about 1965) it was impressed on my mind that I
might be asked to be District Women's Leader at a time when

we had a good, active leader so the message seemed quite un-
likely. But it happened three times during the week before

District Conference and sure enough, the day of conference
when I walked in Nancy approached me with the news that she
could not continue in the office the next year and would I
accept the nomination.
The other time was when my children were in their early teens

and I was praying that the youth leaders would plan some acti-
vities for the young people while we were in our Sunday evening

class. The message that time was "Why don't you do it?" Not

the answer I wanted to my prayer but I did accept the responsi-
bility and we had a good group for quite a while -- and I still

have that testimony of being led in that manner.
That summer the pastor asked if he could come to the house and
visit with Leo and I. Of course, I agreed. I wondered at the
time if the reason for his visit might be a call to the priesthood.
He came, we visited, as the evening wore on I realized he was just
there to visit, which was good. I chided myself a bit after for
thinking what I did.
But -- the message continued to be there -- not often, but unchanged
"If you are called to the priesthood, it will be to the office of
Elder." Always "If you are called," I have never understood that.
In April of 1986 on a Saturday evening we pulled into the True
Value Hardware parking lot and the Robinos were just getting in
their car. Dave asked "Has Bob got hold of you this evening?"
We replied no. He told us Bob wanted to see us that night.
That seemed a little strange but we went on about our business and
got home about 9:00. Just walked in and the phone rang. It was
Bob wanting to come by. It was not unusual for him to come by but
it was unusual for him to come by that late in the evening. He

came over and got right down to business saying "It will probably
not be a surprise to you to learn that you have been called to

the Melchesedic Priesthood to the office of Elder." Well, as pre-
pared as I thought I was for that to happen, when he said Melchese-
dic Priesthood I felt like everything in my body just melted down.

It was an experience very hard to describe.
Three other women in our congregation had also been called -- Lisa
Robina to the office of Priest, Connie Mccroskey to the office of
Teacher and Jane Gardner (Bob's wife) to the office of Elder.
Orething that bothered many of us was that my mother had died just
a little over a month earlier and just missed this occasion which
would have meant so much to her. Bob had been aware of the callings
earlier but had really struggled with his wife's so the processing
did not go through in time for my mother to enjoy it with us.
I could have given him my answer that night but he had indicated
that Leo and I should discuss it first and I should give my answer
later. Especially since Leo is a Lutheran and doesn't understand
why I am the way I am about being involved, it seemed appropriate
that we discuss it. So the next morning at church I told Bob yes.

We were all a little nervous about the Sunday when the announce-
ment would be made in the congregation. There had not been a

lot of negative comments about women being called but sometimes
it's hard to know what people are feeling. That morning came,
the announcement was made and the reaction was overwhelming --
everyone hugging and kissing and crying it was wonderful!
Some of the girls had not completed all of their Temple School
classes required before ordination so it wasn't set up right away.
I had no reason not to wait for them.
Then the Robinos announced they were moving to New York and Lisa

wanted her ordination to be the same place as the call was re-
ceived so hers took place in July. A lovely service and a very

happy time for all of us.
The rest of us were ordained in August. Bob had tried to impress

on me that I should select whoever I wanted to perform my ordina-
tion. I feel very strongly that in any ordinance it should not

matter who the individual is, but he really pressed me to make a

selection. So I finally commented I would really like Calvin
French because years ago Calvin used to half-kiddingly tell me
if women were ever called to the priesthood I would be the first

female Elder in the stake. Of course, I wasn't but I still remem-
bered those words. I contacted him and after several phone calls

it was determined that he could not get out of a previous commit-
ment. So then I asked Bob.

The ordination service was a memorable experience in itself.
Jane's father, Al Carpenter, is a High Priest and he was there
from Missouri to present the challenge to the ordinands. Connie,

Jane and I each had family present who are not usually at our ser-
vices either because they live out of town or because they are

inactive so that in itself was important to us.
There were three Elders in our congregation prior to this time so
when I asked Bob to ordain me I also asked that the other two

assist him. They were my father, Ezra Cooper, and my ex-brother-
in-law, Glen Oswalt. Obviously, I'd had long-time relationships

with both of them so this also added to the experience.
Each of us were given an opportunity during the service to make a
verbal response. I ended mine by mentioning a few who were with
us that day even though we couldn't see them -- the Robinos, Calvin
French and my mother, who was the one responsible for our whole
family being in the church. That was an emotional moment for me.
r

~· Jane had requested that I assist in her ordination so I was imme-
diately involved in responding to my calling during that very

service.
Obviously, Bob knew we was "second choice" for my ordination.
But, as I told him later and as I told my family, I was sorry
Calvin couldn't be there but he couldn't have done a better job
than Bob did.
One year has passed since the ordination and I could not have
asked for more or better support from the congregation and from
the stake. I have been involved in administering to the sick
four or five times; assisted in blessing a baby; baptized two
people; confirmed one and assisted in confirming one; served
the communion, given two communion messages: one sermon and two

r I-'

r:
theme talks at Prayer Service; have presided at Prayer Service
several times as well as Sunday morning and presided at the final
preaching service at Reunion this summer.
All of those have been positive experiences. One slight problem
occurred at the Baptismal Service at Reunion. It was not my
first, it was my second experience at performing this ordinance.
Three women and four or five men were to baptize eleven people.

When the service (which was well planned) was over I heard some-
one asking "When did the church decree that women would raise

their left hand and men their right hand in performing baptism?"
I was certain he was mistaken so quickly spoke up (he didn't know
I had overheard) and told him I had raised my right hand. He very
kindly corrected me, indicating I had not. This was very upsetting
to think not only I but my other two sisters as well had pulled
such a boner. But everyone I mentioned it to reassured me not to
be overly concerned. In fact, a few people independetly mentioned
that what they had noticed was the reassuring hand the women placed
on each child while the men did not. That helped, some -- but I
will be extra careful not to make that mistake again.
I am so thankful for all the kindness and encouragement that I have
received over the years and particularly this past year. At the
same time I am challenged every time I am asked to participate --

I wonder if it will ever seem easier or that I will be able to res-
pond without advance notice and time for preparation. Having said

all that, at the same time I have felt strengthened and blessed by
the Holy Spirit every time I have been asked to take part.
My heart goes out to those women who have had negative reactions
to their callings. My only negative was when I wrote to friends
who used to live in this area and attend our congregation (in fact,
he was pastor for a time) and knowing they were struggling with
Section 156 nevertheless I invited them to my ordination. Their
response was cordial but stating that since they did not believe
they could not share my joy and sorry I hadn't felt that areas I
had served in the past was enough. That was disappointing to
read that they seemed to feel my calling was a choice I had made
rather than a call from God. But very minor compared to what I
understand some have had to endure.

It is my opinion that while we had an active, fun, worshipful con-
gregation before, that the calling of women has had a very positive

~
be
influence on the group. Not just the ministry that these ladies
have performed but the entire process of hearing the news, some
struggling with it, working through that to acceptance, and the
humility expressed through that acceptance. The whole adjustment
in attitude toward priesthood as a whole that has been prompted
by 156 is a fresh breeze blowing in the church.
While my original reaction to the document that morning in 1984
was one of opportunity for those younger than myself, I find I

am very thankful to be a part of the work during this very ex-
citing and challenging time.

MY TESTIMONY
EVALYN WILLIAMS

During the years preceding 1984 and the World Conference, at which time the
revelation was presented, which became Section 156 of the Doctrine and
Covenants, the questions came to my mind such as "Why are women needed in the
priesthood", "What effect might this have between friends, between husband and
wife", "Will it cause jealousy", "Will it break up homes and family
relationships", "Were some the heirarchy of the church bringing pressure to
promote "Women in the Priesthood". Then my corrmon intellect brought peace of
mind in the fact that God is still in charge, if He has need for, and wants
his daughters in the priesthood, He will reveal this through His Prophet.
In 1984 when God did reveal through the Prophet that there were to be women
called to priesthood responsibilities, explaining that all are called according
to the gifts which have been given them, I was not present at Conference, and,
therefore, I did not receive this under the positive influence that I have
always felt at a World Conference. Upon receiving the news, by word of mouth,
that the revelation calling women to the priesthood had been received, some
doubts assailed my thinking. Temporarily was forgotten the fact that for me to
accept it, it would have to come as God's word revealed through the Prophet.
,..- I was not very verbal with my thoughts, as were some, but I listened to others
Q in their expressions both negatively and positively, with my mind open for

F

light and understanding. Unlike many, I did not pray for light and guidance
because never once have I doubted that Wallace B. Smith is the Prophet of God,
and eventually I remembered, "I will accept women in the priesthood if and when
it comes by revelation through the Prophet". Thus acceptance and inner peace
ca~e. I felt sure I would not be called to the priesthood because I was 67
years old at that time, and this was surely the responsibility of younger
people.
In January, 1986, less than two years after the 1984 World Conference, I
received a phone call from the pastor of our congregation asking if he might
come to visit me. I immediately knew the nature of his call, and yet the
thought kept coming back to me - surely not at my age. I took all of the time
the pastor allowed me to make my decision. The feeling of inadequacy that
enveloped me was overwhelming. I fasted and prayed for assurance. My mind
kept going back to the cold November day in Illinois when I was baptized in
the Mississippi River. That day I promised the Lord that with His help I would
do anything I was called upon to do in service to Him and my fellowmen if I
felt that it was within r,ny capabilities to do so. This is a promise I have
faithfully endeavored to fulfill. Thus I called the pastor and gave him my
affirmative answer.
Three women from Blue Ridge Congregation, along with six other women from
Santa Fe Stake were approved for ordination to the priesthood at the Santa Fe
St~ke Conference held February 1, 1986 in the Assembly Room of The Auditorium.
This conference was attended by approximately 900 people. The votes of the women carried about 500 to 400.

I was ordained to the office of Elder at the Blue Ridge Church in Santa Fe
Stake in Independence, Missouri on Sunday morning, April 27, 1986 by my son,
Elder Roger Williams and Elder Roger Sullivan who was the presiding Elder at
Blue Ridge Congregation. This was an impressive service as I, along with two sisters and one brother dedicated our lives to a closer walk with God and a
fuller service to our brothers and sisters.
In the days preceding my ordination, and also when I was ordained, the support
of brothers and sisters, as well as family, was excellent with some few
exceptions. I recall with joy the evening a nephew from Walnut Park
Congregation came over to visit me after hearing of my call. He fairly
radiated with enthusiasm; probably he did not know what a lift that brought to
me. There were some who did not respond in my presence. I received two
friendly letters telling me they could not support me in my call but assured me
they still cared. I had one very unfriendly phone call.
Since my ordination, my greatest urge is as a peacemaker and in visiting those
whom we seldom, or never, see on Sunday and Wednesday evening at Prayer
Service, but their names are on our roll. I have participated in the Calling
and Caring workshop since I was ordained, and I am eager to reach out to
others. Our congregation is making plans and preparations to launch out in a
structured effort to reach those who, perhaps, are hurting or disinterested.
Some rr~y not even know they are on our roll.
It has been encouraging to me when someone asks me to perform the ordinance of
administration. Also, each Sunday in the Blue Ridge Congregation, two Elders
are scheduled to be available to provide the service of administration to the
sick. The Sunday Order of Worship always lists those who shall be serving in
this capacity, and this service is accepted by those in need of such without
hesitancy. This also gives me a feeling of acceptance.
We have had an exodus from our congregation of those who cannot accept women in
the priesthood, and we have some priesthood members who have made it clear
that they will not serve with women. We have some who will come for the 9:30
service, perhaps teach a class, but will not stay for the 11:00 o'clock service
if a woman is scheduled to be on the rostrum. We have some who will get up and
leave a prayer service if a woman is in charge or is assisting. But as in all
things, there are balances, so is it in the church. The people in the
congregation who suport the Prophet, and who are now the dedicated workers in
the congregation, have drawn closer together as a church family but not to the
point that we shut others out. In fact, the thrust in our congregation is to
reach out and extend an invitation to those whom we seldom, or never, see.
I am very pleased to say that the men of the priesthood in our congregation,
with the exception of those few who will not serve with their sisters, are very
supportive and make us feel very much a part of them.

I am sure that the ministry, to some degree, means different things to
different people. To me the thrust is to reach out to others in love and
understanding and concern. I grew up with no religion in my home, however,
in retrospect, I know that God was an influence in my life even then because
there was a desire within myself to know and to serve Him. He led me in
pathways that brought me to His church restored. When I think of what my life
might have been without God and His church, it causes me to want others to know
and serve God and, thus, have a fuller, richer life. My special concern is
for those whose names are on our roll but who are not availing themselves of
the opportunities to share and to receive in richer, fuller lives.
I believe there are some areas where women may be able to bring a stronger
ministry than men through women's inherent compassion, understanding, and
deeper concern. Also there may be some women, or children, or teenagers who

MY TESTIMONY: BY WANDA WING
I was ordained to the office of Elder August third 1986 by
Brothers John Wolf, Dist. Presid9nt, Raymond T. Persall, and Donald
Studebaker, at our eleven o'clock worship service. My ordination
was approved by our congregation and then at our South Central
District Conference.

I have bean a member of the Reorganmzed Church of Jesus Christ
of Latter Day Saints since 1947. I was active in Zions League. I
have dona a lot of studying and took quite a few Temple School
classes. I didn't know why I took some of them, because I didn't
think I would be using any of them, but the classes were made available
so I took them. I guess God was preparing me even then.

Two years ago I fell twice and had to go to a Chiropractor
and I was laid up all winter. I couldn't walk across the room hardly
so I done a lot of sitting, and it was impressed upon my mind to
study, pray and meditate, and I did. I read so much I felt my mind
couldn't hold anymore, but I continued on and than it ~as impressed
f upon my mind to file my Tithing statement, which I did.

Well, it happened. Our Branch President, my Uncle Ray, went
on a trip and as usual I gathered his mail and would go through it
to see if there was anything that should be shared with our branch.
So I opened up this one envelops, and there was my call, accepted,
you could have knocked me over with a feather!!! And Guilty? I felt
so guilty having opened up that letter. You can be sure I didn't
share it with anyone and when Uncle Ray got back I gave it to him
and told him what had happened. He chuckled and then asked, do you
accept this call?, and without hesitation I said yes because I knew
through out the winter God had prepared me for a different service
than what I had been doing.

So, came the day for my Ordination. I was excited and nervous.
Not only was I ordained, but I served the Sacrament of the Lord's
Supper, and I confirmed Carmen Jennison a member of our Church, it
was a beautiful service because the Spirit of God was there in full
measure and I felt sort of running over.

When the Document came saying women would be called to the
Priesthood, it didn't really shock me, but I wondered "why". I still
don't know the answer. But I do feel God is calling us.

One experience I want to share--- One Sacrament Sunday I was
-I

~
down stairs preparing the Emblems, and as I stood there the Spirit
touched my mind and spoke to me, but to my thoughts, Your hands are
my hands, breaking the bread as I broke the bread for my diciples.
Your voice is my voice as you teach my people. As you serve them
you are serving in my stead, or words to that affect. The Spirit
encompassed me, and I know without a doubt, Ha was pleased with me,
and I know He called ma to serve in this way, and there will be other
ways also, I'm sure.
Since my Ordination I have Ordained Derek Sanders to the office
of Deacon, I assisted in the Ordination of Dianne Sanders to the
office of Priest. I assisted in the Confirming of Clifford Holmes
as a member of our Church. I have assisted in Administration and I
have administered.
Another testi~ony I would like to share is that of my sister,
Wilda Balmes. Wilda had never really accepted the calling of women
to the priesthood, but this one evening of our caring and sharing
service, before we want, Wilda was having physical problems, and she
asked if I would take her home before I went to the service, and so I
said yes, but then she felt she should be administered to and she
felt she couldn't wait until we got to Uncle Ray and Aunt Ruths place
where the meeting was, so she asked me to administer to her. I did.
God knew her love and her faith and he granted har an immmediately
healing. from that time on she believes in my calling to be from God.
I was voted in as Pastor of our Branch here at Vestaburg for 1987.
We haven't had any big problems with women being called to the
Priesthood. We use everyone in our services, little ones, Jr. High,
Senior Highs and all.
I feel I am called to God's work, as a member, as a Priesthood
member, to any work or need I can fulfill. In September I have a
wedding ceremony to do. I'm scared or nervous, but it is God's work
and He'll be right there beside me.
When we walk out in Faith, God is with us. He was certainly with
me at the Wedding. It is my prayer that all who oppose the calling
of women to the priesthood, will seek diligently through prayer,
fastiog, meditation, and study, for assurance and insight into God's
_ffE great plan, and will ba reconciled to the Body off Christ--the Church
once again.
The time to work is now. We are told to put away petty differences
and seek to establish the cause of Zion.
THIS IS EVERYONES CALL!!!

In 1973 I was serving as District Women's Leader for the Arizona District. Along

revelation that would make it possible for women to be ordained.
As the vears went by from 1973 to 1984 I knew of a certainty that this would occur
but as one :vear followed another I began to feel that the Lord was waiting too long

for me. I felt my years of service was on the declining scale rather t~n ascend-
ing scale. I might explain that I have served my congregation and district in many

and children's work. I have been active in women's work, in comm.unity volunteer

this not only takes commitment but it takes lots of energy. In 1973 I was 55 years

had been relieved of his administrative work through his call to Evangelist- Pat-
riarch. So, I must admit it was with great relief that the first women were

ordained and I had not been called. I felt the Lord and I were in 'perfect agree-
ment•-- that it was too late for met I was to be a support to the younger women

that would be called. They would need my support and love and I would give it to them unreservedly by voice and action.
But the Lord and I were not in as 'perfect agreement• as I had thought. Our pastor,
Bruce Moore, told me in the summer of 1986 that the Lord was calling me to the
office of Priest. I must admit I felt a rebellion toward my Lord. I told Him it

con~re~ation so it was not a case of need. I had been teaching, expounding exhort-
ing and visiting in the homes of the Saints for many years. (The only thi~ I had

not done was officiate in the sacraments.) However, there was the knowledge that
the call was of God and there was the 'yes, but--' to my arguings. Yes, but I told

the youn~ priesthood rather than just a support. Yes, but I will give you the

you promised early in your life you would do whatever I ask! I had found that in
trying to do that I had found joy and personal fulfillment. so, I was ordained to
the office of Priest on November 6, 1986 under the hands of my husband, Evan /Pat John!. Wolf and my pastor, Elder Bruce Moore. • •

The Lord has been with me this past year and blessed not only me but those of my

Linda Worts - Priest I 72 - I

1. My ordination was approved in Kansas City Stake in Kansas City,
Missouri on October 19th, 1986 at Park College, Parkville, MO.
I was ordained to the office of Priest on November 23, 1986 at
Central Congregation by Presiding Elder Paul Rounds and my
husband, Priest Jim Worts.
2. My husband holds the office of Priest.
3. On August 1, 1970 Jim and I were married, at which time he held
the office of Deacon. Several months later he enlisted in the
Navy as he was about to be drafted. He served our country in
Norfolk, VA. and Memphis, TN. In both areas I served as assistant
women's leader and women's leader. In Memphis I also served as

the secretary and the pianist. Jim was very active in his priest-
hood and various offices. In supporting his responsibilities and

he in mine we grew together. He taught me many things and helped
me understand scriptures and philosophy of the church.
We served in the capacity of activity directors and tried various
ideas. One of the activities was taking a group to Nauvoo, IL.

with a stop at Independence to see the Auditorium. We also con-
tacted enactives as well as active members in both locations for

the outreach programs.
When we moved to Kansas City, my husbands' hometown, we attended
Central Congregation which was also his home congregation. We
became, and still are, very much involved in the activities. I
have been women's commissioner for the past 31⁄2 years. In this
leadership capacity I have relied on God greatly for direction.
I have tried to do the best I can and utilize my talents and the
talents and interests of others.
I have taught children's classes and we have tried to support the
best we can in all areas of ministry. Because of this I have
gained much knowledge and insight into the functioning of the church.
I cannot ignore the events in day to day life that strengthens us
and makes us realize the need we all have for our Heavenly Father
in our lives. We have many temptations and obstacles that tempt
us to go away from the work of the Lord as well as drawing us
closer to Him.
With my husband being in the Navy it forced us to rub shoulders
with many people, and some of which were nonchristian. It helped
us develop our beliefs and strengthened our faith and need for
God. Prayers became a very important part of our lives:and by
being away from family we had to really take charge of our lives
without their support. Also, my husband was in Viet Nam for
9 months while we were living in Norfolk. The church family became
very important to me. It strengthened bonds that can never be
replaced.
In the moving and travels which we have made we have been uplifted
and directed by God. We know that God was with us and protected
us in our decisions and activities. Our moves were very eventful
and God protected us and directed us each time through car trouble
and relocating which can be very much of a problem.

My ordination was a very memorable experience. My mother,

Orlies Lytton of Jefferson City, MO., my sister and family-
Beverly and Kenneth Spring, Scott, and Rrett of Independence,

MO., and my mother-in-law, Elna Worts-Associate Pastor of the
Spanish speaking Nazarene Church, and my husband, Jim, and our
children- Kevin-7, Brian-4, and David-2 all were present. There
were several church friends who were present for the service in
my behalf.
There were four other people who were called to the priesthood
at the same time as 1 was and were ordained on the same day.
We will always have a closeness in our ministry.
I was ordained on November 23, 1986 at Central Congregation. The
monthly theme was "Thank the Lord for Every Blessing and the
weekly theme was "Give Thanks for Life's Blessings". The call to
service is indeed a blessing from God. The theme used for the
service was "We Accept the Call". High Priest Norman Bernauer
presided and Barbara Bernauer played the organ for the service.
They are close friends of my husbands family which helped the
service be even more special to me. The opening sentence used
was "All are called according to the gifts of God unto them ...
therefore, thrust in your sickle with your might, and reap while

the day lasts, that you may treasure up for your soul everlast-
ing salvation in the Kingdom of God."

The congregation then sang "Holy Spirit" #287. Brother Norman
Bernauer gave the welcome and announcements. A salutation was
sung by Cheryl Gaskin. The Invitation to Worship was II Nephi
9:135-137. The hymn of Praise was "Earth and All Stars" #49
and the invocation was given by Deacon David Essig, a good

friend. I was very nervous during this first part of the ser-
vice, but there was a calming spirit that came over me and put

me more at ease.
The offering was then taken and D. & C. 153:9c was printed in

the bulletin, which reads "Be steadfast and trust in the instrue-
tions which have been given for your guidance. I will be with

you and strengthen you for the tasks that lie ahead if you will
continue to be faithful and commit yourselves without reservation
to the building of my Kingdom". This scripture gives strength
and guidance. This needs to be one for us to remember always as
there will come many times that the tasks before us will seem
impossible, but possible with God.
David Essig then offered a prayer of consecration followed by
Presiding Elder Paul Rounds giving the charge to the congregation
and ordinands. He brought the microphone down on the main floor
and stood somewhat in front of us and our families which made it
a closer knit feeling for me. The message that he brought was
about the spirit within us and how we need to listen to the voice
of the spirit within us and act accordingly. I appreciate Pauls'
ministry very much and his charge has great meaning for me.
There was then a "responsive reading" for the minister, congregation,

JOURNEY WITH GOD
An Ordination Testimony

by
Peggy Young

My love for God and my desire to serve Him goes back as
far into my childhood as I can remember, perhaps age three or
four. One day when I was nine or ten, I was walking with a
friend down the hill between our two houses, talking about
church and religion. In response to a remark I can no longer
remember, I said, "But don't you want to be like Jesus?"
"No!" she replied with disgust in her voice.
I was appalled and incredulous that anyone would not want
to be like Jesus. It was one of my first confrontations with
the "real world." To emulate the Lord was always my highest
aspiration. And to me, emulation meant service.
I grew up attending the Towson Mission in Baltimore,
Maryland. My first perceived service began in my early teen
years, playing the piano for Sunday services. By the time I was
in High School I was also teaching the nursery Sunday School
class, and serving as President of the branch Zion's League.
Every summer I went to the Deer Park Reunion grounds to help as
a C-I-T and later as a counselor.
Later, as a student at Graceland College, I was asked one
year to be one of three student speakers at the Lamoni
congregation on College Day. I felt honored, humbled, and
pleased. I prepared that first, short public message with great
care and prayer. And although plagued with nervousness and
concern, I thrilled at this exciting opportunity.
Through all those years and experiences I always felt
encouraged and nurtured in the areas of my giftedness. I always
felt I had important ministry to do for God, and felt totally
fulfilled in the expressions of that ministry which were
available to me.
Never once did it occur to me that I was somehow missing
out on something because service in the priesthood was not
available to me. I always thought the most important thing I

could do was support my future husband in his priesthood
responsibilites, which I always hoped would include World Church
Appointment. This was my desire, and I was frankly glad that I
didn't have to worry about the onerous responsibility of
priesthood for myself.
Perhaps this is so because in every other aspect of my
life I perceived that no options were closed to me because of my
sex. I credit my parents to a great extent for that perception
and attitude. I always felt in control of my life and able to
make my own decisions. I decided to serve God, and I decided I
did not want to belong to the priesthood. I found happiness and
fulfillment in the expressions of my many other gifts which left
me lacking nothing.
In short, I felt happy to serve in whatever ways other
people desired that service. I never wanted more than that.
As a result, I was truly blind to the frustrations of
other women as they struggled for acceptance and fulfillment. I
could not identify with the Women's Movement at all, and I had
no concept whatsoever of the tremendous importance the issue of
priesthood for women had for many many people.
As the years went by I married Leonard Young, and just as
I'd hoped, I found myself playing the ~upportive role as he
became more and more busy in priesthood and church
responsibilities.
Somewhere along the way, however, an interesting thing
occurred. I became aware that there was a priesthood call for
me. It was to the office of Priest. That realization left me
mostly glad that I didn't have to worry about the possibility
that this responsibility would ever be realized.
In 1978, we moved to Harrisonville, Missouri, where Len
worked as Principal of the High School. During our years there,
Len was ordained a High Priest and served as pastor of the
congrgation. He often told me how it offended him to observe so
many capable and competent women in the congregation and not be
able to utilize their gifts in the priesthood role. It was the
first time I began to think there might be a place for women in
priesthood.

?

Those years for me were a growing time. My leadership
involvement in several nondenominational and ecumenical
organizations forced me to reexamine and reevaluate my beliefs
concerning the exclusive nature of the authority of the
priesthood and the church. I could not deny that I saw the
Spirit of God at work in a mighty way among people of other
faiths.
I began to study the scriptures in a more thoughtful and
examining way. I found passages which supported my more
inclusive views, particularly the Mark 9:36-37 reference where
John is upset because he and some others of the disciples came
across someone casting out devils in Jesus' name. He told Jesus
that they had forbade him, because he was not of their company.
But Jesus said: "Forbid him not ... For he that is not
against us is on our part."
My relationship with God grew stronger and more vibrant as
I allowed His Word to nurture me.
I had a very strong feeling that I was being prepared for
something important. Sometime during those years, I had a
subtle perception that my priesthood call had changed from the
office of Priest to the office of Elder. And something else had
changed. I still wasn't bothered to think I would never fulfill
that call, but the feeling of not wanting to serve in that way
was gone. I knew I would be happily willing to accept such a
call if it were possible.
And yet, this was not the thing for which I imagined I was
being prepared. In 1982, I thrilled as one of my fondest dreams
came true. We were accepted by the Joint Council as an
Appointee family. We began the year of orientation that August.
The wives in the class that year were encouraged to
participate in as many of the classes and activities as we
could. Eight of those ten did choose to participate quite
fully. J was one of those eight. The classes excited and
stimulated me. Again, I sensed being prepared for a greater
ministry. But I never dreamed it might mean priesthood for me.

3

E
j
D
;
0

In the spring we received our first assignment and moved
to Saginaw, Michigan. We found the Saints in Michigan to be
very inclusive and supportive of ministry from all persons.
As the first Christmas approached we decided to go home to
Independence to spend the holidays with my parents who had moved
back there after retiring.
During that vacation we had lunch one day with a friend
who is a World Church official. This lunch became a turning
point in my outlook and attitude toward the women's priesthood
issue.
While discussing this issue, he told us of a female friend
who was on the verge of leaving the church because it didn't
ordain women. I realized for the first time that the church
didn't only stand to loose members if women's ordination became
a fact, but that the church had already lost many members
because it was not a fact. The importance of this issue finally
began to dawn on me.
Later I remembered an incident that had occured the year
before, during one of the Temple School classes we took in
orientation. During the class, the discussion somehow wandered
to the topic of women's ordination. Standing behind a desk top
podium, the instructor leaned over it and exclaimed, "If that
doesn't happen in my lifetime, I just don't know what I'll do!"
Remembering the desperation in his voice, the full impact of his
concern for this issue finally hit me.
Little by little I began to discover people whose whole
sense of worth and dignity and purpose had been eroded by the
church's statement that priesthood was reserved for men only.
Each discovery amazed me. I regretted having so casually
dismissed this issue just because it didn't personally concern
me.
For the first time, I began hoping that it would come to
pass. I wanted it to become reality for the sake of all the
persons hurt and damaged by our exclusive policy, and for the
sake of the church which stood to benefit from this vast store
of potential ministry.

4

Then came the First Presidency's article in the February
"Herald," (1984) about disjunctive revelation, which I read with
great anticipation. After reading the same article, Len laid
down the magazine in front of me and asked, "What does that say
to you?"
"It says to me that we're going to have a revelation
allowing women in the priesthood," I said.
"That's what it says to me too," he responded.
We went to Conference that year with happy expectation. I
never doubted what was to come until the moment came for my
first hearing of the reading of the document at the delegate
session.
I made sure my grandmother. Mabel Davey, came that day and
that we sat together. As President Sheehy began to read the
document, I thought for the first time, "What if it's not
there." That's when I realized how important it had become for
me. I was elated when we finally heard the anticipated message.
I returned to Michigan still not caring whether or not my
call would ever be realized. But I found myself becoming more
involved in responsibilities I had always considered to be Len's
arena. I had already joined the branch Worship Commission and
learned how to plan worship services. I was serving on the
Regional Missionary Commission, and later the Zionic Relations
Commission. I accepted invitations to offer guest ministry at
district women's workshops and retreats, and to speak at
banquets and World Day of Prayer services. I wrote articles for
the "Herald" which were accepted for publication.
The growth I experienced amazed and excited me. I began
to consider possibilities I never had before. Something
different happened inside me. What never mattered before,
suddenly mattered. I wanted to be in the priesthood. I wanted
to serve in ways the priesthood would open for me, particularly
in preaching ministry.

5

Len was the pastor of my congregation, the Center Road
branch in the Saginaw Valley Metropolitan District of the
Michigan Region. Just as many men over the years felt ethically
unable to process priesthood calls for their sons, he felt that
way about processing a call for his wife.
I understood that, and supported his stance, but it
bothered me a great deal to think that my call might not be
realized for a long time. What if Len's assignment were changed
to an area not very disposed to women's ministry, where a call
would not be likely to be processed? Who knows how long it
might be, if ever. I hate to admit it now, but I also wanted to
be among the first ordained on that historic November 17th date.
In addition, after the Conference approval of the document
which was to become Section 156, every time I was involved in
public ministry, especially speaking ministry, at least one
person would approach me and ask, "Why haven't you been called?
If anyone should be called you should be."
Although I appreciated people's perception of my gifts and
their confidence in abilities, it increased my desire for the
realization of my call, and my irritation at it's slowness in
coming.
These feelings disturbed me very much. I saw them as

being exactly what the revelation had warned against: self-
agrandizement. I knew that the very fact that I had these

feelings was indication enough that I wasn't ready to assume a
priesthood office.
Many experiences in my own life and also the testimonies
of many other people, have shown me that God usually seems to
wait until a matter ceases to be of overriding importance to a
person before He acts on that matter.
this.

Such was the case with

I struggled with my desire for this call for over a year.
In the fall of 1985, not long before that November service
ordaining the first women, I finally felt relieved of this
burden. I once again didn't care whether or not my service was
in the form of priesthood or not, at that time or ever. My only
concern was that God would give me something to do, and that I

6

'

would have opportunities to serve in whatever ways the people
might desire that service.
When November 17th arrived, I thrilled to watch the
ordinations of several women whom I consider to be good
friends. I was happy for them on their special day, and I was
happy for the church, which was to benefit from a wonderful pool
of gifts and resources resident in these persons. Best of all,
I didn't wish I had been among them.
Well, it wasn't long after that that I had a sense that my
priesthood call had been processed. I felt very pleased, but
mostly I felt curious to know if my perception had been right,
and wondered how long it would be until I found out.
I waited until March 16, 1986. That Sunday Len and I had
taken separate cars to church because he had to stay afterward
for a short meeting. I took the children home after the service
and began fixing dinner. I was standing at the stove when Len
came through the door waving the letter confirming the approval
of my call to the office of Elder. He was so excited he could
barely contain himself. And I think it disappointed him a
little that my enthusiasm didn't quite match his.
Of course I was thrilled. And not being surprised, I was
ready to accept the call. After all, I'd been thinking about it
for years. But I was happier to think that I had correctly
perceived the voice of God concerning the timing of the call,
than I was that the call had actually been processed and
approved. It confirmed to me that I was in touch with His
Spirit, and that was the most important thing.
I received tremendous support from everyone: family,
friends, and all the church people I associate with. I can't
recall one negative experience. Even some church friends who
don't agree with women's ordination and are very unhappy about
it told me that as long as women were being called, they were
glad I was one of them. And some family members who are of the
dissident faction, simply choose not to talk about it. But it
hasn't impaired our relationship, for which I am very grateful.
Jack Ergo, Sr., who was then the District President and a
member of the congregation, spoke to my call at both the branch
and district business meetings as the one having initiated the

7

call. This proved very significant for me, knowing that Jack is
always very careful about initiating a call.

1 Nov ber 30 1986. at the

Center
My ordination took pace on em . ,
Road branch in Saginaw, along with several other men and
women. Len was still the pastor, and had included all the
ordinands in the planning of the service. It was totally
devoted to the ordination experience, and was a beautiful
worship setting. Gary Beebe, the Regional President, gave the
remarks and participated in some of the ordinations. Larry
Norris, the Regional Bishop, and his family also attended the
service. (Sharon Norris was another of the friends whose
ordination service I attended the previous November 17th.) Len
was the spokesman and Mar gar et assisted in the ordination ~( ,',~
itself.
Not long after my ordination, I jumped in with both feet.
The following Sunday I was privileged to assist in the
confirmations of some of the Hmong people we have been reaching
out to in Saginaw. I gave the Communion message in December,
and was called upon for several administrations. The people
responded with enthusiastic support.
The responsibility of performing the ordinances and
sacraments always has and continues to completely awe me. In
nothing else do I feel so completely humbled and unqualified.
Yet I know that response is required.
We went to Independence for Christmas that year. My
sister attends the Grandview congregation of Santa Fe Stake, and
her youngest son was to be baptized while we were there.
Douglas had asked for Len to baptize him, and me to confirm him.
After arriving I discovered that not only would this be
the congrgation's first introduction to a woman priesthood
member performing a sacrament, it would be the first time this
congregation ever saw any woman, ordained or unordained, give
public ministry. I felt a very heavy burden of responsibility,
which made me a little nervous. I prepared my remarks carefully
and practically memorized them.
Over the phone, the pastor told Len that he had informed
the congregation about this the week before, so anyone who might
be offended or upset could stay away if they wished. I wondered

8

if there would be any troublet but I wasn't too worried. I
hoped not for Douglas• sake. and for the others being baptized
and confirmed that morning (there were eight).
Apparently a few did stay away that morning. However. I
sensed a very good spirit during the service, and no one said
anything untoward to me all morning. There was one gentleman,
however, who made some unkind remarks to my Dad about these
women priesthood members, not realizing he was speaking to the
father of the woman who was to participate that morning.
After the service, when it became obvious that I was his
daughter (Dad assisted in the confirmation), this same man came
and apologized to Dad for his remarks. I thought it took a
really big person to do that, and it really added to the meaning
of that day for me.
After the service. many people, both men and women, both
ordained and unordained, came to me and expressed their
appreciation for my contribution, and for breaking the ice at
Grandview. I felt so happy to have had the privilege of serving
God and these people in that way.
The following summer (1987), Len provided guest ministry
at the Detroit Stake Reunion, and I was asked to preach the
Sunday morning sermon. This was a thrill for me, and again I
received a good response.
Someone told me afterward that one couple got up and left
when I stood up to speak. But someone else told me that there
was a man sitting in the front row who was very much against
women's ordination, who after the service asked if anyone had
taped it because there were some people he wanted to have hear
it.
I focus on the positive response to my ministry, and
refuse to let any negative response adversely affect me.
However, that has been easy to do because there has been so
little negative response. I feel very fortunate to be living in
Michigan at the time of this writing.

Another interesting thing occured at one of the 1987
Reunions as a result of my priesthood status. One evening
several of us had stayed up rather late visiting, so by the time
I got to the bathroom, everything was pretty quiet.
Soon, however, a woman came in with a child whom I had
heard crying out in the trailer area. The little girl had been
stung on the eye earlier in the day, and was unable to sleep
because of the discomfort. The mother had brought the crying
child to the bathroom to wash her eye with water. When she saw
me there she asked if I were an elder, and would I administer to
the child.
I'm not sure, if the church were still not ordaining
women, that she would have said anything to me about
administration, and I'm not sure I would have thought to suggest
it either. But because there happened to be a priesthood member
in the women's restroom, this child received the blessings of
administration and slept through the night comfortably, waking
up the next morning much better, with all the previous swelling
gone.
It touched me a great deal to think that such a blessing
could come from such a simple circumstance.
I believe any person can have an experience with God and
speak on God's behalf as directed by God. I believe priesthood
places an added responsibility on persons to minister in God's
stead. I see it as potential for ministry. To say priesthood
can bring ministry and nonpriesthood can't is a gross
restriction on the power of God to work in people's lives. Each
person is required to serve to their fullest capacity in the
areas of their giftedness.
I image a continuum of potential in the area of ministry.
The higher the degree to which persons respond to and are in
tune with God's will, the greater their potential is for
ministry. But because no one will ever fully understand God's
will, we all fall somewhere on that continuum along with others,
some of whom respond more and some of whom respond less. If any
fail to live up to their potential, persons with less ability

10

who respond more fully may appear to have a greater ministry.
That's why the word "potential" is such an important key word.
But as long as we are moving ahead, that is what concerns
God. And no matter where our place on the continuum, we can
always be of service to others in their journey. And that is
what concerns God.
We should not be so concerned with where we fall on the
continuum, but rather with how we are utilizing the potential
within us, with moving ahead in the right direction, and helping
those around us to also move ahead. This view helps keep me
from the pitfall of self-agrandizement.
And so my journey with God continues. I never cease to
grow in my relationship with Him as long as I keep moving ahead
and keep serving others. I expect this journey to never end, in
this life or the next. My testimony is that responding to God's
call, whatever that call may be, is the most rewarding and
fulfilling experience anyone could ever hope to have. My prayer
is that you, reader, will join me in this glorious quest, and
answer God's call to you with all your heart.

Frances Z.ender

Millbra ., CA ~11-0JO

The San Francisco Bay Stake Conference approved my ordination to the
office of teacher at Fremont, California, February 9, 1S)fl6. John Mldgorden,
Stake Pre~ident, ordained me with Elder Frank Kelley and F.lder Lee Zender, my
husband, assisting.
I seem to have been born with a senoe of responsibility, a concern for
others and a desire to serve in the church, so the ministry of meeting needs
of people since 19 10 is the kind which might indicate that a call to teacrer
•"Ould be appropriate. From attending a few of our Spiritual Growth .Retreats,
I sensed a deeper relationship with the Lord which portended more responsibility
in the future. I have been doing much of the work of teacher in many aspects
for a long time but didn't recognize it as such. I feel my call validated my
previo1.lS ministry.
Alice Buckley and I were the first women in our stake to be called and
ordained. This in itself made it a special service to which many people came
tLat might not otherwise have come. Our friends from many congregations around
were present to support us. There was much love in the group for both of us. It
seemed a bit strange to us because we had never seen a woman ordained before and
I felt both privileged and humbled. There was a "rightness" about the ordinations,
it seemed to me.
I have been in favor of ordination for women for a long time though never
considered the possibility of it happening to me. I had thought of it in terms
of younger, professionally qualified women who had much to give to the church.
It was a shock when the call came to me. I had told the stake president and the
presiding elder they day they discussed this with me that I would need a great
deal of time and prayer before giving them my answer. That same afternoon,
however, I went to my desk, prayed and read my Patriarchal Blessing, seeing in
it for the t"irst time things that led to this call. I told the Lord "yes" and
was almost overwhelmed with an intense feeling of joy. There was no indecision

Zender - page c
about my answer to his call that day nor has there been in the days since. I see
that age has little to do with the call. It seems to be dictated by the needs of
the people and I am blessed with a congregation that accepts me and my ministry.
There have been needs in the congregation for classes in ushering which I am
able to initiate. We have no deacons so there is plenty of work for me to do in
the area of teacher and deacon. I have fulfilled two preaching assignments ••••
I prefer to call them talks. I am in charge of either preparing or assigning others
to prepare the twenty-minute worship before each Communion Service. I am the
leader of the Spiritual Growth Commission in our group and am looking forward to
helping with a witnessing weekend with my husband at Berkeley soon. There has
been opportunity for me to work with persons who have economic problems and some
.-ith homosexual problems. Most of my work deals with individuals with individual
problems. More and more I see areas in which I will be able to help others within
the "teacher" sprectrum.
Alice and I have been most fortunate in that our congregation accept and
work with us. There are one or two members who sit quietly on the fence about
the ordination of women but they are not antagonistic; they share with us as they
always have. Every priesthood member in our congregation came to us when they
heard of our calls and gave us their sincere support. No one in the congregation
or in the stake has been anything except kind to us. I know we are blessed in
this.
I see my ministry at this time as reaching to both members and non-members.
Both groups have problems of all kinds and I help where I can or find the help
they need from others. As teacher, it is essential that I know the backgrounds
and problems of others, visit in their homes and have them in mine, to realize
their worth and to relate to them in positive ways. Of course, there are the
specific duties of the teacher in outward ways but I feel I need above all to
love all the lives I touch and help them.
Before my call, I spread my talents in all directions.~ •• teaching, playing
piano, writing, visiting •••• but now I find it easier to focus my energies and

Zender - page 3

talents in specific ways which sometimes include the above ones. It is sometimes
necessary and now easier to say "no" to some activities. Instead of scattering myself
about, I can "center down" and feel more certain of the areas in which I must
grow and serve.
It is exciting and challenging to be helping the Lord in his work at this
time and in this place.

Frances Zender
